###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Convert MainFrame Data [ EBCDIC ] To Ascii

_(reply depth: 8)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-03-30T00:25:39+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e85fd71.343005989@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3e84ea65.272582631@news.optonline.net> <b639dq$561$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e85b8b7.325409414@news.optonline.net> <b64qbd$u60$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>Robert Wagner wrote:
>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>conclusion and then grabbing any silly argument as trying to support it.

That's called an ad hoc argument. It's really not an argument at all, it is an
explanation. 

Saving space has not been a valid reason for at least ten years. Moreover, I
thought we'd agreed that packed and binary should not be stored on external
media. Thus the only reason to use packed is fast arithmetic .. on machines
which support packed instructions. If you cannot do MACHINE LEVEL arithmetic on
a field, such as a date, there is no reason to pack it. Other fields which
should not be packed are id numbers and postal codes, because it makes no sense
to do arithmetic on them.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Convert MainFrame Data [ EBCDIC ] To Ascii

_(reply depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-31T08:10:54-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b69pae$2mgv$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <b5qalm$dp$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <3e81067a.17589963@news.optonline.net> <b5vdm3$1s87$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3e84ea65.272582631@news.optonline.net>`

```
"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3e84ea65.272582631@news.optonline.net...

> The only reason to use PACKED-DECIMAL is to permit high-speed in-inline
machine
> instructions. If you are going to call external functions, as you do
above,
> there is no speed advantage over DISPLAY. Thus my original assertion
stands:
> dates should not be stored in PACKED-DECIMAL format.

I did not call *external* functions.  I called functions that are
specifically defined as being part of the COBOL language.  If the
implementors are capable of implementing, and choose to implement, them as a
sequence of high-speed in-line machine instructions, they are free to do so.
They might also choose to implement it through some sort of execution-time
external support mechanism function, efficient or otherwise.  And they are
just as free to implement them using robotic control of abacuses if they so
desire.

But in any case, the assertion to which I was responding in this message was
"You cannot add 1 to a date".  That's why it was the only part of your
message I quoted.

I have successfully added 1 to a date without any user-written references to
leap years or 28-day months using standard COBOL constructs.  For machines
that do their arithmetic directly in decimal, conversion is a non-issue; for
machines that do it in binary, I would expect those implementors who
produced either in-line instructions or a "canned" support library for this
function would be doing all their internal work on whatever format was most
efficient, converting only the single input argument and the result.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Convert MainFrame Data [ EBCDIC ] To Ascii

_(reply depth: 8)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-04-02T01:23:33+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e8a1bd5.54672411@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <b5qalm$dp$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <3e81067a.17589963@news.optonline.net> <b5vdm3$1s87$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3e84ea65.272582631@news.optonline.net> <b69pae$2mgv$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3e88e0cf.99790075@news.optonline.net> <b6ca6g$p1b$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:

>
>On 31-Mar-2003, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>So what did it explain?

It explained the principle that only mathematical variables should be stored in
binary or packed format. The rule of thumb test is whether you can logically add
1. Fields which happen to be numeric (today) but are not mathematical variables,
should be display. Examples are dates, part numbers, social security numbers,
telephone numbers and postal codes. If it is complex (dates) or randomly
assigned (the others), it is not a mathematical variable. 

The security industry uses codes named CUSIP and SEDOL (Canadian) to identify a
particular stock or bond (ticker symbols are for the amateur public). There are
over 10 million CUSIP codes. In the good old days, a CUSIP was nine digits
numeric. One might be tempted to store that as 32-bit binary to 'save space'.
When they started running out of numbers, some positions became alphabetic.
Imagine the conversion headache suffered by those who unwisely chose to save
space and 'save money'.
```

On  1-Apr-2003, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:

> It explained the principle that only mathematical variables should be stored
> in
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> assigned (the others), it is not a mathematical variable.

But we have found that it can be useful to add to dates - even dates in YYYYMMDD
format.

I have a program in front of me that was coded years ago with SSN in packed
decimal - to save space.  It's a pain, and nobody would code it that way now -
but costs are different now.    People often code postal codes for the format of
their home country - this is probably more due to ignorance than anything else -
some postal codes have non-numeric characters in them.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Convert MainFrame Data [ EBCDIC ] To Ascii

Robert Wagner wrote:

>>> "You cannot add 1 to a date" was an _explanation_, not a postulate.
>>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> stored in binary or packed format. 

That is just an opinion, not a statement of some magic fact.

Storing data that can be packed into a smaller space is also a principle.

> The rule of thumb test is whether you
> can logically add 1. Fields which happen to be numeric (today) but are not
> mathematical variables, should be display. Examples are dates, 

That dates are part of the group which 'happen to be numeric' is just an 
opinion not some magic fact.  As has been shown, dates are often used for 
calculations: when payment is due, when old transactions can be pruned, 
when easter is.

> part
> numbers, social security numbers, telephone numbers and postal codes. 

Some of which are often constructed with check digits which require that 
they be used in calculations.

> If
> it is complex (dates) or randomly assigned (the others), it is not a
> mathematical variable.

If is only an opinion that dates are 'complex', it is true that some 
adjustments need to be made.
 
> When they started running out of numbers,
> some positions became alphabetic.

In what way is that relevant to dates ?

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Convert MainFrame Data [ EBCDIC ] To Ascii

_(reply depth: 10)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-04-02T12:11:43+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e8ac87f.98880631@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3e88e0cf.99790075@news.optonline.net> <b6ca6g$p1b$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e8a1bd5.54672411@news.optonline.net> <b6djoe$n0l$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>Robert Wagner wrote:

>> It explained the principle that only mathematical variables should be
>> stored in binary or packed format. 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>Storing data that can be packed into a smaller space is also a principle.

Only if there is evidence that saving space would be beneficial, which seldom is
the case today. Memories from the distant past to not apply to today's
environment .. unless you can show a connection. 

>> The rule of thumb test is whether you
>> can logically add 1. Fields which happen to be numeric (today) but are not
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>when easter is.

Not by performing machine-language arithmetic on the date as a single field. 
You could make a better case for treating each date component as an integer. One
byte each for month and day (in binary) and two bytes for year takes the same
four bytes as compressing the eight digit date. Then you'd have integers in a
format suitable for machine-language arithmetic. 

>> part
>> numbers, social security numbers, telephone numbers and postal codes. 
>
>Some of which are often constructed with check digits which require that 
>they be used in calculations.

Calculations on individual digits, not on the field as a single entity.

>> If
>> it is complex (dates) or randomly assigned (the others), it is not a
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>adjustments need to be made.

Hardware designers share my 'opinion'. They gave us instructions to deal with
some complex numbers such as floating point, but not dates. 

>> When they started running out of numbers,
>> some positions became alphabetic.
>
>In what way is that relevant to dates ?

It illustrates difficulties caused by inappropriately compressing 'numbers'.

Suppose the application logic requires you to distinguish between 'date not
entered' and 'date known to be missing'. If the date is display, the former
would be spaces and the latter zeros .. an easy, natural solution. If the date
is compressed, you'd have to use -1, all 9s or some other kludgy workaround.
Experience has shown the errors caused by database 'null indicators'.
```

In article <3e8ac87f.98880631@news.optonline.net>,
Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net> wrote:

[snip]

>
>Not by performing machine-language arithmetic on the date as a single field. 

Mr Wagner, you've repeated this a few times as though it might be some 
manner of Magic Mantra which could possibly save you from being... wrong.

To the best of my admittedly limited knowledge 'machine-language 
arithmetic' is of concern to machine-language programmers and Assembley 
language programmers; since COBOL is a third-generation language and all 
machine statements are taken care of by the compiler then a concern for 
machine-language statements seems to be irrelevant.

If a concern for machine-language statements is irrelevant then you are, 
once again, wrong.

If concern for such speed *is* relevant then one is dealing in an 
environment where speed is a paramount  concern.  If speed is of a 
paramount concern then all activities are to designed to maximise speed. 
Now... if reading fewer characters takes less time than reading more 
characters and storing dates as packed-decimal takes fewer characters then 
in order to increase the speed of the system (a paramount concern, 
remember, since one bases an argument on the ability to add 1 in a single 
machine-language statement to a date) one should pack dates... and you 
are, once again, wrong.

Please be so kind as to disprove the errors you see in the assumptions or 
conclusions presented here.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Convert MainFrame Data [ EBCDIC ] To Ascii

_(reply depth: 12)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-04-03T01:11:24+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e8b8742.147723556@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3e8a1bd5.54672411@news.optonline.net> <b6djoe$n0l$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e8ac87f.98880631@news.optonline.net> <b6eq0e$1gg$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
\docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

>In article <3e8ac87f.98880631@news.optonline.net>,
>Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net> wrote:
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>manner of Magic Mantra which could possibly save you from being... wrong.

argument 1
>To the best of my admittedly limited knowledge 'machine-language 
>arithmetic' is of concern to machine-language programmers and Assembley 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>once again, wrong.

argument 2
>If concern for such speed *is* relevant then one is dealing in an 
>environment where speed is a paramount  concern.  If speed is of a 
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>conclusions presented here.

Both arguments fail because their premises (which you call assumptions) are
incorrect. 

Argument 1's stated premise is that machine language is irrelevant. If that were
true, there would be no need for a PACKED DECIMAL type. The compiler would take
care of optimization 'under the covers'. In fact, the programmer needs to
control the format of fields so the compiler can exploit fast machine
instructions in in-line machine-language code. 

Argument 2's fallacious premise is that memory fetches are proportional to the
number of bytes being fetched. That was true 20-30 years ago. Today's machines
fetch through 2-3 layers of cache in widths bigger than a byte .. usually 64
bits at a time. Moreover, instruction execution times are often SLOWER for small
variables, such as one byte, than for the machine's preferred word size.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Convert MainFrame Data [ EBCDIC ] To Ascii

_(reply depth: 13)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2003-04-02T20:40:03-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b6g3dj$7f3$1@panix1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3e8ac87f.98880631@news.optonline.net> <b6eq0e$1gg$1@panix1.panix.com> <3e8b8742.147723556@news.optonline.net>`

```
In article <3e8b8742.147723556@news.optonline.net>,
Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net> wrote:
>\docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
>
…[38 more quoted lines elided]…
>Argument 1's stated premise is that machine language is irrelevant.

A concern for what can be done with a given machine language is irrelevant 
to a third-generation language, correct.

>If that were
>true, there would be no need for a PACKED DECIMAL type. The compiler would take
>care of optimization 'under the covers'. In fact, the programmer needs to
>control the format of fields so the compiler can exploit fast machine
>instructions in in-line machine-language code.

The programmer needs to control the format of the fields not in order to 
invoke or restrain from a given machine-language statement but in order to 
balance requirements; in this case the requirements balanced are those of 
exportability versus size.  Packed decimal takes less space; that 
was, in the experiences many have related, the advantage most frequently 
required of it.
>
>Argument 2's fallacious premise is that memory fetches are proportional to the
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>variables, such as one byte, than for the machine's preferred word size. 

Those are interesting assertions; please be so kind as to document them.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Convert MainFrame Data [ EBCDIC ] To Ascii

_(reply depth: 14)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-04-04T01:59:12+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e8cbd68.227133476@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3e8ac87f.98880631@news.optonline.net> <b6eq0e$1gg$1@panix1.panix.com> <3e8b8742.147723556@news.optonline.net> <b6g3dj$7f3$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

>In article <3e8b8742.147723556@news.optonline.net>,
>Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net> wrote:
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>>>>Not by performing machine-language arithmetic on the date as a single field.

>>>
>>>Mr Wagner, you've repeated this a few times as though it might be some 
…[35 more quoted lines elided]…
>>true, there would be no need for a PACKED DECIMAL type. The compiler would
take
>>care of optimization 'under the covers'. In fact, the programmer needs to
>>control the format of fields so the compiler can exploit fast machine
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>>bits at a time. Moreover, instruction execution times are often SLOWER for
small
>>variables, such as one byte, than for the machine's preferred word size. 
>
>Those are interesting assertions; please be so kind as to document them.

Here is a timing test for arithmetic on 1, 2, 4 and 8 byte binary variables.
Note that 2 byte is twice as slow as 4 byte on a Pentium 3, exactly the same
(within measurement error) on a Pentium 1. Also note that the P3 is 10 times
faster than the P1, although cpu clock speed is only 5 times faster. The
additional speed comes from memory caches. 

 identification division.
 program-id. test22.
*> author. Robert Wagner.
*>  Testing arithmetic speed -- 1, 2, 4 and 8 byte
*>  Findings:            SPARC    PC  PC no opt  100MHz
*>   loop overhead       2.65   1.42    1.00      7.09
*>   one byte            1.50    .35     .06      3.51
*>   two byte            2.52    .60     .11      3.46
*>   four byte           3.42    .35     .09      3.67
*>   eight byte                  .01*    .42
*>  Environment: SPARC   450 MHz, Micro Focus, OPTSPEED.
*>               Pentium 500 MHz, Fujitsu 3, Opt and NoOpt
*>               Pentium 100 MHz, Fujitsu 3, Opt
*> * Optimizer did a single add rather than looping 10M times.
*>   Putting an IF inside the loop slowed it to approx. 1.50
*>   The same optimizer should have deleted the loop overhead loop
*> Note that optimized is 4-6 times SLOWER than non-optimized
*>
 data division.
 working-storage section.
 01  unqualified-variables.
     05  one-byte-number    comp-5         pic s9(02).
     05  two-byte-number    comp-5         pic s9(04).
     05  four-byte-number   comp-5         pic s9(09).
     05  eight-byte-number  comp-5         pic s9(18).

     05  test-name                         pic  x(20).
     05  start-time.
         10  start-hours                   pic  9(02).
         10  start-minutes                 pic  9(02).
         10  start-seconds                 pic  9(02).
         10  start-hundredths              pic  9(02).
     05  end-time.
         10  end-hours                     pic  9(02).
         10  end-minutes                   pic  9(02).
         10  end-seconds                   pic  9(02).
         10  end-hundredths                pic  9(02).
     05  repeat-factor value 100000000 comp-5 pic s9(09).
     05  loop-overhead value zero binary   pic s9(09).
     05  elapsed-time             binary   pic s9(09).
     05  elapsed-time-edited               pic  z(04).99.

 procedure division.
 main.
     move 'loop overhead' to test-name
     perform start-timer
     perform repeat-factor times
         continue
     end-perform
     perform stop-timer
     move elapsed-time to loop-overhead

     move 'one byte' to test-name
     move zero to one-byte-number
     perform start-timer
     perform repeat-factor times
         add 1 to one-byte-number
     end-perform
     perform stop-timer

     move 'two byte' to test-name
     move zero to two-byte-number
     perform start-timer
     perform repeat-factor times
         add 1 to two-byte-number
     end-perform
     perform stop-timer

     move 'four byte' to test-name
     move zero to four-byte-number
     perform start-timer
     perform repeat-factor times
         add 1 to four-byte-number
     end-perform
     perform stop-timer

     move 'eight byte' to test-name
     move zero to eight-byte-number
     perform start-timer
     perform repeat-factor times
         add 1 to eight-byte-number
     end-perform
     perform stop-timer
     display eight-byte-number

     goback

. start-timer.
     accept start-time from time
. stop-timer.
     accept end-time from time
     compute elapsed-time =
        (((((((end-hours * 60) +
           end-minutes) * 60) +
           end-seconds) * 100) +
           end-hundredths) -
        ((((((start-hours * 60) +
           start-minutes) * 60) +
           start-seconds) * 100) +
           start-hundredths))
     compute elapsed-time-edited rounded =
         (elapsed-time - loop-overhead) / 100
     display test-name elapsed-time-edited
```

docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

>In article <3e8cbd68.227133476@news.optonline.net>,
>Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net> wrote:
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…

…[36 more quoted lines elided]…
>>>>true, there would be no need for a PACKED DECIMAL type. The compiler would
take
>>>>care of optimization 'under the covers'. In fact, the programmer needs to
>>>>control the format of fields so the compiler can exploit fast machine
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>>>>Argument 2's fallacious premise is that memory fetches are proportional to
the
>>>>number of bytes being fetched. That was true 20-30 years ago. Today's
machines
>>>>fetch through 2-3 layers of cache in widths bigger than a byte .. usually 64
>>>>bits at a time. Moreover, instruction execution times are often SLOWER for
small
>>>>variables, such as one byte, than for the machine's preferred word size. 
>>>
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>actually deal with people who take this kind of discussion seriously?

I posted source code so that serious investigators could duplicate my results,
or not, on their own platform. I posted results on a Sun SPARC which has no
relationship to Intel. 

It's easier to sit in the background throwing stones than to participate.Doing
so doesn't add credibility to you nor your arguments. Put up or shut up.
```

Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net> wrote:
>docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
>
…[88 more quoted lines elided]…
>relationship to Intel. 

Mr Wagner, you were asked for documentation, not for materials for an 
experiment; when you posted your documentation you referred to a Pentium 3 
(Intel) and a Pentium 1 (Intel).

>
>It's easier to sit in the background throwing stones than to participate.Doing
>so doesn't add credibility to you nor your arguments. Put up or shut up.

Mr Wagner, it seems that we have different ideas about what constitutes 
'documentation'.  You made an assertion about how 'today's machines' dealt 
with memory fetches and when asked to document this posted some source 
code and the results for a single architecture.

When this was pointed out you bleated the equivalent of 'do my homework 
for me'.

I can see little of value to come of this, Mr Wagner; as shown above your 
argument based on a single machine-language instruction appears 
inappropriate when applied to third-generation languages and your argument 
about 'memory fetches' is undocumented beyond the functioning of a single 
program on a single platform.  It seems, then, that you are, once again, 
wrong on both counts.

Your request that others demonstrate your assertions for you are noted, Mr 
Wagner, and given all the attention they appear to deserve.  You may, if 
you desire, consider further response to this matter to be beneath your 
dignity.

DD
```

  The fanatic is perpetually incomplete and insecure. He cannot generate
  self-assurance out of his individual resources -- out of his rejected
  self -- but finds it only by clinging passionately to whatever support
  he happens to embrace. ... It goes without saying that the fanatic is
  convinced that the cause he holds on to is monolithic and eternal -- a
  rock of ages. Still, his sense of security is derived from his
  passionate attachment and not from the excellence of his cause. ...
  The fanatic cannot be weaned away from his cause by an appeal to his
  reason or moral sense. He fears compromise and cannot be persuaded to
  qualify the certitude and righteousness of his holy cause.

                     - Eric Hoffer, The True Believer

> >Large records can be slower to read than small records.   We don't read
> >fields,
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> like it was about fetching memory.

It could be - if the data were never fetched from anything other than RAM.   I
have used packed numbers for calculated & incremented fields that needed to be
compared to data on records.

If he was referring to records, then it wouldn't make sense that the data was
not fetched nor stored on something more permanently.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Convert MainFrame Data [ EBCDIC ] To Ascii

_(reply depth: 11)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-04-02T14:59:33+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b6etsl$idc$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3e88e0cf.99790075@news.optonline.net> <b6ca6g$p1b$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e8a1bd5.54672411@news.optonline.net> <b6djoe$n0l$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e8ac87f.98880631@news.optonline.net>`

```

On  2-Apr-2003, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:

> >Storing data that can be packed into a smaller space is also a principle.
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> environment .. unless you can show a connection.

It is a principle (by your definition) either way.

Costs have changed enough so that we no longer create SSNs in packed format -
but not enough that it is worth while converting existing data for the sake of a
new principle alone.

I'm thinking of a weird example.   Let's say you have a database where it would
be very expensive to increase a record size (I'm sure you can come up with some
cases where this is true).   You have a mandate to replace the SSN with a new ID
(which prints on reports), but you still need to be able to pull up data by SSN,
as the customer remembers this.   So you need to stick an extra 9 characters in
your record - which doesn't have room for them.  Next year this whole system
will be replaced.     Can it be cost effective to pack the ID or SSN to fit it
on the record?

Certainly.   Even today.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Convert MainFrame Data [ EBCDIC ] To Ascii

_(reply depth: 12)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-04-03T01:11:24+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e8b87ae.147831069@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3e88e0cf.99790075@news.optonline.net> <b6ca6g$p1b$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e8a1bd5.54672411@news.optonline.net> <b6djoe$n0l$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e8ac87f.98880631@news.optonline.net> <b6etsl$idc$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:

>I'm thinking of a weird example.   Let's say you have a database where it would
>be very expensive to increase a record size (I'm sure you can come up with some
>cases where this is true).   You have a mandate to replace the SSN with a new
ID
>(which prints on reports), but you still need to be able to pull up data by
SSN,
>as the customer remembers this.   So you need to stick an extra 9 characters in
>your record - which doesn't have room for them.  Next year this whole system
>will be replaced.     Can it be cost effective to pack the ID or SSN to fit it
>on the record?

Adding a new field to a database is very easy. It sounds like you are referring
to indexed files which you have 'promoted' to database status. If so, don't
chastize me for inprecise terminology. 

If I'm correct, the solution is a new file keyed by the 'new ID' and pointing to
the SSN in the old file. If you encapsulated access to the SSN-keyed file, as
you should have, a query to it would try the new file first. If that failed, it
would revert to the old file. The 'new ID' could be implemented by changing a
single program.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Convert MainFrame Data [ EBCDIC ] To Ascii

_(reply depth: 13)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-04-03T14:55:11+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b6hi0e$nus$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3e88e0cf.99790075@news.optonline.net> <b6ca6g$p1b$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e8a1bd5.54672411@news.optonline.net> <b6djoe$n0l$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e8ac87f.98880631@news.optonline.net> <b6etsl$idc$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e8b87ae.147831069@news.optonline.net>`

```

On  2-Apr-2003, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:

> >I'm thinking of a weird example.   Let's say you have a database where it
> >would
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> chastize me for inprecise terminology.

I am not.  I was referring to IDMS.   But it can apply to files just as well.  
If lots of programs are using a file, changing a record size can be costly.  
Lots of times files will have filler available for future needs (and we have
IDMS records with filler for that same reason).   When the filler runs out, we
try to find ways to conserve on space.

So I can  chastize (sic) you for inprecise (sic) terminology  (or spelling)?

> If I'm correct, the solution is a new file keyed by the 'new ID' and pointing
> to
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> single program.

But you're not correct.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Convert MainFrame Data [ EBCDIC ] To Ascii

_(reply depth: 14)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-04-04T01:59:16+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e8cc1bf.228245019@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3e88e0cf.99790075@news.optonline.net> <b6ca6g$p1b$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e8a1bd5.54672411@news.optonline.net> <b6djoe$n0l$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e8ac87f.98880631@news.optonline.net> <b6etsl$idc$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e8b87ae.147831069@news.optonline.net> <b6hi0e$nus$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:

>
>On  2-Apr-2003, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>> >cases where this is true).   You have a mandate to replace the SSN with a
new
>> ID
>> >(which prints on reports), but you still need to be able to pull up data by
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>try to find ways to conserve on space.

IDMS is not a modern relational database. It is not much better than indexed
files. It was a good alternative to IMS/DB when it originated in VSE days, 25
years ago. Now it's just a cash cow for CA. 

>So I can  chastize (sic) you for inprecise (sic) terminology  (or spelling)?

When I see see spelling errors in quotations, which I do frequently, I correct
them without comment.

>> If I'm correct, the solution is a new file keyed by the 'new ID' and pointing
>> to
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>But you're not correct.

It is still the correct solution. You shouldn't have a zillion programs reading
the IDMS file; you should have one called program.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Convert MainFrame Data [ EBCDIC ] To Ascii

_(reply depth: 15)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-04-04T14:58:42+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b6k6j1$cu6$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3e88e0cf.99790075@news.optonline.net> <b6ca6g$p1b$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e8a1bd5.54672411@news.optonline.net> <b6djoe$n0l$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e8ac87f.98880631@news.optonline.net> <b6etsl$idc$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e8b87ae.147831069@news.optonline.net> <b6hi0e$nus$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e8cc1bf.228245019@news.optonline.net>`

```

On  3-Apr-2003, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:

> IDMS is not a modern relational database. It is not much better than indexed
> files. It was a good alternative to IMS/DB when it originated in VSE days, 25
> years ago. Now it's just a cash cow for CA.

It does the job efficiently and reliably.

And your criticism of it has nothing to do with the thread.


> It is still the correct solution. You shouldn't have a zillion programs
> reading the IDMS file; you should have one called program.

My experience is quite contrary to this.  Being dependent upon one called
program has caused us lots of problems - especially when modifying that called
program gets too expensive (testing everything that uses it).   We had a called
program that accessed a KSDS file and all it did was hide problems.

But let me ask you - why should we have one called program?   Are we supposed to
always access our data the same way?  Do you have any idea how a
Hierarchal/Network database works?
```

>

…[4 more quoted lines elided]…

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Convert MainFrame Data [ EBCDIC ] To Ascii

_(reply depth: 17)_

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-04-05T12:30:18+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e8ebe55_2@127.0.0.1>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3e88e0cf.99790075@news.optonline.net> <b6ca6g$p1b$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e8a1bd5.54672411@news.optonline.net> <b6djoe$n0l$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e8ac87f.98880631@news.optonline.net> <b6etsl$idc$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e8b87ae.147831069@news.optonline.net> <b6hi0e$nus$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e8cc1bf.228245019@news.optonline.net> <b6k6j1$cu6$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e8e842b.7566313@news.optonline.net>`

```
Howard,

There are some important points here that relate to previous discussions on
how OO could improve things in your environment.

I wasn't going to get involved in this because I don't believe any minds
will be changed, but as it has come up and it's Saturday....what the
Hell.....<G>

Comments below.

Pete.
"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3e8e842b.7566313@news.optonline.net...
> "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> >
Agree 100%. (At least in principle... you might need several such modules to
handle Owner/Member sets and traverse the relationships.)

> >My experience is quite contrary to this.  Being dependent upon one called
> >program has caused us lots of problems - especially when modifying that
called
> >program gets too expensive (testing everything that uses it).   We had a
called
> >program that accessed a KSDS file and all it did was hide problems.
>
I believe you may have learned the wrong lesson from this experience. Using
layers between your application and the database is sensible and useful. If
you had problems with the KSDS and it simply "hid problems" are you sayng
that these problems would not have arisen if the access code was distributed
all through the applications? At least you could deal with it in one place.

You have stated many times here how important it is to you that regression
testing of everything occurs if a module is modified, and that is your
prerogative. But I don't see how duplicating this functionality throughout
an application avoids regression testing, any more than moving it to a
separate layer of the application design...? Using an encapsulated solution
does NOT preclude regression testing, but I would question the need to do
so, once the encapsulated functionality has been demonstrated to work proper
ly.

I am NOT suggesting that modules which use the modified function shouldn't
be tested, but I believe this can be limited to "main process flows" just to
establish credibility, rather than 1000s of programs just because they MIGHT
call this function SOMETIME. If the design is done properly, modifying the
internals of an encapsulated function should NOT be problematic.

If the INTERFACE to it changes, then you have a point. This comes back to
something Jimmy Gavan raised recently about using passed parameters when
invoking Objects, or driving them instead by pre-setting Properties. I have
learned through experience (and it is only MY opinion and experience; others
may see it differently) that using Properties is the way to go, largely
because it has less impact on the INTERFACE and therefore avoids the need to
regression test EVERYTHING if encapsulated functionality changes.

If your applications are such a jumble of functions that you can't risk NOT
re-testing when one encapsulated function is modified, I would argue that
there is a strong case for reviewing how things are getting built in your
environment.

> You have a 'testing culture' that punishes one for writing reusable code.
Your
> test methodology is not universally accepted/practiced and is out of step
with
> modern software design.
>
I agree again...

> >But let me ask you - why should we have one called program?
>
> Encapsulation.
>
Yep!

> >Are we supposed to always access our data the same way?
>
> No. That's the beauty. You can have multiple 'logical views' rather than
the
> single one returned by IDMS.
>
> True relational databases offer the same by default.
>
OK, this is slightly off the beam, but Robert stated he doesn't know about
IDMS (which really doesn't support "views" if my memory serves me correctly.
I last used it 20 years ago...<G>)

To answer your question, Howard. No, you obviously don't always access your
data in the same way, but there are a finite number of specific accesses
against a given set, which COULD be encapsulated (and reused) into a single,
general, dataset handler. Even if this was only done for maintenance
functions (those that modify the data) you could ease some of the
maintenance burden. Distributing (read only) access to IDMS sets throughout
your applications is not such a bad thing (although I personally wouldn't do
it...) because if one particular "fetch" is wrong it becomes apparent during
testing and can be fixed. When you are talking about update functions, this
is much more critical and there is a very good case for encapsulating them
into a separate reusable layer.

OO COBOL could be used to provide such a layer and you could have multiple
instances of an "update object" being used by your applications, but you
would only maintain the source in one place.

I don't know how you feel about "general solutions" but I like them and use
them wherever I can. Layered Database maintenance, whether it is for
Hierarchic, Network, or Relational DB support, fits nicely in this category.

> >Do you have any idea how a Hierarchal/Network database works?
>
> Nope. Too many polysyllabic words for me to grok. Your grandmother might
be
> impressed.
>

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Testing of OO or common called routines was Re: Convert MainFrame Data [ EBCDIC ] To Ascii

_(reply depth: 18)_

- **From:** "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-07-08T11:17:10-03:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3F0AD266.CA61E27F@istar.ca>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3e88e0cf.99790075@news.optonline.net> <b6ca6g$p1b$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e8a1bd5.54672411@news.optonline.net> <b6djoe$n0l$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e8ac87f.98880631@news.optonline.net> <b6etsl$idc$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e8b87ae.147831069@news.optonline.net> <b6hi0e$nus$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e8cc1bf.228245019@news.optonline.net> <b6k6j1$cu6$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e8e842b.7566313@news.optonline.net> <3e8ebe55_2@127.0.0.1>`

```
I hope that I won't have snipped the wrong things in making the
following points on testing.

"Peter E.C. Dashwood" wrote:
> 
> Howard,
…[35 more quoted lines elided]…
> all through the applications? At least you could deal with it in one place.
Having worked in 2 shops where all batch subroutines were dynamically
linked (execution time binding), I can say it depends.  Even if the
change was purely for efficiency with not change in inputs or outputs,
it was very difficult to change a called subroutine because of the
reluctance to change without testing with all callers.  This meant that
the organization put up with status code 97 not being treated as
equivalent to status code zero on open for input or open for i-o in
common VSAM file handling routines.  If a change is to affect all
callers of a sub-routine (or invokers of an object) and is to affect all
callers, it is actually easier to sell than one that has limited scope
and only affects some callers.  Note that most organizations have
effectively less stringent testing for operating environment changes but
even there, the roll out requirements for a new release or even a set of
fixes can be noticeable in some environments.  I suspect that Howard's
environment is one of them.

It is interesting that JCL (batch script) changes including sort control
record changes many times require far less testing than would comparable
changes to programs.  

If early binding (nested program or non dynamic calls for literals as
opposed to variable fields) is the default and there is an automatic
regression testing for all changes, then changes to commonly used
sub-routines or objects may be easier to manage if the change is
supposed to affect only a small group of callers (invokers).
> 
> You have stated many times here how important it is to you that regression
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> ly.
If you have early binding you can schedule the implementation of the
change gradually rather than have it affect all callers/invokers
immediately upon implementation.   In other words all changes are
intentional, they don't just happen.  In shops that are unintentional
change averse, any time a problem happens after a dynamically invoked
module is put into production that module will be the suspect.  I am
unimpressed by the idea that encapsulation is a real bounding.  Note
that more accurate output can be bad if not expected or previously
explained, hence the various options for handling intermediate results
in Microfocus COBOL.  
> 
> I am NOT suggesting that modules which use the modified function shouldn't
…[54 more quoted lines elided]…
> into a separate reusable layer.
Frankly, for I-O, unless you are going to use a common access module so
that you can access the file as a logical entity which can then be
changed into accessing a data base by just changing the sub-routine, I
don't see the advantage of having separate I-O routines modules for each
file / data base table unless you are going to enforce common checking
such as record counts or other special processing.  The additional
overhead is not great but it is definitely measurable.  If you are doing
it for files, you probably have almost as much coding in the caller to
handle the various legitimate returns as if you were to code the I-O. 
>From what I have seen of Oracle and DB2, if you are using views to
restrict the access to just what is needed, the value of a common
routine seems to be missing.  Even if the code is more general in that
effectively the same set of accesses is done even if some of the columns
aren't needed, obscuring what is going on by using a call or invocation
doesn't seem to be the way to go.  The main use I can see for a common
module is where a series of data base tables is replacing a single
multi-record format file.    
> 
> OO COBOL could be used to provide such a layer and you could have multiple
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Testing of OO or common called routines was Re: Convert MainFrame Data [ EBCDIC ] To Ascii

- **From:** "Harley" <dennis.harleyNoSpam@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2003-07-09T16:56:08+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<IOXOa.46307$3o3.3133563@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3e88e0cf.99790075@news.optonline.net> <b6ca6g$p1b$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e8a1bd5.54672411@news.optonline.net> <b6djoe$n0l$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e8ac87f.98880631@news.optonline.net> <b6etsl$idc$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e8b87ae.147831069@news.optonline.net> <b6hi0e$nus$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e8cc1bf.228245019@news.optonline.net> <b6k6j1$cu6$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e8e842b.7566313@news.optonline.net> <3e8ebe55_2@127.0.0.1> <3F0AD266.CA61E27F@istar.ca>`

```
Much snipage, a few comments.

"Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca> wrote in message
news:3F0AD266.CA61E27F@istar.ca...
| Frankly, for I-O, unless you are going to use a common access module so
| that you can access the file as a logical entity which can then be
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
| such as record counts or other special processing.

Most of the systems I've seen have filler in records to allow for the
addition of fields without having to change the record length.
Unfortunately, the fillers often get used, and the record has to be
expanded.
This CAN be easier to accomodate when there is an I/O module, since every
program that uses the file may not have to be modified.

| The additional
| overhead is not great but it is definitely measurable.  If you are doing
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
| routine seems to be missing.

Sometimes a common routine eases the conversion to a RDB, but it should be a
short term solution.
DB2 etc, are not VSAM, or ISAM.
Selective queries that return what is needed in terms of filtered
selections, and data elements are what make the RDB faster.
i.e. You can select 3 columns for open orders from the orders table, rather
than reading all the columns for each order row, and selecting the open
orders in a program.

Even when you use a view, you may want to return a subset of the view
contents.
A View can be considered to be a "common routine" in that the logic to join
data from more than one table can be included in the View, as well as
selection (filtering) criteria.

|  Even if the code is more general in that
| effectively the same set of accesses is done even if some of the columns
| aren't needed, obscuring what is going on by using a call or invocation
| doesn't seem to be the way to go.

I agree.

| The main use I can see for a common
| module is where a series of data base tables is replacing a single
| multi-record format file.

Even then, with a RDB, a join should handle the situation.
You might want to use a View  that contains the join.

The power of the RDB is that the I/O process runs independently of, and
concurrently with the application program. In general, reading table A, and
manually joining table B using random access tends to slow things down.
```

Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>Robert Wagner wrote:
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>know.

If my arguments were shallow, they would simply be ignored. People are angered
when the things they love, or at least are comfortable with, are threatened by
plausible arguments. They think, 'Gee, what if this guy is right?'

>> You have a 'testing culture' that punishes one for writing reusable code.
>> Your test methodology is not universally accepted/practiced and is out of
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>confirmed to be still working correctly.

If that were true, we wouldn't have utilities called by thousands of programs.
Nor would we have OO class libraries invoked by millions of programs. 

So long as the interface doesn't change, there is no need to re-test every
program. I've changed date utilities at very large companies and put 'em into
production after testing on a test bed and perhaps one application. 

If you think reusable code is crap, I (and the computer community in general)
would be interested in hearing the business case. 

>If you think that another methodolgy is _actually_ universally practiced, 
>rather than this just being a hollow assertion of yours to make you look 
>important and knowledgable, then please do provide references.

When I try to discuss testing methodology here, it falls on deaf ears. In
particular, how to compute sample size for positive- vs. negative-finding tests.
Evidently, software testers don't believe statistics applies to them. 

>>>But let me ask you - why should we have one called program?
>> 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>improved ease of maintenence, better performance.

At shops I managed, there was an access module for every indexed/database file.
When the file format changed, programming took an hour or less. The access
program returned at least two formats: the 'legacy' one and the new one. When
the file changed to a _completely_ different format, e.g. indexed to multiple
tables in a database, the change took a day or two. The access program could
still return the legacy record format.

Using traditional approaches, a copybook would be changed and affected programs
recompiled. Who knows what they are? Traditional shops don't maintain
dependencies in their CM system, as they should. They wind up using grep or its
mainframe equivalent to find references, and praying there aren't
quasi-production programs in private libraries, which there always are. Then
they think they have to re-test all systems. 

Maintenance goes from months to one day, with proportional cost savings. 

Performance is not better, it is very slightly slower. 

>>>Are we supposed to always access our data the same way?
>> 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>also be done by other solutions.

Then why did Howard say changing  the record size in his IDMS database was a
major headache?

>ad hocery.

My argument is not ad hoc, it is based on 25+ years of program maintenance in
large shops. 

>>>Do you have any idea how a Hierarchal/Network database works?
>> 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>ego boost.

How ironic, coming from an 'expert' in ad hominem argumentation. 

I will say this. You don't waste time with cheap shots such as spelling errors.
You go for the jugular almost every time, with substantive issues. Are you
practicing an acerbic form of 'Socratic approach?' I picture Grumpy Old Men (the
movie) sitting at computer keyboards.
```

On  5-Apr-2003, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Convert MainFrame Data [ EBCDIC ] To Ascii

_(reply depth: 19)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-04-07T23:41:55-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0304072241.4c6838a9@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3e8cc1bf.228245019@news.optonline.net> <b6k6j1$cu6$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e8e842b.7566313@news.optonline.net> <b6nfrj$g6c$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e8f522d.60312500@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote

> If my arguments were shallow, they would simply be ignored. People are angered
> when the things they love, or at least are comfortable with, are threatened by
> plausible arguments. 

You flatter yourself if you think that your arguments are in any way
plausible.

> They think, 'Gee, what if this guy is right?'

No. Wrong. I think: 'what is some poor innocent sees these and thinks
it has any validity at all'.

I am not arguing to convince _you_ of anything, I realise that it is
beyond my skills to shake your firmly held convictions even when it
can be shown you are wrong.  I am primarily concerned with providing a
buffer so that 3rd parties can  see a balanced view.

>>Crap.  All changed programs require to be system tested before being
put
>>back into production.   All interactions with changed programs must
be
>>confirmed to be still working correctly.

> If that were true, we wouldn't have utilities called by thousands of programs.

How do you figure that ?  If the utility is _changed_ then all
interactions with it should be confirmed to be working correctly.  How
hard is that to understand ?  Of course the whole point of having
utilities is that they have been proven and remain static.

> Nor would we have OO class libraries invoked by millions of programs. 

Now you show that you misunderstand the basis of OO.  You wouldn't
change a clss library and have to then retest all that use it.  You
would inheret to a new class that used the original for all the
existing behaviour and added its own new behaviour by overloading the
function.  Then only those programs using this new class need to be
retested.

> So long as the interface doesn't change, there is no need to re-test every
> program. I've changed date utilities at very large companies and put 'em into
> production after testing on a test bed and perhaps one application. 

Yes, you probably have done this.
 
> If you think reusable code is crap, 

No, it was your opinion that I called 'crap'.

Let us see what I _actually_ said: 

>> All changed programs require to be system tested before being put 
>> back into production.  

Don't you think that changed programs should be tested before being
put back into production ?

>> All interactions with changed programs must be 
>> confirmed to be still working correctly.

Don't you think that 'interactions with changed programs' should be
'confirmed to be still working correctly' ?

Now, where exactly did you 'dream up' where I claimed anything against
reusable code ?

> When I try to discuss testing methodology here, it falls on deaf ears. In
> particular, how to compute sample size for positive- vs. negative-finding tests.
> Evidently, software testers don't believe statistics applies to them. 

Given that you attempted to pass off 3 samples as some form of
statistics that could be extrapolated to general usage then I don't
think that _your_ statistics would be applicable.

In general terms statistical samples are not of much use for testing. 
What is required is _specific_ samples, samples that exercise the
exact code being tested.

> >And, of course, you also try the 'put-down' as a way of elevating your own 
> >ego boost.
> 
> How ironic, coming from an 'expert' in ad hominem argumentation. 

You are learning _something_ then.
```

riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote

>Don't you think that changed programs should be tested before being
>put back into production ?

Depends what you mean by 'changed programs'. If the appliclication code was
changed, of course it should be tested. If the change was in called utility or
class library method, the change should be tested but not every invoking
application. Not if the interface spec didn't change. 

To insist that every application be re-tested, you're punishing programmers for
reusable code. That's the wrong incentive.

>> When I try to discuss testing methodology here, it falls on deaf ears. In
>> particular, how to compute sample size for positive- vs. negative-finding
tests.
>> Evidently, software testers don't believe statistics applies to them. 
>
>Given that you attempted to pass off 3 samples as some form of
>statistics that could be extrapolated to general usage then I don't
>think that _your_ statistics would be applicable.

If Sigma (standard deviation) was zero, ONE sample would be sufficient. 

>In general terms statistical samples are not of much use for testing. 
>What is required is _specific_ samples, samples that exercise the
>exact code being tested.

That's true of positive-finding tests, but in the case of negative-finding large
samples are very important. You don't 'get it'. You use a single methodology for
all tests.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Convert MainFrame Data [ EBCDIC ] To Ascii

_(reply depth: 21)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-04-09T14:39:11+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b71bae$1r0$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3e8cc1bf.228245019@news.optonline.net> <b6k6j1$cu6$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e8e842b.7566313@news.optonline.net> <b6nfrj$g6c$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e8f522d.60312500@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0304072241.4c6838a9@posting.google.com> <3e938d07.192333028@news.optonline.net>`

```

On  8-Apr-2003, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:

> To insist that every application be re-tested, you're punishing programmers
> for reusable code. That's the wrong incentive.

That's the wrong incentive if the goal is to have reusable code.

But the customers could care less about whether the code is reusable.   All they
care about is whether their programs work.   They are paying for the code and if
something is broken they don't want to know why their program wasn't tested.

There is a problem in deciding how much testing is necessary.   We can never
achieve 100% confidence in a moderately complex system.   How costly are more
tests vs solving more problems?   The solution is a WAG that the users have to
agree upon.   Actually it is multiple WAGs.   We should not have one standard
for testing for the whole enterprise - certain applications require higher
standards than other applications.

So when should we have reusable code?
If the only purpose is to save 20 minutes in writing the code in a new program -
I would pass.
If it is important that many programs follow a business rule exactly the same
way - reusability becomes an issue.
But business rules get changed.   And business rules get overridden for some
members.   If you end up having 8 variations of an object for the 8 divisions of
your company, what have you gained?   It may have been cheaper (and safer) to
have 8 different objects.

An environment that has objects has a big advantage over an environment that
doesn't.   It can easily choose reusability for objects.   But it is important
to know the objects and to make informed decisions about them instead of
automatically using an existing object "because reusability is good".   It isn't
always the best decision, especially when the objects are business rules.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Convert MainFrame Data [ EBCDIC ] To Ascii

_(reply depth: 21)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-04-10T00:15:18-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0304092315.49faf62@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3e8cc1bf.228245019@news.optonline.net> <b6k6j1$cu6$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e8e842b.7566313@news.optonline.net> <b6nfrj$g6c$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e8f522d.60312500@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0304072241.4c6838a9@posting.google.com> <3e938d07.192333028@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 

> Not if the interface spec didn't change. 

Well of course the interface spec changes: different functionality,
different results, new methods, or just corrections.  If the interface
isn't identical then what did the change do ?

> To insist that every application be re-tested, you're punishing programmers for
> reusable code. That's the wrong incentive.

That is why a different approach is used.  Withh OO the change id done
by deriving a new class.  The original class continues to be used by
existing applications and thus no re-testing is required.  The new
derived class gives the new behaviour to programs that need this and
thus thet require re-testing.

The same can be done with called routines by having a filter that
recalls down to the utility for existing behaviour and provides some
new behaviour.
Of course at some time this may get rationalised back into the
original code, during a general system revision perhaps, when it will
all be re-tested anyway.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Convert MainFrame Data [ EBCDIC ] To Ascii

_(reply depth: 22)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-04-11T03:28:24+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e95fefa.352600089@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3e8cc1bf.228245019@news.optonline.net> <b6k6j1$cu6$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e8e842b.7566313@news.optonline.net> <b6nfrj$g6c$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e8f522d.60312500@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0304072241.4c6838a9@posting.google.com> <3e938d07.192333028@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0304092315.49faf62@posting.google.com>`

```
riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>> To insist that every application be re-tested, you're punishing programmers
for
>> reusable code. That's the wrong incentive.
>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>all be re-tested anyway.

That's only one dimension removed from the original maintenance issue. Suppose
you have three client companies -- A, B and C. You write three overloads, one
for each. Then you get a fourth client, D, who wants the 'business rules' of
both A and C. You cannot do that with the linear model, so you write a fourth
overload, D, which combines A and C. Next the business rules for A change, so
you update module A. Whoops, you forgot to apply the same changes to D. We're
only one level removed from redundant code. 

I once worked at a service bureau which had a killer magazine app. Every time
they got a new client, they copied the base programs and modified them for the
client's business rules. When I arrived, they had 20 'systems' running, each
with its own set of files and JCL. The daily cycle took 18 hours. I rewrote it
to one set of programs (and files) which used conditional logic -- IF customer =
A or C, do this. The daily cycle was reduced to 2 hours.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Convert MainFrame Data [ EBCDIC ] To Ascii

_(reply depth: 21)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-04-11T13:35:29-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0304111235.5a3d1bb0@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3e8cc1bf.228245019@news.optonline.net> <b6k6j1$cu6$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e8e842b.7566313@news.optonline.net> <b6nfrj$g6c$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e8f522d.60312500@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0304072241.4c6838a9@posting.google.com> <3e938d07.192333028@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 
 
> >Given that you attempted to pass off 3 samples as some form of
> >statistics that could be extrapolated to general usage then I don't
> >think that _your_ statistics would be applicable.
> 
> If Sigma (standard deviation) was zero, ONE sample would be sufficient. 

Let us examine this claim slowly:

 * You took 3 samples and found '80% wasted space'.

 * You then extrapolated this to _all_ IBM sites (so that you could
criticise)

 * You then assumed that because _all_ IBM sites were like this that
the deviation was zero.

 * Now you claim that because the deviation is zero then just one
sample is sufficient.

Statistics isn't your strong suit is it.  Nor is logic.
Now if I had run a program on 3 sites that had different data and got
the same answer with no deviation, I would be examining my program for
serious flaws, such as truncation.

While disk sizes are in fixed increments determined by the
manufacturer, the size of the actual data is determined by several
factors which are external to the computer, such as number of
customers, number of orders, point in the monthly cycle, time of day,
etc.  It would also depend on other factors such as record sizes,
number of indexes, use of temporary files, and dozens of others.

I would expect different answers on the same site, even on the same
disk, depending on _when_ I ran the program.

Your claim is zero deviation over 3 sites. 

Now obviously this result suits your prejudice, so you not only
believe it, but you probably reinforce it by 'adjusting' or discarding
parts of the results to suit.

Or perhaps you just made it all up anyway.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Convert MainFrame Data [ EBCDIC ] To Ascii

_(reply depth: 22)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-04-12T17:17:46+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e98442d.114845950@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3e8cc1bf.228245019@news.optonline.net> <b6k6j1$cu6$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e8e842b.7566313@news.optonline.net> <b6nfrj$g6c$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e8f522d.60312500@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0304072241.4c6838a9@posting.google.com> <3e938d07.192333028@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0304111235.5a3d1bb0@posting.google.com>`

```
riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 
> 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>Let us examine this claim slowly:

> * You then assumed that because _all_ IBM sites were like this that
>the deviation was zero.

Don't put words in my keyboard. I said IF it was zero. That's a hypothetical.

> * Now you claim that because the deviation is zero then just one
>sample is sufficient.

You set 'em up; you knock 'em down.

>Statistics isn't your strong suit is it.  Nor is logic.

I encountered someone like you once before. He'd go into a newsgroup and attack
the highest volume poster. And he would attack the target's strengths rather
than his weaknesses. I didn't understand his motivation then nor yours, now.
Just an observation.

>While disk sizes are in fixed increments determined by the
>manufacturer, the size of the actual data is determined by several
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>disk, depending on _when_ I ran the program.

When the number and variety of applications is large, those differences tend to
cancel each other out. In a mainframe, time of day is probably the most
significant because batch jobs are usually run at night. 

>Your claim is zero deviation over 3 sites. 

It's not my claim; it's your straw man.
```

robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote

your 'save pennies, waste pounds' assertion and supported this 'claim'
with extrapolations from 3 samples based on zero deviation.
 
> > * Now you claim that because the deviation is zero then just one

you had also claimed to be an authority on Cobol.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Convert MainFrame Data [ EBCDIC ] To Ascii

_(reply depth: 27)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-04-14T12:33:26-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0304141133.277b0408@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3e98442d.114845950@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0304121345.428f6de2@posting.google.com> <3e98ab3e.17028524@news.optonline.net> <b7dbsp$rvn$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e9a633d.41378064@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 

> >You do know how to use 'margin of error' don't you ?
> 
> Based on the above evidence, I understand it better than you. It's called
> Confidence Interval (CI) 

No. Wrong. 'Margin of Error' is _not_ called 'Confidence Interval',
they are actually different things. The Margin of Error is primarily a
factor of the sample size, and is used with the sample results to
calculate the 'Confidence Interval'.

> and it cannot go over 100%.

Of course it can't when any _competent_ statistician is working, with
you it could go anywhere.

When you have used such a tiny sample the Margin of Error is right off
the scale and may well be infinite for all the meaning the results
have when applied, as you did, to all mainframe sites.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Convert MainFrame Data [ EBCDIC ] To Ascii

_(reply depth: 28)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-04-15T02:50:41+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e9b63c1.107055704@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3e98442d.114845950@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0304121345.428f6de2@posting.google.com> <3e98ab3e.17028524@news.optonline.net> <b7dbsp$rvn$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e9a633d.41378064@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0304141133.277b0408@posting.google.com>`

riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>calculate the 'Confidence Interval'.

If you want to show your knowledge, please explain the difference between alpha,
the probability of a false positive, and beta, the probability of a false
negative. They're quite different. One is a factor of the sample size and the
other isn't. Do you know which is?

>> and it cannot go over 100%.
>
>Of course it can't when any _competent_ statistician is working, with
>you it could go anywhere.

Flammage is a weak substitute for hard answers. 

>When you have used such a tiny sample the Margin of Error is right off
>the scale and may well be infinite for all the meaning the results
>have when applied, as you did, to all mainframe sites.

Rant on.
```

Richard Plinston wrote:
<snip>
> 
> And when you extrapolated these 3 samples to the rest of the mainframe 
> sites (20,000? 50,000?) in order to make you "save pennies, waste pounds" 
> generalised insult the margin of error you took into account was what ?  
<snip>

There were ~30,000 "MVS" licenses in 1997. Theoretically, that 
number was increasing at that point (for the first time in years 
it was increasing rather than being stationary or slowly sliding 
down).

It was thought at that time that there were 2-4 times as many VSE 
users (not everyone kept their licenses paid -- they were stable 
and didn't need the maint).

The number of VM licenses at that point was roughly the same as 
MVS licenses.

So, let me see, 3 samples compared to ~30,000 MVS licenses, or 
~90,000 IBM type Mainframe systems... Hmmmmm. Would that fall 
under statistically insignificant?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Convert MainFrame Data [ EBCDIC ] To Ascii

_(reply depth: 27)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-04-15T11:15:23+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e9bddc2.138293371@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3e98442d.114845950@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0304121345.428f6de2@posting.google.com> <3e98ab3e.17028524@news.optonline.net> <b7dbsp$rvn$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3E9B5D2D.4060809@ix.netcom.com>`

```
Steve Thompson <n_o_spam.steve_t@ix.netcom.com> wrote:

>Richard Plinston wrote:
><snip>
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
>under statistically insignificant?

There is a common misconception that sample size should be a percentage of the
population. For the most common tests, that's incorrect. Arbitron and others
measure 100 million TV viewers by sampling a few hunded. Medical research
measures large populations by sampling a few dozen. 

The factor determining sample size is variation. Suppose you had 100 million
widgets and were asked to determine their average weight. Suppose you knew,
somehow, they each weighted EXACTLY the same. How many samples would you need?
The answer is one.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Convert MainFrame Data [ EBCDIC ] To Ascii

_(reply depth: 28)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-04-15T12:52:22-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0304151152.4a1d82e7@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3e98442d.114845950@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0304121345.428f6de2@posting.google.com> <3e98ab3e.17028524@news.optonline.net> <b7dbsp$rvn$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3E9B5D2D.4060809@ix.netcom.com> <3e9bddc2.138293371@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 

> The factor determining sample size is variation. Suppose you had 100 million
> widgets and were asked to determine their average weight. Suppose you knew,
> somehow, they each weighted EXACTLY the same. How many samples would you need?

> The answer is one.

No. Wrong. the answer is _none_, you already know what they weigh if
you know it is all the same.  There is no need to measure any if you
_KNOW_ the answer.

In the case of your wanting to criticise mainframers you _started_
with the CONCLUSION that _all_ mainframers are 'wasters of space',
'old school', 'archaic', mis-managed, manualless, marginalised and
many other things that you have collected over the decades.

Now it just may be that you are all these things (especially
marginalised) and through projection you rationalise that EVERYTHING
is exactly the same as your personal experience.  Otherwise you might
have to think of a reason why you were given all the crap jobs in
poorly managed parts of marginal sites.  It might actually be _your_
fault.

So you construct this idea that ALL sites are the same, ALL
programmers are a bunch of idiots who tell you to fuck off, ALL
managers are incompetent in not recognising your brilliance.

If they are all identical then you only need one sample.

Of course that they are 'all identical' is just your emotional
reaction to decades of bitterness and disappointment.

(why are you a marginalised grunt coder after all the wonderful things
that you claimed you did ?)
```

riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 
>
>> The factor determining sample size is variation. Suppose you had 100 million
>> widgets and were asked to determine their average weight. Suppose you knew,
>> somehow, they each weighted EXACTLY the same. How many samples would you
need?
>
>> The answer is one.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>_KNOW_ the answer.

I didn't say you know how much they weigh; I said you know they're all the same.


Is your reading comprehension or ability to deal with abstractions as bad in
real life as it is here?

>In the case of your wanting to criticise mainframers you _started_
>with the CONCLUSION that _all_ mainframers are 'wasters of space',

No IBM was a space waster. They used to have an economic incentive. So they
delivered systems designed for users (programmers) to shoot themselves in the
foot. 

>'old school', 'archaic', mis-managed, manualless, marginalised and
>many other things that you have collected over the decades.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>fault.

I'm trying to deal with facts; you keep reducing issues to personalities ..
whose fault is it. who's guilty. My approach is rational; yours is emotional. 

Emotion can deal ONLY with personalities. When confronted with non-human
entitities, it first invests them with a personality so it can then judge them
as though they were persons. 

>So you construct this idea that ALL sites are the same, ALL
>programmers are a bunch of idiots who tell you to fuck off, ALL
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>that you claimed you did ?)

On great .. four putdowns in one sentence: marginalised, grunt, coder and
'claimed' (suggesting I made it all up). 

I'm a programmer because I enjoy it and am very good at it. I'd rather crank
code than play politics. I'd rather determine determine the fate of systems
(policy) than of individual people (politics).

This is a forum for programmers. By putting me down for being a programmer, you
simultaneously put down everyone else here.
```

In article <3e9c970f.185737540@news.optonline.net>,
Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net> wrote:
>riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

[snip]

>I'm trying to deal with facts; you keep reducing issues to personalities ..
>whose fault is it. who's guilty. My approach is rational; yours is emotional. 

[snip]

>>(why are you a marginalised grunt coder after all the wonderful things
>>that you claimed you did ?)
>
>On great .. four putdowns in one sentence: marginalised, grunt, coder and
>'claimed' (suggesting I made it all up). 

Mr Wagner, I believe that your use of the third person plural in

<http://groups.google.com/groups?selm=3e9b6630.107679399%40news.optonline.net&output=gplain>

--begin quoted text:

As COBOL programmers, we've ALL been marginalized .. for the last twenty 
years.

--end quoted text

... shows that you are comfortable in applying this term to yourself.  
Your use of the first-person singular in:

<http://groups.google.com/groups?selm=3e925830.113258083%40news.optonline.net&output=gplain>

--begin quoted text:

I'm a grunt.

--end quoted text

... and in

<http://groups.google.com/groups?selm=3e77ca3f.13461885%40news.optonline.net&output=gplain>

--begin quoted text:

I'm an American coder.

--end quoted text

... likewise shows no sign of deprecation.

Finally, since Mr Plinston relies only on what is posted here he has only
claims upon which to base his conclusions; to do otherwise is assumption 
of what has not been evidenced.

It seems, Mr Wagner, that when you use a term or a construct it is 
permissible... but when someone else uses that same term or an analgous 
construct (remember your recent admission of applying a double-standard 
for 'religious arguments'?) you claim it to be an insult.

I was taught that this sort of 'one rule for me, another for you' behavior 
- unless one was applying a more strict standard to one'sself, which seems 
not to be the case here - was utterly contemptible.

DD

robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 

> >So, let me see, 3 samples compared to ~30,000 MVS licenses, or 
> >~90,000 IBM type Mainframe systems... Hmmmmm. Would that fall 
> >under statistically insignificant?

> The factor determining sample size is variation. Suppose you had 100 million

widgets could be made without _any_ being rejects (and thus under or
over weight).

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Convert MainFrame Data [ EBCDIC ] To Ascii

_(reply depth: 29)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-04-16T03:35:08+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e9ca03e.188089178@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3e98442d.114845950@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0304121345.428f6de2@posting.google.com> <3e98ab3e.17028524@news.optonline.net> <b7dbsp$rvn$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3E9B5D2D.4060809@ix.netcom.com> <3e9bddc2.138293371@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0304151358.701e0306@posting.google.com>`

```
riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>> somehow, they each weighted EXACTLY the same. How many samples would you
need?
>> The answer is one.
>
>So what specific 'somehow' did you use to determine that the 30,000 or
>90,000 sites were "EXACTLY the same" ?

What we have here is a conflict between classical logic and statistics. The
former warns against 'hasty conclusion' i.e. extrapolating 'anecdotal evidence'
(a perjorative term) to the general population. The latter says it is perfectly
valid, so long as sample size is based on measured variability. 

I come down on the side of statistics.

>I would also be very interested in how you determined that 100 million
>widgets could be made without _any_ being rejects (and thus under or
>over weight).

It was a hypothetical, which you don't seen to understand.  I could have gone on
to discuss precise widgets built in a ball bearing factory vs. imprecise ones
built in a bowling ball factory. I could have shown how sample sizes differed
between the two. It would have elicited a big yawn here. 

It shouldn't. When we test software, how do we ascertain the number of samples
that provide an 'adequate' test? Fewer than one in a hundred programmers, or
even 'professional testers' (there's an oxymoron), can answer that question.
They just 'ballpark it'. 

Now you can tell me once again how being innumerate is a sign of
'professionalism' and my rants are the product of a damaged ego.
```

robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote

> >> >So, let me see, 3 samples compared to ~30,000 MVS licenses, or 
> >> >~90,000 IBM type Mainframe systems... Hmmmmm. Would that fall 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…

And what you consistently fail to notice is that you haven't measured
the variability of the 30,000 site (or 90,000) with any statistically
significant sampling.

Your 'widgets example' is irrelevant to any argument because you
indicated that you _know_ the variability (by having a conclusion as a
premise).

In the 30,000 sites it is only your prejudice that 'knows' they don't
vary.  It is only your emotional attachment to blaming IBM and salemen
that drives you to making incompetent claims about statistics.

If I looked out my window I would see 3 white cars.  Does that mean
that I should then conclude that variability is zero, therefore 3
samples is enough to establish that all cars are white ?

I would indeed in your version of 'statistics'.
