###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Convert MainFrame Data [ EBCDIC ] To Ascii

_(reply depth: 31)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-04-17T02:20:01+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e9dfe0f.277655873@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3e98442d.114845950@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0304121345.428f6de2@posting.google.com> <3e98ab3e.17028524@news.optonline.net> <b7dbsp$rvn$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3E9B5D2D.4060809@ix.netcom.com> <3e9bddc2.138293371@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0304151358.701e0306@posting.google.com> <3e9ca03e.188089178@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0304160106.c492648@posting.google.com>`

```
riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote
>
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>premise).

Statistical studies have an inherent 'chicken and egg problem'. To compute the
sample size, you must know the standard deviation of the population. This is
usually solved reiteratively. You first make an informed estimate of the
deviation, or simply a guess. You take that many samples and, from them, compute
their deviation. If your original estimate was too low, you take a new sample
based on measured deviation. No, you cannot reuse the first batch because it may
misrepresent the deviation of the population. You repeat the process until you
reach the desired Confidence Interval, which is typically 95%. 

Reiterative problem solving, aka 'converging on a solution', is a pretty basic
programming concept. 

>In the 30,000 sites it is only your prejudice that 'knows' they don't
>vary.  It is only your emotional attachment to blaming IBM and salemen
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>samples is enough to establish that all cars are white ?

The answer is you'd sample cars beyond view of your window, or over a longer
period of time. What if all cars in your neighborhood were white and you didn't
have access to the outside world. That's a real problem .. but too abstract for
this forum.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Convert MainFrame Data [ EBCDIC ] To Ascii

_(reply depth: 30)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-04-21T16:14:42+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b815di$2um$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3e98442d.114845950@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0304121345.428f6de2@posting.google.com> <3e98ab3e.17028524@news.optonline.net> <b7dbsp$rvn$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3E9B5D2D.4060809@ix.netcom.com> <3e9bddc2.138293371@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0304151358.701e0306@posting.google.com> <3e9ca03e.188089178@news.optonline.net>`

```

On 15-Apr-2003, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:

> It shouldn't. When we test software, how do we ascertain the number of samples
> that provide an 'adequate' test? Fewer than one in a hundred programmers, or
> even 'professional testers' (there's an oxymoron), can answer that question.
> They just 'ballpark it'.

Generally by experience.    We take enough samples to derive a pattern.   Then
we do the same thing and check to see if we get the same pattern.  We re-check
to see if our samples are biased and try again.  If the results are pretty
constant, we can work from there.

Then we qualify our results.  For instance, I have only programmed in 3 states
of one country.    I would not presume that my experience was typical of the
whole world.  (I've read that in Europe, the use of SECTIONs is much greater
than in the US).   My CoBOL programming in PCs is limited as well, and was
pre-Windows.   I have worked on a variety of mainframes and minis though, with
various sized U.S. customers, both as a contractor, and as an employee.

It is apparent that your environments and mine were very different.   It is
obvious that if I assumed the companies that you work for are like the companies
I have worked for, I would be very wrong.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Convert MainFrame Data [ EBCDIC ] To Ascii

_(reply depth: 31)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-04-23T00:01:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ea5ce82.11348522@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3e98442d.114845950@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0304121345.428f6de2@posting.google.com> <3e98ab3e.17028524@news.optonline.net> <b7dbsp$rvn$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3E9B5D2D.4060809@ix.netcom.com> <3e9bddc2.138293371@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0304151358.701e0306@posting.google.com> <3e9ca03e.188089178@news.optonline.net> <b815di$2um$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:

>On 15-Apr-2003, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:
>
>> It shouldn't. When we test software, how do we ascertain the number of
samples
>> that provide an 'adequate' test? Fewer than one in a hundred programmers, or
>> even 'professional testers' (there's an oxymoron), can answer that question.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>constant, we can work from there.

Sounds vague. I doubt you could write a generalized procedure. You'd have to
describe it with examples aka anecdotal evidence, and we all know how unreliable
that is. 

>Then we qualify our results.  For instance, I have only programmed in 3 states
>of one country.    I would not presume that my experience was typical of the
>whole world.  (I've read that in Europe, the use of SECTIONs is much greater
>than in the US).

Use of SECTIONs has nothing to do with testing methodology. 

>My CoBOL programming in PCs is limited as well, and was
>pre-Windows.   I have worked on a variety of mainframes and minis though, with
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>companies I have worked for, I would be very wrong.  

Mine were Fortune 100 companies and equivalent government agencies that used
formal methodologies. The difference between formal and informal, when it comes
to testing, is that informal tests the program against programming
specifications. Formal ignores programming specs; it tests the whole system
against business requirements and high level design.  

This changes the way programmers think. Programming specs can be, and often are,
wrong. As as programmer, I didn't rely on them much. I went 'back to the source'
-- the business requirement and high design -- to see what we were trying to
accomplish. Manuals may not have been available but these documents always were.
When there was discord between documents, the highest level one always
prevailed. 

That's how big companies think and (perhaps) explains why they prevail over
medium-sized companies. Today I listened to the new CEO of my current big
company explaining her philosophy and style. She said "If you're given a
problem, find the data is correct in YOUR database and dismiss it as somebody
else's problem, that's not good enough. You must find out whose problem it is,
refer it to that entity, and follow up to insure it was resolved." I find her
Total Quality attitude enlightened.
```

- **Date:** 2003-04-08T15:11:27+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b6uoqu$hkr$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3e88e0cf.99790075@news.optonline.net> <b6ca6g$p1b$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e8a1bd5.54672411@news.optonline.net> <b6djoe$n0l$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e8ac87f.98880631@news.optonline.net> <b6etsl$idc$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e8b87ae.147831069@news.optonline.net> <b6hi0e$nus$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e8cc1bf.228245019@news.optonline.net> <b6k6j1$cu6$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e8e842b.7566313@news.optonline.net> <b6s5o9$9dv$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e924038.107121980@news.optonline.net>`

```

On  8-Apr-2003, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:

> >Yep.  My culture puts a priority on code being absolutely reliable according
> >to the business needs.
>
> Your culture is inefficient. In the real world of competition, your company
> will sooner or later go belly up.

No company is immortal.   But I see plenty of cases where companies have gone
belly up because of bad business decisions.


> >> >But let me ask you - why should we have one called program?
> >>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> It is. Reduced development and maintenance is a sound business reason.

These are two sound business reasons.  "Encapsulation" is a means, not the
reason.

But I find that having one big complicated compromise program that requires me
to test hundreds of programs that call it - every time it is changed does no
reduce maintenance.


> >I mean in the same method.   Sometimes one method is more efficient,
> >sometimes another method is more efficient.
>
> When you say 'efficient' you mean 'fast'.  Don't pre-optimize. In most cases,
> speed doesn't matter.

I mean "efficient".  But there are other reasons for using different methods to
get data - such as security.

> In the Good Old Days, they paid us Big Bucks to hold the machine's hand
> because it was inadequate. Those days are OVER. Computers are all fast now.

That doesn't mean there should be only one way to access data.    For instance,
let's say you have a distributed system and you want to match up data on your
computer with data on the remote computer.  You should know the data enough to
figure out which direction to move data - maybe in one direction you move 1/10
as much data as in the other direction.   Bandwidth still matters.
```

>But I find that having one big complicated compromise program that requires me

The program doesn't require all that testing; your company's procedures do. 

>That doesn't mean there should be only one way to access data.    For instance,
>let's say you have a distributed system and you want to match up data on your

>as much data as in the other direction.   Bandwidth still matters.

When you're on an IBM Global fiber network which can copy a 1GB file in 30
seconds, bandwidth doesn't matter. That's 33MB/s, and the limiting factor is
disk speed rather than network.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Convert MainFrame Data [ EBCDIC ] To Ascii

_(reply depth: 12)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-04-03T01:11:25+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e8b881d.147942768@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3e8a1bd5.54672411@news.optonline.net> <b6djoe$n0l$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e8ac87f.98880631@news.optonline.net> <b6fc0n$h08$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>Robert Wagner wrote:
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>phones, embedded systems are also computers.

I know; I've worked on them quite a bit. My solution there was deleting leading
zeros from numeric fields and trailing spaces from strings when storing them
into the 'object-base'.  Compression was much better than afforded by PACKED or
BINARY. 

How ironic that proponents of PACKED simultaneously support fixed-length strings
and tables over lists. They're penny wise and pound foolish. 

>> Memories from the distant past to not apply to
>> today's environment .. 
>
>How ironic that it is you that makes this comment.

My middle name is ironic. 

>>>In what way is that relevant to dates ?
>> 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>In what way is that relevant to dates ?

We've replowed this ground a dozen times. I think you're just egging me on. 

>> Suppose the application logic requires you to distinguish between 'date
>> not entered' and 'date known to be missing'. If the date is display, the
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>using spaces ?

Because programmers are lazy and databases do not force checking null
indicators. As a result, it often doesn't happen. SQL logic checking WHERE
(my-date <= parm-date) would return false. A COBOL programmer writing 'if
my-date not less than parm-date' would  get the correct decision.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Convert MainFrame Data [ EBCDIC ] To Ascii

_(reply depth: 14)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-04-04T01:59:17+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e8cc4e8.229053531@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3e8a1bd5.54672411@news.optonline.net> <b6djoe$n0l$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e8ac87f.98880631@news.optonline.net> <b6fc0n$h08$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e8b881d.147942768@news.optonline.net> <b6hi7f$nv0$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:

>
>On  2-Apr-2003, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>strings and tables is pound foolish?

In the case of fixed length strings, you're wasting lots of storage (disk and
memory)  holding trailing spaces. Figure, on average, 15 bytes for every line of
name and address. In a file with 1M records, the waste will be at least 60MB.

Adding the waste of leading zeros on numeric fields, you're supporting hundreds
of MB. 

>Are lists always more cost effective than fixed format data?

Almost always. Speed is about the same, but lists have the advantage being
(nearly) infinitely expandable and use only the memory required. In the jargon,
they're 'scaleable'. 

They're also more functional in terms of user-friendliness. Compilers provide a
good example. If you are writing a COBOL compiler using fixed-length strings,
you must set an arbitrary maximum word length. COBOL uses 30. If you are using
variable-length strings in a list, there is no limit. A data name can be 100
bytes long. The COBOL limit of 30 bytes isn't based on what's reasonable, it is
based on false assumptions about the internal compiler structures. More modern
compilers, including even PL/I, can handle names of any length.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Convert MainFrame Data [ EBCDIC ] To Ascii

Robert Wagner wrote:

> memory)  holding trailing spaces. Figure, on average, 15 bytes for every
> line of name and address. In a file with 1M records, the waste will be at
> least 60MB.

Don't you know that files can be compressed ?  For MF see DATACOMPRESS and 
KEYCOMPRESS.  For Fujitsu it is automatic on indexed.

Wasted space ? what wasted space ?
 
> Adding the waste of leading zeros on numeric fields, you're supporting
> hundreds of MB.

Well, you may be.

> Almost always. Speed is about the same, but lists ...
> use only the memory required. 

Didn't you make some argument about virtual memory.  When you coded a word 
list and wasted half the space in the table with a huge high-values area 
that would never be used, you dismissed this as being something the OS 
should care about.

Now you are arguing exactly the opposite.
 
Am I surprised ?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Convert MainFrame Data [ EBCDIC ] To Ascii

_(reply depth: 16)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-04-04T09:56:41+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e8d513b.5582563@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3e8b881d.147942768@news.optonline.net> <b6hi7f$nv0$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e8cc4e8.229053531@news.optonline.net> <b6itaa$r2s$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>Robert Wagner wrote:
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>Wasted space ? what wasted space ?

VSAM and C-ISAM are not so enlightened. The mainframe folks fret about saving
pennies while wasting pounds. 
 
>> Adding the waste of leading zeros on numeric fields, you're supporting
>> hundreds of MB.
>
>Well, you may be.

How do you conclude I'm the problem? I'm arguing to reduce wasted space. 

>> Almost always. Speed is about the same, but lists ...
>> use only the memory required. 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>Now you are arguing exactly the opposite.

Unreferenced memory will never be paged in. Actively referenced list entries
need to be resident.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Convert MainFrame Data [ EBCDIC ] To Ascii

_(reply depth: 17)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-04-04T12:59:04-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0304041259.54d8d7cd@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3e8b881d.147942768@news.optonline.net> <b6hi7f$nv0$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e8cc4e8.229053531@news.optonline.net> <b6itaa$r2s$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e8d513b.5582563@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 

> >Don't you know that files can be compressed ?  For MF see DATACOMPRESS and 
> >KEYCOMPRESS.  For Fujitsu it is automatic on indexed.
> 
> VSAM and C-ISAM are not so enlightened. 

C-ISAM  is old stuff, just specify IDXFORMAT"3" DATACOMPRESS.  The
files will be faster too.

> The mainframe folks fret about saving
> pennies while wasting pounds. 

What evidence do you have (other than your assumption), that they do
'waste pounds' ?

Back in my mainframe days we used to compress data by concatenating
address lines and using a separator.  This saved tape read/write time
as well as tape changes.  Using packed dates and binary numbers also
saved space and time.

You have said that we should never have done that.

Today, large drives and big memories mean we don't care much about
space saving - the system can do it for us.

> >> Adding the waste of leading zeros on numeric fields, you're supporting
> >> hundreds of MB.

> How do you conclude I'm the problem? I'm arguing to reduce wasted space. 

How do you conclude that we have the 'problem' ?  Why do you think
that we waste space ?

> Unreferenced memory will never be paged in. 

Unreferenced bytes will be paged in if just one byte is referenced in
the page.  As you cannot specify exactly where the page boundaries are
then you cannot know if it is paged in or not.
Saying "never" is wrong.
In any case virtual memory systems are not just the 'demand paged'
mechanisms of 50 years ago.  In many cases the system uses 'locality'
and a whole segment is paged in if there is one reference.
What mechanisms do you use to specify the paging settings of your
programs ?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Convert MainFrame Data [ EBCDIC ] To Ascii

_(reply depth: 18)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-04-05T18:45:59+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e8ef7a0.37127268@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3e8b881d.147942768@news.optonline.net> <b6hi7f$nv0$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e8cc4e8.229053531@news.optonline.net> <b6itaa$r2s$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e8d513b.5582563@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0304041259.54d8d7cd@posting.google.com>`

```
riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 

>How do you conclude that we have the 'problem' ?  Why do you think
>that we waste space ?

Indirectly, because of IBM. In the good old days they made most of their money
by selling hardware upgrades. As a result, they delivered languages(like COBOL
and file systems like SAM and VSAM which had a bias toward inefficient use of
space. The generic technique was *preallocation*. Rather than obtaining disk or
memory as needed, the programmer was required to guess at the worst case and
allocate that much in the beginning. 

I once wrote a program to report percentage of allocated disk space that was
unused. Its finding on several mainframes were consistantly 80%. 

Now IBM has SMS, which solves the wasted disk problem fairly well. But we still
have residuals such as COBOL that encourage wasted space. A key technique is
tables rather than lists. It is especially wasteful when a table is stored in a
file. I've seen hundreds of examples in production files .. OCCURS 20 when 90%
of the records used three or less. 

>> Unreferenced memory will never be paged in. 
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>and a whole segment is paged in if there is one reference.

The page size is probably 2K. Waste is insignificant. 

I once wrote a memory manager that used variable page size -- the exact size of
an object, down to one byte. Experts said I was crazy; everyone knows page size
should be fixed at 512 or 2K. The hardware, Intel in this case, certainly
encourages that approach. Getting it to do variable was a struggle.

>What mechanisms do you use to specify the paging settings of your
>programs ?

Paging is set at the operating system level, not per program.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Convert MainFrame Data [ EBCDIC ] To Ascii

_(reply depth: 19)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-04-06T08:31:14+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b6neh4$fi7$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3e8d513b.5582563@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0304041259.54d8d7cd@posting.google.com> <3e8ef7a0.37127268@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:

> The generic technique was
> *preallocation*. Rather than obtaining disk or memory as needed, the
> programmer was required to guess at the worst case and allocate that much
> in the beginning.

Preallocation of disk space was very common and for very good reason.  In 
fact it continues today with Unix 'raw partitions' where a block of the 
disk is given to, say, a database to manage.

Pre-allocation has the advantage that any position in the file can be 
accessed by block and cylinder number from just one parameter in the file 
table.

Compare this with, say, FAT where to get to a specific point in the file 
the system has to follow a trail of FAT entries from the directory entry.  
To get to byte 1million with 2k clusters it has to read 500 FAT entries 
just to know where the block to read is located.

The first disk drive that I used were 4 Megabyte 14inch with a 250 msec 
access time an 8.3msec latency.  We used to design the layout of the 2 
drives so that the program could process the data with the minimum number 
of head moves.

Just dumping data onto a drive would have put the run times out by several 
fold.

In fact in the late 80s I was running multiuser systems on FAT disks.  
Fortunately I had a formatter that could specify the cluster size.  By 
changing this from 2k to 8k this reduced the number of FAT entries needed 
to be read and gave a 3 fold improvement in disk access speed.

> technique is tables rather than lists. It is especially wasteful when a
> table is stored in a file. I've seen hundreds of examples in production
> files .. OCCURS 20 when 90% of the records used three or less.

Rant on again about lists why don't you.

But then they _may_ have done actual timing tests and found an optimum 
solution for balancing the speed against the disk usage.  You look at just 
one factor and think that you can come up with a better answer.

> Experts said I was crazy; 

Yes.  But then they may have considered factors that you hadn't even 
thought about.

> Paging is set at the operating system level, not per program.

That depends on the system.  Burroughs and ICL VME, for two, can have the 
paging strategy set at the segment level.  For VME this means that a 
segment may be set so if any byte is refenced then the whole segment is 
paged in, or set so only the page is loaded, or so pages either side are 
loaded with a page.  This saves paging interrupts.

Most compilers on these systems set the strategy based on most likely, but 
can be configured for specific cases.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Convert MainFrame Data [ EBCDIC ] To Ascii

_(reply depth: 20)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-04-06T03:49:10+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e8f9b30.79006238@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3e8d513b.5582563@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0304041259.54d8d7cd@posting.google.com> <3e8ef7a0.37127268@news.optonline.net> <b6neh4$fi7$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>Robert Wagner wrote:
>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>table.

I don't fully understand what this means. Why can't a modern file system like
NTFS do the same?

>Compare this with, say, FAT where to get to a specific point in the file 
>the system has to follow a trail of FAT entries from the directory entry.  
>To get to byte 1million with 2k clusters it has to read 500 FAT entries 
>just to know where the block to read is located.

Not a problem on my 2G FAT with 32k units of allocation. NT lets you go up to 4G
with 64k units of allocation. My current wastage is about 20% (per a program I
wrote decades ago).

>In fact in the late 80s I was running multiuser systems on FAT disks.  
>Fortunately I had a formatter that could specify the cluster size.  By 
>changing this from 2k to 8k this reduced the number of FAT entries needed 
>to be read and gave a 3 fold improvement in disk access speed.

I had a similar experience on Honeywell mainframes. I wrote a 'reformatter'
which moved sectors 180 degrees away from each other. Latency was instantly cut
in half; programs ran twice as fast. Recent computers do this without making a
big deal over it. 

>> technique is tables rather than lists. It is especially wasteful when a
>> table is stored in a file. I've seen hundreds of examples in production
>> files .. OCCURS 20 when 90% of the records used three or less.
>
>Rant on again about lists why don't you.

I hoped this was going to be a flame-free post. Oh well..

>> Paging is set at the operating system level, not per program.
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>can be configured for specific cases.

Burroughs used to capable of loading up to three sets of microcode. One for the
operating system, one for 'business' (COBOL & RPG) and one for 'scientific'.
Each task specified its instruction set. 

Superior technology didn't help Burroughs (nor CII/Bull) survive. The fate of
systems is all about money and politics.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Convert MainFrame Data [ EBCDIC ] To Ascii

_(reply depth: 21)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-04-07T05:54:39+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b6ofg1$245$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3e8ef7a0.37127268@news.optonline.net> <b6neh4$fi7$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e8f9b30.79006238@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:

>>Preallocation of disk space was very common and for very good reason.  In
>>fact it continues today with Unix 'raw partitions' where a block of the
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> like NTFS do the same?

Because every time that a new allocation block is added to the file it must 
have a pointer to that new block.  This means that in order to read a 
specific position in the file that list has to be followed to determine 
where that position is on the disk.

With a fixed allocation there is just one entry so a calculation is all 
that is needed and the base can be stored in memory. So _no_ extra disk 
reads required.

With FAT the disk FAT entry blocks must be read in order to follow the FAT 
entries to determine the disk position of the file.

When DOS had 640Kb and BUFFERS=20 (and no separate cache) the number of FAT 
sector reads was _significant_, and easily exceeded the number of data file 
reads on Cobol data files.
 
>>Compare this with, say, FAT where to get to a specific point in the file
>>the system has to follow a trail of FAT entries from the directory entry.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> program I wrote decades ago).

I don't think that you are following the problem at all well.

The issue isn't disk space wastage (slack space), the problem is about disk 
access overhead.  With pre-allocated files (and no cache) on old mainframes 
it required just one access to get to any point in the file.  With, say, 
FAT system there may be dozens of head moves, latency and reads just to 
dtermine where the data block required lies.

Of course with hundreds of megabytes of RAM it all gets dumped into a cache 
and so the huge overhead is mostly hidden.

With your 32Kb clusters if you had a 32 Megabyte file then to access the 
last byte in the file (say to add a new record) it would take 1000 FAT 
reads to get to that point, it is the _only_ way of know which cluster that 
point was in.  With a defragged disk these FAT entries will be in 4 disk 
sectors.  If the disk ws fragmented then it could be a dozen sectors or 
several dozen.

Just like your lists these FAT entries can only be accessed serially.

NTFS organises itself rather better but there is still an overhead required 
for tracking where the file block are located, an overhead that just could 
not be coped with when mainframes had 250 ms access drives.


> I had a similar experience on Honeywell mainframes. I wrote a
> 'reformatter' which moved sectors 180 degrees away from each other.
> Latency was instantly cut in half; programs ran twice as fast. Recent
> computers do this without making a big deal over it.

Sector interleaving has been done in most systems since disks were 
attached.  But it usually only helps when doing sequential reading and 
writing on single user systems.  If you got such an improvement it either 
meant that the interleaving had been badly done to start with or that your 
program was slow such that it kept just missing the next sector read.

Changing the interleave factor certainly can be a tuning parameter.

When random reads are done then advanced systems do RPS (Rotational 
Position Sensing) and elevator queueing.  

> Burroughs used to capable of loading up to three sets of microcode. One
> for the operating system, one for 'business' (COBOL & RPG) and one for
> 'scientific'. Each task specified its instruction set.

And the relevence of this to disk accessing or paging is ?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Convert MainFrame Data [ EBCDIC ] To Ascii

_(reply depth: 22)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-04-06T16:30:09+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e903b62.120022984@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3e8ef7a0.37127268@news.optonline.net> <b6neh4$fi7$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e8f9b30.79006238@news.optonline.net> <b6ofg1$245$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>Robert Wagner wrote:

>With a fixed allocation there is just one entry so a calculation is all 
>that is needed and the base can be stored in memory. So _no_ extra disk 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>reads on Cobol data files.

Yes, I knew that. One solution is increasing the size of an allocation unit.
Another is caching FAT sectors separately, so they don't compete with data in
the general cache. 

>Sector interleaving has been done in most systems since disks were 
>attached.  But it usually only helps when doing sequential reading and 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>Changing the interleave factor certainly can be a tuning parameter.

It would be useful to tune it differently, based on file type. For instance,
executable programs are always read sequentially, with short delay between
reads. Same for ascii text files. A smart file system could change interleave
dynamically. 

>When random reads are done then advanced systems do RPS (Rotational 
>Position Sensing) and elevator queueing.

Those techniques benefit multi-user systems. They don't help much on a
single-user PC. 

PCs need burst mode speed. Most reads are sequential, whether the file is
declared so or not. After a program reads record n, there is a fairly high
probability it will ask for n+1. So rather than only reading record n, read the
whole track into a buffer. Having positioned the heads, it doesn't cost much. In
that case, interleave doesn't matter.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Convert MainFrame Data [ EBCDIC ] To Ascii

_(reply depth: 23)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-04-07T13:18:23-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0304071218.57fae8a6@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3e8ef7a0.37127268@news.optonline.net> <b6neh4$fi7$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e8f9b30.79006238@news.optonline.net> <b6ofg1$245$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e903b62.120022984@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 

> >When DOS had 640Kb and BUFFERS=20 (and no separate cache) the number of FAT 
> >sector reads was _significant_, and easily exceeded the number of data file 
> >reads on Cobol data files.
> 
> Yes, I knew that. One solution is increasing the size of an allocation unit.

Bingo.  You _do_ understand.  The ultimate solution is to have just
one allocation unit per file, of whatever size it needs to be (as
determined by system specification).  This reduces the number of
allocation table reads to _zero_ (the parameters are in the directory
entry) and maens that every file access is done in just one read.

> Another is caching FAT sectors separately, so they don't compete with data in
> the general cache. 

Certainly the problem is now mostly avoided, or actually just hidden,
by using megabytes of memory for caching.  FAT type systems are
_exteremely_ poor at handling large files, but then it was originally
designed for floppy disks.

Unix allocation structures are much better, requiring far fewer
average disk accesses per data block access.  However, Unix usually
has the option of 'raw partition' where a preallocated block of the
disk is given over to a system such as a database so that it achieves
best performance.

Your criticism was that 'IBM blah blah wastage of disk space'. Once
you understand the actual issues involved, such as disk access
overheads, memory constraints, you should be able to rise above a
rather shallow single parameter argument and provide a balanced view.
 

> It would be useful to tune it differently, based on file type. For instance,
> executable programs are always read sequentially, with short delay between
> reads. Same for ascii text files. A smart file system could change interleave
> dynamically. 

<choke>. Yeah right. The system pops up a little message "I can make
this system save 42 milliseconds on a complete file read - please wait
2 hours while I shuffle all the sectors on the drive".

You should patent this and sell it to Microsoft.

> Those techniques benefit multi-user systems. They don't help much on a
> single-user PC. 

When did this change to being 'single user PC' ?
 
> PCs need burst mode speed. Most reads are sequential, whether the file is
> declared so or not. 

If you are talking about Cobol files then the records are located in
the file in roughly _chronological_ sequence, up to the point that
deleted space is reused. Even in a sequential read of an indexed file
the actual disk reads are from random points in the file.

> After a program reads record n, there is a fairly high
> probability it will ask for n+1. So rather than only reading record n, read the
> whole track into a buffer. Having positioned the heads, it doesn't cost much. 

Duh.  Today's drives do that.  The drive itself has a track buffer of
a megabyte or two and every read request puts the whole track to the
buffer, the blocks being picked out from that.

Caches have done 'read ahead', full track reads and other mechanisms
for years.

> In that case, interleave doesn't matter.

I am so glad that you realise that your suggestion about 'dynamic
interleave change' was really silly.

Perhaps if you took some time to read what has been happening in the
computer world over the last several decades then you won't need to
work through all these beginner's questions about disk accessing and
caching.

You may also begin to realise that taking a stance on a single issue
is somewhat superficial.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Convert MainFrame Data [ EBCDIC ] To Ascii

```
riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 

>> It would be useful to tune it differently, based on file type. For instance,
>> executable programs are always read sequentially, with short delay between
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>2 hours while I shuffle all the sectors on the drive".

Not all sectors on the drive, just the track or two containing the file. PCs
spend 99% of their time idle. It could be done while you're sleeping.

>I am so glad that you realise that your suggestion about 'dynamic
>interleave change' was really silly.

Yes, it was just a discussion point. 

>Perhaps if you took some time to read what has been happening in the
>computer world over the last several decades then you won't need to
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…

I'm not a single issue kinda guy. In this case, I take it you mean 'IBM blah

not high speed access to large files, it was the salesman's annual bonus. In

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Convert MainFrame Data [ EBCDIC ] To Ascii

_(reply depth: 25)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-04-08T13:34:50-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0304081234.376fb05e@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3e8ef7a0.37127268@news.optonline.net> <b6neh4$fi7$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e8f9b30.79006238@news.optonline.net> <b6ofg1$245$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e903b62.120022984@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0304071218.57fae8a6@posting.google.com> <3e924319.107858480@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 

> ><choke>. Yeah right. The system pops up a little message "I can make
> >this system save 42 milliseconds on a complete file read - please wait
> >2 hours while I shuffle all the sectors on the drive".
> 
> Not all sectors on the drive, just the track or two containing the file.

With dynamic allocation 'the file' may be scattered in parts over
several tracks intermixed with other files.

While this may be just a 'discussion point', it is obvious that you
haven't/can't/don't understand enough to realise why the suggestion is
entirely silly.

 * how do you imagine that the system knows what the interleave factor
is ?
 * how would it notice that this file or track is different ?
 * if the file is deleted or moved what happens to interleave ?

In any case interleave factor is an obsolete way of arranging data on
a disk.

Back in the 60s we were manually arranging out disks for optimum read
times, and setting block offsets to suit the hardware and access
types.

So your ideas are 40 years too late.

Back in the 80s there were utilities that would test a disk in the
machine and would format it with the optimum interleave for continuous
reads.

So your 'idea' is 20 years to late for PCs.

For the last decade disks have had track buffers which suck the whole
track into RAM on the drive and give these out to the PC without any
latency.

So you 'idea' is completely irrelevant.

>  PCs
> spend 99% of their time idle. It could be done while you're sleeping.

There are some actual things that FAT systems do overnight:
defragging.

Beacuse of dynamic allocation files are, potentially, scattered over
the disk leading to huge access overheads (another issue that you
didn't grok).  Defragging brings the file back to being contiguous in
order to cut down all the FAT accesses required to read the file.
 
Later Windows versions also reorganise disks while idle by bringing
all the MS documents and programs to the front of the directory list
and put all the non-MS (ie competitors) files to the end of the
directory.

Then MS programs and files will load faster and competitors products
will load slower.

> I'm not a single issue kinda guy. In this case, I take it you mean 'IBM blah
> blah .. waste disk space'. Well, they did from 1960 to 1980. The motivation was
> not high speed access to large files, 

_ALL_ manufacturers in the 60s used pre-allocation for data files on
their disks.  This was because there would have been far too much
overhead to support dynamic allocation on the slow disk without cache
memory that the machines didn't have.

For time-sharing systems, such as TCO, Multics, MOP, MAC, etc, there
were pre-allocated user areas which were internally using dynamic file
allocation in a variety of ways.  Unix and PCs derived from the ideas
of time-sharing systems that did not use large data files.

For high-speed access on Unix a _pre-allocated_ raw partition is used
exactly like the pre-allocated files that you 'complain' about.

It wasn't just IBM, it was all manufacturers having to deal with 250
ms drives and no memory to waste on caching.

Now to the 'wasted space'.

It may surprise you to know that IBM did not personally allocate the
disk files. It wasn't IBM creating those '80% wasted' disk layouts, it
was systems people.

The disks were exchangable. Usually a system was allocated a set of
disks to use on specific disk drives.  When the system was running it
had exclusive use of those drives.  There was _no_point_ in leaving
free space, the files required may as well be built out to fill the
disk.

The cost of having a run fail due to running out of file space _far_
outweighed the cost of the disk pack and having someone monitor it.

_No_other_system_ would use those disks.  They would use the drive but
with their disks installed.

> it was the salesman's annual bonus. 

You really, really, don't understand why you get flamed do you.

Just in all other discussions, you start by demonstrating that you
don't understand all the issues, in fact you only seem to know one: in
this case you saw '80% wastage' and 'blamed' IBM.

You obviously never understood how disk mechanisms worked and have
'ideas' completely beyond any form of understanding of the underlying
mechanisms.

Now, having worked with salesmen in the 60s and 70s, I also know that
they _never_ understood anything about computers and thought that
_everything_ was, or should be, about their commission. If you asked
them why it was painted blue, they would say it was to boost their
commission, or should be.

The joke was: What is the difference between a used car salesman and a
computer salesman: the used car salesman _knows_ that he is lying to
you.

Even when the the actuality is explained to you, and you acknowledge
it, your prejudice is still what you believe.

Now I have met others that exhibit the same form of mindset (like its
in concrete), they knock on doors on saturday mornings and wave their
Book.

Eventually the only course open is to tell them to fuck off, too. 
And, just like you, they 'know' they _must_ be 'right' because of
that.

You don't knock on doors on saturday mornings do you ?  You seem well
equipped for it.
```

Thank you for the insight.

>

>disk files. It wasn't IBM creating those '80% wasted' disk layouts, it

IBM created the conditions/environment, then sat back and watched users shoot

>The disks were exchangable. Usually a system was allocated a set of
>disks to use on specific disk drives.  When the system was running it
>had exclusive use of those drives.  There was _no_point_ in leaving

Removable disks are another issue. I referred to non-removable 3310s and 3370s.


>> it was the salesman's annual bonus. 
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>mechanisms.

I worked on SCERT, which simulated how disk mechanisms (and other IO devices)

>Now, having worked with salesmen in the 60s and 70s, I also know that
>they _never_ understood anything about computers and thought that
>_everything_ was, or should be, about their commission. If you asked
>them why it was painted blue, they would say it was to boost their
>commission, or should be.

I knew a salesman who went around his house and labelled everything with the
'conquest' that financed it. The living room carpet was labelled 'XYZ disk
upgrade'. The sofa was labelled 'ABC leased PL/I'. 

>You don't knock on doors on saturday mornings do you ?  You seem well
>equipped for it.

I too send them on their way, but I'm more diplomatic than telling them to fuck
off. They are, after all, human beings .. and somewhat compromised in their
logical ability. I try to reject them in a way that avoids hurt feelings.
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3e8d513b.5582563@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0304041259.54d8d7cd@posting.google.com> <3e8ef7a0.37127268@news.optonline.net> <b6neh4$fi7$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e8f9b30.79006238@news.optonline.net>`

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3e8f9b30.79006238@news.optonline.net...

> Burroughs used to capable of loading up to three sets of microcode. One
for the
> operating system, one for 'business' (COBOL & RPG) and one for
'scientific'.
> Each task specified its instruction set.

I think you're referring to the Burroughs B1000 (B1700/B1800/B1900) series
here.  And your understanding of the microcode for that system is a bit out
of date -- pretty much as of the introduction of the series, not as it
evolved.  There was a General Input-Output Service Module that basically ran
the system; an instruction set for the language in which the operating
system, most system software, and some user programs were written; and an
instruction set for ForTran.  While in the days of say, Mark 3.2 MCPII on
that system (circa 1974) there was a single instruction set for COBOL (68)
and RPG, shortly thereafter a separate instruction set for RPG was
introduced, and some time later instruction sets for each of TWO COBOL74
implementations.

> Superior technology didn't help Burroughs (nor CII/Bull) survive. The fate
of
> systems is all about money and politics.

I think maybe your knowledge of the history of computer manufacturers, and
their parent companies, is a bit lacking.  The main Burroughs lines of
computers at the large-scale end of the spectrum have been in continuous
manufacture for decades.  The Burroughs B5000, B5500, B6700, B7700, B6800,
B7800, B5900, B6900 and B7900 were succeeded by the Burroughs A Series,
which became the Unisys A Series when Burroughs Corporation bought Sperry
Univac, and has since been transformed into the ClearPath NX and LX series.
Were you laboring under the mistaken impression that the only large-scale
computers that Unisys still manufactured were ex-Sperry-Univac designs?

True enough, the B1000 and the V Series (ex-Burroughs Medium System) are no
longer with us.  Both were discontinued after a run of decades, and
significant changes were made to the A Series software to make migration
from these systems as straightforward for our users as possible.   But the
descendants of the Burroughs Large System are alive and well, thank you very
much.  I have been told that more "Burroughs Large Systems" were sold last
year than in any calendar year in history.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Convert MainFrame Data [ EBCDIC ] To Ascii

_(reply depth: 23)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-04-08T09:03:25-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b6ursd$1pc5$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3e8d513b.5582563@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0304041259.54d8d7cd@posting.google.com> <3e8ef7a0.37127268@news.optonline.net> <b6neh4$fi7$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e8f9b30.79006238@news.optonline.net> <b6s5g6$2v3c$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3e9247f2.109099783@news.optonline.net>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3e9247f2.109099783@news.optonline.net...

> I was referring to the B3500/4700 line of Medium Systems, circa. late '60s
and
> early '70s.

The B2500/3500 system had a single hard-wired instruction set.  The later
systems (B3700, B4700, B3800, B4800, B3900, B4900, V Series) had microcode,
but that microcode was loaded statically.  Floating-point support was
provided within the instruction set, but access to it was based simply on
the presence of support in the various languages.  Dynamic microcode loading
of an instruction set designed for each individual language was
characteristic of the B1700, B1800 and B1900 systems.

> Evidently "Unisys" is sensitive about their name's image. The company
chose that
> name, not I.

Burroughs bought Sperry Univac.  In an effort to make it look LESS like the
pretty-much-unilateral takeover everyone knew it to be, the company decided
that neither predecessor name was appropriate.  In the employee contest, my
vote went to a return to the roots of both companies:  "American
Arithmometer and Gyroscope Company".

> Weren't the early machines successors to General Electric, and the
operating
> system modelled after GECOS?

No, and no.

From my personal contacts, and from published histories:

The very earliest Burroughs commercial computers were successors to the
Electrodata Datatron, the manufacturer of which Burroughs purchased in 1956.
These systems were I believe rebadged as the Burroughs B205 and I think
evolved into the B300/B500 series much beloved of banks.

So far as I know, there was no design or hardware commonality that I know of
between the Burroughs B5000 and the earlier Electrodata machines and their
successors.  About the closest tie I know of between the Electrodata line
and the B5000 was that the first ALGOL compiler designed to generate B5000
code was written in assembler on a B265 (?), for use by a B5000 simulator
running on a Philco 2000.

Once that cross-compiler was reasonably desk-checked and seemed to be
working, its first real task was to compile an ALGOL compiler written in
ALGOL for the new hardware design using a simulator for that hardware that
was by then running on a Philco 2000.  The first task of that ALGOL compiler
on the simulator was to compile itself, basically the second was to compile
the rudiments of the operating system.  When the first physical prototype of
the B5000 was ready, the testing was transferred from the Philco-based
simulator to the actual machine.  The hardware and the software design was
done in parallel.  As far as I know, the object code generated by the
B265-based ALGOL cross-compiler was never run on the B5000 itself, only the
simulator, and every ALGOL compiler on every Burroughs Large System and its
successors since then has been compiled from an ALGOL source.    The B5000
was announced as a product in 1961.

The B5500, introduced in 1964, was an improved B5000 (triple the speed, and
disk instead of drum memory, among other things).   The B6700 was a redesig
of the B5500, providing (among other things) additional hardware support for
memory protection and enhancements to the virtual memory system that had
been introduced in the B5000.

General Electric's FIRST entry into the computer business was in 1963; the
GE-625/635 design was established in that same year.  There were some
similarities between the B5000/B5500 and the GE-635 -- both systems were
early proponents of multiprocessing -- but the Burroughs Large System
design, as well as the design of its operating system, was years earlier.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Convert MainFrame Data [ EBCDIC ] To Ascii

_(reply depth: 20)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e8f8c80.75245795@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3e8d513b.5582563@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0304041259.54d8d7cd@posting.google.com> <3e8ef7a0.37127268@news.optonline.net> <b6nnp2$kus$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>Robert Wagner wrote:

>So, once again, you 'discovered' something 30 years ago at a couple of 
>sites and you extrapolate that to mean everyone, everywhere as a factoid.

You should have said factlet, meaning small fact. Factoid means 'resembling a
fact' or 'an invented fact'. It does not mean small, as commonly thought. 

>If a program on several runs with different data consitently gave "80%" 
>then I would doubt that it was giving any meangingful information. Given 
>that you are such an expert at statistics then if there had been any 
>deviation you would have specified it.

A standard deviation from three samples is pretty much meaningless. Besides, it
was close to zero.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Convert MainFrame Data [ EBCDIC ] To Ascii

_(reply depth: 21)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-04-07T06:13:14+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b6ogir$2o1$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3e8ef7a0.37127268@news.optonline.net> <b6nnp2$kus$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e8f8c80.75245795@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:

>>So, once again, you 'discovered' something 30 years ago at a couple of
>>sites and you extrapolate that to mean everyone, everywhere as a factoid.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> commonly thought.

I know exactly what the meaning of the word I used is.  I should not have 
said 'factlet', I meant what I said: your claim was a factoid: it resembled 
a fact but wasn't one.
 
>>If a program on several runs with different data consitently gave "80%"
>>then I would doubt that it was giving any meangingful information. Given
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Besides, it was close to zero.

Actually your 80% is also quite meaningless.  If it was only '3 samples' 
then anyone with two clues about statistics would not have claimed a result 
at all.

It may be, for example, that the 3 files you tested were monthly 
transaction files that are added to during the month (eg invoices and 
payments for the statements and sales transactions for sales analysis) and 
you had run your test in the first week of a month.

Or it maybe that the files were sized for maximum transaction volumes and 
only a small run had just be done.

Either of these may well explain 3 consistent results, but they are 
meaningless results.

In fact the disk must be able to store the combined maximum file sizes, if 
the files are, for example, monthly transactions, then doing it with 
dynamic allocation simply introduces a huge overhead to arrive at the same 
point at the end of each month when the size may collapse back to small 
sizes.  

It is not like the space could be used for something else.

Preallocated sizes simply saves thousands or millions of disk accesses.

Of course dynamic allocation _is_ appropriate where there is unpredicatable 
usage, such as on PCs.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Convert MainFrame Data [ EBCDIC ] To Ascii

_(reply depth: 22)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-04-06T16:30:11+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e904667.122843536@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3e8ef7a0.37127268@news.optonline.net> <b6nnp2$kus$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e8f8c80.75245795@news.optonline.net> <b6ogir$2o1$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>Robert Wagner wrote:

>It may be, for example, that the 3 files you tested were monthly 
>transaction files that are added to during the month (eg invoices and 
>payments for the statements and sales transactions for sales analysis) and 
>you had run your test in the first week of a month.

I wasn't measuring files, I was measuring all disks on a mainframe system.
Preallocation occurred on several levels, not just the file level. For instance,
VSAM user catalogs, which are similar to directories, were preallocated. 

>In fact the disk must be able to store the combined maximum file sizes, if 
>the files are, for example, monthly transactions, then doing it with 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>It is not like the space could be used for something else.

It could be used for temporary files. Mainframe applications love temp files. 

Applications do not hit max file size simultaneously. Accounting systems might
max out at end of year whereas production systems do so in the middle of summer
(assuming north of the equator here). 

>Preallocated sizes simply saves thousands or millions of disk accesses.

Thank you for the explanation. I hadn't heard that one before now.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Convert MainFrame Data [ EBCDIC ] To Ascii

_(reply depth: 24)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-04-07T02:49:49+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e909f52.379813@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3e8f8c80.75245795@news.optonline.net> <b6ogir$2o1$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e904667.122843536@news.optonline.net> <b6pu56$ri6$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>Robert Wagner wrote:
>
>> Mainframe applications love temp files.
>
>Is this some sort of criticism ?

Not especially. My only criticism is they're a waste of time when used to pass
data thru space rather than time. In other words, when they are used as a
'pipeline' to pass data from program A to B, usually in a batch job. The
application should load both programs and pass the data in memory.

For example, suppose program A extracts data for three reports and writes
records to three temp files which are read by job steps B, C and D. Rather than
writing the records to files, it would be better to CALL programs B, C and D
with the record images. 

In the very old days, temp files were necessary because programs A and B might
not fit in memory. Also because sorts were standalone steps in the batch job.
Those reasons disappeared 15-20 years ago, but batch jobs are still designed the
old way.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Convert MainFrame Data [ EBCDIC ] To Ascii

_(reply depth: 25)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-04-07T00:41:14-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0304062341.8b84b9c@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3e8f8c80.75245795@news.optonline.net> <b6ogir$2o1$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e904667.122843536@news.optonline.net> <b6pu56$ri6$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e909f52.379813@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote

> Not especially. My only criticism is they're a waste of time when used to pass
> data thru space rather than time. In other words, when they are used as a
> 'pipeline' to pass data from program A to B, usually in a batch job. The
> application should load both programs and pass the data in memory.

> For example, suppose program A extracts data for three reports and writes
> records to three temp files which are read by job steps B, C and D. Rather than
> writing the records to files, it would be better to CALL programs B, C and D
> with the record images. 
 
> In the very old days, temp files were necessary because programs A and B might
> not fit in memory. Also because sorts were standalone steps in the batch job.

Phew, I am so glad there actually was a reason for it, I was beginning
to think that you had a brilliant revellation about how people had
been doing it all wrong since tabulator days.  Obviously not.

> Those reasons disappeared 15-20 years ago, but batch jobs are still designed the
> old way.

I am so glad that you have done an extensive survey and analysis of
all systems designed in the last two decades.  Now if you could just
publish the figures...

However, just like all your other overly simplistic evaluations, you
don't like it only because you don't like it.  It is just your
prejudice.

Having done detailed timing analysis of various methods of batch file
updating, it is often the step processing that shows advantages in
performance.  For example if there are huge transactions to match
against different master files, - accounts of some type or another,
for example.  It is often much faster to sort to a particular sequence
and sequentially match against one account and then sort to another
sequence for matching sequentially against that.

Processing each transaction and passing it to several programs, as you
say, would require random access to all the master files (or all but
one) and each transaction could (or would) require an account read and
rewrite, while batched transactions would only require a sequential
read and rewrite for each different key.

Now _I_ wouldn't hazard a guess at which would be 'better' without a
great deal of detail analysis of the data, but you seem to be quite
happy to claim that you know more about every system than the
designers. Obviously we have to add something to your skill range, the
only question is whether it is physic or bullshit.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Convert MainFrame Data [ EBCDIC ] To Ascii

_(reply depth: 26)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-04-07T22:31:14+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e91be3a.73838938@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3e8f8c80.75245795@news.optonline.net> <b6ogir$2o1$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e904667.122843536@news.optonline.net> <b6pu56$ri6$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e909f52.379813@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0304062341.8b84b9c@posting.google.com>`

```
riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote
>
>> Not especially. My only criticism is they're a waste of time when used to
pass
>> data thru space rather than time. 

>I am so glad that you have done an extensive survey and analysis of
>all systems designed in the last two decades.  Now if you could just
>publish the figures...

Your arguments are not bolstered by demanding 'facts' that you (and everyone
else) know are infeasible to compile. 

>Having done detailed timing analysis of various methods of batch file
>updating, it is often the step processing that shows advantages in
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>sequence for matching sequentially against that.

That depends on the speed ratio of random vs. sequential. I did exactly what you
describe when the ratio was 50:1 or even 20:1. Now that it is 2:1, as you
demonstrated here recently, there is no longer a reason for multiple passes. 

>Processing each transaction and passing it to several programs, as you
>say, would require random access to all the master files (or all but
>one) and each transaction could (or would) require an account read and
>rewrite, while batched transactions would only require a sequential
>read and rewrite for each different key.

You were compensating for inefficiency in the file system. In the old days, I
had to write code like this: 

perform read-next varying gap-size from 1 by 1 until
    gap-size greater than 20 or file-key not less than transaction-key
if  file-key less than transaction-key
    perform start-file
    perform read-next.

Why did I have to do that? Why didn't the file system do it internally?
Rhetorical questions. It didn't, so we had to 'help' it. 

>Now _I_ wouldn't hazard a guess at which would be 'better' without a
>great deal of detail analysis of the data, but you seem to be quite
>happy to claim that you know more about every system than the
>designers. Obviously we have to add something to your skill range, the
>only question is whether it is physic or bullshit.

If you're so knowledgable, why must you resort to ranting?
```

S

can move through processor storage to another job without going to DASD or
tape."

> >The old standard for mainframes was Extract - Sort - Report.   This type of
> >modularization still makes for ease of maintenance.
>
> COBOL's SORT verb makes it very easy to do all three steps in a single
> program.   Even a called program.

Yes.  But there are advantages to following that old standard.    Sometimes I
use CoBOL sorts, other times I use external sorts.   My default is external
sorts, as I would rather have small modules for maintenance sake - but look at
each job individually to find out which technique works best for each job.

Howard Brazee wrote:
> On  5-Apr-2003, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:
> 
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> data on more or less disk space?

I have a customer that is moving from a mainframe environment to 
a mixture of NT and AIX. The mainframe, using that inefficient 
storage allocation method via VSAM and xSAM takes 1.5TB to hold 
all the production system (data and software, including the O/S 
and related software). The "OPEN SYSTEMS" group needs a 5TB SAN 
to hold approximately 50% of the production DATA between Oracle 
and Siebel.

I dunno, which method is more efficient? One doesn't have a data 
base, and uses packed decimal. The other uses binary, BCD and 
floating point with relative databases. The second one takes much 
more disk space.

Now could a factor in the disk utilization differences be due to 
"FBA" v. CKD?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Convert MainFrame Data [ EBCDIC ] To Ascii

_(reply depth: 16)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-04-05T09:13:30+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e8e87ae.8465523@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3e8a1bd5.54672411@news.optonline.net> <b6djoe$n0l$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e8ac87f.98880631@news.optonline.net> <b6fc0n$h08$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e8b881d.147942768@news.optonline.net> <b6hi7f$nv0$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e8cc4e8.229053531@news.optonline.net> <b6k6no$d44$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:

>
>On  3-Apr-2003, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>But gain speed.   Which is more valuable today?

The speed of disk transfer is more valuable today. Compression is a tradeoff
between cpu time and disk transfer. Cpu speed is cheap today. As Richard
correctly points out, modern file systems do compression by default. There is no
reason for the programmer to do it.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Convert MainFrame Data [ EBCDIC ] To Ascii

_(reply depth: 15)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-04-05T10:01:58+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b77c9f$211$2@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3e8b881d.147942768@news.optonline.net> <b6hi7f$nv0$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e8cc4e8.229053531@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:

>>Are lists always more cost effective than fixed format data?
> 
> Almost always. Speed is about the same, but lists have the advantage being
> (nearly) infinitely expandable and use only the memory required. In the
> jargon, they're 'scaleable'.

Tables and Lists have _different_ characteristics and are rarely 
interchangable.

Lists _might_ save space, that depends entirely on the nature of the data 
stored and the overheads.  If the text data is very variable in length then 
the ends of the text will be saved, but against that there is the 'next' 
pointer (and possibly 'previous') and the 'malloc' header overhead (which 
is invisible).  With text that is constrained and nearly always of full 
length than a list item will be _larger_ than a table item by many bytes.

Of course the table size is fixed and the data may use only the initial 
part of a table.  Whether the list is larger or smaller in total bytes 
depends on how well there is a match between the actual data size and the 
table size.

And, of course, if a table only uses the front part of a table then your 
argument was that the rest will never be paged in so who cares.

With speed, however, you are wrong.  Lists that are accessed via a 'next' 
pointer can _only_ be traversed serially.  Every random access must start 
at the first item and load every item until the one found.  This may be 
similar to serial table access.

But tables may be accessed at any item and binary chop searches are _much_ 
faster than serially searching a list.  Tables can also be used as hashed 
tables which are infinitely faster than serial lists.

Also try sorting a serial list using bubble or quick sort.

Evaluate the data, choose your algorithm, _then_ choose a suitable storage 
mechanism.  

If I want 'nearly infinite expansion' and scalability then I will probably 
also want sharability and reuse.  Lists don't do that.  With very large 
data sets the load and unload time may be unsuitable if there is a small 
hit rate.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Convert MainFrame Data [ EBCDIC ] To Ascii

_(reply depth: 16)_

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-04-12T19:30:44-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<joe_zitzelberger-BA7DE7.19304412042003@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3e8b881d.147942768@news.optonline.net> <b6hi7f$nv0$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e8cc4e8.229053531@news.optonline.net> <b77c9f$211$2@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```

"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:

>
>On 12-Apr-2003, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>> If you do, I'll hack up my radix sort, which will outperform yours by at
least
>> 10:1.
>
>Will it outperform a simple comb sort by 10:1?   For any data?
>http://world.std.com/~jdveale/combsort.htm

Comb sort is yet another 'N log(N)' performer, same as QuickSort and Shell sort,
so yes.

When I was trying to develop a faster sort using calculus, thirty years ago,  my
solutions usually involved prime numbers and the value of e. Comb sort is
replowing that ground. Note that the fractional part of his 'divisor' is .2796.
That's darn close to the value of e = 2.71... as close as you can get when using
primes. 

That approach turned out to be a dead end. 

>I haven't coded a radix sort in a long time.   When I can't use commercial
>sorts, I want to combine performance with maintainability.

This one will be so maintainable, you'll slap yourself in the forehead. Let's
see whether I can elicit the correct solution rather than giving it. 

Have you ever used a card sorter? Describe the procedure. (I'm serious, this is
not a joke.) Have you ever sorted a LARGE deck of cards, say 50,000, and used a
collater to speed up the process? Describe that procedure.
```

On 22-Apr-2003, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:

> >Will it outperform a simple comb sort by 10:1?   For any data?
> >http://world.std.com/~jdveale/combsort.htm
>
> Comb sort is yet another 'N log(N)' performer, same as QuickSort and Shell
> sort, so yes.

Not quite the same.   But even if it was - that link provided testing results.
10:1 is a bigger difference than what I have found.   I suppose that may be data
related, but it would be interesting to test the code from that link with CoBOL
code for your radix test.

> When I was trying to develop a faster sort using calculus, thirty years ago,
> my
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> That approach turned out to be a dead end.

But these guys provided testing results which look good.    Testing results
rule.

> >I haven't coded a radix sort in a long time.   When I can't use commercial
> >sorts, I want to combine performance with maintainability.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Have you ever used a card sorter?

Yep.

> Describe the procedure. (I'm serious, this is not a joke.)

I put a pile of cards in the input bin.  I pick which card column to sort by.  I
press a button and the cards go into bins depending upon which hole was punched.
  I pick up the cards and put them back in the in bin.   If the data are
alphanumeric, I repeat the process for the extra alphanumeric columns.   Once
the column is sorted, I dial to the next column in the card and repeat.  
Everything was mechanical.

> Have you ever sorted a LARGE deck of cards, say 50,000, and used a
> collater to speed up the process? Describe that procedure.

Size yes, collator no.  Don't know the procedure.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Convert MainFrame Data [ EBCDIC ] To Ascii

_(reply depth: 21)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-04-23T13:33:53-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0304231233.47c2b0ab@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3e8b881d.147942768@news.optonline.net> <b6hi7f$nv0$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e8cc4e8.229053531@news.optonline.net> <b77c9f$211$2@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <joe_zitzelberger-BA7DE7.19304412042003@corp.supernews.com> <3e98df71.30393272@news.optonline.net> <b815pt$362$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ea5c65f.9265079@news.optonline.net> <b865nj$ete$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote 

> But these guys provided testing results which look good.    Testing results
> rule.

Sorts can be sensitive to initial data order.  Some take roughly the
same time regardless of initial order (they go through all the
motions), some will take longer if the data is initially in the
correct order.

> > Have you ever sorted a LARGE deck of cards, say 50,000, and used a
> > collater to speed up the process? Describe that procedure.
> 
> Size yes, collator no.  Don't know the procedure.

I suspect that he is thinking of polyphase pre-string and merge.  When
I started on computers it was a 16Kword machine with 4 tape decks.  To
sort a full tape file required some interesting mechanisms to be used.
 Sorting large card decks was done in the same way by sorting small
(2000) decks and then merging using the collater.  This may have been
faster partly because two machines were being used, but I suspect that
it just _seemed_ faster because there was someone to talk to   ;-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Convert MainFrame Data [ EBCDIC ] To Ascii

_(reply depth: 22)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-04-23T23:15:50+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ea71d0e.97005515@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3e8b881d.147942768@news.optonline.net> <b6hi7f$nv0$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e8cc4e8.229053531@news.optonline.net> <b77c9f$211$2@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <joe_zitzelberger-BA7DE7.19304412042003@corp.supernews.com> <3e98df71.30393272@news.optonline.net> <b815pt$362$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ea5c65f.9265079@news.optonline.net> <b865nj$ete$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <217e491a.0304231233.47c2b0ab@posting.google.com>`

```
riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote 
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>correct order.

Nearly all sorts are fast if the data is in perfect order. They make one pass,
realize there were no exchanges (or a single intermediate string) and terminate.


Superior sorts go faster if the data is in approximate sequence. (see below)

>> > Have you ever sorted a LARGE deck of cards, say 50,000, and used a
>> > collater to speed up the process? Describe that procedure.
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>it just _seemed_ faster because there was someone to talk to   ;-)

No, that's not it. The technique is to sort on a few columns at the beginning of
the key, thus putting the cards in approximate order. Use the collater to pull
out of sequence cards, typically 10%. The other 90% form a single string. Sort
the 10% and merge them into the 90%. 

This requires careful choice of how many columns are in the 'first few'. If you
pick five and 90% of the cards contain zeros or spaces in the first five, you'll
wind up doing a full sort on 80%.

Note that only one machine is used at a time. There is no multitasking.
```
