###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Convert MainFrame Data [ EBCDIC ] To Ascii

_(reply depth: 21)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-04-23T23:15:50+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ea71d51.97072274@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3e8b881d.147942768@news.optonline.net> <b6hi7f$nv0$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e8cc4e8.229053531@news.optonline.net> <b77c9f$211$2@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <joe_zitzelberger-BA7DE7.19304412042003@corp.supernews.com> <3e98df71.30393272@news.optonline.net> <b815pt$362$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ea5c65f.9265079@news.optonline.net> <b865nj$ete$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:

  But even if it was - that link provided testing results.
>10:1 is a bigger difference than what I have found.   I suppose that may be
data
>related, but it would be interesting to test the code from that link with CoBOL
>code for your radix test.

My radix wasn't written in COBOL. It was written in C, then 'manually compiled'
to asm. But it can be written in COBOL easily.

>But these guys provided testing results which look good.    Testing results
>rule.

I was being conservative when I said 10:1. My testing results against the C
library qsort() were 23:1. 

>> Have you ever used a card sorter?
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>I put a pile of cards in the input bin.  I pick which card column to sort by.
I
>press a button and the cards go into bins depending upon which hole was
punched.

The output things were called pockets and the input thing was called a card
reader. Now imagine a card sorter with 256 pockets. We are going to read through
the input and throw each record into one of 256 pockets. More technically, we
are going to create 256 strings and link each record to the end of one of them.

>  I pick up the cards and put them back in the in bin.   

You link the last card in each pocket to the first card in the next. 

>Once
>the column is sorted, I dial to the next column in the card and repeat.  
>Everything was mechanical.

Not quite mechanical, it relies on your manual dexterity. <g> This sort is
proceeding right to left, least significant to most. When you say "dial to the
next column" you mean -1, the next column to the left. 

Imagine a COBOL program which uses the EXACT SAME algorithm. It starts on the
right, sorting one column per iteration. If the key is, say 16 bytes long, it
makes 16 iterations. On each iteration, it runs through the records tossing them
into one of 256 strings. At the end of the iteration,  it gathers the 256
strings into one string by connecting the last of each to first of the next. 

This is so easy, I know you could write such a program in a hour or two. No need
to use dynamic memory allocation and pointers. For this exercise, fixed length
strings, tables and subscripts will be adequate. Populate the data with 50,000
random numbers or text strings of your choice. 

>> Have you ever sorted a LARGE deck of cards, say 50,000, and used a
>> collater to speed up the process? Describe that procedure.
>
>Size yes, collator no.  Don't know the procedure.

Below is a quote of my answer to Richard. We'll get to this enhancement next,
after the basic radix sort. It can be done 'in situ', without requiring a
physically separate stack of cards (data entries).

"The technique is to sort on a few columns at the beginning of the key, thus
putting the cards in approximate order. Use the collater to pull out of sequence
cards, typically 10%. The other 90% form a single string. Sort the 10% and merge
them into the 90%. 

This requires careful choice of how many columns are in the 'first few'. If you
pick five and 90% of the cards contain zeros or spaces in the first five, you'll
wind up doing a full sort on 80%.

Note that only one machine is used at a time. There is no multitasking. "
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Sorting algorithms

_(reply depth: 22)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-04-24T14:39:19+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b88sun$o3s$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3e8b881d.147942768@news.optonline.net> <b6hi7f$nv0$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e8cc4e8.229053531@news.optonline.net> <b77c9f$211$2@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <joe_zitzelberger-BA7DE7.19304412042003@corp.supernews.com> <3e98df71.30393272@news.optonline.net> <b815pt$362$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ea5c65f.9265079@news.optonline.net> <b865nj$ete$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ea71d51.97072274@news.optonline.net>`

```

On 23-Apr-2003, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:

> I was being conservative when I said 10:1. My testing results against the C
> library qsort() were 23:1.

There was a paper that I can't get to at
http://www.etk.com/papers/sorting/sorting.htm

It compared bubble sort, heapsort, quicksort, and comb sort with tests.   This
comb sort wasn't "improved" as shown in
http://world.std.com/~jdveale/combsort.htm

Here are some of the test results:

		Items	Order	Time AS/400

Bubble Sort	1600	random	401785
Heapsort	1600	random	    3857
Quicksort	1600	random	    2974
Combsort	1600	random	    6299

It included CoBOL source code for these, and there was no comparison on how
complicated and long the Quicksort algorithm was compared to the others.

Combsort is about twice as slow as QuickSort in most of the tests (there were
lots of tests with similar results), but with very simple code.

If your sort is 23 times faster than QuickSort (with any but specialized data),
then you probably should patent it.  Then sell it to one of the competitors of
IBM's DFSORT.   Those companies got their start when IBM's sort was in need of
improvement, but now that IBM has caught up, they could use such an improvement.
```

_(reply depth: 23)_

- **From:** "Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org>

```
contributed them to www.cbttape.org.  Here is a copy of the timimg results:
```

_(reply depth: 25)_

- **From:** Michael Wojcik <mwojcik@newsguy.com>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3EAE9F8B.80800@newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3e8b881d.147942768@news.optonline.net> <b6hi7f$nv0$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e8cc4e8.229053531@news.optonline.net> <b77c9f$211$2@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <joe_zitzelberger-BA7DE7.19304412042003@corp.supernews.com> <3e98df71.30393272@news.optonline.net> <b815pt$362$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ea5c65f.9265079@news.optonline.net> <b865nj$ete$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ea71d51.97072274@news.optonline.net> <b88sun$o3s$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <01c30ac6$981ac740$e893f243@chottel> <3eaabd5d.167825654@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:
 >
 > To further confuse the issue, there are two algorithms with that
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
 > is the latter?

Erm, Radix Exchange is most definitely a radix sort.  It sorts based on
the absolute values of the keys, not their values relative to one
another.  That's what distinguishes radix sorts from comparison sorts.

I don't have a copy of Knuth v2 handy here, but I'd be surprised if the
Radix Exchange sort he describes is different from the standard Radix
Exchange sort (which is not "radix sort followed by an exchange sort").
Standard radix exchange swaps elements based on the value of the
appropriate bit position in their keys.  (In the standard example, after
the first pass, all the records with keys that begin with a 0 bit will
come before all records with keys that begin with a 1 bit.)
```

- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3e8b881d.147942768@news.optonline.net> <b6hi7f$nv0$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e8cc4e8.229053531@news.optonline.net> <b77c9f$211$2@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <joe_zitzelberger-BA7DE7.19304412042003@corp.supernews.com> <3e98df71.30393272@news.optonline.net> <b815pt$362$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ea5c65f.9265079@news.optonline.net> <b865nj$ete$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ea71d51.97072274@news.optonline.net> <b88sun$o3s$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <01c30ac6$981ac740$e893f243@chottel> <3eaabd5d.167825654@news.optonline.net> <3EAE9F8B.80800@newsguy.com>`

```
From Knuth's volume 3 Sorting and Searching, 2nd printing, March 1976, page
123:

"Radix Exchange, ... makes use of the binary representation of the keys, so
it is intended only for binary computers. Instead of comparing two keys
with each other, this method inspects individual bits of the keys, to see
if they are 0 or 1. In other respects it has the characteristics of
exchange sorting, and , in fact it is rather similiar to quicksort. Since
it depends on radix 2 representations, we call it "radix exchange sorting".
"
```

```
Michael Wojcik <mwojcik@newsguy.com> wrote:

>Robert Wagner wrote:
> >
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>another.  That's what distinguishes radix sorts from comparison sorts.

Read this:

http://www.geocities.com/josephgama/GamaSort.htm
>
>I don't have a copy of Knuth v2 handy here, but I'd be surprised if the
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>come before all records with keys that begin with a 1 bit.)

You're wrong on several counts: 

1.  Radix proceeds right to left, not left to right. After the first pass,
records that END with 0 would preceed records that end with 1. 

2.  Knuth's description was poor.  It shows in his timings, posted here.

3.  Radix Exchange normally IS a Radix sort followed by an Exchange (or other)
sort. That is clear in Sedgewick, not so clear in Knuth. 

Forget appeals to authority. Look at the code and results posted here. My
radix-insertion, written in COBOL, runs seven times faster than the Micro Focus
library function. 

Granted, some of the speed can be explained by lack of generalization. If I were
writing a generalized sort and they key was not a string, I'd hand it off to the
MF library function. The corrolary is that when the key IS a string (which it is
99% of the time), the library function should have used the radix-insertion
algorithm.
```

```
Robert Wagner wrote:
 > Michael Wojcik <mwojcik@newsguy.com> wrote:
 >
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
 > http://www.geocities.com/josephgama/GamaSort.htm

Read it.  What's your point?

 >> I don't have a copy of Knuth v2 handy here, but I'd be surprised if
 >> the Radix Exchange sort he describes is different from the standard
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
 > You're wrong on several counts:

Quite possibly, but you haven't identified any correctly.  Feel free to
try again.

 > 1.  Radix proceeds right to left, not left to right. After the first
 > pass, records that END with 0 would preceed records that end with 1.
 >
True for a straight radix sort, which wasn't what I was describing.  You
mentioned Sedgewick's book yourself, I think; his initial description of
Radix Exchange sort is left-to-right.

 > 2.  Knuth's description was poor.  It shows in his timings, posted
 > here.

Since I said nothing about Knuth's description (except that I didn't
have a copy handy at the time), this statement, true or not, does not
make me wrong.

 > 3.  Radix Exchange normally IS a Radix sort followed by an Exchange
 > (or other) sort. That is clear in Sedgewick, not so clear in Knuth.

That's a completely incorrect reading of Sedgewick's description of
radix exchange sort, unless he rewrote it in whatever edition of his
book you're looking at.  From the corrected first edition of _Algorithms
in C_:

	Radix Exchange Sort
	-------------------
	Suppose we can rearrange the records of a file so that all those
	whose keys begin with a 0 bit come before all those whose keys
	begin with a 1 bit....  scan from the left to find a key which
	starts with a 1 bit, scan from the right to find a key which
	starts with a 0 bit, exchange, and continue the process until
	the scanning pointers cross.  This leads to a recursive sorting
	procedure...  (135)

No "radix sort followed by an exchange sort".  There's one sorting
procedure which is recursive and very similar to Quicksort except that
it uses the absolute values of the keys (taken bitwise, left to right)
rather than their relative values.

 > Forget appeals to authority.

Unwise advice in general, but inapplicable here in any case, since I
appealed to no authority in the post to which you are responding.

 > Look at the code and results posted here. My radix-insertion, written
 > in COBOL, runs seven times faster than the Micro Focus library
 > function.

I did not dispute that claim.  You may note that in another post in this
thread I commented that radix sorting was an important technique and
ought to be studied by those interested in sorting performance.  I only
disputed your error in declaring Radix Exchange sort "not a radix sort
at all", which it indubitably is.
```

```
Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3eb40734.89383720@news.optonline.net...
> Michael Wojcik <mwojcik@newsguy.com> wrote:
>
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> We must be reading different versions (it appears there were three). I
don't
> have my copy here, it's in storage. But I remember using it when I wrote
the
> straight radix (on incomplete key) followed by an insertion or exchange
sort.
> I posted the code here.

I have the Third Edition of Sedgewick's "Algorithms" in which
he describes five radix sorts: Binary Quicksort (Radix Exchange),
MSD Radix, Three-Way Radix Quicksort, LSD Radix, and
LSD Radix on MSD bits (followed by insertion sort).

LSD Radix (both) operate right-to-left, the others left-to-right.

>   From the corrected first edition of _Algorithms
> >in C_:
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> Recursion causes it to 'really' operate right to left. Granted, not on the
whole
> table per pass,

No, it 'really' operates left-to-right, one bit at a time, as
described above. The exchanges, like quicksort, occur
during each pass. Recursion orders the '0' and '1'
'subfiles'. The inner loop is shown as:

  while (j != i)
    {
      while (digit(a[i], w) == 0 && (i < j)) i++;
      while (digit(a[j], w) == 1 && (j > 1)) j--;
      exch (a[i], a[j]);
    }

This is followed by recursion on the subfiles. All exchanges
for the left-most bits (in the subfile) are completed before
the subfile is ordered. If there is only one item in the subfile,
return is immediate

> >I did not dispute that claim.  You may note that in another post in this
> >thread I commented that radix sorting was an important technique and
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> You are correct on that point.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Radix sort

_(reply depth: 23)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-04-25T02:09:07+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ea86ae5.15618351@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3e8b881d.147942768@news.optonline.net> <b6hi7f$nv0$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e8cc4e8.229053531@news.optonline.net> <b77c9f$211$2@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <joe_zitzelberger-BA7DE7.19304412042003@corp.supernews.com> <3e98df71.30393272@news.optonline.net> <b815pt$362$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ea5c65f.9265079@news.optonline.net> <b865nj$ete$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ea71d51.97072274@news.optonline.net> <b88sun$o3s$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:

>There was a paper that I can't get to at
>http://www.etk.com/papers/sorting/sorting.htm
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>complicated and long the Quicksort algorithm was compared to the others.

Quicksort is not difficult or lengthy, but it requires recursion. Historically
and traditionally, COBOL didn't support recursion. Newer compilers do, using
local-storage (MF) or similar. 

>If your sort is 23 times faster than QuickSort (with any but specialized data),
>then you probably should patent it.  

It's public domain. You can find a good treatment in Sedgwick and a poor one in
Knuth. Heck, you can find it an IBM 084 card sorter manual. <g>

I wrote a COBOL radix sort this morning in about two hours. Improvement was 7:1,
not quite the 10:1 I predicted. Later I realized I should have turned off bounds
checking and turned on optimization. I'll try that tomorrow. 

I used Micro Focus because it has a readily available 2002-style table sort. I'm
guessing it's probably a Quicksort. Performance seems to be N log(N). With
another compiler I'd have to write my own baseline algorithm or call a C library
function. I didn't want to spend the time. This one sorts 50,000 30-byte random
numbers in .24 seconds compared to Micro Focus' 1.7 seconds. With the right
compiler options, it'll probably go to .15 seconds. 

I'd appreciate timings for MF on a PC platform. I don't have a native code MF PC
compiler. 

My 23:1 was for hand crafted asm vs. whatever the C library was written in. If
it was written in C, that would explain half the difference. 

      $SET SOURCEFORMAT"FREE"
 identification division.
 program-id. radix.
 author. Robert Wagner.
* Testbed for radix-sort.
* results: Sparc Ultra 10, 450 MHz, 10 x sort
*
*    N log(N)      16.75
*    radix cN+      2.40
*
 data division.
 working-storage section.
 01  test-data GLOBAL.
     05  n                           comp  pic s9(09). *> current card
     05  first-n                     comp  pic s9(09). *> first card
     05  radix-columns               comp  pic s9(02).
     05  card      occurs 50000.   *> the data to sort
         10  sort-key.
             15  key-column occurs 30      pic x(01).
     05  next-ns.     *> link to next card
         10  next-n occurs 50000     comp  pic s9(09).
     05  next-n-1s.   *> new order for next iteration
         10  next-n-1 occurs 50000   comp pic s9(09).

 01  init-data.
     05  init-n                      comp  pic s9(09).
     05  init-first value 1          comp  pic s9(09).
     05  init-columns value 7        comp  pic s9(02).
     05  init-card occurs 50000.
         10  init-key-1                    pic v9(18).
         10  init-key-2                    pic v9(12).
     05  .
         10  init-next occurs 50000  comp  pic s9(09).
     05  .
         10  init-next-1 occurs 50000 comp pic s9(09).

 01  timer-variables.
     05  repeat-factor value 10      comp  pic s9(09).
     05  previous-key                      pic  x(30).
     05  card-count                  comp  pic s9(09).
     05  start-time.
         10  start-hours                   pic  9(02).
         10  start-minutes                 pic  9(02).
         10  start-seconds                 pic  9(02).
         10  start-hundredths              pic  9(02).
     05  end-time.
         10  end-hours                     pic  9(02).
         10  end-minutes                   pic  9(02).
         10  end-seconds                   pic  9(02).
         10  end-hundredths                pic  9(02).
     05  elapsed-time                      pic  z(04).99.

 procedure division.
   perform varying n from 1 by 1 until n > 50000
       compute init-key-1 (n) = function random
       compute init-key-2 (n) = function random
       compute init-next (n) = n + 1
       compute init-next-1 (n) = 0
   end-perform
   move zero to init-next (50000)

   display 'N log(N) sort'
   perform timer-on
   perform repeat-factor times
       move init-data to test-data
       call 'mf-sort'
   end-perform
   perform verify-sort
   perform timer-off

   display 'radix sort'
   perform timer-on
   perform repeat-factor times
       move init-data to test-data
       call 'radix-sort'
   end-perform
   perform verify-sort
   perform timer-off

   goback

 . verify-sort.
   move zero to previous-key, card-count
   move first-n to n
   if  n equal to zero
       display 'test failed, no first'
   end-if
   perform until n equal to zero
       if  sort-key (n) less than previous-key
           display 'test failed '
               previous-key space sort-key (n)
           move zero to n
       else
           move sort-key (n) to previous-key
           move next-n (n) to n
           add 1 to card-count
       end-if
   end-perform
   if  card-count not equal to 50000
       display 'test failed, count is ' card-count
   end-if

 . timer-on.
     accept start-time from time
 . timer-off.
     accept end-time from time
     compute elapsed-time rounded =
        (((((((end-hours * 60) +
           end-minutes) * 60) +
           end-seconds) * 100) +
           end-hundredths) -
        ((((((start-hours * 60) +
           start-minutes) * 60) +
           start-seconds) * 100) +
           start-hundredths)) / 100
     display  elapsed-time ' secs'
 .

 identification division.
 program-id. mf-sort.
* Performance of a typical N log(N) sort.
 procedure division.

* if you do not have MF, call qsort() or other
   sort card on ascending sort-key

   perform varying n from 1 by 1 until n > 50000
       compute next-n (n) = n + 1
   end-perform
   move zero to next-n (50000)
   goback.
 end program mf-sort.

 identification division.
 program-id. radix-sort.
* Demo radix sort
 data division.
 working-storage section.
 01  radix-variables.
     05  c                         comp   pic s9(02).
* reverse this if your machine is small-endian
     05  pocket                    comp   pic s9(04).
     05       redefines pocket.
         10                               pic  x(01).
         10  pocket-column                pic  x(01).
     05  swap-count                comp   pic s9(09).
     05  last-n                    comp   pic s9(09).
     05  previous-n-0              comp   pic s9(09).
     05  previous-n-1              comp   pic s9(09).
     05  previous-n-2              comp   pic s9(09).
     05  previous-card.
         10  previous-key                 pic  x(30).
     05  radix-pockets.
         10  radix-pocket occurs 256.
             15  first-in-pocket   comp   pic s9(09).
             15  last-in-pocket    comp   pic s9(09).

 procedure division.
*  one iteration per column. Speed is cN
   perform varying c from radix-columns by -1 until c < 1
*  RUN through the input string, tossing entries into pockets
       move low-values to radix-pockets
       move zero to pocket
       move first-n to n
       perform until n equal to zero
*   determine pocket
           move key-column (n, c) to pocket-column
           add 1 to pocket
           if  first-in-pocket (pocket) equal to zero
               move n to first-in-pocket (pocket)
           end-if
           move last-in-pocket (pocket) to last-n
*   link to previous last in pocket
           if  last-n not equal to zero
               move n to next-n-1 (last-n)
           end-if
*   make this last in pocket
           move n to last-in-pocket (pocket)
           move next-n (n) to n
       end-perform
*  GATHER - link tail of each pocket to head of next
       move zero to first-n, last-n
       perform varying pocket from 1 by 1 until pocket > 256
           move first-in-pocket (pocket) to n
           if  n not equal to zero
               if  first-n equal to zero
                   move n to first-n     *> first card
               end-if
               if  last-n not equal to zero
                   move n to next-n-1 (last-n)  *> link first to last
               end-if
               move last-in-pocket (pocket) to last-n
           end-if
       end-perform
       move zero to next-n-1 (last-n) *> last card
*  MOUNT - input is now in the new order
       move next-n-1s to next-ns
   end-perform

*  If radix-columns = 30, the above is all you need.
*  By itself, it runs faster than the MF sort when radix-columns < 18.
*  The following is optimization. It permits radix-columns
*  to be set less than 30 for speed. It then merges out of order
*  cards into place. It almost always works in two iterations,
*  will run once if the above radix put entries into perfect sequence.
*  On the test data, this happens with radix-columns is 7 or more.

*  MERGE out of order cards
*  This is a modified insertion sort which does not
*  require backward links. When finding a step down, it searches
*  forward looking for a step up, then exchanges the big record before
*  the step down with the little record before the step up, then takes
*  a step backward. It cannot work in one pass because the step back
*  cannot find (n - 2) when there are two consecutive steps back.
*  I don't think this is a documented algorithm; I just made it up.

   move 1 to swap-count
   perform until swap-count = zero
       move zero to previous-key, previous-n-0, previous-n-1
       move zero to swap-count
       move first-n to n
       perform until n equal to zero
           if  sort-key (n) less than previous-key
               perform until n equal to zero or
                   sort-key (n) not less than previous-key
                   move n to previous-n-2
                   move next-n (n) to n
               end-perform
*  exchange two cards
   call 'cbl_xor' using card (previous-n-1) card (previous-n-2) by value 30
   call 'cbl_xor' using card (previous-n-2) card (previous-n-1) by value 30
   call 'cbl_xor' using card (previous-n-1) card (previous-n-2) by value 30
*  alternative to the above 3 lines, if you don't like xor
*              move card (previous-n-1) to previous-card
*              move card (previous-n-2) to card (previous-n-1)
*              move previous-card to card (previous-n-2)
               move previous-n-0 to n
               if  n equal to zero
                   move previous-n-1 to first-n, n
               end-if
               add 1 to swap-count
           end-if
           move previous-n-1 to previous-n-0
           move n to previous-n-1
           move sort-key (n) to previous-key
           move next-n (n) to n
       end-perform
   end-perform

   goback.
 end program radix-sort.
 end program radix.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Radix sort

_(reply depth: 24)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-04-25T15:09:00+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b8a8uv$j7$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3ea71d51.97072274@news.optonline.net> <b88sun$o3s$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ea86ae5.15618351@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:

> Quicksort is not difficult or lengthy, but it requires recursion.

No. it doesn't _require_ recursion, though typically it is implemented 
using recursion.  It is just as easy to implement using iteration.

>>If your sort is 23 times faster than QuickSort (with any but specialized
>>data), then you probably should patent it.

I note that you didn't claim '23 times faster than Quicksort' but '23 times 
faster than qsort()'.  The latter is a very generalised routine that has 
unavoidable overheads to implement generality.  

> I used Micro Focus because it has a readily available 2002-style table
> sort. I'm guessing it's probably a Quicksort. Performance seems to be N
> log(N). 

I thought you were going to sort one of your 'lists'.

> My 23:1 was for hand crafted asm vs. whatever the C library was written
> in. If it was written in C, that would explain half the difference.

And generalising your code into a function with a similar spec to qsort() 
may explain the rest.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Radix sort

_(reply depth: 25)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-04-25T11:06:22+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ea910da.58109665@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3ea71d51.97072274@news.optonline.net> <b88sun$o3s$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ea86ae5.15618351@news.optonline.net> <b8a8uv$j7$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>Robert Wagner wrote:

>I note that you didn't claim '23 times faster than Quicksort' but '23 times 
>faster than qsort()'.  The latter is a very generalised routine that has 
>unavoidable overheads to implement generality.

True. Especially the call to a user supplied swap. They should have provided a
default function that swaps fixed length strings. 

>> I used Micro Focus because it has a readily available 2002-style table
>> sort. I'm guessing it's probably a Quicksort. Performance seems to be N
>> log(N). 
>
>I thought you were going to sort one of your 'lists'.

I did. The entries are sequenced by forward-pointing subscripts. I don't
physically rearrange entries except in the merge phase, which is not executed in
the demo because the radix sort does the whole job. The merge could have been
done by swapping pointers, as well, but the code would have been longer and
harder to understand at a glance. 

>> My 23:1 was for hand crafted asm vs. whatever the C library was written
>> in. If it was written in C, that would explain half the difference.
>
>And generalising your code into a function with a similar spec to qsort() 
>may explain the rest.

It was generalized, with the exception of the swap function. It sorted strings
and big-endian integers only, not floating point. It also sorted lists with true
pointers, which the 'generalized' qsort() cannot do.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Radix sort

_(reply depth: 26)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-04-29T01:33:06-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0304290033.3fdf9cf3@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3ea71d51.97072274@news.optonline.net> <b88sun$o3s$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ea86ae5.15618351@news.optonline.net> <b8a8uv$j7$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ea910da.58109665@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 

> >faster than qsort()'. 

> True. Especially the call to a user supplied swap. They should have provided a
> default function that swaps fixed length strings. 

No wonder your use of qsort() was slow, that user defined function is
not to swap the two, it is only to compare them.

    qsort(array, number, width, func)

    "" func is passed pointers to the two elements being compared, it
is to return an integer less than, equal to, or greater than zero
depending on ..."

For a default compare use strcmp() if appropriate.  That is _why_
strcmp returns the results it does.

If you've been swapping the two array elements, no wonder you made it
slow.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Radix sort

_(reply depth: 24)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-04-26T18:42:12+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3eaacf33.172392018@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3e8b881d.147942768@news.optonline.net> <b6hi7f$nv0$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e8cc4e8.229053531@news.optonline.net> <b77c9f$211$2@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <joe_zitzelberger-BA7DE7.19304412042003@corp.supernews.com> <3e98df71.30393272@news.optonline.net> <b815pt$362$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ea5c65f.9265079@news.optonline.net> <b865nj$ete$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ea71d51.97072274@news.optonline.net> <b88sun$o3s$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ea86ae5.15618351@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:

I tried optimizing the Radix code I posted here. 

1.  Turned bounds checking off and optimization on.

Speed improved only slightly. 

Also, I discovered my initial timing of Micro Focus' sort was off. There must
have been another job running when I timed it. At this point, Radix is 4.5 times
faster than Micro Focus.

2.  Changed compiler options.

When we discussed options to 'make all my COMPs something specific', we
overlooked one. Micro Focus has COMP, which says 'make all my COMPs COMP-X'.
That's machine native arithmetic, without COBOL's requirement for overflow
checking. In other words, bypass checking whether s9(4) exceeds 9,999. Sounds
like just the ticket. Results showed it to be slower. 

Overflow checking can be bypassed with the NOTRUNC option. That improved it
some. So did IBMCOMP, which is similar to putting SYNCHRONIZED on everything. It
inserts slack bytes in order to put variables on word boundaries. I wouldn't
have thought word boundaries mattered to a modern machine. They do to this RISCy
SPARC, but only slightly.

For laughs, I tried COMP-3. Results were only 1.5 times slower. Hmmm, maybe
numeric format isn't that important. Maybe it's spending most of its time doing
something else. That insight produced the answer below.

3.  Changed key size. 

Keep in mind, both of these sorts have speed proportional to N, the number of
entries. The number of characters (c) in an entry shouldn't make any difference.
Someone might ask, 'Doesn't it take twice as long to compare 60 bytes as 30?'
The answer is no. The machine or library function stops on the first unequal. It
takes twice as long to MOVE 60 bytes, but I don't move keys. I do it all with
pointer-like subscripts. 

           key len   30       18        60        30
    N log(N)      12.08     3.71      7.20   IBMCOMP
    radix            2.68     1.88      2.27      2.26
    ratio             4.5        2.0       3.2        5.3

The findings defy logical explanation. Radix speed was relatively flat, but
Micro Focus' performance was all over the place. Increasing the key from 18 to
30 made it run 3.2 times slower. Further increasing the key to 60 made run 1.6
times FASTER. How can that be? Could there be a boundary condition? With key
size 18 and 50,000 entries, the table size is 900K, just under the range of a
16-bit pointer. Increasing the size to 30 would kick it into 32-bit 'mode', if a
SPARC has such modes, which I doubt. I repeated each test at least five times to
make sure I wasn't getting a false reading. Also, I was watching 'top' in
another window to insure other tasks weren't active. 

Does this mean Micro Focus' sort is NOT an (n ln(n)) performer? My instinct says
no, it's something in the environment. I cannot imagine an algorithm which goes
faster when keys are bigger. 

4.  Eliminate library calls

Examining generated code shows MANY instructions per line of COBOL -- generally
10-20. Worse, there are library calls for every use of a subscript and every
string comparison. When there are two subscripts, sending and receiving, there
are two calls. After changing one of the subscripts to an index and examining
the code, I see the library call is gone. AHA. Speed is not a function of
numeric format -- comp vs. comp-5 -- nor bounds checking nor optimization (loop
control produces only 4-5 instructions), it is a function of library calls. 

I rewrote the program using exclusively indexes, no subscripts. Not because
indexes are inherently faster, rather because they produce inline code. Well
almost always. My first attempt was setting an index on one table to an index on
another. That produced a library call, which made performance about the same as
subscripted. The second version uses a single table. It generates no library
calls at all. It runs 1.6 times faster than typical subscripted versions. 

The latest finding: 1.72 seconds for 10 sorts, which is 7.0 times faster than
Micro Focus.
```

- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3e8b881d.147942768@news.optonline.net> <b6hi7f$nv0$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e8cc4e8.229053531@news.optonline.net> <b77c9f$211$2@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <joe_zitzelberger-BA7DE7.19304412042003@corp.supernews.com> <3e98df71.30393272@news.optonline.net> <b815pt$362$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ea5c65f.9265079@news.optonline.net> <b865nj$ete$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ea71d51.97072274@news.optonline.net> <b88sun$o3s$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sorting algorithms

_(reply depth: 24)_

- **From:** joe_zitzelberger@vsam.org (Joe Zitzelberger)
- **Date:** 2003-04-29T10:53:56-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c99d007f.0304290953.4eba78a2@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <b6hi7f$nv0$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e8cc4e8.229053531@news.optonline.net> <b77c9f$211$2@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <joe_zitzelberger-BA7DE7.19304412042003@corp.supernews.com> <3e98df71.30393272@news.optonline.net> <b815pt$362$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ea5c65f.9265079@news.optonline.net> <b865nj$ete$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ea71d51.97072274@news.optonline.net> <b88sun$o3s$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3EA8F0CB.BB80CBE2@worldnet.att.net>`

```
As promised a few weeks ago, Quicksort for pointers.

Easy to code, easy to understand.  If I were a smart-ass I might say
that some people need a few pointers on the ease (of ability) of
quicksorting linked lists, but they wouldn't be very punny.

Enjoy...

       Identification Division.
       Program-ID. PTRQSORT is recursive.
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      * This is a cobol implementation of quicksort for a linked list.
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
       Environment Division.
       Data Division.
       Local-Storage Section.

       01 PTRQSORT-Local-Storage.

         05 Lo                   Pointer.
         05 Hi                   Pointer.

         05 Swap-Key             Pic X(8).
         05 Swap-Data            Pic X(72).

         05 Pivot                Pic 9(08) Value 0.

       Linkage Section.

       01 Lo-Item.
         05 Lo-Prev              Pointer.
         05 Lo-Next              Pointer.
         05 Lo-Key               Pic 9(08).
         05 Lo-Data              Pic X(72).

       01 Hi-Item.
         05 Hi-Prev              Pointer.
         05 Hi-Next              Pointer.
         05 Hi-Key               Pic 9(08).
         05 Hi-Data              Pic X(72).

       01 Low-Element            Pointer.
       01 High-Element           Pointer.

       Procedure Division using Low-Element High-Element.

           Set address of Lo-Item to Low-Element
           Set address of Hi-Item to High-Element

      * ----- A simple performance enhancement for Quicksort is to
      * ----- use a less resource intensive sort when the partitions
      * ----- become very small.  This will network sort partitions
      * ----- of size 2 or 3.  Otherwise it will continue
partitioning.
           Evaluate true

             when (Low-Element = High-Element)
                Continue

             when (Low-Element = Hi-Prev) or (Lo-Next = High-Element)
                Perform Network-Sort-2

             when (Lo-Next = Hi-Prev)
                Perform Network-Sort-3

             when other
              Set Lo to Low-Element
              Set Hi to High-Element
              Perform Select-Pivot

      * ----- Loop through table until indices cross
              Perform until (Hi = Lo)

      * -------- Locate an item that should not be in less partition
                 Set address of Lo-Item to Lo
                 Perform until (Lo = High-Element)
                            or (Lo = Hi)
                            or (Lo-Key >= Pivot)
                    Set Lo to Lo-Next
                    Set address of Lo-Item to Lo
                 End-Perform

      * -------- Locate an item that should not be in greater
partition
                 Set address of Hi-Item to Hi
                 Perform until (Hi = Low-Element)
                            or (Hi = Lo)
                            or (Hi-Key <= Pivot)
                    Set Hi to Hi-Prev
                    Set address of Hi-Item to Hi
                 End-Perform

      * -------- Exchange the two and keep looking
                 If ( Lo Not = Hi )
                    Perform Swap-Elements
                 End-If

              End-Perform

              Perform Qsort-Partitions

           End-Evaluate

      *--------------------------------------------------------------*
      * Return in a manner that does not wreck our environment
      *--------------------------------------------------------------*
           Goback.

      *--------------------------------------------------------------*
      * Select a pivot guarenteed not to be a worst case
      *--------------------------------------------------------------*
       Select-Pivot.
           Set address of Lo-Item to Lo
           Set address of Hi-Item to Hi
           Compute Pivot = Function Mean ( Lo-Key Hi-Key )
           Exit.

      *--------------------------------------------------------------*
      * Exchange elements in the wrong partition
      *--------------------------------------------------------------*
       Swap-Elements.
           Move Lo-Key    to Swap-Key
           Move Lo-Data   to Swap-Data
           Move Hi-Key    to Lo-Key
           Move Hi-Data   to Lo-Data
           Move Swap-Key  to Hi-Key
           Move Swap-Data to Hi-Data
           Exit.
           
      *--------------------------------------------------------------*
      * Sort the two sub-partitions
      *--------------------------------------------------------------*
       Qsort-Partitions.

      * -- Sort the less sub-partition
           Set address of Hi-Item to Hi
           If Hi Not = High-Element
              Call 'PTRQSORT' Using
                 Low-Element
                 Hi-Prev
              End-Call
           End-If

      * -- Sort the greater sub-partition
           Set address of Lo-Item to Lo
           If Lo Not = Low-Element
              Call 'PTRQSORT' Using
                 Lo
                 High-Element
              End-Call
           End-If

           Exit.
      
      *-----------------------------------------------------------
      * Sort small partitions using networks
      *------------------------------------------------------------
       Network-Sort-2.
           Set address of Lo-Item to Low-Element
           Set address of Hi-Item to High-Element
           If Lo-Key > Hi-Key
              Perform Swap-Elements
           End-If
           Exit.
     
       Network-Sort-3.
           Set address of Lo-Item to Low-Element
           Set address of Hi-Item to Lo-Next
           If Lo-Key > Hi-Key
              Perform Swap-Elements
           End-If
           Set address of Hi-Item to High-Element
           Set address of Lo-Item to Hi-Prev
           If Lo-Key > Hi-Key
              Perform Swap-Elements
           End-If
           Set address of Lo-Item to Low-Element
           Set address of Hi-Item to Lo-Next
           If Lo-Key > Hi-Key
              Perform Swap-Elements
           End-If
           Exit.
     
       End Program PTRQSORT.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sorting algorithms

_(reply depth: 25)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-04-29T18:37:24-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0304291737.118171b2@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <b77c9f$211$2@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <joe_zitzelberger-BA7DE7.19304412042003@corp.supernews.com> <3e98df71.30393272@news.optonline.net> <b815pt$362$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ea5c65f.9265079@news.optonline.net> <b865nj$ete$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ea71d51.97072274@news.optonline.net> <b88sun$o3s$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3EA8F0CB.BB80CBE2@worldnet.att.net> <c99d007f.0304290953.4eba78a2@posting.google.com>`

```
joe_zitzelberger@vsam.org (Joe Zitzelberger) wrote 

> Easy to code, easy to understand.  If I were a smart-ass I might say
> that some people need a few pointers on the ease (of ability) of
> quicksorting linked lists, but they wouldn't be very punny.

This has two differences from what RW described as his 'lists' and
claimed to be advantageous.

First your list also has prior pointers which are used. While this is
certainly not wrong in any way, it is not how RW had created his list.
The requirement for prior pointers makes the list creation slower and
the code more complex.

The second is that RW had claimed an advantage for lists in the
ability to have variable sized data items. This means that data cannot
be simply swapped between two elements.  It may be that new elements
are malloc()ed and the old ones free()ed, or the pointers are adjusted
in the next and prior elements.

This again makes the code much more complex, and the run time much
slower.  As RW's lists were already slower (or slow as) sequentially
accessed tables, then the claimed 'advantages' were disappearing.

The issue that I raised was whether RW could sort one of _his_ lists,
not whether someone else could create a better list.

In fact the issue of sorting a list is a moot one anyway.  Why not
just create a tree and not only have it already sorted when complete,
but also have fast access via the tree structure.

Of course the code for that will be even more complex (don't forget
you may need rotations) and likely much slower than a table load, SORT
and SEARCH.

Solutions should be appropriate to the problem.  The issue that I took
with RW was his insistence 'that we should try them and won't look
back'.  He was arguing with his ego, his lists give no advantage over
a table for what I do.

(BTW: EXIT should be in a paragraph by itself and is just a noise
word, but I guess it is just a style thing unless you want
portability)
```

```
riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>joe_zitzelberger@vsam.org (Joe Zitzelberger) wrote 
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>the code more complex.

True. If the only reason for having prior pointers is sorting, use an exchange
sort rather than an insertion sort. The former doesn't require prior pointers.
Nor does my 'modified insertion sort', posted here.  

>The second is that RW had claimed an advantage for lists in the
>ability to have variable sized data items. This means that data cannot
>be simply swapped between two elements.  It may be that new elements
>are malloc()ed and the old ones free()ed, or the pointers are adjusted
>in the next and prior elements.

The latter - pointers are adjusted. 

>This again makes the code much more complex, and the run time much
>slower.  As RW's lists were already slower (or slow as) sequentially
>accessed tables, then the claimed 'advantages' were disappearing.

How is 'set address of line-entry to next-line' complex, or "much more complex?"
It is no more complex than 'set x-line up by 1'. 

>The issue that I raised was whether RW could sort one of _his_ lists,
>not whether someone else could create a better list.

I showed how in the radix sort I posted here. It sorted a list by manipulating
pointer-like subscripts. It does not swap entries in memory. 

>In fact the issue of sorting a list is a moot one anyway.  Why not
>just create a tree and not only have it already sorted when complete,
>but also have fast access via the tree structure.

Because it is slower. 

>Solutions should be appropriate to the problem.  

Why does that phrase keep coming up when people are defending Bad Practices?
They cannot think of a valid defense so they say 'best tool for the job', in the
hope no one will notice they haven't substantiated that their tool is better
than others. 

>The issue that I took
>with RW was his insistence 'that we should try them and won't look
>back'.  He was arguing with his ego, his lists give no advantage over
>a table for what I do.

You don't have variable length tables, or tables with variable length entries
(without padding them with trailing spaces)? I find that hard to believe.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sorting algorithms

_(reply depth: 29)_

- **From:** "Paul Raulerson" <praulerson@hot.NOSPAM.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-03T08:35:18+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<abLsa.18831$8e7.801711@twister.austin.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <c99d007f.0304290953.4eba78a2@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0304291737.118171b2@posting.google.com> <3eb06873.539359712@news.optonline.net> <b8rh9p$qne$1@panix5.panix.com> <3eb3289c.32390864@news.optonline.net>`

```
There is a real kernel of truth here; the most correct program in the world
does not a bit of good if it runs too slowly to do the job. For example, an
insurance daily cycle application that takes longer than a day to run is
pretty useless. At the very least, you are not going to be running it daily!

The sad part is there are a lot of places out there that have spent a ton of
money to create correct programs that simply run too slowly to be used. That
isn't to say that OOP or OOD is at fault there, just that usually the
implementers were inexperienced and did know or thoroughly consider that the
implementation would have to be fast as well as correct.

-Paul
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sorting algorithms

_(reply depth: 30)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-05-04T07:28:48+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b915b4$4pe$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <b8rh9p$qne$1@panix5.panix.com> <3eb3289c.32390864@news.optonline.net> <abLsa.18831$8e7.801711@twister.austin.rr.com>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message

>> >>>In fact the issue of sorting a list is a moot one anyway.  Why not
>> >>>just create a tree and not only have it already sorted when complete,
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> fallacies.

Except of course it hasn't either sharpened your wits or exposed any 
fallacies, except one that you have created.

If one builds data into a tree structure then this may well be faster than 
building a list and sorting it, no matter how clever the sort.

When you have substanciated your assertion of 'because it is slower' with 
an actual demonstration then I may be inclined to see that the tre may be 
slower to build.

However, one doen't build a sorted list just to show how clever one can be, 
if it in fact does that, one builds it to access in various way.  When 
random access as a 'look up' is required, such as with a dictionary or for 
parsing, then trees are _much_ faster than simple sorted lists.

Even if the building of a tree is slower than a build and sort (and I am 
not convinced that it would be), the 'fast access via the tree structure' 
would certainly _not_ be 'slower' as you claim.

In any real use it may well be that the _slowest_ mechanism is to build a 
list then sort it leaving serial access, depending on how many accesses 
were required.  This may also be the least functional.

If serial access in order is all that is really required then building the 
list by inserting would likely be faster than sorting after a random build.
```

```
"Paul Raulerson" <praulerson@hot.NOSPAM.rr.com> wrote:

>There is a real kernel of truth here; the most correct program in the world
>does not a bit of good if it runs too slowly to do the job. For example, an
>insurance daily cycle application that takes longer than a day to run is
>pretty useless. At the very least, you are not going to be running it daily!

Nowadays they just throw more hardware at it. Too slow on one machine? Partition
the database onto 20 disks and run the application on 20 machines in parallel. 

Or get a faster machine. The place I'm at now had daily batch jobs that ran 12
hours and more. A faster (and cheaper!) machine cut the time to less than 2
hours. (They could have accomplished the same by switching from interpreted to
native code.) 

>The sad part is there are a lot of places out there that have spent a ton of
>money to create correct programs that simply run too slowly to be used. That
>isn't to say that OOP or OOD is at fault there, just that usually the
>implementers were inexperienced and did know or thoroughly consider that the
>implementation would have to be fast as well as correct.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sorting algorithms

_(reply depth: 31)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-05-04T10:48:55+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b91h2i$9ka$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3eb3289c.32390864@news.optonline.net> <abLsa.18831$8e7.801711@twister.austin.rr.com> <3eb411ad.92064859@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:

> Or get a faster machine. The place I'm at now had daily batch jobs that
> ran 12 hours and more. A faster (and cheaper!) machine cut the time to
> less than 2 hours. (They could have accomplished the same by switching
> from interpreted to native code.)

That last is probably an incorrect assertion.  In most cases a batch system 
is entirely limited by disk I-O.  Actual disk access is cached and occurs 
asynchronously relieving the CPU to process the code.  This means that the 
amount of free CPU time is mostly quite large and changing from 'int' to 
'gnt' makes only a small differce to the actual run time.

Of course you may have non-typical workload where it was entirely compute 
intensive.  In that case going from .int to .gnt _may_ have reduced the 
time by half, which is _not_ the "same thing" as 12 hours to 2 hours.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sorting algorithms

_(reply depth: 32)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-05-04T02:22:41+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3eb468ad.12150088@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3eb3289c.32390864@news.optonline.net> <abLsa.18831$8e7.801711@twister.austin.rr.com> <3eb411ad.92064859@news.optonline.net> <b91h2i$9ka$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>Robert Wagner wrote:
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>'gnt' makes only a small differce to the actual run time.

Very astute of you to catch that. You're correct.

>Of course you may have non-typical workload where it was entirely compute 
>intensive.  In that case going from .int to .gnt _may_ have reduced the 
>time by half, which is _not_ the "same thing" as 12 hours to 2 hours.

The difference for compute intensive programs is 9:1. That was the first thing I
measured. 

ALL my programs are compute intensive. They are also native code .gnt. The
interactive ones respond in less than 100 ms, the PC standard for
responsiveness. It's a heck of a lot better than the mainframe standard of 2
seconds.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sorting algorithms

_(reply depth: 27)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-05-01T14:52:52-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0305011352.7401a14e@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3e98df71.30393272@news.optonline.net> <b815pt$362$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ea5c65f.9265079@news.optonline.net> <b865nj$ete$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ea71d51.97072274@news.optonline.net> <b88sun$o3s$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3EA8F0CB.BB80CBE2@worldnet.att.net> <c99d007f.0304290953.4eba78a2@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0304291737.118171b2@posting.google.com> <3eb06873.539359712@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 

> >The second is that RW had claimed an advantage for lists in the
> >ability to have variable sized data items. This means that data cannot
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> The latter - pointers are adjusted. 

Which, or course, requires retrieving the next and prior item for each
of the two being swapped, a total of 6 items to juggle for each swap.

> >This again makes the code much more complex, and the run time much
> >slower.  As RW's lists were already slower (or slow as) sequentially
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> It is no more complex than 'set x-line up by 1'. 

It is 'more complex' because 6 list entries need to be juggled (where
there are next and prior).

> >In fact the issue of sorting a list is a moot one anyway.  Why not
> >just create a tree and not only have it already sorted when complete,
> >but also have fast access via the tree structure.
> 
> Because it is slower. 

The advantage of a tree is that the search time is significantly
faster than a serial search of a simple linked list.
 
> >Solutions should be appropriate to the problem.  
> 
> Why does that phrase keep coming up 

Are you arguing that solutions should _not_ be appropriate to the
problem ?

You seem to be trying to say that we should do it _your_ way
regardless of whether it is appropriate.

> when people are defending Bad Practices?

Which particular 'Bad Practice (tm)RW' do you think is being
'defended' ?

> They cannot think of a valid defense so they say 'best tool for the job', in the
> hope no one will notice they haven't substantiated that their tool is better
> than others. 

Quite the reverse actually.  It is _your_ job to substanciate that
your tool is better than others _for_the_purpose_.

As you haven't even bothered to find out what I do then I can't see
how you can claim that your tool is better.

In fact I have found that your claim for single linked lists being
faster was untrue (its about the same for create/load and serial
search) and this would degrade if additional pointers were used.

While it is true that lists can be created to be any size, I have no
need for such a tool in anything that I do.  Large amounts of data are
sharable in all systems that I work on (at the moment) and lists make
them unsharable (so do tables) and so large amounts of data are
accessed on sharable media.

In any case, the whole point of having a table is to have it
accessible. A sorted table can use SEARCH ALL or some other direct
access by index.  A list such as yours must be serially searched or
accessed and this makes it slow and cumbersome.

Thus a list is a good tool where it fits the requirements of the
problem, a double linked list is a good tool where serial access back
and forth is required, a tree is a good tool where random lookups are
required.

A table makes a good approximation of all of those with only the minor
disdvantage that it has to be created with a fixed size.

If that actually is a major disadvantage then use a list or tree - or
a disk file.

> >The issue that I took
> >with RW was his insistence 'that we should try them and won't look
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> (without padding them with trailing spaces)? I find that hard to believe.

I don't have tables that the data exceeds.

'Padding with trailing spaces' is completely irrelevant.  Making the
program use a minor amount less of memory is of no interest at all
comapred to losing fast access to the data (via SEARCH ALL or directly
with an index).

While you promote the 'advantages', real or imagined, and whether they
apply or not, you simply dismiss the _disadvantages_ as if they didn't
exist.

You are the embodiment of the phrase: 'to a hammer all problems look
like nails'.
```

```
>>

complex?"
>> It is no more complex than 'set x-line up by 1'. 
>
>It is 'more complex' because 6 list entries need to be juggled (where
>there are next and prior).

You are confusing sorting with using. There is no need to swap entries when
advancing to the next element in a list. Your statements are FUD. Were you
formerly an IBM salesman?

>> >Solutions should be appropriate to the problem.  
>> 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>problem ?

No, I'm arguing that people should not use 'appropriate for the task' to cover
their lack of substantiation. 
>
>You seem to be trying to say that we should do it _your_ way
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>'defended' ?

I don't understand what you're talking about here. 

>> They cannot think of a valid defense so they say 'best tool for the job', in
the
>> hope no one will notice they haven't substantiated that their tool is better
>> than others. 
>
>Quite the reverse actually.  It is _your_ job to substanciate that
>your tool is better than others _for_the_purpose_.

I did that by posting a sort 23 times faster than qsort(). You brushed it aside.
So much for substantiation. When the other side rejects facts, you're facing an
irrational opponent. 

>As you haven't even bothered to find out what I do then I can't see
>how you can claim that your tool is better.

You sort tables or lists, right? That's all I need to know.

>In fact I have found that your claim for single linked lists being
>faster was untrue (its about the same for create/load and serial
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>accessed on sharable media.

That's not true. A table or list in memory can be shared between tasks. 

>In any case, the whole point of having a table is to have it
>accessible. A sorted table can use SEARCH ALL or some other direct
>access by index.  A list such as yours must be serially searched or
>accessed and this makes it slow and cumbersome.

As I said above, it can be treed. But my text editor is blindingly fast on pure
sequential searches. Searching 4 meg of text takes less than 50 ms. It stores
lines of text as lists. The reason it's so fast is because it uses a 'repne
scasb' to look for the first byte of the search key. If the first byte is
wildcarded, it slows down noticably. 

Would you like to see regular expression search written in COBOL? I thought not.

>A table makes a good approximation of all of those with only the minor
>disdvantage that it has to be created with a fixed size.

No, a maximum size. OCCURS DEPENDING ON can specify the actual size. 

>> You don't have variable length tables, or tables with variable length entries
>> (without padding them with trailing spaces)? I find that hard to believe.
>
>I don't have tables that the data exceeds.

Typical RP disengeninuity. I'll bet you're in there monthly adjusting table
sizes. 

>'Padding with trailing spaces' is completely irrelevant.  Making the
>program use a minor amount less of memory is of no interest at all
>comapred to losing fast access to the data (via SEARCH ALL or directly
>with an index).

The amount of memory is not minor when dealing with hundreds of thousands of
lines of text. I have no need to search that text except sequentially. Best tool
for the job. 

>You are the embodiment of the phrase: 'to a hammer all problems look
>like nails'.

Yadda, yadda. If you're trying to make a point, please use discursive statements
rather than cliches.
```

- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <b77c9f$211$2@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <joe_zitzelberger-BA7DE7.19304412042003@corp.supernews.com> <3e98df71.30393272@news.optonline.net> <b815pt$362$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ea5c65f.9265079@news.optonline.net> <b865nj$ete$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ea71d51.97072274@news.optonline.net> <b88sun$o3s$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3EA8F0CB.BB80CBE2@worldnet.att.net> <c99d007f.0304290953.4eba78a2@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0304291737.118171b2@posting.google.com>`

```
In article <217e491a.0304291737.118171b2@posting.google.com>,
 riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

> joe_zitzelberger@vsam.org (Joe Zitzelberger) wrote 
> 
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
> not whether someone else could create a better list.


I'm not trying to sort one of RWs lists, just showing that quicksort can 
easily do lists.  It will also do single linked lists, but the code gets 
slightly ugly -- and writing a value to a word is about the fastest 
memory access you are ever going to find.  Why not make always make 
lists doubles, it is really a free operation?

The above code could also be easily modified to swap pointers instead of 
moving data, but that too adds to the ugly factor.  As Cobol lacks a 
rich syntax for using pointers I went with clarity.

(How about that as a syntax DATA-ITEM of POINTER-NAME -- sort of a named 
using.  Does that have anyone recoiling in horror? I rather like it, is 
that J4 thing still open?)


> In fact the issue of sorting a list is a moot one anyway.  Why not
> just create a tree and not only have it already sorted when complete,
> but also have fast access via the tree structure.


Because solutions should be appropriate for the problem.  And lists are 
sometimes the right tool.


> Of course the code for that will be even more complex (don't forget
> you may need rotations) and likely much slower than a table load, SORT
> and SEARCH.


Neither lists or trees are complex.  And while front loading might be 
slower, frequent inserts and deletes interspersed with reads will be 
faster then a complete table reorg every time.  The overall access 
pattern needs to be known to determine which is the better structure.  
There are many cases where tables are a bad idea.


> Solutions should be appropriate to the problem.  The issue that I took
> with RW was his insistence 'that we should try them and won't look
> back'.  He was arguing with his ego, his lists give no advantage over
> a table for what I do.


Then don't use his lists.


> (BTW: EXIT should be in a paragraph by itself and is just a noise
> word, but I guess it is just a style thing unless you want
> portability)


I use "EXIT." instead of "." to terminate paragraphs because of a bug 
(feature) in the CICS4.1 precompiler that would assume "END-EXEC" 
followed by a "." was really "END-EXEC." -- this lead to a few broken 
compiles and finally to using this IBM extension.

Yesterday I took a look at the latest precompiliers for CICSTS1.2, 1.3 
and 2.2 -- all have corrected the problem and v2.2 has even discovered 
the END-CALL scope terminator.  So I'll be changing my style to fit this 
reality.  Thanks for making me revisit this.

I do find that it is easier to see the end of a paragraph with "EXIT." 
than ".", so I might take the suggestion of someone on the periods 
thread (Howard?) and start using "CONTINUE." to terminate.

Is there any consensus on CLC about the proper way to terminate a 
paragraph?
```

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-05-01T14:03:48+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b8r9g4$7ti$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <b77c9f$211$2@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <joe_zitzelberger-BA7DE7.19304412042003@corp.supernews.com> <3e98df71.30393272@news.optonline.net> <b815pt$362$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ea5c65f.9265079@news.optonline.net> <b865nj$ete$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ea71d51.97072274@news.optonline.net> <b88sun$o3s$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3EA8F0CB.BB80CBE2@worldnet.att.net> <c99d007f.0304290953.4eba78a2@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0304291737.118171b2@posting.google.com> <joe_zitzelberger-F026C6.08553501052003@corp.supernews.com>`

```

On  1-May-2003, Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com> wrote:

> I do find that it is easier to see the end of a paragraph with "EXIT."
> than ".", so I might take the suggestion of someone on the periods
> thread (Howard?) and start using "CONTINUE." to terminate.

One advantage of terminating with a CONTINUE. instead of an EXIT. is that only
some compilers will accept EXIT in a paragraph with other statements.

CONTINUE. will always work.  You can add debug statements before or after it and
not have to change it.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sorting algorithms

_(reply depth: 28)_

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-02T08:47:10-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<joe_zitzelberger-C2560B.08471002052003@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3e98df71.30393272@news.optonline.net> <b815pt$362$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ea5c65f.9265079@news.optonline.net> <b865nj$ete$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ea71d51.97072274@news.optonline.net> <b88sun$o3s$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3EA8F0CB.BB80CBE2@worldnet.att.net> <c99d007f.0304290953.4eba78a2@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0304291737.118171b2@posting.google.com> <joe_zitzelberger-F026C6.08553501052003@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0305011416.540be8e@posting.google.com>`

```
In article <217e491a.0305011416.540be8e@posting.google.com>,
 riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

> Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com> wrote> 
> > There are many cases where tables are a bad idea.
> 
> 'bad' ?   

bad ('bad) adj.
1 : failing to reach an acceptable standard 
2 : inadequate or unsuited to a purpose <a bad plan> <bad lighting>
3 : incorrect or faulty <bad grammar>
Inflected Form(s):   worse  /'w&rs/; worst  /'w&rst/

Consider a table that must remain sorted and accept frequent inserts or 
deletes.  With a tree structure the insert or delete is mostly harmless, 
but with the table it required moving all subsequent elements down one 
to make a hole for the new element.

Think about the alternatives.

SEARCH doesn't really need ordering, so it can just plug items at the 
end, but it has a serial search pattern that will require looking at an 
average of half the elements to find the one it is looking for.

SEARCH ALL has the deficiency that it requires extensive moves of 
elements to insert or delete, but lookups are Nlog2.

A tree enjoys the best features of each without the deficiencies of 
each.  Fast, easy inserts and fast, easy lookups.

Thus, the TABLE/SEARCH is bad(1,2) when compared to the tree.  And the 
decision to use that structure was bad(3) when made by the programmer.


Consider also a table that needs a large allocation for a worst case, 
but the normal case is quite small.  Certainly you could preallocate and 
let the paging system take care of of for your, but they would require 
extensive knowledge of the target machine.  It also would not work if 
that table was a hash with something like even distrubution as all pages 
would likely be touched no matter how sparse.

This is a perfect case for a dynamic collection.  When a table in this 
circumstanc is compared to a (list, tree, vector, map, whatever) it is 
also found bad(1,2).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sorting algorithms

```
In article <3eb3085b$1_2@127.0.0.1>,
Peter E.C. Dashwood <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>
>"Joe Zitzelberger" <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com> wrote in message
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>"Cutting to the chase", eh, Joe...<G>

Dear me, Mr Dashwood, are you *so* annoyed that you didn't see that one 
coming when I first used it?

>
>Doc, see what moral decline you have exemplified to the youth of
>today?...For shame, Sire, ...for shame....

Exemplified?  Shucks, I ain't showed nobody no directions... remember 
Steinbeck's 'The Grapes of Wrath'?  One of the characters was the 
Preacher, who didn't preach any more... a man of God who liked his bottle 
and the company of a young girl who'd been all fired-up by the Spirit... 
not a *bad* man, mind you, just one that is human, all-too-human...

... anyhow, there was a scene where the bunch of 'em are standin' 'round 
th' fire one evenin', passin' 'round' th' jug... an' the Preacher tells 
it, clean 'n simple...

... 'I'm a Leader of Men... but I ain't got no place to lead 'em to.'

DD
```

```
Actually, I'm really amused... but some people say they can't tell the
difference when I'm annoyed or amused. It's since the Botox...

> >
> >Doc, see what moral decline you have exemplified to the youth of
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>
LOL!

I read the book as a 13 year old (we were required to read it). Found it
fairly hard going so went for Hemingway as a  modern American author instead
( We had to have at least ONE, and I could really relate to the "Old Man and
the Sea" as where I live we have a fair bit of big game fishing and, as a
high school student, I used to help out on the boats occasionally...)

All I can remember of the Grapes of Wrath was a big dumb guy who had a pet
mouse...(?) (I think Stephen King may have used this idea in the Green
Mile...) and I remember being shocked at the girl offering her milk to one
of the blokes when they were starving... (was that the Grapes of Wrath...?
hope I'm not doing Steinbeck a disservice here...). I like the sound of the
Preacher, maybe I'll read the book again....

Pete.
```

```
>(I think Stephen King may have used this idea in the Green
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sorting algorithms

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>

```
Thanks Doc.

I was genuinely confused there between "Of Mice and Men" and "The Grapes of
Wrath", although you might have thought I was faking it in the interest of
humour...(I have to agree that reading it again after you clued me in, did
make me smile <G>)

As penance for such a grave error I shall now read both books again. (So
that's MY late night Internet stopped for a while ...<G>)

Contritely,

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sorting algorithms

_(reply depth: 30)_

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-05-03T12:05:49+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3eb307d6_2@127.0.0.1>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <joe_zitzelberger-F026C6.08553501052003@corp.supernews.com> <joe_zitzelberger-C2560B.08471002052003@corp.supernews.com> <b8tqb1$8i6$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
<docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:b8tqb1$8i6$1@panix1.panix.com...
> In article <joe_zitzelberger-C2560B.08471002052003@corp.supernews.com>,
> Joe Zitzelberger  <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com> wrote:
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Good for you, Doc...

I thought I was the last remaining person on the Planet (I see I'm not <G>)
who sees "good" and "bad" as moral terms. I remember having this drummed
into me when of tender years by a lecturer now, sadly, long departed.

It is common usage (particularly in the US) to say: "You did good..." when
they mean "You did well..." (unless of course, there was a moral implication
on the action, like, helping an old lady across the street is "good", but
getting high marks in an exam means you did well...)

Perhaps Your Majesty would consider a proclamation to apprise the populace
of the correct usage for these terms? (Or maybe just send out for Pizza and
forget it, right? <G>)

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sorting algorithms

_(reply depth: 31)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2003-05-02T21:01:30-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b8v4da$ga2$1@panix1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <joe_zitzelberger-C2560B.08471002052003@corp.supernews.com> <b8tqb1$8i6$1@panix1.panix.com> <3eb307d6_2@127.0.0.1>`

```
In article <3eb307d6_2@127.0.0.1>,
Peter E.C. Dashwood <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>
><docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:b8tqb1$8i6$1@panix1.panix.com...
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
>into me when of tender years by a lecturer now, sadly, long departed.

... and I remember seeing 'right' and 'wrong' labelled as such more 
recently in this very forum, Mr Dashwood... and that was the use I was 
attempting to lampoon.  I believe that a small book was written about 
going beyond good and evil by that Nietzsche feller a century-and-change 
back.

>
>It is common usage (particularly in the US) to say: "You did good..." when
>they mean "You did well..." (unless of course, there was a moral implication
>on the action, like, helping an old lady across the street is "good", but
>getting high marks in an exam means you did well...)

The more common locution with which I am familiar is 'Ya done good'... and 
it is phrased in such an ungrammatical manner to indicate that the 
qualities of one's acts are readily visible even to the unschooled and 
unsubtle.

>
>Perhaps Your Majesty would consider a proclamation to apprise the populace
>of the correct usage for these terms? (Or maybe just send out for Pizza and
>forget it, right? <G>)

We shall consider having it taken under advisement.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sorting algorithms

_(reply depth: 31)_

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-03T23:47:22-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<joe_zitzelberger-ACCC33.23472203052003@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <joe_zitzelberger-F026C6.08553501052003@corp.supernews.com> <joe_zitzelberger-C2560B.08471002052003@corp.supernews.com> <b8tqb1$8i6$1@panix1.panix.com> <3eb307d6_2@127.0.0.1>`

```
In article <3eb307d6_2@127.0.0.1>,
 "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:

> <docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:b8tqb1$8i6$1@panix1.panix.com...
> > In article <joe_zitzelberger-C2560B.08471002052003@corp.supernews.com>,
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> Pete.

Aack!

Do you never wish anyone a "good" day?

Have you never owned a "good" auto?

When you leave the theater do you say "that was a well movie"?

Does food never taste "good"?  (And if not, is the bad food in danger of 
burning in some immoral hell for eternity for not pleasing your senses?)

When have good and bad ever been exclusively moral terms?  There are, 
and always have been, terms of comparitive judgement on the part of the 
speaker.
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3eb54381_2@127.0.0.1>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <joe_zitzelberger-F026C6.08553501052003@corp.supernews.com> <joe_zitzelberger-C2560B.08471002052003@corp.supernews.com> <b8tqb1$8i6$1@panix1.panix.com> <3eb307d6_2@127.0.0.1> <joe_zitzelberger-ACCC33.23472203052003@corp.supernews.com>`

```

"Joe Zitzelberger" <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com> wrote in message
news:joe_zitzelberger-ACCC33.23472203052003@corp.supernews.com...
> In article <3eb307d6_2@127.0.0.1>,
>  "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Do you never wish anyone a "good" day?

This is interesting...

Ever thought about WHY we say "Good Day" and "Goodbye"?

See, in the Middle Ages, it was considered blasphemous to say the name of
GOD (except in Church or other appropriate circumstances.)  So, the
euphemism "Good" slowly developed to replace "God".

The farewell "God be with ye" eventually became corrupted into "Goodbye".
And the original greeting "God gives another day" became shortened into
"Good Day" (Well, let's face it, it was a bit unwieldy...<G>).

The expression "God Bless me!" became "Goodness me!" in the same way.

The point is that "good" as we understand it today, had nothing to do with
it.

>
> Have you never owned a "good" auto?
>

I've owned some satisfactory ones... (I don't drive Fords <G>).

> When you leave the theater do you say "that was a well movie"?

No, I say that was a(n) <pick one> {entertaining, uplifting, refreshing,
hilarious, boring, tedious, enjoyable, unenjoyable, well made, well acted,
musically interesting, overpriced, good way to spend an evening, } movie, in
the perhaps mistaken belief that the English Language is rich and can be
used to communicate effectively, rather than dragged into a morass of
greyness by overworking a limited command of adjectives.

>
> Does food never taste "good"?  (And if not, is the bad food in danger of
> burning in some immoral hell for eternity for not pleasing your senses?)

Lol! Same argument as above. There are dozens of words to describe food.
Most of them are much better than "good".
>

> When have good and bad ever been exclusively moral terms?  There are,
> and always have been, terms of comparitive judgement on the part of the
> speaker.

Comparative judgement has nothing to do with "good" and "bad", which are
moral dichotomies.

Having said all of the above, Joe, I recognise your position and agree that
it is now pedantic to insist on the exclusively moral connections of "good"
and "bad". Language and usage change  and these two words are a case in
point. It doesn't really bother me when I hear them misused, and it is
arguable whether this is any longer "misuse". The examples you cite above
are all quite reasonable. However, I  (personally) still reserve them for
primarily moral purposes, although I wouldn't inflict that requirement on
you <G>....

Now don't start me on "nice"...<G>.

Pete.
```
