```
Peter E.C. Dashwood <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>
><docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:b93gsk$etm$1@panix1.panix.com...
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>Nope. Haven't got one. Guess you caught me out, Doc <G>

Word-use and definitions are hobbies of mine, Mr Dashwood; I am usually 
interested in learning new ones.  Your imputing a moral sense to 'good' 
and 'bad' seemed to be remarkably close to Mr Wagner's applying a similar 
sense to 'right' and 'wrong'... this was demonstrated not to exist in any 
of several cites I made and Mr Wagner's own attempts to provide 
documentation did not appear to bolster his assertions.

>
>It is something I have believed since youth and never checked.

Oh, I *cannot* resist...

... you might be *very* glad, Mr Dashwood, that in your youth you were not 
entrusted to the tender care of the Jesuits.

(Note - I have nothing but respect for the Jesuit tradition of 
scholarship... and nothing but awe and fear at the ruthless tendencies 
demonstrated historically by these 'soldiers of Christ'.  A while back the 
Sunday Washington Post Magazine had a one-page biography of... 
somebody-or-other, a lad who attended a Jesuit school in upstate New York 
in the late 1950's - early 1960's... anyhow, the lad did something wrong 
and had been caught at it by a priest, the following interchange - or 
something similar - ensued:

Kid: 'Gee, Fawddah, I'm sawrry, reely, I'm sawrry, Fawddah...' Priest:
'For mercy you go to the Lord, my son... *we* are the Jesuits and *we*
dispense Justice!'

... and the Utter Certainty of that still kind of... chills me.)

>
>> >
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>agressive little upstart who didn't fight fair.

It has been said that one should enter combat with the intention to *win*, 
Mr Dashwood, and that 'fighting fair' is for children or sportsmen.  
Attacking at night after the changing of the watch, with noise and 
surprise, is a tactic looked upon favorably as far back as the Book of 
Judges.

DD
```

```
"Joe Zitzelberger" <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com> wrote in message
news:joe_zitzelberger-ACCC33.23472203052003@corp.supernews.com...
> In article <3eb307d6_2@127.0.0.1>,
>  "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>
> > <docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message
news:b8tqb1$8i6$1@panix1.panix.com...
> > > In article
<joe_zitzelberger-C2560B.08471002052003@corp.supernews.com>,
> >
> > Good for you, Doc...
> >
> > I thought I was the last remaining person on the Planet (I see I'm not
<G>)
> > who sees "good" and "bad" as moral terms. I remember having this drummed
> > into me when of tender years by a lecturer now, sadly, long departed.
> >
> > It is common usage (particularly in the US) to say: "You did good..."
when
> > they mean "You did well..." (unless of course, there was a moral
implication
> > on the action, like, helping an old lady across the street is "good",
but
> > getting high marks in an exam means you did well...)
> >
> > Perhaps Your Majesty would consider a proclamation to apprise the
populace
> > of the correct usage for these terms? (Or maybe just send out for Pizza
and
> > forget it, right? <G>)
> >
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Double Aack!

My poor dead father rolled over in his grave when I read Pete's post.  The
difference between "well" and "good" is not moral, it is gramatical.  "Good"
is an adjective.  "Well" is an adverb. When you do something well, the
result may be good, though you can do even bad things well, and you can do
good things poorly.  The result is never well,  and you never do good ...
you do good things and bad things, and you do them either well, or poorly.

Just what is "good" may be a moral judgement, and whether it is done well or
not may be a professional judgement, but the distinction between the word
usage is gramatical.

Donald
```

- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <joe_zitzelberger-F026C6.08553501052003@corp.supernews.com> <joe_zitzelberger-C2560B.08471002052003@corp.supernews.com> <b8tqb1$8i6$1@panix1.panix.com> <3eb307d6_2@127.0.0.1> <joe_zitzelberger-ACCC33.23472203052003@corp.supernews.com> <439ta.60$ER4.22969@news20.bellglobal.com> <3eb5451a_2@127.0.0.1>`

```
"Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
news:3eb5451a_2@127.0.0.1...

> All these years I have never made that particular connection until you
> pointed it out. I guess I got sidetracked by the idea of good and bad as
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I will.  My dad was a stickler for grammar, and saying something like "real
good" instead of "really well" was grounds for a five minute lecture at the
dinner table. I was likely to get the lecture in Latin, so I learnt fast. I
always hated Latin, and never could see the advantage of it. Even Greek had
letters that were usefull for math.

Since this is a room full of language theorists, I will give you a puzzle.

There is a word ... (loosely defined) ... that exists in every human
language. Not only do humans use the word, but it crosses species
boundaries, and is recognized by just about every domestic animal from the
horse to the cat.  It is also common to many wild species of animals, and is
understood by rats, whales, deer, etc.  What is the word?

For bonus points, why and how is it taught by each generation of each
species to the the next generation of each species that uses it?

Donald

Hint:  Your "good/evil" thesis, tracable to the "god/devil" dichotomy in
english, reminded me of the word.  BTW, I tend to agree that "good" is often
a moral evaluation in English, and prefer to use other words for that very
reason.  However "good/bad" vs "good/evil" are subtly different, and I am
not sure the reasoning always applies.

Donald
```

```
"Donald Tees" <Donald_Tees@sympatico.ca> wrote:

>There is a word ... (loosely defined) ... that exists in every human
>language. Not only do humans use the word, but it crosses species
>boundaries, and is recognized by just about every domestic animal from the
>horse to the cat.  It is also common to many wild species of animals, and is
>understood by rats, whales, deer, etc.  What is the word?

Second attempt: vowel-oriented baby words indicating pleasure -- aah, ee, ooh.
```

```
"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3eb5c014.5428448@news.optonline.net...
> "Donald Tees" <Donald_Tees@sympatico.ca> wrote:
>
> >There is a word ... (loosely defined) ... that exists in every human
> >language. Not only do humans use the word, but it crosses species
> >boundaries, and is recognized by just about every domestic animal from
the
> >horse to the cat.  It is also common to many wild species of animals, and
is
> >understood by rats, whales, deer, etc.  What is the word?
>
> Second attempt: vowel-oriented baby words indicating pleasure -- aah, ee,
ooh.

Closer.  It is the sound of the kiss, which is actually the sound of a
mammal at it's mothers breast. It is the first "word" learnt by most
mammals, and animal trainers and such use it all the time.

While many would debate it is a "word", it can also be used as a sort of
primeval curse when directed at another male.

Donald
```

```
I apologize for that, you are quite right.  In fact, I might have robbed
myself of a chance to gloat, which was rather dumb in itself. It is a rather
neat little observation though ... I was watching a ten year old speak to a
kitten, and the kitten responding, and it struck me as an astonishing
realization. I've been thinking of "primevial" objects, and what they might
be.  Are the only true objects the zero and the one, or do you also need a
"bit" object to contain them?  Does the turing set turn into the "primevial
object set" with the addition of the "turing strip object" ... that is, a
container for a unniverse of bits?

Donald
```

```
"Donald Tees" <Donald_Tees@sympatico.ca> wrote:

>"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
>news:3eb5c014.5428448@news.optonline.net...
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>primeval curse when directed at another male.

A "word" is comprised of phonemes which can be spelled using the alphabet. The
kiss sound doesn't qualify, nor does an infant's cry. 

I prefer my answer because it tells us something about human linguistics. A
human aged one through six seems programmed to learn language by practicing it.
If you put a child in a room with a TV or radio and never talk to him, he will
not learn to speak English. This makes it appear that language is learned
behavior, specifically self-learned through exercise. 

However all infants use the same ahh ooh words. Those words are not learned; he
will sey them even if you never talk to him. They seem to be hard-wired in our
genes. They are the seminal language skills .. the 'self-starter'.
```

```
In article <3eb76766.113815115@news.optonline.net>,
Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net> wrote:

[snip]

>A "word" is comprised of phonemes which can be spelled using the alphabet. The
>kiss sound doesn't qualify, nor does an infant's cry. 

... nor does *any* speech during most of the human's species preliterate 
history.

I recall a fellow, long ago, who insisted that 'perfect pitch' was the 
ability to associate a 'name' with a tone... this sound was an 'A', this 
sound was a 'G#', etc.  I asked him how someone who was not familiar with 
his particular naming-convention could have 'perfect pitch'... say, a 
rural Bangladeshi or a 17th-century Aleut who was able to reproduce a tone 
exactly (difference in octave allowed) but had never been taught to 
associate pitches with names.

He was... nonplussed, to say the least.  Likewise, Mr Wagner, by your 
definition... before there were alphabets there were no 'words'.

DD
```

```
>Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net> wrote:
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>definition... before there were alphabets there were no 'words'.

Preliterate words were comprised of phonemes which could have been expressed in
an alphabet, if one had existed. Same for words when the writing system was
hieroglyphic, cuneiform or logographic (e.g. Chinese).

The number of phonenes in all natural languages is surprisingly consistant,
between 20 and 40, usually around 25.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sorting algorithms

_(reply depth: 40)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-05-07T16:18:14+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b9a1g4$utn$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3eb76766.113815115@news.optonline.net> <b984j8$c2h$1@panix1.panix.com> <3eb86c21.14703208@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:

>>>A "word" is comprised of phonemes which can be spelled using the
>>>alphabet. The kiss sound doesn't qualify, nor does an infant's cry.

> Preliterate words were comprised of phonemes which could have been
> expressed in an alphabet, if one had existed. Same for words when the
> writing system was hieroglyphic, cuneiform or logographic (e.g. Chinese).

Chinese written words don't represent sounds.  The same graphic may be said 
completely differently in different areas but will mean the same thing.

In other words the phonemes are _not_ represented in the written language, 
yet in the written language and the spoken ones they are still 'words'.
 
> The number of phonenes in all natural languages is surprisingly
> consistant, between 20 and 40, usually around 25.

If (and I say _IF_) your assertion is true, it is irrelevant. Bushmen use a 
sound in their words which is unique. Whether that sound is represented in 
writing is irrelevant to how they speak.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sorting algorithms

_(reply depth: 41)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-05-08T08:39:06+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3eba027b.118744531@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3eb76766.113815115@news.optonline.net> <b984j8$c2h$1@panix1.panix.com> <3eb86c21.14703208@news.optonline.net> <b9a1g4$utn$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>Robert Wagner wrote:
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>completely differently in different areas but will mean the same thing.

Correct. The Chinese writing system and derivative Japanese Kanji are
logographic, meaning a symbol represents a word. Hundreds of natural languages
independently discovered the superiority of alphabets over logographs. The only
reactionary languages, to my knowledge, are Chinese, math and programming. 

Programmers use symbols such as = and > to represent words without understanding
that they're taking step backward in linguistics. They think all that matters is
less typing. 

>In other words the phonemes are _not_ represented in the written language, 
>yet in the written language and the spoken ones they are still 'words'.

No modern language lacks an alphabet. Japanese phonetic alphabets are called
kana. The Chinese phonetic alphabet is called pinyin.

>> The number of phonenes in all natural languages is surprisingly
>> consistant, between 20 and 40, usually around 25.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>writing is irrelevant to how they speak.

I did not say the vowels and consonants were the same across languages. They are
not. But the variety within a single language is about 25. Bushmen's popping
sounds can be represented by one or a few letters.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sorting algorithms

_(reply depth: 43)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-08T14:50:49-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b9ejfp$2tma$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3eb76766.113815115@news.optonline.net> <b984j8$c2h$1@panix1.panix.com> <3eb86c21.14703208@news.optonline.net> <b9a1g4$utn$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3eba027b.118744531@news.optonline.net> <b9dmcf$shq$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
On  8-May-2003, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:

> Programmers use symbols such as = and > to represent words without
> understanding
> that they're taking step backward in linguistics. They think all that
matters
> is less typing.

I don't know if Robert deleted this or what, but I can't find the original;
this is cited from Howard's response.

I'm not sure what Robert's trying to do here -- define "programmer" or
define "words", but I don't agree with the assertion implied in the fragment
"Programmers use symbols such as = and > to represent words".   Some
programmers might, but not all programmers, nor do I believe all programmers
fluent in COBOL or any other language do so.

I for one, in the case of COBOL, don't use the symbols "=" and ">" to
represent English words,  I use them to represent relationships between
operands.   "IF A = B" means the same thing to a competent COBOL programmer
whose native language is Swahili, Tagalog or Hill Bondo as it does to a
competent COBOL programmer whose  native language is English.

Moreover, the characters "=" and ">" do not "represent words" in this
context precisely because they *are themselves* words, according to the
grammar for the COBOL language.  The set of "special-character words" that
includes them is that subset of "required words" that are not "keywords";
"required words" are in turn a subset of "reserved words".

See ISO/IEC 1989:2002 pages 66-74, 8.3.1.1, "COBOL words".

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sorting algorithms

_(reply depth: 44)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-05-10T03:12:55+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ebc2966.109372458@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3eb76766.113815115@news.optonline.net> <b984j8$c2h$1@panix1.panix.com> <3eb86c21.14703208@news.optonline.net> <b9a1g4$utn$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3eba027b.118744531@news.optonline.net> <b9dmcf$shq$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <b9ejfp$2tma$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
"Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

>On  8-May-2003, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>this is cited from Howard's response.

I did not delete it.

>I'm not sure what Robert's trying to do here -- define "programmer" or
>define "words", but I don't agree with the assertion implied in the fragment
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>See ISO/IEC 1989:2002 pages 66-74, 8.3.1.1, "COBOL words".

The fragment you quoted did not dispute anything you said .. with the technical
exception of "are words" and "represent words". A word 'is' an intangible
creature of the language. How we represent it in audio or writing doesn't define
the word (per linguistics, not the COBOL standard); it is defined by its meaning
within the language.  Thus = ,  equal, �gal, igual, equus, pari and kin all
represent words with nearly identical meaning. The first is a logograph; the
others are alphabetic representations of the spoken word, which in turn is a
representation of 'the word'.

My argument is that virtually all natural languages once used logographs but
have since abandoned them in favor of alphabetic representation. Math and
programming are the only 'languages' that use logographs. Because math and
programming antedate alphabets, I see it as a step backward. 

 are both written representations of the same word
```

```
In article <3ebc2966.109372458@news.optonline.net>,
Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net> wrote:

[snip]

>My argument is that virtually all natural languages once used logographs but
>have since abandoned them in favor of alphabetic representation.

Mr Wagner, you have made this assertion before and I asked you to document 
it; second request, then: can you please indicate what evidence or sources 
you use in generating this assertion?

>Math and
>programming are the only 'languages' that use logographs. Because math and
>programming antedate alphabets, I see it as a step backward. 

'... programming antedate(s) alphabets'?  What are you calling 
'programming', Mr Wagner?  Where might one find examples of it that 
antedate, say, the use of a syllabic alphabet by the Assyrians?

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabet vs. logograph (was Sorting algorithms)

_(reply depth: 46)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-05-11T21:54:57+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ebec3d8.280007959@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <b9dmcf$shq$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <b9ejfp$2tma$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3ebc2966.109372458@news.optonline.net> <b9j05m$js9$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

>In article <3ebc2966.109372458@news.optonline.net>,
>Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net> wrote:
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>you use in generating this assertion?

As promised, I checked the library's copy of David Crystal's Cambridge
Encyclopedia of Language. On page 196 et seq it describes early writing systems
as pictographic (he doesn't pictographs to be 'written language'), middle-era
(1K-3K BCE) as logographic and hieroglyphic, modern (CE) as alphabetic. That was
the gist of my argument. But it does not say any single language or language
family switched from logographic to alphabetic (although we know from other
sources that Greek did). I was wrong. I retract the assertion.

Two quotes I found interesting:

"The earliest examples of a conventional use of written symbols are on clay
tablets discovered in various parts of the Middle East and south-east Europe
from around 3500 BC. Large numbers of tablets made by the Sumerians have been
found in sites around the Rivers Tigris and Euphrates in present-day Iraq and
Iran. For example, on tablets from the city-state of Uruk, about 1,500 symbols
have been listed, most of them abstract in character. They seem to have recorded
such matters as land sales, business transactions and tax accounts."

As to the notion that alphabets are derived from each other, he says:

"It now seems most likely that writing systems evolved independently of each
other at different times in several parts of the world -- in Mesopotamia, China,
Meso-America and elsewhere. There is nothing to support a theory of common
origin. There are of course similarities between these systems, but these are
not altogether surprising, given the limited ways of devising a system of
written communication."
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabet vs. logograph (was Sorting algorithms)

_(reply depth: 49)_

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2003-05-13T08:01:10+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3EC0A690.519D8C70@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3ebc2966.109372458@news.optonline.net> <b9j05m$js9$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ebec3d8.280007959@news.optonline.net> <b9mpr8$jsl$1@panix1.panix.com> <8VOdnVUumdt7G12jXTWcpQ@comcast.com>`

```
Russell Styles wrote:
> (snip)
> 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> how certain they are about Mayan.

Here's a review that doesn't do justice to the book:
http://raphael.math.uic.edu/~jeremy/crypt/contrib/schultz.html

Mayan can now be read well enough to extract place names and names of
rulers as the Mayan pronounced them.  Most of this work has been done
since 1975.

Part of the problem was that there are multiple Mayan languages that
shared the same number system, calendar, and writing system.  Also,
none of the early Mayan archaeologists bothered to learn any of the
local Indian languages, which are direct descendants of the original
Mayan Languages.

Most of the Mayan books (codices) were burned by the Spanish
conquistadors.  There was a "rosetta stone" found in a library, which
was a document written by a Monk who taught Spanish to the natives. 
It contained notes on the Mayan alphabet (this became known as the
Landa alphabet) and approximate equivalents in the Roman alphabet.  So
even though the Mayan civilization collapsed about 900 A.D., the
natives could still read Mayan writing in the 16th century.

The Mayan word for fish ("xoc" with "x" pronounced as "sh") became the
English word "shark".

Tuesday, May 13th, 2003 is Mayan date 12.19.10.4.8 (2 Lamat 16 Uo). 
The first part is the long count date, and equates to 1,868,488 days
since the world began (in 3114 BC).  The second part is the "calendar
round" date, which has a 260 day period.  It's really two calendar
systems.

In the Mayan calendar, the world ends on 13.0.0.0.0 (4 Ahau 3 Kankin),
which is Sunday, December 23rd, 2012.

The book by Michael Coe (see the URL) has a lot to say about
linguistics and archaeology.  
```

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3ebec3d8.280007959@news.optonline.net...

> As promised, I checked the library's copy of David Crystal's Cambridge
> Encyclopedia of Language. On page 196 et seq it describes early writing
systems
> as pictographic (he doesn't pictographs to be 'written language'),
middle-era
> (1K-3K BCE) as logographic and hieroglyphic, modern (CE) as alphabetic.
That was
> the gist of my argument. But it does not say any single language or
language
> family switched from logographic to alphabetic (although we know from
other
> sources that Greek did). I was wrong. I retract the assertion.

But, of course, since according to you the first alphabet used to record a
(proto-)Indo-European language was the Roman alphabet, not the Greek, the
retraction of your assertion about the origins of the Greek alphabet doesn't
seem to impact your assertion that the Roman alphabet predates the Greek.
Or is it your intent to assert that Greek isn't an Indo-European language
and thus any discussion of its history here is irrelevant?

> As to the notion that alphabets are derived from each other, he says:
>
> "It now seems most likely that writing systems evolved independently of
each
> other at different times in several parts of the world -- in Mesopotamia,
China,
> Meso-America and elsewhere. There is nothing to support a theory of common
> origin. There are of course similarities between these systems, but these
are
> not altogether surprising, given the limited ways of devising a system of
> written communication."

I think this has to do with what might be perceived as similarities between,
say, Mesoamerican and Chinese writing systems, not between similarities
between, say, Hebrew and Greek, or Greek and Roman, or Greek and Cyrillic
writing systems.  I reject outright the premise that, say, Latin and Greek
writing systems are completely unrelated.  The invention of Cyrillic based
on Greek as a part of Cyril and Methodius' missionary work to the Slavic peo
ple in the ninth century is undisputed as historical fact.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabet vs. logograph (was Sorting algorithms)

_(reply depth: 49)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-14T07:55:28-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b9tld1$1dfg$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <b9dmcf$shq$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <b9ejfp$2tma$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3ebc2966.109372458@news.optonline.net> <b9j05m$js9$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ebec3d8.280007959@news.optonline.net> <b9ofus$ktj$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3ec1aa6c.74142797@news.optonline.net>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3ec1aa6c.74142797@news.optonline.net...

> I was using Indo-European to refer to Germanic languages. If Greek and
Latin are
> included as Indo-European then of course Greek had the first alphabet. If
> Phonecian is also included, it was first.

Well, Robert, I'm sorry to say that if that's the way you used the term
"Indo-European", then you were using it *incorrectly*.   A quick glance at
relevant encyclop[a]edia[e/s] -- for example Brittanica -- or even a good
dictionary -- see the "Indo-European Languages Table" in a Webster's
Collegiate -- should provide more than enough evidence of what the term
actually means.  I can't imagine where you'd get the idea that Indo-European
referred to Germanic languages and excluded those of Italic and Hellenic
origin; I am unaware of any credible reference supporting such a stance.

> If Greek and Latin are
> included as Indo-European then of course Greek had the first alphabet.

I haven't been able to clarify to my own satisfaction whether the use of
Linear B to represent proto-Greek predates or postdates the earliest (not
the earliest extant) use of a writing system to represent Sanskrit.

> If Phonecian is also included, it was first.

Ancient Phoenecian is a Semitic language, and Semitic languages are *not*
part of the Indo-European language family.

There's an excellent article in the Brittanica on writing systems, as well
as on the history of Indo-European languages.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabet vs. logograph (was Sorting algorithms)

_(reply depth: 49)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-05-14T15:25:04+00:00

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sorting algorithms

_(reply depth: 45)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-05-12T15:06:08+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b9od90$c11$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3eb76766.113815115@news.optonline.net> <b984j8$c2h$1@panix1.panix.com> <3eb86c21.14703208@news.optonline.net> <b9a1g4$utn$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3eba027b.118744531@news.optonline.net> <b9dmcf$shq$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <b9ejfp$2tma$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3ebc2966.109372458@news.optonline.net>`

```

On  9-May-2003, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:

> My argument is that virtually all natural languages once used logographs but
> have since abandoned them in favor of alphabetic representation. Math and
> programming are the only 'languages' that use logographs. Because math and
> programming antedate alphabets, I see it as a step backward.

So if math or programming preceded alphabets, their written representations
would be a step forward?

When did math start?   When did alphabets start?

When did using logograms for numbers start?

But regardless - when you mention "a step backward", we can infer one of two
things:
1.   This is a value judgment.   Backwards is wrong.
2.   It is an observation only.   We are not progressing forward in time, but
are going backwards in time.

Obviously 2 isn't true.   If we are getting new logograms as we go forward in
time, then we are still going forward in time.

So this leads to 1.   You think that using logograms is inferior to using
phonetic spelling.    Logograms are more international, not requiring everybody
to pronounce words the way we do.   Is that what's wrong with them?    Logograms
are less likely to be misunderstood (that bar across a symbol in a sign won't be
overlooked as often as the word "no" spelled out).    It this what is wrong with
them?

Or are they backwards because we see so many more of them now than we did when
we were children?

I feel that I am missing your point.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sorting algorithms

_(reply depth: 46)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-05-13T03:02:13+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ec0404b.27617346@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3eb76766.113815115@news.optonline.net> <b984j8$c2h$1@panix1.panix.com> <3eb86c21.14703208@news.optonline.net> <b9a1g4$utn$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3eba027b.118744531@news.optonline.net> <b9dmcf$shq$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <b9ejfp$2tma$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3ebc2966.109372458@news.optonline.net> <b9od90$c11$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:

>
>On  9-May-2003, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>When did using logograms for numbers start?

Antedate was the wrong word; I should have said postdate. 

Alphabets started in 3,500 BC with the Phonecians. Math and logographs started
about the same time, also in the Arab world. Between 3500 BCE and 0 CE, some
languages used hieroglyphics or logographs for all writing, not just math. 

Writing systems developed in the last 2,000 years are nearly all alphabetic,
excepting Chinese and Japanese. 

>But regardless - when you mention "a step backward", we can infer one of two
>things:
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>to pronounce words the way we do.   Is that what's wrong with them?
Logograms
>are less likely to be misunderstood (that bar across a symbol in a sign won't
be
>overlooked as often as the word "no" spelled out).    It this what is wrong
with
>them?

They are bad because we (collectively) abandoned that writing system 2,000 years
ago in favor of alphabets. 

Granted, logographs are more 'international' or language-independent. But COBOL
is based on English. Changing three relational operators to symbols is a spit in
the ocean compared to hundreds of 'reserved words' in the COBOL vocabulary. 

>Or are they backwards because we see so many more of them now than we did when
>we were children?
>
>I feel that I am missing your point.

My point is that techies ignore the linguistic history. They use > and = simply
to avoid typing, without understanding  the evolution of writing systems.
```

```
On 12-May-2003, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:

> My point is that techies ignore the linguistic history. They use > and =
> simply to avoid typing, without understanding  the evolution of writing
> systems.

I worked at a shop in 1980 that wouldn't allow us to use > or > because one of
those wasn't on the print band of our printer.

I've never been hesitant to type longer words.  Typing isn't hard.   I tend to
use those symbols and align my code up for multiple line comparisons.   It could
be that there's an advantage in using the words though.  Just because I haven't
come across one since 1980 doesn't mean one doesn't exist.
```

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote

> How is > better communication than 'greater than'? 

It is faster to scan and thus easier to understand (once learnt).  It
also does not rely on using a specific language and thus can be more
easily understood by people who do not speak english.
 
> I'm using the evolution of nearly all natural languages, which independent of
> each other dropped logographs in favor of alphabets.

When did english ever use logographs ?  When were these dropped ?

Or Lithuanian, or Maori, or Latin ?

What evidence do you have that any language that did actually drop
logographs in favour of alphabets did so _independently_ ?

(as distinct, for example from being invaded and an alphabet imposed,
or invading another and copying the alphabet).
```

```
riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote
>
>> How is > better communication than 'greater than'? 
>
>It is faster to scan and thus easier to understand (once learnt). 

I don't find > faster to scan. If anything, it's slower because I must look
closely to see which direction it's pointing. 

> It
>also does not rely on using a specific language and thus can be more
>easily understood by people who do not speak english.

But all the rest of COBOL is English. I recall seeing a French COBOL produced by
Bull ca. 1970. A quick scan on 'french cobol' found no modern compilers. Are
there any non-English COBOLs? How do they write PERFORM in Russia, Japan and
other places that don't use the Roman alphabet?
 
>> I'm using the evolution of nearly all natural languages, which independent of
>> each other dropped logographs in favor of alphabets.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>Or Lithuanian, or Maori, or Latin ?

I should have said language families. English was derived from Latin (and
Indo-European), which was derived from Etruscan, which was derived from Greek.
Greeks used a logograph-like system based on Canaanite until about 1,000 BC,
when they dropped it in favor of an alphabet based on the Phoenician alphabet. 

At about the same time, South Arabians were adopting the Canaanite system. They
dropped it in favor of the Arabic alphabet, also based on Phoenician, some 500
years later. 

Here is a list of more than 100 independently developed alphabets.

http://gurpsnet.sjgames.com/Archive/AdsDisSkills/Languages/languages.writing

Few of these changes were imposed by military invasion. The most common reason
was to facilitate the business of trade.




>
>What evidence do you have that any language that did actually drop
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>or invading another and copying the alphabet).
```

```
"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3ebd1249.169000960@news.optonline.net...
> riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…

look
> closely to see which direction it's pointing.
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> But all the rest of COBOL is English. I recall seeing a French COBOL
produced by
> Bull ca. 1970. A quick scan on 'french cobol' found no modern compilers.
Are

and
> other places that don't use the Roman alphabet?
>
> >> I'm using the evolution of nearly all natural languages, which
independent of
> >> each other dropped logographs in favor of alphabets.
> >
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Indo-European), which was derived from Etruscan, which was derived from
Greek.
> Greeks used a logograph-like system based on Canaanite until about 1,000
BC,
> when they dropped it in favor of an alphabet based on the Phoenician
alphabet.

no, not correct.

English was derived from west germanic, which came from proto-indo-european.
Celtic came indirectly from proto-indo-european through the goidelic and the
bythonic branches.  The former evolved into Irish, Scottish, and Manx, while
the latter evolved into Welsh, Cornish, Breton, and Gaulish.. Greek, on the
other hand, came through another branch .... the hellenic. Other Helinic
languages are Albanian and Thracian and Illyrian. Latin was a derivitive of
Faliscan, and gave rise to Portugese, Spanish, French, etc. The Latin
influence on English is mainly through the French/Norman; english is not a
latino language.  Faliscan came from a proto language familly ussually named
Italic, which is also a proto-indo-european derivitive.  It evolved into
Latino-faliscan, which gave rise to the "latin" languages, and Osco-umbrian,
which became Oscan and Umbrian. The diect line of familial descent for
modern english is proto-indo-european, germanic, west germanic (middle
german(?)), old english, middle english, modern english.

Writing and alphabets evolved quite separately from the spoken versions of
languages.  The earliest known is classical hebrew, which is half and half
... the consonants are alphabetic, but syllables are constructed by adding
points to them, thus constructing what you are calling logographs.  Vowels
expressed as letters evolved with the greeks. The middle east languages are
all members of either the Tocharian, Indo-Iranian, Iranian, or Anatolian
families, each of which evolved from the original proto-indo-european.
Remnants of "logographs" remain in many modern languages as accent marks. In
english, they have been replced by letter combinations, with the exception
of the apostrophe.

As to the arguement you were trying to advance ... that alphabetic systems
are superior to logographic systems (I am attempting to use your terminology
here, though I am not sure I agree with it), one has to ask, "superior for
what?".  Both systems have advantages and disadvantages.  The modern GUI has
advantages over the command line, and the command line has advatages over
the GUI.  The words "CocaCola" and the coke logo, complete with colour,
serve different purposes.  To argue that the logo representation has no
advantage is to ignore reality.  I too prefer words to logos, but that is
because I can read ... I have a vested interest.

I'd say your statement that "it is a step backwards" is not only untrue, but
a rather gross oversimplification.  A step forward is to recognize that both
systems are usefull for specific things, and to use them appropriately.  A
step backwards is to view 5000 years of knowledge, and discard 4800 years of
it in favour of a rather pedantic and biased version of utility, based on
facts that are rather nebulous to start with.

Donald

BTW, I'd define "word" as a sound which is used by different entities, and
is both used and understood by those different entities to convey the same
meaning. The kiss does that, but none of the other baby sounds do.  Most of
the others are species specific.

Donald




Donald

>
> At about the same time, South Arabians were adopting the Canaanite system.
They
> dropped it in favor of the Arabic alphabet, also based on Phoenician, some
500
> years later.

> Here is a list of more than 100 independently developed alphabets.

http://gurpsnet.sjgames.com/Archive/AdsDisSkills/Languages/languages.writing

> Few of these changes were imposed by military invasion. The most common
reason
> was to facilitate the business of trade.
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Indo-European Languages (was: Alphabet vs. logograph (was Sorting algorithms)

_(reply depth: 50)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-10T22:00:47-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b9ked1$4gm$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3eb76766.113815115@news.optonline.net> <b984j8$c2h$1@panix1.panix.com> <3eb86c21.14703208@news.optonline.net> <b9a1g4$utn$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3eba027b.118744531@news.optonline.net> <b9dmcf$shq$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ebad992.23387836@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305082115.72995c0e@posting.google.com> <3ebd1249.169000960@news.optonline.net> <zKbva.7246$J%5.773845@news20.bellglobal.com> <b9k0an$dpo$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net> <3ebdac50.208437759@news.optonline.net> <b9kcpd$rdm$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Concerning English and its "derrivation" from Germanic versus Latin, you
might want to study Romanian and its derrivation from Latin versus Slavic.

 English CLEARLY derrives (in "structure" and "native" words, e.g. articles,
counting pronouns) from Germanic as does Romanian from Latin.  However, in
each case, there has been SIGNIFICANT *vocabulary* borrowing from another
branch of the Indo-European familly.

Actually, English has Multiple periods of borrowing from Latin (sove via
French - some directly).  When it comes to vocabulary (not basic
"structure") there really are some interesting stories - which point out WHY
historical linguists do *not* use "full vocabulary" in classifying the
origins of languages.  See for example the following from:

    http://www.bartleby.com/61/46/B0154600.html

    BEEF
WORD HISTORY:That beef comes from cows is known to most, but the close
relationship between the words beef and cow is hardly household knowledge.
Cow comes via Middle English from Old English c**, which is descended from
the Indo-European root *gwou-, also meaning "cow." This root has descendants
in most of the branches of the Indo-European language family. Among those
descendants is the Latin word bs, "cow," whose stem form, bov-, eventually
became the Old French word buef, also meaning "cow." The French nobles who
ruled England after the Norman Conquest of course used French words to refer
to the meats they were served, so the animal called c by the Anglo-Saxon
peasants was called buef by the French nobles when it was brought to them
cooked at dinner. Thus arose the distinction between the words for animals
and their meat that is also found in the English word-pairs swine/pork,
sheep/mutton, and deer/venison. What is interesting about cow/beef is that
we are in fact dealing with one and the same word, etymologically speaking.

    ****

and

for a comparison of a variety of English words with (eventually) common
origins see,

     http://www.bartleby.com/61/20/V0122000.html
vision -
    ETYMOLOGY:Middle English, from Old French, from Latin vsi, vsin-, from
vsus, past participle of vidre, to see.

 versus

    http://www.bartleby.com/61/59/V0095900.html
 view -
    ETYMOLOGY:Middle English vewe, from Anglo-Norman, from feminine past
participle of veoir,


versus

     http://www.bartleby.com/61/30/G0303000.html
guide -
    ETYMOLOGY:Middle English, from Old French, from Old Proven�al guida,
from guidar, to guide, of Germanic origin.

versus

    http://www.bartleby.com/61/37/V0123700.html
vista -
    ETYMOLOGY:Italian, from feminine past participle of vedere, to see, from
Latin vidre.
```

```
Latin		German
compute	go (now a 'bad word' in COBOL)
perform		if
move		call
evaluate
```

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <Donald_Tees@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-05-11T10:33:56-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ibtva.241$st.26019@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3eb76766.113815115@news.optonline.net> <b984j8$c2h$1@panix1.panix.com> <3eb86c21.14703208@news.optonline.net> <b9a1g4$utn$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3eba027b.118744531@news.optonline.net> <b9dmcf$shq$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ebad992.23387836@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305082115.72995c0e@posting.google.com> <3ebd1249.169000960@news.optonline.net> <zKbva.7246$J%5.773845@news20.bellglobal.com> <b9k0an$dpo$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net> <3ebdac50.208437759@news.optonline.net>`

```
"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message

> Anyway, I cited Latin/Greek in the context of langages that abandoned
> logographs. Indo-European had no written language until they adopted the
Roman
> alphabet.

Indo European had no written language period.  "They" did not adopt anything
from the romans, as "they" are a hypothetical construct and "they" left no
records beyond the languages that survived them. "They" were also dead for
some 4500-5000 years before the Roman civilization even existed.

One would think you personally knew a member of a proto-indo-european tribe,
the way you do go on.  This is pre-historic, Robert, and the data is
*surmised* on the basis of 7000 year old evidence.  By the time the Romans
even existed, 5000 years had gone by, and there *WERE* no
prot-indo-europeans.

Nevertheless, there were at least three alphabets in existance by the time
of the Romans. Maybe more, I do not know.  I only know the three I've
studied in detail, and learned to roughly translate.  I'm also 35 years out
of date on them, though that is not a huge problem as two of them disbanded
their standards committe's at least three millenium back.

For someone that claims to be talking only for the sake of giving us
information, ... out of a sense of duty, goodwill and an attempt to overcome
our ignorance, ... you have an appalling ignorance of the basics.  If you
claimed that Julius Caesar was an ally of President Bush, you would be 3000
years closer to reality.

Arre you perfectly sure that ego has nothing to do with your posts, and that
showing other people up has nothing to do with your posting style?  I have a
hunch that your subconscious is not quite telling the truth to you.

Donald


Donald
```

```
In this context, Indo-European is the accepted term for a family of languages
descended from a common ancestor, probably the precursor of Anatolian. 

>  By the time the Romans
>even existed, 5000 years had gone by, and there *WERE* no
>prot-indo-europeans.

The Goths who sacked Rome spoke an Indo-European language, as did Germanic
tribes such as the Vandals. 

>Nevertheless, there were at least three alphabets in existance by the time
>of the Romans. Maybe more, I do not know.  I only know the three I've
>studied in detail, and learned to roughly translate.  I'm also 35 years out
>of date on them, though that is not a huge problem as two of them disbanded
>their standards committe's at least three millenium back.

There were more than three:

Greek
Cypriotic
Demotic
Carian
Lydian
Messapian
Akkadian (3)
Ugarit
Elamite

Those were in Europe. On other continents, there were dozens more. 

http://gurpsnet.sjgames.com/Archive/AdsDisSkills/Languages/languages.writing

>Arre you perfectly sure that ego has nothing to do with your posts, and that
>showing other people up has nothing to do with your posting style?  I have a
>hunch that your subconscious is not quite telling the truth to you.

Did you psychoanalyze 3 people 35 years ago, as well?
```

```
Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
> > Indo-European had no written language until they adopted the Roman
alphabet.
> >

Robert,
  Removing ALL other parts of this thread, exactly what DID you mean by this
sentence?

 - The Roman alphabet was not the first for any Indo-European language (I
think you agree)

 - There was *no* written system for "proto-Indo-European" (as opposed to
later Indo-European languages)

 - Linguists, who in the last couple of centuries have "recreated"
proto-Indo-European have used IPA (as much as any) alphabet to represent the
"reconstructed" language.

    ***

Therefore, if you could simply explain what EXACTLY you meant by that single
sentence (or whether like "antedate" versus "postdate" you made a mistake),
I think it would "help" us all.
```

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote:

>Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
>> > Indo-European had no written language until they adopted the Roman
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>sentence?

I meant Germanic speakers had no written language. Later I corrected myself to
say they used the runic alphabet to a limited extent before adopting Roman. 

> - The Roman alphabet was not the first for any Indo-European language (I
>think you agree)

Nope. Greek, Etruscan and Phonecian predated Latin. 

> - There was *no* written system for "proto-Indo-European" (as opposed to
>later Indo-European languages)

The first 'written system' (by reasonable definition) was created 4,000 BC by
proto-Canaanite or 3,500 BC by Phonecian. 

> - Linguists, who in the last couple of centuries have "recreated"
>proto-Indo-European have used IPA (as much as any) alphabet to represent the
>"reconstructed" language.

Ok. How can they do that without audio samples? 

>Therefore, if you could simply explain what EXACTLY you meant by that single
>sentence.
>I think it would "help" us all.

Again, I meant 'barbarians' from northern Europe had no written language.
```

```
Yes, proto- means 'original'.

>Did the Goths who sacked Rome use logographic writing ? (hint: no).

They probably didn't use any writing. 'We don't got to show you no steenking
"documents".'
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Indo-European Languages (was: Alphabet vs. logograph (was Sorting algorithms)

_(reply depth: 49)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-12T08:01:26-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b9od06$ioi$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3eb76766.113815115@news.optonline.net> <b984j8$c2h$1@panix1.panix.com> <3eb86c21.14703208@news.optonline.net> <b9a1g4$utn$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3eba027b.118744531@news.optonline.net> <b9dmcf$shq$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ebad992.23387836@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305082115.72995c0e@posting.google.com> <3ebd1249.169000960@news.optonline.net> <zKbva.7246$J%5.773845@news20.bellglobal.com> <b9k0an$dpo$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net> <3ebdac50.208437759@news.optonline.net>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3ebdac50.208437759@news.optonline.net...
> The second reference is awesome.
>
> Both say English is an Indo-European language derived from German while
> downplaying the Latin/Romance influence. I think the heritage is close to
50-50.
> Look at all the Latin and Greek word roots we have.

Word roots have very little to do with the language family affiliations.
What determines the relationships among languages is the syntactic structure
of the language itself.  The vocabulary that the language uses is
immaterial.  If I recall correctly, there are hardly any lexical remains of
Latin in Romanian, yet it is nonetheless a Romance language.

Moreover, there are *two* major periods of Latin influence on the vocabulary
that has continued into modern English -- the Roman occupation in the first
millenium, and the Norman conquest.  From the former come such things as the
"-chester" suffix, ex-Latin "Castra".  The Germanic "cow" and the Norman
"beef" stand side-by-side in the language today, as do "pig" and "pork",
"deer" and "venison".  That "skirt" and "shirt" have the same germanic root,
and that "ship" and "skiff" have the same Germanic root does not mean that
English is actually a Scandinavian language in structure, but rather that
its vocabulary was influenced by invasion from Scandinavian countries.  The
Viking influence around York is significant.

>
> Anyway, I cited Latin/Greek in the context of langages that abandoned
> logographs. Indo-European had no written language until they adopted the
Roman
> alphabet.

Proto-Indo-European had split off into its numerous branches long before the
development of the Roman alphabet.

And, of course, there's another small flaw in your argument:  it completely
ignores the history of Greece, the influence of the Greek language (written
and otherwise) in the history of western civilization, and the derivation of
the written alphabet used for *Latin* from the alphabet originally used in
*Greece* (which itself is derived at least in part from Hebrew script).

    -Chuck Stevens
```

```
roots but that these 40% comprise 70% of the words used for 
advertising-copy on the posters in subways; the conclusion was that these 
words were more 'immediate' (the author called them 'grabbers') and were 
better suited to getting attention.  (compare 'Decelerate!' and 'Slow 
Down!')
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Indo-European Languages (was: Alphabet vs. logograph (was Sorting algorithms)

_(reply depth: 49)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-12T11:21:36-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b9oonh$r41$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3eb76766.113815115@news.optonline.net> <b984j8$c2h$1@panix1.panix.com> <3eb86c21.14703208@news.optonline.net> <b9a1g4$utn$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3eba027b.118744531@news.optonline.net> <b9dmcf$shq$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ebad992.23387836@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305082115.72995c0e@posting.google.com> <3ebd1249.169000960@news.optonline.net> <zKbva.7246$J%5.773845@news20.bellglobal.com> <b9k0an$dpo$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net> <3ebdac50.208437759@news.optonline.net>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3ebdac50.208437759@news.optonline.net...

> Indo-European had no written language until they adopted the Roman
alphabet.

In addition to the previously-noted dismissal of Greek writing, this also
ignores entirely the fact that Sanskrit is an Indo-European language (in
fact, it basically supplies the "Indo-" part of that term).   Sanskrit
existed in written form long before the rise of the Roman empire (the
written system having evolved, as did Greek, from Semitic systems).    The
writing system is arguably syllabic because absent a written vowel the
default is "a" -- but the modification of that written vowel to any of the
alternatives is regular throughout the writing system, and appears to be
attested in its *current* form from about 650BC.

For that reason, I believe that the writing system of Sanskrit qualifies
under the definition of "alphabet" that has been proposed here -- a system
that has both vowels and consonants -- and in that form is attested as early
as the 7th century BC.

Certainly it qualifies as a system in which a subset of the native speakers
of Indo-European languages wrote stuff down well before Rome's rise to
power.

From what I can tell, Linear A is an ancestor of Linear B and Cypriot, but
since Linear A hasn't been deciphered yet it's not certain that it
represents a writing system used for Indo-European languages (though Cypriot
was).

The use of Linear B to represent an Indo-European language appears to me,
based on a brief survey on the internet, to date from a millennium or so
before the rise of Rome (more specifically, the Doric invasion of Crete
circa 1200BC).

    -Chuck Stevens
```

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote

> >It is faster to scan and thus easier to understand (once learnt). 
>

> closely to see which direction it's pointing. 

I did say 'once learnt'.  Learning is done by repitition and 'trial
and error'. Avoiding '>' and '<' just means that you never learn then.

I do recall being 9 or 10 and having to think 'that's the big end',
'that's the little end' when I saw '>' and '<', but had no trouble
since.
 
> But all the rest of COBOL is English. I recall seeing a French COBOL produced by
> Bull ca. 1970. A quick scan on 'french cobol' found no modern compilers. Are
> there any non-English COBOLs? How do they write PERFORM in Russia, Japan and
> other places that don't use the Roman alphabet?

All keywords are English, all programming that I know of is done using
ASCII or EBCDIC charcter sets (except APL of course).  Only the French
would try to write a French version of Cobol, though obviously they
have their own assembler and other programming languages.

> >> I'm using the evolution of nearly all natural languages, which independent of
> >> each other dropped logographs in favor of alphabets.
 
> I should have said language families. English was derived from Latin (and

No.

> Greeks used a logograph-like system based on Canaanite until about 1,000 BC,
> when they dropped it in favor of an alphabet based on the Phoenician alphabet. 

The ancient Greeks were not the same people as the Classic Greeks. 
Your argument is like saying that the people that lived in France
dropped Celtic and adopted French.  The Gauls were displaced by
various groups moving westwards until the Francs arrived.

So also with Greece.  A new group arrived and displaced the previous
occupiers and brought new methods with them.

> Few of these changes were imposed by military invasion. The most common reason
> was to facilitate the business of trade.

And in what way is this 'independent' if it adopted from outside
because of traders ?
```

```
riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>Programming langugaes have nothing to do with linguistics.  

Then why does COBOL advertise that it's English-like?

>But are
>you implying that Japanese and Chinese are 'backwards' because their
>language is pictures ?

Yes. But Japanese is largely alphabetic. Computer screens, newspapers,
billboards and most magazines are in kana. Kanji is used primarily for 'fine
literature'. 

It used to be said that only college graduates could read and write kanji; those
with high-school or less were limited to kana. If true, that makes it a
class-distinction issue.

I think computers will encourage Chinese to use pinyin more. 6,000 logographs
are just too unwieldy for keyboad input. 

>> They think all that matters is less typing. 
>
>No, it conveys information more precisely and faster.

It's faster when typing, the same when reading. Precision is exactly the same
linguistically. Logically, I find >= decidedly inferior to 'not less than'.

Granted, natural languages are optimized for a 'noisy channel'. Shannon
estimated that each letter contains one bit of information. We don't have much
noise in the programming environment (unless the programmer is visually
impaired), so redundancy does not add as much value. Still, I'd rather flavor my
English-like language with an English-like style.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabet vs. logograph (was Sorting algorithms)

_(reply depth: 44)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-09T15:13:46-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b9h96r$1oeo$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3eb76766.113815115@news.optonline.net> <b984j8$c2h$1@panix1.panix.com> <3eb86c21.14703208@news.optonline.net> <b9a1g4$utn$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3eba027b.118744531@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305081248.5d16aa5a@posting.google.com> <3ebad9a2.23403279@news.optonline.net>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3ebad9a2.23403279@news.optonline.net...
> riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> Then why does COBOL advertise that it's English-like?

In the current standard, it doesn't, and I don't believe it does in the '74
standard either.

In the '85 standard, the only reference to "English" is in the discussion of
the May 1959 meeting that resulted in the formation of CODASYL, in which it
states "It was agreed that the language must be open-ended and capable of
accepting change and amendment, that it should be problem-oriented and
machine-independent, and that it should use simple English or pseudo-English
and avoid symbolism as far as possible."

The IBM DOS manual for '68 COBOL (File S360-24, Order GC28-6394-4) I have
handy says about this same meeting:  "The proposed language would ... use a
language closely resembling English, avoiding the use of special symbols as
much as possible.  The COmmon Business Oriented Language (COBOL) which
resulted met most of these requirements."

The use of "=" and ">" in comparisons appears to be standard COBOL as early
as 1968; it is not marked as an extension in either of the '68 COBOL
reference manuals I have handy, and both explicitly allow them.  That leads
me to believe that this was either included in the initial COBOL-60
specification as an exception to the 5/29/1959 recommendation or a very,
very early extension beyond it.

COBOL is defined by what the standard says, not by what is syntactically
valid in English.  That the procedural portions of COBOL resemble English
insofar as possible was a goal of those who met in 1959.  It is not
necessarily a goal that has been pursued relentlessly and compulsively in
the evolution of COBOL over the intervening forty-four years.

Frisian, Dutch, Icelandic and German are, to some degree and in various
ways, "English-like".  Does that mean that any speaker of Icelandic who
follows a rule that differs from English practice is speaking gibberish in
his native language?

COBOL syntax and rules are *defined* by the standard, not by the rules of
English.  The rules of "sentence" and "statement" formation in most
generations of COBOL could equally well have been derived from the syntax of
just about any Indo-European language, and those rules apply just about as
well to the syntax as the English ones do.

> Still, I'd rather flavor my English-like language with an English-like
style.

Go for it.  So would I.  That doesn't mean that "IF A = B" and "IF C > D"
aren't fragments of valid COBOL statements.  I would personally avoid ">="
and "<=" for the same reasons I would avoid "IS GREATER THAN OR EQUAL TO"
and "IS LESS THAN OR EQUAL TO" as introduced in the '85 standard -- I don't
think either pair is all that clear, legible or intuitive.  My choice to
avoid them as a matter of style has nothing to do with the linguistic
history of the English language.

Now, the other side of that same point:  The framers of the latest standard
for COBOL have worked diligently to rectify COBOL's historical bias toward
American usage.  For those not literate in English, "IF A >= B" is more
legible than "IF A IS GREATER THAN OR EQUAL TO B".

Insisting that COBOL is "by gum a good ol' 'Murkin product" and any
accommodation of programmers not fluent in English is a degradation of the
language strikes me as not dissimilar to assertions like "Since the King
James version of the Bible was good enough for Moses and Jesus, it's good
enough for me."

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabet vs. logograph (was Sorting algorithms)

_(reply depth: 45)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-05-10T20:08:43+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ebd4dfe.184288340@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3eb76766.113815115@news.optonline.net> <b984j8$c2h$1@panix1.panix.com> <3eb86c21.14703208@news.optonline.net> <b9a1g4$utn$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3eba027b.118744531@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305081248.5d16aa5a@posting.google.com> <3ebad9a2.23403279@news.optonline.net> <b9h96r$1oeo$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
"Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

>
>"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
…[33 more quoted lines elided]…
>very early extension beyond it.

I never saw the 1959 document but remember seeing > and = in the first IBM COBOL
manual ca. 1960. 

>COBOL is defined by what the standard says, not by what is syntactically
>valid in English.  That the procedural portions of COBOL resemble English
>insofar as possible was a goal of those who met in 1959.  It is not
>necessarily a goal that has been pursued relentlessly and compulsively in
>the evolution of COBOL over the intervening forty-four years.

In this case the standard provides =,  'equal' and "equal to" without suggesting
a preferred form. My quibble is with those who choose = over 'equal to'. 

>> Still, I'd rather flavor my English-like language with an English-like
>style.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>history of the English language.

I avoid them because they look like two conditions joined by a OR. I know
they're not, but I can understand how a neophyte might think they are. To my
thinking, NOT LESS THAN is more logical. When I'm reversing the logic of an
expression, adding or deleting NOT is more straightforward than converting >= to
<. 

>Now, the other side of that same point:  The framers of the latest standard
>for COBOL have worked diligently to rectify COBOL's historical bias toward
>American usage.  For those not literate in English, "IF A >= B" is more
>legible than "IF A IS GREATER THAN OR EQUAL TO B".

As you said, relational operators are unchanged from the previous standard. 

There used to be superfluous words such as EQUALS and EXCEEDS. I don't know
whether they were in the standard or an IBM extension. I never used them.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(reply depth: 42)_

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2003-05-09T04:49:30+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3EBB33A7.4914E942@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3eb76766.113815115@news.optonline.net> <b984j8$c2h$1@panix1.panix.com> <3eb86c21.14703208@news.optonline.net> <b9a1g4$utn$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3eba027b.118744531@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:
> (snip)
> No modern language lacks an alphabet. Japanese phonetic alphabets are called
> kana. The Chinese phonetic alphabet is called pinyin.

Isn't Kana a syllabary rather than an alphabet?  I have read that all
native Japanese words consist of syllables comprised of either a
vowel, or an initial consonant followed by a vowel, and that no
syllable in Japanese ever has a terminal consonant.  Think "Basu-Boru"
for baseball.

> (snip)
> I did not say the vowels and consonants were the same across languages. They are
> not. But the variety within a single language is about 25. Bushmen's popping
> sounds can be represented by one or a few letters.

How do we spell the glottal stop in the English expression "Uh-oh"?

It's a mistake to see written alphabets as purely phonetic.  The words
"Dough" and "Rough" are separated by only a single letter, but have no
sounds in common that I can hear.

Consider the words "Write", "Right", "Rite", and "Wright".  All of
them are pronounced the same but have very different meanings. 
Obviously, the spelling variations allow readers to disambiguate them,
but why do they sound the same?  It's not clear that these words have
any common root meaning.  Even the word "Right" has multiple meanings,
such as "constitution right" and "the right answer". 
```

```
What is interesting is when another language such as Spanish has the same word
for both types of "right".   This implies that they have the same root - that
right handed is right and left handed is sinister.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

```
In article <b9gct6$9f5$1@peabody.colorado.edu>,
Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
>
>On  8-May-2003, Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net> wrote:
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>right handed is right and left handed is sinister.

Hmmmmm... 'interesting' is, of course, in the mind of the beholder; as I 
recall both Spanish and English have a wee bit of Latin influence (Pax 
Romana, anyone?  The White Cliffs of Dover were the 'albus' that lead to 
Albion, I was told) and both 'right' and 'derecha' seem derivates of 
'rectus' (along with the French 'droit').

What might be fruitful would be to find a language not influenced by Latin 
which independent of European influence shows the development of a term 
equivalent to 'legal right' (hey... if it were easy then *everyone* would 
be doing it!) and compare that language's word for this to its word for 
the hand/direction it has.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(reply depth: 45)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-09T10:34:25-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b9gor2$1cvv$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3eba027b.118744531@news.optonline.net> <3EBB33A7.4914E942@worldnet.att.net> <b9gct6$9f5$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <b9geff$2er$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```

<docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:b9geff$2er$1@panix1.panix.com...

> ...  as I
> recall both Spanish and English have a wee bit of Latin influence ...
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> the hand/direction it has.

I think the common derivation of "right", "droit", "derecha" may predate
*direct* Latin influence from "rectus" and "regere".  Much more likely is
that its immediate ancestor is a Germanic root surviving also as "recht" in
German and Dutch and "rettur" in Icelandic.  The apparent existence of a
root form of this word common in the Germanic, Italic, Hellenic and Albanian
branches of Indo-European suggests to me that the word's origins in English
and its ancestors almost certainly predates any direct influence from Latin.
"Rectus" and "regere" are no closer than an uncle to Old English "riht", I
believe, and probably more like a cousin once, or twice, removed.  "Pravda"
in the Balto-Slavics might even turn out to be cognate.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <b9gct6$9f5$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <b9geff$2er$1@panix1.panix.com> <b9gor2$1cvv$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
In article <b9gor2$1cvv$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>,
Chuck Stevens <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:
>
><docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:b9geff$2er$1@panix1.panix.com...
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>German and Dutch and "rettur" in Icelandic.

The OED traces it to a Norse re'ltr and indicates a Danish ret and a
Swedish raett (ae being substituted for an umlauted 'a'); Baeda's History
is cited for a use of the 'legal' persuasion ... and Nicodemus (c 1000) is
cited for the first use of 'right hand' so the uses seem similarly ancient 
and both antedating Roman influence.

>The apparent existence of a
>root form of this word common in the Germanic, Italic, Hellenic and Albanian
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>in the Balto-Slavics might even turn out to be cognate.

These beliefs might be interesting, Mr Stevens, but Germany, Greece and
Albania - perhaps even Italy! - just might possibly have had a bit of
Roman influence over the millennia; a connection between 'rectus' and
'recht' is not all that difficult to imagine.  This is one of the reasons
I was interested in languages specifically outside of the European.  

(Old Testament Hebrew - which appears to pre-date most Roman influences -
uses a 'ymn' (yud-mem-nun) root to indicate direction but I an not
familiar with Hebrew uses of this root or its derivatives in the sense of
'legal right'.)

DD
```

```
According to my sources, the icelandic comes from north germanic, the
english comes from west germanic via middle english and old english.  Both
north and west germanic come from indo-european, and the texts that I have
on that language suggest the common root "rek" ... to judge, councel,
determine.  In old english, "regen" is power, and in old Norse "regin" is a
decree.

Donald
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(reply depth: 43)_

- **From:** jallan@smrtytrek.com (jallan)
- **Date:** 2003-05-09T18:09:17-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<299f1138.0305091709.6c811b6c@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3eb76766.113815115@news.optonline.net> <b984j8$c2h$1@panix1.panix.com> <3eb86c21.14703208@news.optonline.net> <b9a1g4$utn$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3eba027b.118744531@news.optonline.net> <3EBB33A7.4914E942@worldnet.att.net>`

```
Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net> wrote in message news:<3EBB33A7.4914E942@worldnet.att.net>...
> Robert Wagner wrote:
> > (snip)
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> for baseball.

Most Hindu scripts also contain letters which have a default vowel
sound, usually _a_. When you want another vowel to follow you must
specifically include it.

This is close enough to being alphabetic that most count it, as well
as kana.

But things like Biblical Hebrew and Arabic without vocalic letters
(except as extra dots and squiggles) are now often called abjads
rather than alphabets, reserving _alphabet_ for writing systems which
represent (admittedly rather approximately in some languages like
English) both the consonants and vowels of the language by separate
symbols for each.

However there are also living syllabic writing systems.

There is the Yi syllabary used for a language within China (see
http://www.unicode.org/charts/PDF/UA490.pdf and
http://uk.geocities.com/BabelStone1357/Yi/unicode.html).

There is the Ethiopic syllabary (see
http://www.unicode.org/charts/PDF/U1200.pdf and
http://www.abyssiniacybergateway.net/fidel/unicode).

There are the syllabic writing systems used for Algonquian and Inuit
languages (see http://www.unicode.org/charts/PDF/U1400.pdf and
http://www.nunavut.com and http://www.assembly.nu.ca/unicode/fonts).

Of course, these are all based on the phonetic principle in which
individual symbols represent speech sounds rather than words or
concepts or ideas.

What Robert Wagnar doubtless intended was to indicate the greater
efficiency of phonemic writing systems compared to ideographic systems
in respect to the number of characters necessary, and it is more
useful to discuss his intent rather than nitpicking about his not
distinguishing alphabets and abjads and syllabaries.

Yet ancient Egyptian had an abjad within their character set and the
Sumerian cuneiform writing system, though containing thousands of
idiographs, could write everything syllabically.

But they stuck to largely to their ideographic symbols. Why? Just
conservatism?

Robert Wagner also wrote:

> Correct. The Chinese writing system and derivative Japanese Kanji 
> are logographic, meaning a symbol represents a word. Hundreds of natural
> languages independently discovered the superiority of alphabets over
> logographs. The only reactionary languages, to my knowledge, are Chinese,
> math and programming. 

> Programmers use symbols such as = and > to represent words without
> understanding that they're taking step backward in linguistics. They think
> all that matters is less typing. 

Now "math and programming" are intesting. Why do they take this step?

Here is the same information in two notations: 

the quantity of one hundred and eight-five point zero two squared
times fifty-six plus twenty-seven, this quantity divided by
ninety-eight

(185.02ï¿½ ï¿½ 56 + 27) / 98

Which is better?

I think most find the second notation far more vivid and
understandable.

Similarly for "$25.99" versus "twenty-five dollars and ninety-five
cents".

Ideographic writing systems provide a more vivid experience to a
reader, which is surely *partly* why Chinese and Japanese aren't eager
to use only the phonetic symbols in their writing systems and throw
away the rest, despite the saving in other ways.

There is something clear and concise about seeing a concept symbolized
directly rather than seeing symbols for the sounds of a word that
indicates the concept.

A recently invented ideographic system is Blissymbolics which has been
devised for and found useful for communication with people and among
people afflicted by cerebral palsy and other kinds of brain damage.
See http://home.istar.ca/~bci and
http://std.dkuug.dk/jtc1/sc2/wg2/docs/n1866.pdf.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(reply depth: 44)_

```
jallan@smrtytrek.com (jallan) wrote:

>There is the Ethiopic syllabary (see
>http://www.unicode.org/charts/PDF/U1200.pdf and
>http://www.abyssiniacybergateway.net/fidel/unicode).

The language of Ethopia came from proto-Canaanite (aka proto-Sinaitic), which
once used characters derived from Egyptian hieroglyphics as logographs. It is
now described as phonetic, so it appears to have changed over the last 3,000
years. Does that sound right?

>There are the syllabic writing systems used for Algonquian and Inuit
>languages (see http://www.unicode.org/charts/PDF/U1400.pdf and
>http://www.nunavut.com and http://www.assembly.nu.ca/unicode/fonts).

No unicode for Cherokee? It's another syllabic system.

>Now "math and programming" are interesting. Why do they take this step?
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>Which is better?

Numeric digits are actually logographs, so it would be inconsistant to write
185.02  squared times 56 plus 27 divided by 98. But relational operators > and =
are used in conditional statements comprised of words. For example, 'if due-date
not greater than date-today and invoice-amount not < past-due-threshold ...
Mixing logographic relationship tests with words seems as inconsistant in the
second example as in the first. 

>Ideographic writing systems provide a more vivid experience to a
>reader, which is surely *partly* why Chinese and Japanese aren't eager
>to use only the phonetic symbols in their writing systems and throw
>away the rest, despite the saving in other ways.

Japanese have abandoned kanji for daily communication. Its use is limited to
'fine literature'.

Thank you for an excellent, informativie article.
```

```
I thought that some of those still reading this thread might be interested
in a fairly comprehensive web article that I found at:

 http://pandora.cii.wwu.edu/vajda/ling201/test4materials/WritingHANDOUT.htm

It doesn't address the "what type of writing evolves to what other type of
writing" - but it does provide an excellent (IMHO) set of DIFFERENT
classifications of writing systems.

It does, of course, start out with the accurate (and possibly appropriate)
statement,

"Remember that writing is secondary to human speech. Even today many
languages remain unwritten."
```
