###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(reply depth: 45)_

- **From:** jallan@smrtytrek.com (jallan)
- **Date:** 2003-05-10T19:48:31-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<299f1138.0305101848.20bb3ecf@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3eb76766.113815115@news.optonline.net> <b984j8$c2h$1@panix1.panix.com> <3eb86c21.14703208@news.optonline.net> <b9a1g4$utn$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3eba027b.118744531@news.optonline.net> <3EBB33A7.4914E942@worldnet.att.net> <299f1138.0305091709.6c811b6c@posting.google.com> <3ebd5572.186196194@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote in message news:<3ebd5572.186196194@news.optonline.net>...
> jallan@smrtytrek.com (jallan) wrote:
> 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> years. Does that sound right?

It is theorized that the Ethiopic Ge'ez script is derived from
heiroglyphics. See http://www.intelligirldesign.com/paper_gabriella.pdf
for one discussion. I don't know enough to comment on its accuracy.

> No unicode for Cherokee? It's another syllabic system.

Yes, actually. I just forgot about it. See 
http://www.unicode.org/charts/PDF/U13A0.pdf.

> Numeric digits are actually logographs, so it would be inconsistant to write
> 185.02  squared times 56 plus 27 divided by 98. But relational operators > and =
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> second example as in the first. 

But people do mix alphabetic characters and ideographs for numbers.

See http://www-gap.dcs.st-and.ac.uk/~history/HistTopics/Greek_numbers.html
for a discussion of Greek numeric systems. Roman numerals are built
from what appear to be Latin letters, but when used as numerals they
are use as ideographs rather than alphabetic characters, that is VI
stands for the number "six" in English and the corresponding number
name in other languages, but does not at all stand for the sound of
_v_ followed by the sound of _i_.

Roman numerals are normally used for numbers in medieval texts until
replaced by Arabic numbers. Inconsistant? Sure. But it makes the text
more readable, then why should this inconsistancy matter.

There is a general feeling that spelling out numbers is more literary
and high-toned, but to take a few quotes from one famous book by a
professor of English at Oxford:

... but as the years go by it seems now often forgotten that to be
caught in youth by 1914 was no less hideous an experience than to be
involved in 1939 and the following years. By 1918 all but one of my
close friends were dead.

He returned to his home at Bag End on June the 22nd in his
fifty-second year (S.R. 1342), and nothing very notable occurred in
the Shire until Mr. Baggins began the preparations for the celebration
of his hundred-and-eleventh birthday (S.R. 1401).

Bilbo was going to be _eleventy-one_, 111, a rather curious number and
a very respectable age for a hobbit (the Old Took himself had only
reached 130); and Frodo was going to be _thirty-three_, 33) an
important number: the date of his 'coming of age'.

On September 20th two covered carts went off laden to Buckland, ...

We have digits used mostly for dates, but sometimes for other
purposes, and numbers both given as digits and spelled out in full in
the same sentences.

I don't see anything philosphically _wrong_ with mixing in other
ideographs. The &-sign used to appear often in printed texts in
English.

Also _<_ meaning "greater than" (and many another such symbol) is
better called an _ideograph_ rather than a _logograph_ as it (and many
another such symbol) is normally rendered in English and other
languages by more than a single word.

Some Han characters may also represent more than a single word in some
languages for which they are used.

Akkadian cuneiform, which I could once barely read, is basically a
syllabic script but with thousands of individual ideographs (which in
theory weren't needed), and the scribes sometimes seemed to delight in
being arbitrary as to whether to spell out a word in syllables or use
a single ideograph.

I gather Japanese is somewhat like that.

Essentially it seems that when the exact wording is most important,
use spelled-out words, but in technical writing where the exact
concept is more important, as in logic or mathematics or computer
languages, then ideographic symbols are quite rightly used more
because they make things clearer.

But I've noticed that some programmers in languages that allow
alternates such as either "=" or "eq", either "<>" or "ne" and so
forth ... some programmers will always choose the symbols and some
will always choose the alphabetic abbreviations.

There seems to be an innate difference in personal taste in this
matter.

I always choose the symbols over the abbreviations.

> Japanese have abandoned kanji for daily communication. Its use is limited to
> 'fine literature'.

Apparently true for Japanese communities in some countries, but not in
Japan. See http://www.mmtaylor.net/Literacy_Book/DOCS/21.html and
http://www.iar.ubc.ca/centres/cjr/jsac2000/kess.pdf and especially
http://ancienthistory.about.com/library/bl/uc_gill1.htm and any
Japanese newspaper.

Finally, at http://www.unicode.org/glossary Unicode defines _Alphabet_
as:

<< A collection of symbols that, in the context of a particular
written language, represent the sounds of that language. The
correspondence between symbols and sounds may be either more or less
exact; most alphabets do not exhibit a one-to-one correspondence
between distinct sounds (phonemes) and distinct symbols (graphemes).
>>

_Syllabary_ is defined as:

<< An alphabet whose symbols typically represent multiple phonemes of
a language. These multiple phonemes are generally combinations of
consonants and vowels. >>

So as far as Unicode is concerned then, a syllabary is a particular
kind of alphabet.

Of course, other definitions still distinguish them.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote

> Numeric digits are actually logographs, so it would be inconsistant to write
> 185.02  squared times 56 plus 27 divided by 98. But relational operators > and =
> are used in conditional statements comprised of words. For example, 'if due-date
> not greater than date-today and invoice-amount not < past-due-threshold ...

> Mixing logographic relationship tests with words seems as inconsistant in the
> second example as in the first. 

Only because you have contrived the examples to appear so.

Arithmetic expressions can be written with variables (words) and
conditionals written with numvbers jaus as easily reversing your
claim.

It seems that your argument is not based on 'inconsistency' at all, it
is just that you haven't learnt to tell the difference between < and >
and so contrive an argument about whether it is 'backward' to use
them.

> Japanese have abandoned kanji for daily communication. Its use is limited to
> 'fine literature'.

"""To read Japanese newspapers with ease you need to learn the 1,945
general-use characters --there is no escaping this fact. Your Japanese
teacher quotes a National Language Research Institute study: the 1,000
most frequently used characters comprise 94 percent of the kanji used
in newspapers."""
```

```
"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3ebf01e7.295898087@news.optonline.net...
> Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> Nor do I care what you think. Hopefully another reader, perhaps a
neophyte, will
> learn that > represents more than 'less typing' or 'a question of style'.
Its
> use is a linguistic issue.

That is absolute rubbish, Robert, given that you have failed to establish
the "linguistic" association.

I would hope that a neophyte (and I DO care about them...even if I don't
about this endless pointless tedious thread) WILL use > as an accepted
representation of "is greater than" in COBOL.

It IS less typing, it stands out more, and it IS a question of style and
preference.

On the other hand, if they choose not to, that's OK too. (The important
thing is that they make their OWN choice.)

Let's not have the choice made FOR them, based on  pseudo-intellectual
babble about Linguistics (which is totally irrelevant here anyway, and in no
way connected with COBOL or any other computer programming "Language"...)

 Last time I looked, the fucking Goths were not doing a lot with
COBOL...neither were the proto-Indo-Europeans, who never managed to produce
a Sanskrit COBOL compiler that would be approved by ANSI/ISO/J4...

Have all the bullshit conversation you want to...it's a free forum, but
DON'T pretend to neophytes that you have a point and they MUST code the way
you say.

As you may have gathered I'm a tad irritated by this...I won't be responding
further.

Pete.
```

```
Just so no one is confused.  The inline OO invocation symbol (in the 2002
Standard) is:

   ::         *> two colons, and not
  ==       *> two equal signs - which is the pseudo-text delimiter
```

```
On 12-May-2003, "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote:

> My  way "L" for Left
> is the left bracket and same as "Less" - so the other one has got to be
> Greater - right <G>

I never had any shortcut to remember which symbol was which.   Do you have a
shortcut to tell you what = means?   How about 3?

I do like the concept that numerals can be determined by counting their acute
angles.

0 = 0 angles
1 = 1 angle (depending on your font)
2 = 2 angles (make a z)
3 = 3 angles (sharpen those curves)
4 = 4 angles.  Make sure to cross the vertical line.
5 = 5 angles.  Sharpen those curves again.  Make sure the tail goes up.
6 = 6 angles.
7 = 7 angles.   Make sure to cross the 7 with a little 7 figure.
8 = 8 angles (one square on top of another)
9 = 9 angles. (follow the tail around and down)
```

- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3ebd5572.186196194@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305102213.463660bf@posting.google.com> <3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <b9m745$akc$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ebf01e7.295898087@news.optonline.net> <b9ocfi$bmk$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message news:<b9ocfi$bmk$1@peabody.colorado.edu>...
> On 11-May-2003, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:
> 
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> logogram such as 5?

He totally ignored my post in this thread on that subject which also
indicated from my own experience that given a language that allows
both symbols and abbreviations for things like "equal", "greater
than", "less than", some programmers will always go with the symbols
and some will always go with the abbreviations.

Aphabetical scripts (including syllabaries) are a good solution for
representing speech on paper.

But programming languages are not primarily speech, though COBOL is
more like English speech than most.

You could read a COBOL program aloud to someone. A short and simple
program might even be understandable that way. But not a complex
program with multiple nested IF's and PERFORM's.

It is not accidental that people used the word "code" when referring
to computer source language.

Indentations of particular lines and sections of code are not mandated
by any language I know of. The computer doesn't need it.

But the indentations are considered by many essential to writing good
source code to allow it to be understood, though nothing in speech
corresponds to the multiple levels of indentations.

Mathematics only gradually adopted a complex series of symbols, but
they are used and continue to be used and expanded on because they
make things clearer, allowing diagramming of relations that are
awkward to present in speech.

Computer languages must of necessity handle very clearly and precisely
with the same kind of things as mathematics and logic, with values
that must be computed and tested. This kind of notation bypasses
spoken language altogether, going  directly from symbol to meaning.

It is natural that computer languages borrow the methods of
representation already predominant in mathematics and logic.

As to linguistic sensibilities, I find COBOL offends mine in ways more
serious than the sensible use of common logical symbols to express
numbers, logical relations and mathematical calculations.

How did the creators of COBOL come up with something so stupidly
uintuitive and un-English as STOP RUN?

And MOVE?

If I tell someone to "move" a chair from the corner to beside the
window, and they do it, then the chair is no longer in the corner, it
is now beside the window. But if I use COBOL to tell the computer to
move "chair", which is the contents of CORNER, from CORNER to WINDOW,
to my surprise "chair" is now duplicated, being both in CORNER and in
WINDOW.

Shouldn't it be COPY rather than MOVE?

Some good discussions of various writing systems and there supposed
advantages and disadvantages can be found at
http://home.vicnet.net.au/~ozideas/#writing.

The Han character mixed deograph/alphabetic system exists and other
similar mixed ideographic/alphabetic systems have existed in the past.
The symbols used in mathematics and logic have appeared and become
predominant not because of any fiat or political or philosophical
movement toward ideographs, but because individual mathematicians and
logicans invented symbols and used symbols that others had invented in
preference to spelling out words.

Linguistics can study what has been done, but I don't believe there is
any linguistic issue about what _should_ be done in respect to mixing
phonetic characters and ideographs, unless it can be demonstrated that
mixing is *practically* worse in particular circumstances than either
pure alphabetic or pure ideographic scipt.

Perhaps everyone should adopt APL?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(reply depth: 51)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-05-13T03:02:20+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ec054aa.32832663@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3ebd5572.186196194@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305102213.463660bf@posting.google.com> <3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <b9m745$akc$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ebf01e7.295898087@news.optonline.net> <b9ocfi$bmk$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <299f1138.0305121044.5119ff42@posting.google.com>`

```
jallan@smrtytrek.com (jallan) wrote:

>"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message
news:<b9ocfi$bmk$1@peabody.colorado.edu>...
>> On 11-May-2003, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:
>> 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>> > learn that > represents more than 'less typing' or 'a question of style'.
Its
>> > use is a linguistic issue.
>> 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>and some will always go with the abbreviations.

I did not ignore your post. I read it several times trying to think of something
I could add, each time coming up empty. It was so comprehensive, I couldn't
improve on it. 

>Indentations of particular lines and sections of code are not mandated
>by any language I know of. The computer doesn't need it.

APS and Python honor indentation, as do precompilers I've written.

>Computer languages must of necessity handle very clearly and precisely
>with the same kind of things as mathematics and logic, with values
>that must be computed and tested. This kind of notation bypasses
>spoken language altogether, going  directly from symbol to meaning.

I dispute that.The symbol > is no more (or less) precise than the words 'greater
than'.

>It is natural that computer languages borrow the methods of
>representation already predominant in mathematics and logic.

The design objective of COBOL was to use "as few symbols as possible".
>
>As to linguistic sensibilities, I find COBOL offends mine in ways more
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>uintuitive and un-English as STOP RUN?

In those days a 'run' was a batch program. I'm not defending it.

>And MOVE?
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>Shouldn't it be COPY rather than MOVE?

Yes, it should. The very first program I wrote, in IBM 1401 assembly language,
assumed that 'move' cleared the sending field. I was dumbfounded when I learned
move was really copy. Silly me for assuming words meant what they said. 

The terminology comes from hardware engineers who named assembly language
instructions. Their illiterate terminology found its way into COBOL.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(reply depth: 52)_

- **From:** jallan@smrtytrek.com (jallan)
- **Date:** 2003-05-13T08:28:00-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<299f1138.0305130728.3468ca00@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3ebd5572.186196194@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305102213.463660bf@posting.google.com> <3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <b9m745$akc$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ebf01e7.295898087@news.optonline.net> <b9ocfi$bmk$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <299f1138.0305121044.5119ff42@posting.google.com> <3ec054aa.32832663@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote in message news:<3ec054aa.32832663@news.optonline.net>...
> jallan@smrtytrek.com (jallan) wrote:
> 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> >> 

> I did not ignore your post. I read it several times trying to think of something
> I could add, each time coming up empty. It was so comprehensive, I couldn't
> improve on it. 
Thank you, very much.
> 
> >Indentations of particular lines and sections of code are not mandated
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
Yes, and I've used editors that enforce indentation on languages that
do not require it. I like them.

What I was trying to indicate, however, is that this diagrammatic,
visual, and very desireable indication of logic is not at all derived
from speech.

Mathematics using symbols is a different kind of language than speech.
One could   invent regular ways in speech to indicate start brackets
and end brackets, for example, accept that the word "start-bracket"
and "end-bracket" themselves have a meaning that what is spoken
between them is to be considered as a whole.

Yet that would miss the point of why mathematical ideographic notation
has increased. It is a visual language, more distinct from speech than
sign-language is from speech, in which one can visually grasp complex
relationships in one single view, relationships that are much harder
to comprehend if represented serially in speech.

> >Computer languages must of necessity handle very clearly and precisely
> >with the same kind of things as mathematics and logic, with values
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> than'.

Ah, but word take longer to process mentally than a single symbol.

Would you refer to write "greater than" instead of "GT"? 

"GT" is already a symbol, like "lb." for "pound". "GT" is effectively
already an ideograph.

Do you agree with standard use in geometry of _d_ for "diamater", _r_
for "radius", but argue against use of the _pi_ symbol for the
relation between radius/diameter and circumference.

What would you use instead? What English word or expression would you
subtitute?

> The terminology comes from hardware engineers who named assembly language
> instructions. Their illiterate terminology found its way into COBOL.

I don't feel "illiterate" is the right word.

I think "careless" better expresses the situation in many fields where
jargon *accidently* develops.

Of course, once established within the jargon, the special meanings
become so basic to those using the jargon that they may not realize or
remember that their special use within the jargon is not used at all
outside the jargon.

That is probably what happened with those who invented COBOL.

They intended to use largely English-like syntax but had so fully
internalized the use of MOVE (usually written MV) in assembler
language mnemonics and used in speech to describe copying of data from
one memory location to another that they had forgot that this common
word did not have any such meaning outside of computerese.

Another oddity: "Data check" is an odd IBM jargon expression for a
"data error", and does not mean "data investigation".

Languages diverge into separate dialects and then separate languages
in this way.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(reply depth: 53)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-05-13T16:58:52+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b9r88b$p6b$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3ebd5572.186196194@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305102213.463660bf@posting.google.com> <3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <b9m745$akc$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ebf01e7.295898087@news.optonline.net> <b9ocfi$bmk$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <299f1138.0305121044.5119ff42@posting.google.com> <3ec054aa.32832663@news.optonline.net> <299f1138.0305130728.3468ca00@posting.google.com>`

```

On 13-May-2003, jallan@smrtytrek.com (jallan) wrote:

> Yes, and I've used editors that enforce indentation on languages that
> do not require it. I like them.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> from speech.

One could have languages with greater use of pauses.   Musical notation actually
has a symbol for "white space", and even a blank composition (or two).

Commas and semicolons are used for pauses in English - but in CoBOL, these are
ignored.

I have seen people write newsgroup messages with annotated blank spaces
<pregnant pause...>.

Our phonetic alphabets leave a lot to be desired, as they miss quite a bit of
our verbal communication - not to mention non-verbal communication.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)

- **Message-ID:** `<3ec2273b.106098230@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3ebd5572.186196194@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305102213.463660bf@posting.google.com> <3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <b9m745$akc$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ebf01e7.295898087@news.optonline.net> <b9ocfi$bmk$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <299f1138.0305121044.5119ff42@posting.google.com> <3ec054aa.32832663@news.optonline.net> <299f1138.0305130728.3468ca00@posting.google.com>`

```
jallan@smrtytrek.com (jallan) wrote:

>robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote in message 

>What I was trying to indicate, however, is that this diagrammatic,
>visual, and very desireable indication of logic is not at all derived
>from speech.

Speech and writing use words and pauses to indicate subordination. For example
'if it is raining, take the umbrella'  or 'if it is raining then take the
umbrella otherwise leave it home'. 

Poetry uses indentation.

>Mathematics using symbols is a different kind of language than speech.
>One could   invent regular ways in speech to indicate start brackets
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>to comprehend if represented serially in speech.

Good point .. and perhaps a good argument for other programming languages. But
COBOL is modelled on English. 


>> I dispute that.The symbol > is no more (or less) precise than the words
'greater
>> than'.
>
>Ah, but word take longer to process mentally than a single symbol.

I find words faster to comprehend. We don't read a letter at a time, we read the
whole word at once. 

>Would you refer to write "greater than" instead of "GT"? 

Yes, because a typo will be detected by the compiler.

>Do you agree with standard use in geometry of _d_ for "diamater", _r_
>for "radius", but argue against use of the _pi_ symbol for the
>relation between radius/diameter and circumference.

Pi is universally known, but generally I don't like Greek letters in math. They
look like the author is showing off or making a class distinction between
educated and uneducated. 

>What would you use instead? What English word or expression would you
>subtitute?

The letters 'pi'.

>> The terminology comes from hardware engineers who named assembly language
>> instructions. Their illiterate terminology found its way into COBOL.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>jargon *accidently* develops.

I like your terminology. Careless is a better description of the process

>Of course, once established within the jargon, the special meanings
>become so basic to those using the jargon that they may not realize or
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>That is probably what happened with those who invented COBOL.

We've seen an instance of that here in re integer. 'A word means whatever the
standard says it means.'

>Another oddity: "Data check" is an odd IBM jargon expression for a
>"data error", and does not mean "data investigation".

My favorites are 'Press Enter to exit' and 'Print check' (when the light comes
on, I look for a box of checks).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(reply depth: 54)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-05-14T16:58:49-07:00

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(reply depth: 52)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-05-14T17:29:00+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b9pvti$uf9$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <b9ocfi$bmk$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <299f1138.0305121044.5119ff42@posting.google.com> <3ec054aa.32832663@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:


> I dispute that.The symbol > is no more (or less) precise than the words
> 'greater than'.

It is more precise to a large number of people who don't speak English.

> Yes, it should. The very first program I wrote, in IBM 1401 assembly
> language, assumed that 'move' cleared the sending field. I was dumbfounded
> when I learned move was really copy. Silly me for assuming words meant
> what they said.

Silly you for not reading the manual.
 
> The terminology comes from hardware engineers who named assembly language
> instructions. Their illiterate terminology found its way into COBOL.

It is not 'illiterate' terminology.  A MOVE does not necessarily result in 
a COPY of the data, it may result in a transformation of it.  That is, the 
use of COPY may be just as 'illiterate'.  In terms of the receiving field 
the data bits are MOVEd into it in ways that may not be a simple COPY of 
those bits.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(reply depth: 55)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-05-15T03:06:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ec2da35.151923574@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3ec054aa.32832663@news.optonline.net> <b9pvti$uf9$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ec225e5.105756418@news.optonline.net> <b9u5aj$ol1$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>Robert Wagner wrote:
>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>This is a Cobol discussion, MOVE can do transformation.

Oh, right, I was on a different wavelength. 

Following the Doc's proclivity for seeking answers in the dictionary, I found
this in m-w:

Main Entry: 1move 
Pronunciation: 'm�v
Function: verb
Inflected Form(s): moved; mov�ing
Etymology: Middle English, from Middle French movoir, from Latin movEre;
probably akin to Sanskrit mIvati he moves, pushes
Date: 13th century
intransitive senses
1 a (1) : to go or pass to another place or in a certain direction with a
continuous motion <moved into the shade> (2) : to proceed toward a certain state
or condition <moving up the executive ladder> <moved into second place in the
tournament> (3) : to become transferred during play <checkers move along
diagonally adjacent squares> (4) : to keep pace <moving with the times> b : to
start away from some point or place : DEPART c : to change one's residence or
location
2 : to carry on one's life or activities in a specified environment <moves in
the best circles>
3 : to change position or posture : STIR <told him to be quiet and not to move>
4 : to take action : ACT
5 a : to begin operating or functioning or working in a usual way b : to show
marked activity <after a brief lull things really began to move> c : to move a
piece (as in chess or checkers) during one's turn
6 : to make a formal request, application, or appeal
7 : to change hands by being sold or rented <goods that were moving slowly>
8 of the bowels : EVACUATE
transitive senses
1 a (1) : to change the place or position of (2) : to dislodge or displace from
a fixed position : BUDGE b : to transfer (as a piece in chess) from one position
to another
2 a (1) : to cause to go or pass from one place to another with a continuous
motion <moved the flag slowly up and down> (2) : to cause to advance b : to
cause to operate or function : ACTUATE <this button moves the whole machine> c :
to put into activity or rouse up from inactivity
3 : to cause to change position or posture
4 : to prompt or rouse to the doing of something : PERSUADE <the report moved
the faculty to take action>
5 a : to stir the emotions, feelings, or passions of <was deeply moved by such
kindness> b : to affect in such a way as to lead to an indicated show of emotion
<the story moved her to tears>
6 a obsolete : BEG b : to make a formal application to
7 : to propose formally in a deliberative assembly <moved adjournment>
8 : to cause (the bowels) to void
9 : to cause to change hands through sale or rent
synonyms MOVE, ACTUATE, DRIVE, IMPEL mean to set or keep in motion. MOVE is very
general and implies no more than the fact of changing position <moved the
furniture>. ACTUATE stresses transmission of power so as to work or set in
motion <turbines actuated by waterpower>. DRIVE implies imparting forward and
continuous motion and often stresses the effect rather than the impetus <a ship
driven aground by hurricane winds>. IMPEL is usually figurative and suggests a
great motivating impetus <a candidate impelled by ambition>. 
---------end quotation ---------------

I see no definition which could be interpreted in the way MOVE is used in COBOL.
The most COBOL-like syntax would be SET destination TO source. The 'newer' COBOL
data types (indexes, pointers and indicators) seem to prefer SET over MOVE. But
I don't like the discord between two semantics -- one for new and one for old
types. It seems like a political compromise. 

The topic here is word order between Subject, Verb and Object. Move a to b is
VSO on the surface but really SVO (see below); set b to a is VOS on the surface
but really SVO.  

75% of the world's natural languages use SVO (e.g. English, French)
or SOV (e.g. Japanese). Another 15% use VSO (e.g. Welsh). The remainder
use free word order (e.g. Latin) or VOS. VOS is rare; the best known
examples are Malagasy, Tzotzil and Houailou.

For many years it was thought that there were no Object-initial
languages, however a group of OVS languages were found recently. They're
all in the Amazon basin and belong to the Carib family. Examples are
Hixkaryana, Apalai, Bacairi and Makusi. We have never found a pure OSV
language, although it's sometimes used in Japanese & a two obscure
languages: Jamamadi & Apurina (& one other, see below).

The word order of  Cobol seems to be
VO, to which I would add a constructive Subject 'you' meaning the
computer, giving SVO. When we say 'move a to b' we're saying 'the
computer moves a to b' or 'computer: you move a to b'. In languages with
mathematical notation, one doesn't get the sense of
talking to the computer. In 'dest = src' the Verb is the assignment
operator, the word order is OVS. In OO we have Obj::Verb(Subj), again
OVS. In the case of a function call (e.g. strcpy(*dest, *src)) the word
order is VOS.

I wonder why OO emulates the logic patterns of stone-age cultures living
in the jungles of Brazil and Malaysia. Do they know something we don't?
(Julian Jaynes says they do, but it's not better logic.) For example,
here's how programming sequences would look in English:

OVS    Jones I invited, not Smith.
VOS    Take my word for it, will you?

Not to be left out, the most wierd of all --- OSV --- is what we call
postfix. Here's what it looks like in the language of the Jedi, who
obviously speak in OO:

OSV Strong with the Force you are.
       Your father he is.
       Sick I've become.
       When nine hundred years you reach, look as good you will not.

[Whoever wrote those lines understood linguistics. He used object-initial OSV to
make the language sound alien to all of Earth's languages.]

But perhaps Jedi programming languages are not OO. Perhaps their illiterate
programming language designers choose SVO, which brings us full circle.
```

```
jallan wrote:
> (snip)
> It is not accidental that people used the word "code" when referring
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> corresponds to the multiple levels of indentations.


There was an interpreted language named MANTIS, supported by Cincom
Systems (other products included TOTAL and SUPRA databases), that ran
in CICS/VS in the early 1980's.  It had an editor that automatically
indented each line when necessary, and filled the beginning of the
line with dots, one per indentation level.  It was impossible to write
a syntactically correct MANTIS program without correct indentation.

MANTIS was good for quickly producing small CICS applications with
vsam files, screens, and help screens (called prompters for some
reason), but it tended to perform poorly as the number of users
increased.

It's the only counter example that I know of, but it wasn't because
the computer needed it.  It was supposed to make things easier for the
programmer.

> (snip)
> As to linguistic sensibilities, I find COBOL offends mine in ways more
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> uintuitive and un-English as STOP RUN?

That one never bothered me, for some reason.

> 
> And MOVE?
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> Shouldn't it be COPY rather than MOVE?

Well, yes.  But COPY already has a specific meaning in COBOL, which
could be replaced by INCLUDE (in the way Panvalet used it).  MOVE is a
bit counterintuitive now, but I have gotten used to its COBOL
meaning.  IBM assembler has the same feature in the MVC (Move
Character) and MVI (Move Immediate) instructions.  Logically, it
copies data with no "delete behind".   

> (snip)
> 
> Perhaps everyone should adopt APL?

Well, that would *not* be my preference.  But I hear that some people
liked APL quite a lot.
```

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:

>
>On 12-May-2003, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>> That doesn't bother me because language hasn't produced an adequate
substitute
>> for math. Romans tried and failed miserably with their XVCII dates.
>
>Both > and 5 are math logograms that can be spelled out in English.

It is inconvenient to do arithmetic on words. We don't perform comparable
operations on relational operators. Even when we do Boolean algebra, the logical
operators (and, or, not) are the ones that get changed, not the relational ones.
```

```
Chuck Stevens <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(reply depth: 57)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-05-17T20:12:57+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ba3gqr$1sf$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3ec2e6c7.155141897@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305151728.19202068@posting.google.com> <3ec4b5f6.68160950@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:

> I did not answer the question for you, nor even say how I would do it, nor
> conclude how everyone _should_ do it.

You appeared to 'rest your case' on the basis of an assumption of what my 
answer would be.  It is extremely unlikely that I would write any such 
code, I am not prone to doing such things.  However, I don't see any 
particular problem in directly manipulating Roman notation for arthmetic 
except that it would be quite tedious to do so.

Just pick up each 'column' group in turn, revert any 'IV' form to 'IIII' 
and string together.  Take the length to pass the carries to the next 
column.  Multiplication is done just as arabic notation by multiplying by 
each column in turn and adding. 

What I would be more likely to do would be to emulate a Roman abacus, which 
they used for any serious working.  Instead of a frame of 10 (9 beads) it 
has a frame of 5 (4 beads) and a '5' row. Multiplying by X is exactly the 
same as multiplying by I except you start one row along on the abacus, by C 
is one row more.

Perhaps you may see why they didn't need a character for zero.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Computers and Roman Numerals (was: Alphabets

_(reply depth: 58)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-16T16:51:13-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ba3mgj$bik$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3ec2e6c7.155141897@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305151728.19202068@posting.google.com> <3ec4b5f6.68160950@news.optonline.net> <ba3gqr$1sf$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
For anyone interested in an existing "mainstream" use of Roman Numerals for
which there is "arithmetic" and other support, see the IBM LE Callable date
services which support "RRRR" formatting as described at:

 http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/ceea3130/B.0

Admittedly this only support I thru XII - and works in "Modular 12" (as it
represents months) so

 doing the LE Callable statement equivalent to a PSEUDO- arithmetic
statement

  "Add 1 to field-with-XII" will yield a result of "I" not "XIII"

but it does demonstrate the CURRENT use of Roman Numerals as "valid
numbers".
```

```
I think that YOU are correct in the fact that "modulo" is the correc
"preposition" - but I would be correct if "modular 12" is an adjective
followed by a noun.)  According to:

     http://www.bartleby.com/61/53/M0365350.html

"modular arithmetic

NOUN:A form of arithmetic dealing with the remainders after whole numbers
are divided by a modulus: Clocks use modular arithmetic with modulus 12, so
4 hours after 9 o'clock is 1 o'clock."

but according to:

    http://www.bartleby.com/61/58/M0365850.html

"modulo

PREPOSITION:Mathematics 1. With respect to a specified modulus: 18 is
congruent to 42 modulo 12 because both 18 and 42 leave 6 as a remainder when
divided by 12."

and also

    http://www.bartleby.com/61/59/M0365900.html

"modulus

NOUN: .... 2a. Mathematics . b. abbr. mod A number by which two given
numbers can be divided and produce the same remainder. ..."
```

```
"William M. Klein" wrote:

…[9 more quoted lines elided]…

I read Jack's reference to Modulo 12 - thought, can't be what I'm thinking of -
but the last quote above does seem to indicate it is :

Since Day One in this game, (systems, not programming),  I've always known it as
'Modulus 11 check digit'. Similarly. Modulus 97 for the check digit on UK VAT
Numbers, and Modulus 10 for Canadian SIN check digits.

What I didn't remember for sure was whether it was 'Modulus' or 'Modulous'
(Spelling confirmed as MODULUS from Websters and the OED)  - so I went
googling.:-

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Im supposed to write a report on the modulous function (im in 7th grade) and I
have no clue what in the world that is. What is the modulous function?

The modulus function returns the remainder when two numbers are divided. For
example, 3 divided by 2 is 1 remainder 1, so 3 modulus 2 (or 3 mod 2) is 1. This
function becomes useful in computer programming and various aspects of number
theory.

Good Luck!
Mr. Math
-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(reply depth: 58)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)

- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3ec2e6c7.155141897@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305151728.19202068@posting.google.com> <3ec4b5f6.68160950@news.optonline.net> <ba3gqr$1sf$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>Robert Wagner wrote:
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>code, I am not prone to doing such things. 

I am very prone to doing such things. I love programming and logic. 

>However, I don't see any 
>particular problem in directly manipulating Roman notation for arthmetic 
>except that it would be quite tedious to do so.

Tedious for the machine, not the programmer. 

>Just pick up each 'column' group in turn, revert any 'IV' form to 'IIII' 
>and string together.  Take the length to pass the carries to the next 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>is one row more.

You just described the Japanese abacus, called sorobon. Each column has four
beads and two carry bits -- one for 'five carry' and the other for 'tens carry'.

When I lived in Japan it always amused me to watch a checkout clerk adding
prices on a sorobon and, at the end, showing it to me so I could 'verify' that
the sum was correct. At first I thought they did it as a form of communication,
because they couldn't say the amount due in English and thought I wouldn't
understand it in Japanese. Then I noticed they did it to Japanese speakers as
well. 

>Perhaps you may see why they didn't need a character for zero.

Because on an abacus, a column shift is equivalent to a zero. Thanks for the
good explanation.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(reply depth: 51)_

- **From:** jallan@smrtytrek.com (jallan)
- **Date:** 2003-05-13T09:18:20-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<299f1138.0305130818.6c218a3@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3ebd5572.186196194@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305102213.463660bf@posting.google.com> <3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <b9m745$akc$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ebf01e7.295898087@news.optonline.net> <b9ocfi$bmk$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ec03239.24014939@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote in message news:<3ec03239.24014939@news.optonline.net>...
> "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
> 
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> for math. Romans tried and failed miserably with their XVCII dates.

"XVCII" is just as precise in meaning as "1502" and "fifteen hundred
and two".

The first two methods are ideographic.

The third is alphabetic, providing not only the concept but the words
used to express the concept. (The other two might equally well be read
in English as "one thousand five hundred and two").

But alphabetic sytems seem to have preferred to use ideographs for
numbers. The surviving system in Greek and Hebrew both use alphabetic
characters, but as ideographs, in which their phonetic value is
disgregarded.

People writing in Latin alphabets used ideographic Roman numerals and
then the less clumsy Arabic numerals. (Medieval texts normally present
numbers as ideographic Roman numerals, not spelled out.) Modern use of
often spelling out such numbers is part of the general movement away
from the numerous abbreviations found in text before the use of
printing.

If you claim that "greater than" is innately better than the ideograph
">", why not also claim that in mathematics "one thousand five hundred
and two" is  superior to "1502"?

"GT" or even "G" are in any ideographic when used to mean "greater
than"?

The only reason I can see for feeling use of "GT" or "G" is superior
is that because these ideographics originate as abbreviations from
English they mnemonically call to mind the English words which they
represent.

On the other hand ">" works equally well (or badly if you don't like
symbols) in any language.

"GT" works no better than "<" in any language but English and a few
where "GT" can also be mnemonically associated with natural speech.

One could easily invent a mnemonic numbering system for English, for
example 1502.6 represented by "on.fi.ze.tw;si"? Would some such system
be superior to the Arabic numerals? It is still ideographic, of
course.

Or is there an advantage for quick mental interpreation when
ideographs look like ideographs and cannot be confused with alphabetic
symbols?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(reply depth: 52)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-13T09:31:21-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b9r6kr$2hqh$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3ebd5572.186196194@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305102213.463660bf@posting.google.com> <3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <b9m745$akc$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ebf01e7.295898087@news.optonline.net> <b9ocfi$bmk$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ec03239.24014939@news.optonline.net> <299f1138.0305130818.6c218a3@posting.google.com>`

```
"jallan" <jallan@smrtytrek.com> wrote in message
news:299f1138.0305130818.6c218a3@posting.google.com...

> > That doesn't bother me because language hasn't produced an adequate
substitute
> > for math. Romans tried and failed miserably with their XVCII dates.
>
> "XVCII" is just as precise in meaning as "1502" and "fifteen hundred
> and two".

Actually, I *think* "XVCII" is *meaningless* if treated as a Roman numeral.
"CXVII" and the combinations of four characters so long as the left-to-right
order "C", "X", "V", "I" is maintained, appear meaningful to me.

So if the Romans did try to express dates or numbers as for example "XVCII",
they did indeed fail miserably, as Robert stated.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(reply depth: 52)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-05-13T17:04:34+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b9r8j2$pd7$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3ebd5572.186196194@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305102213.463660bf@posting.google.com> <3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <b9m745$akc$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ebf01e7.295898087@news.optonline.net> <b9ocfi$bmk$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ec03239.24014939@news.optonline.net> <299f1138.0305130818.6c218a3@posting.google.com>`

```

On 13-May-2003, jallan@smrtytrek.com (jallan) wrote:

> One could easily invent a mnemonic numbering system for English, for
> example 1502.6 represented by "on.fi.ze.tw;si"? Would some such system
> be superior to the Arabic numerals? It is still ideographic, of
> course.

I like the alphabet used by pilots - which includes the word "niner" for nine.
It is a modification of what the military used in WWII, modified to be more
universally pronounceable, even if we don't pronounce Q (Quebec) the way most
Americans do.   In every international airport in the world except Montreal,
pilots speak a subset of English designed to make sure they could communicate
with each other and the controllers.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(reply depth: 53)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3EC12D7B.7AD78C8D@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3ebd5572.186196194@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305102213.463660bf@posting.google.com> <3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <b9m745$akc$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ebf01e7.295898087@news.optonline.net> <b9ocfi$bmk$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ec03239.24014939@news.optonline.net> <299f1138.0305130818.6c218a3@posting.google.com> <b9r8j2$pd7$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:

> On 13-May-2003, jallan@smrtytrek.com (jallan) wrote:
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> with each other and the controllers.

Reminds me of the RAF Air Traffic Control Centre at Uxbridge (Hillingdon), which
controlled military flights, primarily over southern England, and particularly to
avoid cock-ups with Heathrow ! All military, including USAF reported in. Don't ask
me the actual lingo, but one junior controller (an airman) was advising the USAF
pilot of his 'markings', destination points (?). I was chatting to the
'controller' as the pilot came on the air.

"Foxtrot Tango one-niner three, turn 150 decimal point 5 then 456 decimal point 8
etc...."  These precise instructions went on for a few more sentences.

"OK buddy. I do *know* what a goddam decimal is !".

Jimmy
```

```
fwiw: XVCII is roman numeral nonsense.  The most similar meaningful
roman numeral sequence is XCVII, but this is equivalent to 97.
MDII is the roman numeral equivalent of 1502.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(reply depth: 52)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-05-14T11:47:52+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ec21484.101307304@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3ebd5572.186196194@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305102213.463660bf@posting.google.com> <3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <b9m745$akc$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ebf01e7.295898087@news.optonline.net> <b9ocfi$bmk$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ec03239.24014939@news.optonline.net> <299f1138.0305130818.6c218a3@posting.google.com>`

```
jallan@smrtytrek.com (jallan) wrote:

>robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote in message
news:<3ec03239.24014939@news.optonline.net>...
>> "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
>> 
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>> >> Nor do I care what you think. Hopefully another reader, perhaps a
neophyte,
>> >> will
>> >> learn that > represents more than 'less typing' or 'a question of style'.
Its
>> >> use is a linguistic issue.
>> >
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>> That doesn't bother me because language hasn't produced an adequate
substitute
>> for math. Romans tried and failed miserably with their XVCII dates.
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>in English as "one thousand five hundred and two").

The three are equally precise for expressing a single number. The superiority of
"1502" becomes evident when we do arithmetic operations on it, for example
adding 9 to get 1511. 

>But alphabetic sytems seem to have preferred to use ideographs for
>numbers. The surviving system in Greek and Hebrew both use alphabetic
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>printing.

We see that in street addresses such as 'Five Hundred Park Avenue'. We don't
normally do arithmetic on street addresses except when we compare them. In that
case we mentally convert them to numbers to figure out that 'Four Hundred' is
south of 'Five Hundred'. 

>If you claim that "greater than" is innately better than the ideograph
>">", why not also claim that in mathematics "one thousand five hundred
>and two" is  superior to "1502"?

As I said, the difference is arithmetic. 

>"GT" or even "G" are in any ideographic when used to mean "greater
>than"?
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>represent.

I don't like that notation because a single typing error can change GT to LT or
GE. One must inspect the two symbols as closely as >. 

'Greater' had redundancy built in. An error in one letter cannot change it to a
word that compiles. 

>On the other hand ">" works equally well (or badly if you don't like
>symbols) in any language.

What about the remaining hundreds of words in COBOL's lexicon? Would you create
an ideograph for each? Doing so would make COBOL as cryptic as APL. 

>One could easily invent a mnemonic numbering system for English, for
>example 1502.6 represented by "on.fi.ze.tw;si"? Would some such system
>be superior to the Arabic numerals? It is still ideographic, of
>course.

That's basically what numerals are. They started as letters.

We all know the frustration of dialing telephone numbers like 1-800-FLOWERS.
They turn us into hunt-and-peck typists. I wish they'd never put letters on the
telephone keypad. 

>Or is there an advantage for quick mental interpreation when
>ideographs look like ideographs and cannot be confused with alphabetic
>symbols?

Yes. I wish the symbols were more distinct. Some fonts make it difficult or
impossible to distinguish 1 (one) from l (lowercase L). That's a problem in file
names, where the symbol could represent either.
```

```
On 14-May-2003, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:

> I don't like that notation because a single typing error can change GT to LT
> or
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> word that compiles.

But we type in the number 123, when we meant 124.   Or we wrote HEADER-1 when we
meant HEADER-2.   It's easy enough to read >, we don't need to inspect it.


> Yes. I wish the symbols were more distinct. Some fonts make it difficult or
> impossible to distinguish 1 (one) from l (lowercase L). That's a problem in
> file names, where the symbol could represent either.

I remember trying to teach old time typists to not use l for 1, or o for 0.

But we have this problem with words as well.   Lots of ambiguity in real life.
  I try to state numbers from left to right if someone is writing them down,
don't say one-hundred-fifteen, especially when I mean 100-15.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(reply depth: 53)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-05-15T07:38:04+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b9u60i$osh$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3ec03239.24014939@news.optonline.net> <299f1138.0305130818.6c218a3@posting.google.com> <3ec21484.101307304@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:

>>"XVCII" is just as precise in meaning as "1502" and "fifteen hundred
>>and two".

Actually it doesn't.  RW probably didn't know that it was an invalid number 
when he wrote it.  RW: what number does it represent ?

> The three are equally precise for expressing a single number. The
> superiority of "1502" becomes evident when we do arithmetic operations on
> it, for example adding 9 to get 1511.

Roman numeral arithmetic is just as easy to anyone who has practiced it as 
arabic notation aritmetic is to you.  The differerce is only in what one 
has been taught.  It is also just as easy to do octal, hexadecimal or any 
other given equal amounts of training and practice.


> LT or GE. One must inspect the two symbols as closely as >.

People who have learnt to recognise the symbols do not need to 'inspect 
closely' to see what it means.  If you need to think about which <> is 
which then perhaps you should use them more, you certainly need to for C.

> That's basically what numerals are. They started as letters.

No they didn't, not the arabic ones anyway.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(reply depth: 57)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-05-16T10:09:48+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ec4b595.68064449@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3ec03239.24014939@news.optonline.net> <299f1138.0305130818.6c218a3@posting.google.com> <3ec21484.101307304@news.optonline.net> <b9u60i$osh$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <b9uaji$9et$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ec2ecfa.156728865@news.optonline.net> <ba078p$7ak$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:

>
>On 14-May-2003, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>sounding choice of the three.

Ok, preferred by me. I prefer it because:

'equals' is not standard COBOL. I don't think it's a good idea to extend the
standard unless you get something valuable in return. 

'is equal to' supplies is as a verb. But we don't need another verb because
COBOL uses IF as a verb. In English we can use other tenses and cases for is. We
can say 'a used to be equal to b' or 'a will soon be equal to b' or 'a is almost
equal to b'. In COBOL the only option is present tense is, which makes it a
noise word which supplies no information. 

'equal to' vs. 'equal' lacks the compelling arguments above. Some would say 'to
b' is counterproductive because a search on 'to b' seeking places where b is
modified will return false hits on equal tests. I use 'equal to' because it
seems more grammatical, supplying a preposition to the comparative adjective.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(reply depth: 56)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>

```
news:3ec2ecfa.156728865@news.optonline.net...

> The preferred syntax is 'equal to', not 'is equal to' nor 'equals'.

In standard COBOL, I would expect "if a equals b ..." to receive a syntax
error.  As to the others:

*The* preferred syntax, or *your* preferred syntax?  If the former,
according to which authority?   I see no such preference in any standard, or
in any manual documenting an implementation of that standard.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

```
Howard Brazee raised the same objection. Quoting my answer to him:

Ok, preferred by me. I prefer it because:

'equals' is not standard COBOL. I don't think it's a good idea to extend the
standard unless you get something valuable in return. 

'is equal to' supplies is as a verb. But we don't need another verb because
COBOL uses IF as a verb. In English we can use other tenses and cases for is. We
can say 'a used to be equal to b' or 'a will soon be equal to b' or 'a is almost
equal to b'. In COBOL the only option is present tense is, which makes it a
noise word which supplies no information. 

'equal to' vs. 'equal' lacks the compelling arguments above. Some would say 'to
b' is counterproductive because a search on 'to b' seeking places where b is
modified will return false hits on equal tests. I use 'equal to' because it
seems more grammatical, supplying a preposition to the comparative adjective.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(reply depth: 58)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-05-16T14:21:42+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ba2s5m$dhq$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3ec03239.24014939@news.optonline.net> <299f1138.0305130818.6c218a3@posting.google.com> <3ec21484.101307304@news.optonline.net> <b9u60i$osh$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <b9uaji$9et$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ec2ecfa.156728865@news.optonline.net> <ba08lv$787$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3ec4b5be.68104893@news.optonline.net>`

```

On 16-May-2003, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:

> 'is equal to' supplies is as a verb. But we don't need another verb because
> COBOL uses IF as a verb. In English we can use other tenses and cases for is.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> 'equal to' vs. 'equal' lacks the compelling arguments above.

It seems to be the opposite argument - you're picking the compromise position.
Nothing wrong with that - but nothing particularly right with it either.

> Some would say 'to b' is counterproductive because a search on 'to b' seeking
> places where b is

Does Hamlet say 'to b' or not 'to be'?

What's the question?

> modified will return false hits on equal tests. I use 'equal to' because it
> seems more grammatical, supplying a preposition to the comparative adjective.

To me, "is equal to" seems more grammatical, and certainly more English like.  
If "English like" is valued, it is the obvious choice.  If "English like" is not
valued, then the symbol "=" is the obvious choice.

But I won't claim any of these is "The preferred syntax" unless I make it very
clear who is doing the preferring.
```

```
>> seems more grammatical, supplying a preposition to the comparative adjective.
>
>To me, "is equal to" seems more grammatical, and certainly more English like.  
>If "English like" is valued, it is the obvious choice.  If "English like" is
not
>valued, then the symbol "=" is the obvious choice.

I supported the reasons behind my preference. You didn't.

>But I won't claim any of these is "The preferred syntax" unless I make it very
>clear who is doing the preferring.

I thought it was understood that all postings here are the opinion of the
author. Why is it necessary to post disclaimers like 'in my opinion' and 'in my
limited experience'? 

If we are speaking in cathedra, as WMK and Chuck Stevens do, it's usually
obvious from the tone, cites and quotations.

The rest is all personal opinion.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(reply depth: 64)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-21T08:11:08-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bag4ud$25t2$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3ec6ca9b.97736635@news.optonline.net> <baas6m$ejv$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ec9cf5f.64521958@news.optonline.net> <bae206$lln$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ecb0f5c.146451151@news.optonline.net>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3ecb0f5c.146451151@news.optonline.net...

>> Cobol
> >does not rely on 'linguistics', it does rely on mathematics.
>
> It would help if COBOL knew more about both.

Help *what*?

The rules for the COBOL language are more precise and more predictable than
that of any natural language.  Every COBOL construct is expected to mean
exactly one, and only one, thing taken in context, and that is most
definitely *NOT* true for natural languages.  What is it about historical,
comparative or computational linguistics that the COBOL language (or any
implementor's COBOL compiler) needs to know about?

I would agree, by the way, that COBOL's long-standing focus on
English-speaking cultures and American conventions in particular has
resulted in some significant shortcomings in the applicability of the
language to other cultures, but I would also assert that the standards
committee has put forth a great deal of effort to eliminate many of those
shortcomings.   I think it ought to be just as easy for a programmer to
"float" the currency symbol "Bs." when printing financial reports as it
currently is to do so using the currency symbol "$".

But I don't think that's so much a *linguistics* issue as it is a *cultural*
one.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(reply depth: 53)_

- **From:** jallan@smrtytrek.com (jallan)
- **Date:** 2003-05-14T20:14:29-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<299f1138.0305141914.286dc784@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3ebd5572.186196194@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305102213.463660bf@posting.google.com> <3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <b9m745$akc$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ebf01e7.295898087@news.optonline.net> <b9ocfi$bmk$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ec03239.24014939@news.optonline.net> <299f1138.0305130818.6c218a3@posting.google.com> <3ec21484.101307304@news.optonline.net>`

```
I will respond in one place to both your previous posts to prevent
unnecessary splitting of the discussion.

Roget Wagner wrote:
> jallan@smrtytrek.com (jallan) wrote:

>> Yet that would miss the point of why mathematical ideographic
notation
>> has increased. It is a visual language, more distinct from speech
than
>> sign-language is from speech, in which one can visually grasp
complex
>> relationships in one single view, relationships that are much
harder
>> to comprehend if represented serially in speech.

> Good point .. and perhaps a good argument for other programming languages. 
> But COBOL is modelled on English.

The exact modelling of various features in COBOL may be good or bad.
If an argument works for mathematics and other programming languages,
then one should show why that argument does not apply to COBOL.

But COBOL's long-winded style of syntax has not been copied by any
other programming language that I know of. Few see it as a virtue.

That "IS GREATER THAN" fits COBOL's verbosity more than ">" is just as
good a reason not to use it as to use it.

>>> I dispute that.The symbol > is no more (or less) precise than the
words
>>> 'greater than'.
>>
>> Ah, but word take longer to process mentally than a single symbol.

> I find words faster to comprehend. We don't read a letter at a time, we read 
> the whole word at once.

Words still take, on the average, longer to recognize than a single
familiar symbol.

In fact people read sometimes by entire phrases, sometimes by words,
sometimes by parts of words, sometimes by invidividual letters,
depending on how familiar to them the phrases, words, parts of words,
and alphabetical clusters that make up the words are.

>> Would you refer to write "greater than" instead of "GT"? 

> Yes, because a typo will be detected by the compiler.

A slip of logic which produces "IS GREATER THAN" when "IS LESS THAN"
is meant will not be spotted by the compiler.

If the programmer misspells "IS GREATER THAN", the compiler will catch
it, but normally the programmer will look no further than the spelling
error, will not recheck the logic.

I think one could make a case for "GT" and "LT" over ">" and "<", in
that the latter two are not so mnemonic and look more alike especially
to many who are slightly dyslexic (as well as those who are more
severely dyslexic).

But could one make the same case for "IS EQUAL TO" or even "EQ" over
"="? Empty little words get it the way.

>Do you agree with standard use in geometry of _d_ for "diamater", _r_
>for "radius", but argue against use of the _pi_ symbol for the
>relation between radius/diameter and circumference.

> Pi is universally known, but generally I don't like Greek letters in math.
> They look like the author is showing off or making a class distinction
> between educated and uneducated.

I don't think use of Greek letters is what separates those who know
calculus from those who do not, or the use of special characters in
HTML from those who know HTML for those who do not, or the use of
special characters in linguistics from those who know linguistics and
those who do not.

Learning such characters is one of the least difficult things about
learning those disciplines. Learning the special characters in c is
perhaps the least difficulty in learning c.

One could esasly replace symbols in c that one finds to short and
confusing by words which could be translated back into symbols by the
compiler using preprocessor #define statement.

I've never seen it done.

>> What would you use instead? What English word or expression would
you
>> subtitute?

> The letters 'pi'.

Syntax error!!  The letters "pi" mean p times i.

The use of single alphabet letters for variables and constants in
common algebraic notation is principally why Greek letters and
typographically variant  letters are used for special cases.

robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote in message news:<3ec21484.101307304@news.optonline.net>...
> jallan@smrtytrek.com (jallan) wrote:
> 
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> adding 9 to get 1511. 

Yes. Therefore ideographs *can* be superior.

But in computer languages, the computer does the mathematical work. We
don't need to line up the numbers in columns, so we could spell out
numbers in words without worrying about ease of calcuation, if
redundancy in typing is helpful.

> We see that in street addresses such as 'Five Hundred Park Avenue'. We don't
> normally do arithmetic on street addresses except when we compare them. In that
> case we mentally convert them to numbers to figure out that 'Four Hundred' is
> south of 'Five Hundred'. 

Agreed. 

Writing "500" instead of "five hundred" avoids mental conversion of
words into concept.

Similarly writing "=" instead of "is equal to" avoids mental
conversion of words into concept.

The counter-argument is that here the concept is more precisely
indicated by the the word "equal", that we have less need of a symbol.

The counter-counter-argument is that "=" and other symbols produce
statements that are shorter and more easily grasped.

In part the use of symbols in mathematics comes because of algebraic
use of letters as variables. Abbreviations were considered desirable,
but "eq" means e times q.

Of course "eq." is not equal to "eq", or perhaps preferrably "eq." ï¿½=
"eq" or "eq." != "eq".

But is an appended dot enough difference for immediately clarity?
Underling doesn't work well in hard type. I supposed some such
notation as |eq| might have been used for operators.

Instead special symbols, simpler in form than most letters, were
devised one by one, and those found most useful fell into common use.

Such symbols help distinguish operators and relational indicators from
the data.
 
> What about the remaining hundreds of words in COBOL's lexicon? Would you
> create an ideograph for each? Doing so would make COBOL as cryptic as APL. 

Why not, as a variant of COBOL?

But in fact the most general use for symbols in programming languages
is as operators. Commands and structural indicators and variables are
mostly indicated by words.

There are languages like Postscript and Lisp that make almost no use
of symbols. But such exceptions don't make a case that using symbols
is a backward step. These may be the backward languages. Perhaps their
creators just happend to share your particular (but possibly
objectively unjustified for most) dislike of symbols. Or perhaps for
the particular philosphy behind those languages and the use to which
they were to be put symbols were not the way to go.

With the age of Unicode upon us there is unbounded freedom to use more
symbols in new languages. That will certainly happen. Whether such use
catches on depends on how many people like using those languages, and
imitate their use of symbols.

> >One could easily invent a mnemonic numbering system for English, for
> >example 1502.6 represented by "on.fi.ze.tw;si"? Would some such system
> >be superior to the Arabic numerals? It is still ideographic, of
> >course.
 
> That's basically what numerals are. They started as letters.

Irrelevant.

First, Roman numerals are believed to predate the Roman alphabet,
being ideographs, I for one finger, V thumb and other fingers spread,
X for two of V (one above the other, the bottom one inverted), L
probably as a variant form for V, and C as two L's combined (one above
the other, the top one inverted).

Each later fell together with the character it most resembled in the
Roman alphabet.

The so-called Arabic numerals can be traced back to Hindu forms, but
their ultimate origin is lost in antiquity. The earliest known forms
are not obviously connected to Hindu letter forms.

In any case, the standard numeric digits associated with modern Latin
alphabets are ideographs, whatever their origin, just as &, #, @, $,
%, +, =, \, ~ and many other symbols are ideographs, whatever the
characters from which they derived once were.

Use of variables such as I, J, and K for first counter, second
counter, and third counter, are ideographic. The meanings of these
letters here have nothing to do with their phonetic values or with
words in the language.

Your original claim was that using ideographs in mathematics and
programming was step backwards. You wish not to include numbers in
this, which is fair enough.

Though numeric ideographs were not part of the original Phoenician
alphabet from which it appears all later non-syllabary alphabets
derive by direct descent or by imitation, ideographic renderings for
numbers spring up almost everywhere beside the phonetic scripts.

The Canaanite Ugartic cuneiform alphabet (http://www.jhu.edu/ice/ufu)
had a separate set of digits
(http://www.jhu.edu/ice/ufu/numerics.html).

The Old Persian cuneiform alphabet, a syllabary apparently invented
about 512 BCE, contains numeric characters separate from the syllabic
characters. See http://std.dkuug.dk/jtc1/sc2/wg2/docs/n2583.pdf.

It seems that however reasonable a phonetic script might be for normal
use, still additional ideographic symbols for numbers are devised or
adapted to go beside it.

Other ideographs are also used on occasion, but we find no case out of
the many alphabets and syllabaries where the users begin introducing a
large number of additional non-numeric ideographs for commonly used
words, despite the saving in writing and perhaps greater ease of
reading.

Phonetic notation seems to suffice for almost everything but numbers,
even when so badly out of synchronization with the spoken language as
in modern English.

But we do find many abbreviations. Using abbreviations partly
counteracts the long-windedness of phonetic notations. Books before
moveable type used abbreviations far more widely than we do today.

Sometimes a word or name used particular text will be used often in
that text yet will *never* appear in full spelling. You are just
supposed to know that "G." means "Gauuain" or "Gauuains" or "Gawain"
or "Galvain" or whatever variant spelling you feel like.

Some of our current ideographs are distorted verions of such
abbreviations and others are probably so

Yet it is significant that most of them have to do with weights and
measures and working with numbers.

Beginning in the Renaissance ideographs started appearing in
mathematics.

See http://www.roma.unisa.edu.au/07305/symbols.htm for a brief
discussion.

Why?

Because ideographs do make mathematical argument and the mathermatical
templates called forumlas clearer to the understanding, and enable a
reader to more easily follow mathematical reasoning. If not, why did
mathematicians introduce them, and then introduce more and more them,
despite the problems in publishing them properly?

The notation "5 ï¿½ 8 + 5 - 3 = 42" is less lengthy and leaps into the
mind more easily than "5 multiplied by 8 plus 5 minus 3 is equal to
42". There is further confusion possible in a notation like "ab plus b
minus c is equal to d".

Different mathematicians used different symbols, but none to my
knowledge eschewed the use of ideographs so obviously, on the whole,
despite what might be particular misuses, improved mathematical
clarity.

Can you find any mathematician who wants to drop ideographs (except
for numbers) from mathemtical notation? I am sure one can find
complaints of overuse and misuse. Style suggestions indicate, for
example, that any special symbols used in an article or long
mathematical argument should normally appear at least twice.

I would not mind find and reading something by a mathematician who
felt that mathematical notation would be improved by dropping almost
all ideographs other than numbers. It would be interesting reading.

But would you prefer that COBOL's COMPUTE statement did not used the
normal English arithemtic operators, that it used the corresponding
English words instead?

Would page on page of mathematical formula be better comprehended if
the calculations were rendered in the English words that might be used
to read those calculations aloud, except for numeric digits?

I can understand personally preferring the long forms for conditions
in COBOL. Nothing is completely open-and-shut and personal tastes
unaccountable differ.

But I don't see any justification that such forms are, in general,
innately superior to single ideographs, even in the context of COBOL.

For me they leap out from the fog of variables and keywords in any
computer language.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(reply depth: 54)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-05-17T19:18:35+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
