- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ed71bec.43474368@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <banspq$6hg$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ecfabd5.130744221@news.optonline.net> <baorcc$knc$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ed00d5d.155715291@news.optonline.net> <bapo8t$p3t$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ed0ff38.217639364@news.optonline.net> <barv8b$b50$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net> <3ed187e1.252629954@news.optonline.net> <bb01ar$re$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ed40410.415518198@news.optonline.net> <bb2gad$a7m$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ed5d4b8.43939830@news.optonline.net> <vdc6jpba6c6i25@corp.supernews.com>`

```
"Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote:

>
>Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
>news:3ed5d4b8.43939830@news.optonline.net...

>> To me, it seems clearer to say the condition you want rather than the
>condition
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>end-if

In that case the program would perform an error handler; it wouldn't continue.

>>
>> Maintenance programmers slowed down by NOT are the kind who hold their
>heads and
>> say 'I'm so confused by logic'.

>Maintenance programmers may be slowed when a program
>does not clearly implement a specification. This is not a question
>of style, nor a matter of logic, it matters not that the example is
>simple, it is whether the original programmer did their job well.

As a matter of style and communication, I put the normal or most common case
first. For example,

if  transaction-type = 'a' or 'b' or 'c'
    if  store-number not = spaces
        perform process transaction
    else
        perform reject-no-store
    end-if
else
    perform reject-invalid-type
end-if

-- better yet --

set no-error to true
perform validate-1
if  no-error
    perform validate-2
    if  no-error
        perform validate-3
    end-if
end-if
if  no-error
    *> default processing
else
    perform report-error
end-if

A 'positive thinking' program puts normal processing in the forefront. A
'negative thinking' program talks about all the things that might go wrong then,
almost as an afterthought, does normal processing if it cannot find anything to
complain about. 

Someone reading a program for the first time wants to know 'what does this
program do?'. Making him wade through exception tests before getting to 'the
meat' doesn't answer the question  clearly.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(reply depth: 76)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2003-05-30T08:09:58-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bb7hmm$65p$1@panix1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <3ed5d4b8.43939830@news.optonline.net> <3ed71bec.43474368@news.optonline.net>`

```
In article <3ed71bec.43474368@news.optonline.net>,
Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net> wrote:

[snip]

>As a matter of style and communication, I put the normal or most common case
>first.

[snip]

>A 'positive thinking' program puts normal processing in the forefront. A
>'negative thinking' program talks about all the things that might go wrong then,
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>meat' doesn't answer the question  clearly. 

How very interesting... different ways to the same end and different ways 
to a different end.

I was taught, lo, those many years ago, to start coding by Knowing Your
Data.  After you got to Know Your Data then the first condition to code
for was the most common... in order to save machine-time.  Do most of your
customers come from states for which you have to charge sales-tax on a
purchase?  Fine, early in the code gets written

MOVE G1752-PURCH-ST  TO  WS-TST-ST.
IF NOT VALID-STATE
    PERFORM 81653-INVALID-ST-RTN  THRU  81653-EX
    GO TO (current-paragraph-label)-EXIT.
IF STATE-TAX-NEEDED
    SEARCH ALL WS-STATE-TBL
        AT END PERFORM 81654-TBL-NEEDS-UPDT  THRU  81654-EX
        WHEN G1752-PURC-ST = WS-TAXING-ST (ST-IDX)
            MOVE LIT-Y                       TO  WS-CHARGE-TAX-FL
            MOVE WS-STATE-TAX-RATE (ST-IDX)  TO  WS-TAXRATE-USED.

(One might ask the need for setting a flag with LIT-Y when it is obvious 
that WS-TST-ST is 88'd... this is because LIT-Y and WS-CHARGE-TAX-FL are 
single character fields and WS-TST-ST is multi-character; a 
single-character test compiles to a CLI and a CLI is at least three times 
faster than a CLC)

(Notice also two different error-routines, one for a state not found in 
the 88s for WS-TST-ST and another for an out-of-date table of state tax 
rates... the advantages and disadvantages of such a structure might be 
reserved for later discussion.)

(Notice also that I could not help myself and coded, first and foremost, a 
negative conditional check on the state's validity... maybe I had better 
stop here... naaaahhhhhh.)

Anyhow... if the program in question is aways down deep in processing, 
after the edit programs have been run, then there's less of a case to be 
made for checking for the purity of the data and more of a case to be made 
for checking in order of frequency of occurrence; if you are generating 
offers to be mailed to the subscribers to Teen Droolers magazine you 
waste fewer CPU cycles looking for WS-AGE-RANGE-12-17 first and if you are 
generating offers to be mailed to the subscribers to Geezer Droolers it 
makes more sense to check for WS-AGE-RANGE-GT-60.

Likewise... if you are writing one of the edit programs that is higher up 
in the processing stream (or an online data-entry program) then it makes 
more sense to check for the most frequent error first... consider:

MOVE A15SSNI  TO  WS-TST-SSN.
IF WS-TST-SSN = SPACES OR HIGH-VALUES OR LOW-VALUES
    MOVE WS-NEED-SSN-MSG  TO  A15MESSO
    MOVE LIT-M            TO  A15-ACT-COD
    MOVE -1               TO  A15SSNL
    PERFORM 61528-SEND-MAP
    GO TO 71425-RETURN-TRANSID.

IF DUMMY-SSN-ENTERED
**NO MORE DUMMY SSN (111111111, 222222222 ETC) AS PER SFA 12/85)
    MOVE WS-NO-DMY-SSN-MSG  TO  A15MESSO
    MOVE LIT-M              TO  A15-ACT-COD
    MOVE -1                 TO  A15SSNL
    PERFORM 61528-SEND-MAP
    GO TO 71425-RETURN-TRANSID.

MOVE WS-TST-SSN  TO  WS-FRAMTXN-KEY.

EXEC CICS READ FILE('FRAMTXN')
               RIDFLD(WS-FRAMTXN-KEY)
...etc.

These rules, of course, were from the days when Every I-O Cost A Lot, 
Every CPU Cycle Cost A Bit Less (But Still Cost *Something*) and Every 
Data-Entry Operator Would Get Up For Lunch In Mid-Screen.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(reply depth: 77)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-05-31T10:13:21+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ed842a9.118939465@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <3ed5d4b8.43939830@news.optonline.net> <3ed71bec.43474368@news.optonline.net> <bb7hmm$65p$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

>In article <3ed71bec.43474368@news.optonline.net>,
>Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net> wrote:
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>>'negative thinking' program talks about all the things that might go wrong
then,
>>almost as an afterthought, does normal processing if it cannot find anything
to
>>complain about. 
>>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>to a different end.

I used to do it for speed; now I do it for clarity. In this case the end is the
same, but I've seen lots of Bad Code forged on the anvil of 'efficiency'.
Pre-optimization is bottom-up design .. the machine tail wagging the system dog.
We should design top-down, later optimize only the points of congestion. 

>I was taught, lo, those many years ago, to start coding by Knowing Your
>Data.  After you got to Know Your Data then the first condition to code
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>faster than a CLC)

You're bit-twiddling while overlooking better speed improvement available
through 'caching'. It is likely the state will be the same as the previous one
. perhaps the previous line on the same invoice, perhaps the previous customer
because customers tend to be clustered by geography. I would write it:

if  purchase-state not = previous-state or previous-state = spaces
    move purchase-state to previous-state
    evaluate true
        when  not valid-state
              perform report-invalid-state
              move spaces to previous-state
        when not state-tax-needed
              move zero to tax-rate-used
        when other
              search all state-table
                   at end
                       perform table-needs-update
                   when taxing-state (state-index) = purchase-state
                       move state-tax-rate (state-index) to tax-rate-used
               end-search
        end-evaluate
end-if

That's not only faster but also easier on the eye. 

>(Notice also two different error-routines, one for a state not found in 
>the 88s for WS-TST-ST and another for an out-of-date table of state tax 
>rates... the advantages and disadvantages of such a structure might be 
>reserved for later discussion.)

Yes, also thousands of city and county sales taxes. Calculating US sales tax
isn't as simple as searching a table of states. 

>(Notice also that I could not help myself and coded, first and foremost, a 
>negative conditional check on the state's validity... maybe I had better 
>stop here... naaaahhhhhh.)

I fixed that problem for you.  <g>

>Likewise... if you are writing one of the edit programs that is higher up 
>in the processing stream (or an online data-entry program) then it makes 
>more sense to check for the most frequent error first

This is a good example of sacrificing clarity by  'pre-optimizing' code that
needs no optimization. There is no point in 'optimizing' error detection because
errors are infrequent. The vast majority of transactions will pass through all
the tests. 

<ugly code snipped>

>These rules, of course, were from the days when Every I-O Cost A Lot, 
>Every CPU Cycle Cost A Bit Less (But Still Cost *Something*) and Every 
>Data-Entry Operator Would Get Up For Lunch In Mid-Screen.

If you wrote pseudo-conversational, lunch didn't cost anything. If you'd used
'caching', IO would have been less expensive because you would have done fewer
of them. If you were truly concerned about CPU cycles, you would have optimized
CICS. *It* was the 'MIPS sink', much more than application programs. I used to
front-end CICS functions and provide equivalent  functionality with 100 times
the speed. 

Bottom-up saves two clocks with a CLI while top-down makes the CICS beast run 20
times faster. My can discuss my documentation later. 

Speaking of documentation, yesterday I stumbled into this management article:

http://www.bcentral.com/articles/special/boss.asp
```

Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net> wrote:

>Pre-optimization is bottom-up design .. the machine tail wagging the system dog.
>We should design top-down, later optimize only the points of congestion. 

Plural majestatus est, Mr Wagner... please note that I was relating 
anecdotes about what I was taught 'lo, those many years ago' and not 
seeking to make recommendations of any kind.

Also please note that COBOL code has a habit of... hanging around for a 
few years.  It might just be that someone ('oh, do it For The Newbies!') 
reading here might have seen constructs *exactly* like those I post and 
wondered 'what is the reason for doing it that way?'... and such a reason 
might be found here.

>
>>I was taught, lo, those many years ago, to start coding by Knowing Your
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
>through 'caching'.

No, Mr Wagner, I am making sure that I use the cheapest, fastest set of 
instructions... every CPU-cycle costs, remember?

>It is likely the state will be the same as the previous one
>. perhaps the previous line on the same invoice, perhaps the previous customer
>because customers tend to be clustered by geography.

It is 'possible', Mr Wagner, not 'likely'... I have seen situations where 
customers are geographically grouped and where they are geographically 
random (last name sort, SSN sequence, etc.)  This is part of 'Konwing Your 
Data', as mentioned above.

>I would write it:
>
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>That's not only faster but also easier on the eye. 

Depends on *whose* eye, Mr Wagner... what is 'easier on the eye', Samuel
Taylor Coleridge or e e cummings?  It also relies on compiler-mechanisms
that were not available 'lo, those years ago' and as such I could not have
been taught it... but that's all right, I was taught that some people need
such constructs... I was taught that some folks need more than 16K of
core, too.

>
>>(Notice also two different error-routines, one for a state not found in 
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>I fixed that problem for you.  <g>

By coding it second... trying to slip it through later in the code, when 
QC's eyes are tired, obviously.

>
>>Likewise... if you are writing one of the edit programs that is higher up 
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>If you wrote pseudo-conversational, lunch didn't cost anything.

Mr Wagner, pardon my subtlety; not only was I pointing out that one *had* 
to code psuedoconversationally in order to allow for such a lunch but that 
the date-time stamp of every record had to be checked before update in 
order to insure that a lunch did not occur between the READ and the 
UPDATE.


>If you'd used
>'caching', IO would have been less expensive because you would have done fewer
>of them.

Mr Wagner, you seem to be confusing 'overall IO costs' - which might be 
lower - with 'cost per IO', which is a function of hardware.  Be that as 
it may... 'caching' costs core and core costs money, as well; there is a 
fine art to balancing the two about which Many Great Tomes have been 
written.

>If you were truly concerned about CPU cycles, you would have optimized
>CICS.

I do not know who is being referred-to as 'you' here; I do know that 
operating-system optimisation was not, in the shops in which I worked, 
usually the province of those who were tasked to write applications.  
Granted that at times there would be crossover between the groups... but 
the fundamental approaches seemed different.  Apps jockies were concerned 
with getting the users Something To Do Their Jobs With, systems jockies 
were concerned with 'bit twiddling' and trying to make the machine run 
infinitely fast with an infinitely small load.

>*It* was the 'MIPS sink', much more than application programs. I used to
>front-end CICS functions and provide equivalent  functionality with 100 times
>the speed. 

How very nice for you... not every shop I worked in seems to have had the 
benefit of your unique combination of skills and experiences.

>
>Bottom-up saves two clocks with a CLI while top-down makes the CICS beast run 20
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>http://www.bcentral.com/articles/special/boss.asp

Mr Wagner, of what is this 'documentation'... the quality of writing that 
fills in-flight magazines?

DD

- **Message-ID:** `<3ed8d114.155403457@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <3ed71bec.43474368@news.optonline.net> <bb7hmm$65p$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ed842a9.118939465@news.optonline.net> <bba3e7$c7m$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

>In article <3ed842a9.118939465@news.optonline.net>,
>Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net> wrote:
>>docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

>>I used to do it for speed; now I do it for clarity.
>
>Mr Wagner, the very next sentence here indicated what I was taught 'lo, 
>those many years ago' and 'what was written'... nothing whatsoever 
>indicates that this is what is done now.

That was not clear. I thought 'lo, those many years ago' was intended to support
its veracity. People say 'I learned long ago that ...', as though old knowledge
is better than new. 

>>I would write it:

<snip>

>>That's not only faster but also easier on the eye. 
>
>Depends on *whose* eye, Mr Wagner... what is 'easier on the eye', Samuel
>Taylor Coleridge or e e cummings? 

Coleridge didn't write in upper case. If he had, Hallmark wouldn't have copied
his style. 

Why is it ok when mainframers write in upper case, but not ok when I write in
lower case? 

>>>(Notice also that I could not help myself and coded, first and foremost, a 
>>>negative conditional check on the state's validity... maybe I had better 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>QC's eyes are tired, obviously.

Are you saying QC would reject code because it contains NOT? The pathology (sick
logic) is worse than I dreamed. Ok, I'll rewrite it without any nots, and
without evaluate. 

if  purchase-state = previous-state and previous-state > spaces
    continue
else
    move purchase-state to previous-state
    if  valid-state
        if  state-needed
            search all state-table
                at end perform table-needs-update
                when taxing-state (state-index) = purchase-state
                   move state-tax-rate (state-index) to tax-rate-used 
             end-search    
        else
             move zero to tax-rate-used
    else
        perform report-invalid-state
        move spaces to previous-state
end-if

>Mr Wagner, pardon my subtlety; not only was I pointing out that one *had* 
>to code psuedoconversationally in order to allow for such a lunch but that 
>the date-time stamp of every record had to be checked before update in 
>order to insure that a lunch did not occur between the READ and the 
>UPDATE.

Timestamp raises questions about clock resolution and multitasking. Someone
might update the record between your read and your write. Using LOCK raises more
issues .. like it tends to be slow and buggy. 

On database (at least) the technique I favor uses a SERIAL field that increments
by one on each update (via a trigger). Make that field the last one in the key.
Now an extra read is unnecessary. When you say UPDATE, you'll get 'key not
found' if it has been updated since you read it.

Finding a record (row) has been updated is not necessarily a 'start over' error.
Suppose you're doing order entry, the customer orders 1 widget, you check
inventory and find there are 10,000 on the shelf, give the thumbs up. The order
taker chats with the customer five minutes before hitting enter. Meanwhile, 20
order takers have drawn down the available inventory. You re-read the inventory
and learn there are now 9,980 widgets available. There's no need to annoy the
order taker with this information because the order is still fillable. You
simply update the inventory and continue.

>>If you'd used
>>'caching', IO would have been less expensive because you would have done fewer
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>it may... 'caching' costs core and core costs money, as well;

I was using 'caching' in a more generic sense of remembering. The table lookup
above is caching the previous lookup. IO against reference files are doing the
same thing. An IO module can be called with repeated requests for the same
record (due to closeout sale on widgets). If it already has the record, it
shouldn't do another read.  

>>If you were truly concerned about CPU cycles, you would have optimized
>>CICS.
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>infinitely fast with an infinitely small load.

I managed medium-sized shops (6-20) where I did both. 

>>Speaking of documentation, yesterday I stumbled into this management article:
>>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>fills in-flight magazines?

Everything executives know about computers, they learned from in-flight
magazines.
```

>The table lookup
>above is caching the previous lookup. IO against reference files are doing the

DD

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(reply depth: 81)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-06-01T20:19:40+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3eda3913.247576966@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <3ed842a9.118939465@news.optonline.net> <bba3e7$c7m$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ed8d114.155403457@news.optonline.net> <bbasbh$pfq$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

>In article <3ed8d114.155403457@news.optonline.net>,
>Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net> wrote:
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>>That was not clear. I thought 'lo, those many years ago' was intended to
support
>>its veracity.
>
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
>'easier on the eye', Salmuel Taylor Coleridge or e e cummings?


Coleridge is easier on the eye; cummings is more pleasing to the brain. He used
that style so the reader wouldn't be distracted by punctuation and upper case.
Many English Majors use it in informal communication, as well. 

>>Why is it ok when mainframers write in upper case, but not ok when I write in
>>lower case? 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>interpretations or their quality.

You just did, above, when you likened my programming style to e e cummings'
poetry. 

>>>>>(Notice also that I could not help myself and coded, first and foremost, a 
>>>>>negative conditional check on the state's validity... maybe I had better 
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>I have worked in shops where such things were standards, aye.

I find that sickening. Prohibiting a programmer from using logic is like
prohibiting an accountant from using a computer .. just because some LCD
couldn't figure it out twenty years ago. 

>>The pathology (sick logic) is worse than I dreamed.
>
>We have worked in different shops, Mr Wagner... perhaps you might get out 
>more.

I wouldn't work in a shop like that.

>>>Mr Wagner, pardon my subtlety; not only was I pointing out that one *had* 
>>>to code psuedoconversationally in order to allow for such a lunch but that 
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>>On database (at least) the technique I favor uses a SERIAL field that
increments
>>by one on each update (via a trigger). Make that field the last one in the
key.
>
>Database?  What kind of database is supported by a mainframe with 16K of 
>core?

That explains everything. You're still programming a 1401. 

>>>>If you'd used
>>>>'caching', IO would have been less expensive because you would have done
fewer
>>>>of them.
>>>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>it should have been so *very* obvious.

It is common for system designers to use the word 'caching' that way. They also
use 'syntax pointer' for what you would call 'current instruction', especially
in the context of a p-code interpreter. I first heard those from a Brit. 

>>The table lookup
>>above is caching the previous lookup. IO against reference files are doing the
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>manager wanted to supply the resources out of his budget.

Managers would rather write and MAINTAIN redundant code. That doesn't save
money; it costs money. Worse, it inhibits progress, such as moving to a
database. 

>>>>If you were truly concerned about CPU cycles, you would have optimized
>>>>CICS.
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>that the world is larger than that with which you might be familiar.

For the last five years, I've changed shops once a year. I've found them quite
similar.
```

for those who would rather light a candle than curse the darkness.

>

>>>>>If you'd used
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…

On  1-Jun-2003, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:

> Coleridge is easier on the eye; cummings is more pleasing to the brain.

How did you determine this latter?

> He used
> that style so the reader wouldn't be distracted by punctuation and upper case.

If so, he failed with me.  Upper case and punctuation doesn't distract when it
is our normal mode of communication.  All lower case writing DOES distract,
because we aren't used to it.

What do people remember of e.e. cumming's poems?   For most people they remember
that he doesn't use caps.   Is that his legacy?

> Many English Majors use it in informal communication, as well.

Why?

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(reply depth: 86)_

- **From:** Michael Wojcik <mwojcik@newsguy.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-10T17:14:40-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3EE64A40.7070201@newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <bbfq3u$4qr$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3eddd214.483389661@news.optonline.net> <3EDF5EBA.4000200@newsguy.com> <bbr2gf$66e$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
 > In article <3EDF5EBA.4000200@newsguy.com>, Michael Wojcik
 > <mwojcik@newsguy.com> wrote:
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
 >  become an Englishman... errrrr... Englishperson.

Jobs that require English PhDs are relatively scarce.  The field is
overproducing PhDs terribly - English departments generally get hundreds
of applications for entry-level tenure-track jobs.

I studied for and received my English and CS bachelor's degrees
simultaneously.  I briefly flirted with the idea of becoming an English
professor while I was a grad student, but it didn't take.  For me,
teaching proved to be a lot more work and a lot less fun for a lot less
money than software development.

But I do know some people who work professionally in the field.  My wife
has an English PhD (well, a PhD from an English department; it's
actually in nineteenth-century Native American rhetoric and literature),
and she's an associate professor in Michigan State's Writing, Rhetoric,
and American Cultures department, and editor of a scholarly journal,
which I suppose is about as Englishy a job as you can get.
```

For the record...<G>

People from Manchester, England:  "soccer hooligans" ...Oops, sorry,
"Mancunians".
People from Liverpool, England: "Scousers"...officially, "Liverpudlians"
People from the East End of London, England (officially born within the
sound of the Great Bell at Bow, but with the roar of modern traffic this is
down to about 100 yards; 200 years ago it was apparently several miles...)
"Cockneys".

Pete.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Multi Users (was Alphabets)

_(reply depth: 80)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-05-31T19:56:38+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ED90560.E81E873B@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <3ed71bec.43474368@news.optonline.net> <bb7hmm$65p$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ed842a9.118939465@news.optonline.net> <bba3e7$c7m$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ed8d114.155403457@news.optonline.net>`

```


Robert Wagner wrote:

>
> On database (at least) the technique I favor uses a SERIAL field that increments
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Robert,

I know you were being hypothetical in your example above - BUT - there is a real
world problem with this multi-user game.

My own experience, so long ago I can't even remember the coding. Designed/programmed
a production accounting system for a local agricultural fertilizer firm. RM/COBOL on
Radio Shack Model 16s using MS Xenix (read 'Unix').

Four lassies taking orders. First time implemented with more than one user, I looked
in horror as the system froze ! Internally 'exploding' I demonstrated aplomb by
bullshitting my way through the problem, and did a quick fix then re-compiled. I
*think* it was associated with record locking. Totally forgotten the details but the
memory stuck in my head like the proverbial to a blanket. Multi-user - a whole ball
of wax all on its own.

Multi-user came up as a topic here about 2-3 years ago. I cited a hypothetical,
based on above memory  -  Flossie is entering a bunch of invoices, many for the same
vendor. With one entry on the screen not completed, she dives off for a coffee and a
chat-fest. You know what's coming next - somebody else is also trying to access the
vendor record while she is AWOL.

Used to be a great contributor here, Ken Foskey from Sydney, Kangaroo-land - both
MVS (I think) and bloody good on OO theory, which he was studying for a late degree.
BEZOOOOM ! Ken jumps in. My theoretical, he can cite an actual case in Australia
where such a string of events shut down some 6 or 10 warehouse locations !

In your example they've got 10K on the shelf. (Hasn't that outfit heard of JIT
<G>).  Suppose they were down to 5 and take 10 orders -  Order Taker #1 is in for a
surprise if he is lackadaisical on hitting the <OK> button.

Yes - there are all sorts of solutions to multi-user, and I've now doubt you can
come up with some. Others will also probably make a case.

Just wonder - can I test others' reaction to the following. No, I'm not going to say
who, although the COBOL documentation is on the Web if you search. To overcome the
multi-user problem, Programmer X gets over it by opening a file and closing a file
per transaction. Some may react in horror at opening/closing - but it sure gets rid
of the headache.

Gotta watch even the hypotheticals - some nit picker will jump in <G>

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Multi Users (was Alphabets)

_(reply depth: 81)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-06-01T20:19:58+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ed94e47.187459274@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <3ed71bec.43474368@news.optonline.net> <bb7hmm$65p$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ed842a9.118939465@news.optonline.net> <bba3e7$c7m$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ed8d114.155403457@news.optonline.net> <3ED90560.E81E873B@shaw.ca>`

```
"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote:

>Robert Wagner wrote:
>
>>
>> On database (at least) the technique I favor uses a SERIAL field that
increments
>> by one on each update (via a trigger). Make that field the last one in the
key.
>> Now an extra read is unnecessary. When you say UPDATE, you'll get 'key not
>> found' if it has been updated since you read it.
>>
>> Finding a record (row) has been updated is not necessarily a 'start over'
error.
>> Suppose you're doing order entry, the customer orders 1 widget, you check
>> inventory and find there are 10,000 on the shelf, give the thumbs up. The
order
>> taker chats with the customer five minutes before hitting enter. Meanwhile,
20
>> order takers have drawn down the available inventory. You re-read the
inventory
>> and learn there are now 9,980 widgets available. There's no need to annoy the
>> order taker with this information because the order is still fillable. You
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>I know you were being hypothetical in your example above - BUT - there is a
real
>world problem with this multi-user game.

The problem is almost always the same -- depending on vendor supplied locking
mechanisms which don't work right. Forget READ .. WITH LOCK. It doesn't work and
can make the system hang. Devise your own. 

When it does work, it's ungodly slow. Witness IBM's lockfile. 

> Programmer X gets over it by opening a file and closing a file
>per transaction. Some may react in horror at opening/closing - but it sure gets
rid
>of the headache.

Files don't belong in application programs. They should be in 'IO access
modules'. That's apparent in the three tier client-server model, where they ARE
the third tier. 

Opening and closing the file is something Basic and other deadhead languages
would do. It's not especially slow on a decent operating system, but it's
obviously a kludge.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Multi Users (was Alphabets)

_(reply depth: 82)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-01T15:59:09-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bbdphh$7ik$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net>`

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3ed94e47.187459274@news.optonline.net...
> "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote:
>
<snip>
>
> The problem is almost always the same -- depending on vendor supplied
locking
> mechanisms which don't work right. Forget READ .. WITH LOCK. It doesn't
work and
> can make the system hang. Devise your own.

> When it does work, it's ungodly slow. Witness IBM's lockfile.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Multi Users (was Alphabets)

_(reply depth: 83)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-06-02T00:52:58+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3eda9092.269978523@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <3ed71bec.43474368@news.optonline.net> <bb7hmm$65p$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ed842a9.118939465@news.optonline.net> <bba3e7$c7m$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ed8d114.155403457@news.optonline.net> <3ED90560.E81E873B@shaw.ca> <3ed94e47.187459274@news.optonline.net> <bbdphh$7ik$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote:

>"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
>news:3ed94e47.187459274@news.optonline.net...
>> "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote:
>>

>> When it does work, it's ungodly slow. Witness IBM's lockfile.
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>with run-time performance information on it being "slow")

It was also known as "Shared DASD" under VSE. Locks resided in a disk file, thus
every access required two to the lock file.  It was the only way to share files
between two VSE machines, even when the 'machines ' were on the same physical
machine (running under VM). 

Disk manufacturers made small 'RAM disk' boxes which specifically targeted 'the
lock file market'. Later, VM provided 'virtual disks' in its memory. 

http://www.vm.ibm.com:2003/pdfs/E532up.pdf   (page 7)

http://www.gartner.com/reprints/computerassociates/89954.html

http://www.bsiopti.com/flex-es.htm

http://www.bimoyle.com/biminfo/archives/e008a01.txt
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Multi Users (was Alphabets)

_(reply depth: 82)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-06-01T21:55:19-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0306012055.5f310704@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <3ed71bec.43474368@news.optonline.net> <bb7hmm$65p$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ed842a9.118939465@news.optonline.net> <bba3e7$c7m$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ed8d114.155403457@news.optonline.net> <3ED90560.E81E873B@shaw.ca> <3ed94e47.187459274@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 

> The problem is almost always the same -- depending on vendor supplied locking
> mechanisms which don't work right. Forget READ .. WITH LOCK. It doesn't work
> and can make the system hang. Devise your own. 

I have used READ ... WITH LOCK (whether implicit or explicit)
sucessfully and without 'hangs' for nearly a quarter of a century and
on several systems.

> When it does work, it's ungodly slow. Witness IBM's lockfile. 

It has never been slow for my use.  There is a small overhead in using
shared files with locking when compared to exclusive use, but less
than (on systems I have used) any alternate mechanism such as
open/close exclusive.

> Files don't belong in application programs. They should be in 'IO access
> modules'. That's apparent in the three tier client-server model, where they ARE
> the third tier. 

Not everyone has '3 tiers', or even 2.  Small businesses may have a
separate file server or just use one machine to act as a file server.
 
> Opening and closing the file is something Basic and other deadhead languages
> would do. It's not especially slow on a decent operating system, but it's
> obviously a kludge.

You mean like Realia programmers might do ?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Multi Users (was Alphabets)

_(reply depth: 84)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-06-03T12:31:58-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0306031131.78fbebcd@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <3ed71bec.43474368@news.optonline.net> <bb7hmm$65p$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ed842a9.118939465@news.optonline.net> <bba3e7$c7m$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ed8d114.155403457@news.optonline.net> <3ED90560.E81E873B@shaw.ca> <3ed94e47.187459274@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0306012055.5f310704@posting.google.com> <c8035ce7.0306030124.397968c6@posting.google.com>`

```
charles.godwin@ca.com (Charles Godwin) wrote

> The Realia file system has supported automatic and manual record
> locking since release 6.0, at least five years.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Computer Associates

Thank you for the update. Obviously this has been a point of
discussion for many years here.  On small business systems, since
MP/M, Oasys and Xenix there has been a need for a simple and efficient
multi-user file sharing mechanism. The X/Open standard provided the
syntax for such and, it seems, it has finally become part of ANSI
Cobol.

It seems now, however, since there are inexpensive and robust database
systems, (MySQL, PostgeSQL, and many more) that many are moving to
embedded SQL for the multi-user sharing as this also allows safe
interaction with many languages.

Can you send me a copy of Realia 6 please.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Multi Users (was Alphabets)

_(reply depth: 83)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-06-04T11:35:39+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3eddcd16.482110518@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <3ed71bec.43474368@news.optonline.net> <bb7hmm$65p$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ed842a9.118939465@news.optonline.net> <bba3e7$c7m$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ed8d114.155403457@news.optonline.net> <3ED90560.E81E873B@shaw.ca> <3ed94e47.187459274@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0306012055.5f310704@posting.google.com>`

```
riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 

>> Files don't belong in application programs. They should be in 'IO access
>> modules'. That's apparent in the three tier client-server model, where they
ARE
>> the third tier. 
>
>Not everyone has '3 tiers', or even 2.  Small businesses may have a
>separate file server or just use one machine to act as a file server.

Tiers have nothing to do with machines. All three tiers can run on the same
machine. The IO tier need not run on the machine where the file resides. 
Tiers are just a packaging convention to make you structure logic.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Multi Users (was Alphabets)

_(reply depth: 84)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-06-04T12:22:32-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0306041122.103942f4@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <3ed71bec.43474368@news.optonline.net> <bb7hmm$65p$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ed842a9.118939465@news.optonline.net> <bba3e7$c7m$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ed8d114.155403457@news.optonline.net> <3ED90560.E81E873B@shaw.ca> <3ed94e47.187459274@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0306012055.5f310704@posting.google.com> <3eddcd16.482110518@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 
> 
> Tiers have nothing to do with machines. 

Tiers have _everything_ to do with machines when you _do_ have 3 tiers
of machines.  When there are thin clients (eg over WAN or VPN),
application servers  and separate database servers, then it is
compulsory to have 3 tier client/server software.

> All three tiers can run on the same machine. 

They may do, but there is no requirement to make it 'thin client' when
the user has a fat one.

> The IO tier need not run on the machine where the file resides. 

The point of doing this in a _small_ business is ?

> Tiers are just a packaging convention to make you structure logic.

Structuring software into tiers is a way of distributing the workload
and making the system scale into large or very large systems.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Multi Users (was Alphabets)

_(reply depth: 85)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-06-06T10:24:15+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ee03e4f.642191669@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <3ed71bec.43474368@news.optonline.net> <bb7hmm$65p$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ed842a9.118939465@news.optonline.net> <bba3e7$c7m$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ed8d114.155403457@news.optonline.net> <3ED90560.E81E873B@shaw.ca> <3ed94e47.187459274@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0306012055.5f310704@posting.google.com> <3eddcd16.482110518@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0306041122.103942f4@posting.google.com>`

```
riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 
>> 
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>and making the system scale into large or very large systems.

Distributing workload is rationalization. It's really about distributing
software  upgrades. When a new software version comes out, how do administrators
field it? If it lives on a single server, distribution is simple. If it lives on
30,000 machines in the field, some powered off or disconnected laptops,
distribution is a headache. That's why we have three tiers. 

Ever hear of Citrix? They added a fourth tier by moving client-side software to
central servers and turning PCs into dumb terminals. Each server supports 20
users and sells for $20K (high profit on a diskless PC motherboard). Big
companies buy hundreds of them, just to simplify distributing new releases.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Multi Users (was Alphabets)

_(reply depth: 86)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-06-06T14:41:10-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0306061341.728d312@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <3ed71bec.43474368@news.optonline.net> <bb7hmm$65p$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ed842a9.118939465@news.optonline.net> <bba3e7$c7m$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ed8d114.155403457@news.optonline.net> <3ED90560.E81E873B@shaw.ca> <3ed94e47.187459274@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0306012055.5f310704@posting.google.com> <3eddcd16.482110518@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0306041122.103942f4@posting.google.com> <3ee03e4f.642191669@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 

> >The point of doing this in a _small_ business is ?
> >
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> >and making the system scale into large or very large systems.
 
> Distributing workload is rationalization. It's really about distributing
> software  upgrades. When a new software version comes out, how do 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> distribution is a headache. That's why we have three tiers. 

Yet another RW 'definition' of why multi-tier software is used.

But I ask again 'What is the point of doing this in a _small_ business
?
 
> Ever hear of Citrix? 

Only for the last 10 years or so, when Iacobucci was building
multi-user terminal services onto OS/2. Later moved to NT.

> They added a fourth tier by moving client-side software to
> central servers and turning PCs into dumb terminals.

In which case, of course, multi-tier software is not required.  Just
put some monolithic application on the TerminalServer and run it
anywhere.  If it is using SQL the DB can be on the same machine or
another.

So, as one part of your argument you advocate writing in tiers "to
make you structure logic", Then later you argue this is unnecessary
because Citrix will make it tiered for you.

In fact SP/2 will make my software 'tiered' as well with the thin
client, when required.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Multi Users (was Alphabets)

_(reply depth: 87)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-06-07T20:21:42+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ee24057.123827445@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <3ed71bec.43474368@news.optonline.net> <bb7hmm$65p$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ed842a9.118939465@news.optonline.net> <bba3e7$c7m$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ed8d114.155403457@news.optonline.net> <3ED90560.E81E873B@shaw.ca> <3ed94e47.187459274@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0306012055.5f310704@posting.google.com> <3eddcd16.482110518@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0306041122.103942f4@posting.google.com> <3ee03e4f.642191669@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0306061341.728d312@posting.google.com>`

```
riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 
>
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>Yet another RW 'definition' of why multi-tier software is used.

No, I have first-hand experience with software distribution in large
organizations. In addition, administrators have told me that's why they went
multi-tier. 

>But I ask again 'What is the point of doing this in a _small_ business?

There isn't much point, assuming you don't want it to be scalable, and there are
significant reasons to avoid multi-tier. 

For small business, I advocate (and practice) putting the whole app on the
desktop and using ODBC (JDBC) to access data. For better performance, you can
use the remote thing provided with the database, but then you lose wonderful
tools which only talk ODBC. One of my favorites is msquery, which comes free
with Excel. 

>> Ever hear of Citrix? 
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>another.

True. But when the application is already written 2+ tier, Citrix is a
convenient drop-in replacement.

It's not painless, though. Citrix sells servers for $17K, which is pretty good
markup on a diskless PC motherboard in a funny shaped box to make it look
'unique'. One place where I worked bought 150 of them for $2M+. 

>So, as one part of your argument you advocate writing in tiers "to
>make you structure logic", Then later you argue this is unnecessary
>because Citrix will make it tiered for you.

It could, I suppose. Citrix is most often used to REMOVE the client tier. Well,
not really remove it, but centralize it for ease of maintenance. 

>In fact SP/2 will make my software 'tiered' as well with the thin
>client, when required.

I don't know why anybody uses thin clients.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Multi Users (was Alphabets)

_(reply depth: 88)_

- **From:** Bob Wolfe <rtwolfe@flexus.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-10T13:19:59+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<jimbev05vuc4a99vvetl8gtg36olfbp0cp@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<bb7hmm$65p$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ed842a9.118939465@news.optonline.net> <bba3e7$c7m$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ed8d114.155403457@news.optonline.net> <3ED90560.E81E873B@shaw.ca> <3ed94e47.187459274@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0306012055.5f310704@posting.google.com> <3eddcd16.482110518@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0306041122.103942f4@posting.google.com> <3ee03e4f.642191669@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0306061341.728d312@posting.google.com> <3ee24057.123827445@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:

>riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:
>
…[63 more quoted lines elided]…
>I don't know why anybody uses thin clients. 


Robert:

I don't believe that Richard is referring to hardware-oriented thin
client machines.  I believe that he is referring to the Flexus Thin
Client software which allows a COBOL sp2 GUI program or a COBOL
FormPrint printing program (or a combination of the two) to run
unchanged across a TCP/IP network.

We supply a server daemon to detect when a client machine requests a
COBOL program to be launched as well as the server-side and client
side components to allow the COBOL program to remain entirely on the
server and display screens or print forms on the client-side machine.




Bob Wolfe
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
When replying by e-mail, make sure that you correct the e-mail address.
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Multi Users (was Alphabets)

_(reply depth: 89)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-06-10T16:30:36+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ee60534.150270449@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<bb7hmm$65p$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ed842a9.118939465@news.optonline.net> <bba3e7$c7m$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ed8d114.155403457@news.optonline.net> <3ED90560.E81E873B@shaw.ca> <3ed94e47.187459274@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0306012055.5f310704@posting.google.com> <3eddcd16.482110518@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0306041122.103942f4@posting.google.com> <3ee03e4f.642191669@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0306061341.728d312@posting.google.com> <3ee24057.123827445@news.optonline.net> <jimbev05vuc4a99vvetl8gtg36olfbp0cp@4ax.com>`

```
Bob Wolfe <rtwolfe@flexus.com> wrote:

>robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:
>
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>>>> field it? If it lives on a single server, distribution is simple. If it
lives
>>>> on 30,000 machines in the field, some powered off or disconnected laptops,
>>>> distribution is a headache. That's why we have three tiers. 
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>>There isn't much point, assuming you don't want it to be scalable, and there
are
>>significant reasons to avoid multi-tier. 
>>
…[30 more quoted lines elided]…
>>It could, I suppose. Citrix is most often used to REMOVE the client tier.
Well,
>>not really remove it, but centralize it for ease of maintenance. 
>>
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>server and display screens or print forms on the client-side machine.

I didn't know you had that product in your line. That's the same thing Citrix
does. Their server runs NT; yours is Unix. How many active clients can a
'typical' server support? Citrix estimates 20-30, which seems low to me.

What's your selling point? Why is this a good idea?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Multi Users (was Alphabets)

_(reply depth: 90)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-06-10T14:27:26-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0306101327.70da390a@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<bb7hmm$65p$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ED90560.E81E873B@shaw.ca> <3ed94e47.187459274@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0306012055.5f310704@posting.google.com> <3eddcd16.482110518@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0306041122.103942f4@posting.google.com> <3ee03e4f.642191669@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0306061341.728d312@posting.google.com> <3ee24057.123827445@news.optonline.net> <jimbev05vuc4a99vvetl8gtg36olfbp0cp@4ax.com> <3ee60534.150270449@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote

> I didn't know you had that product in your line. That's the same thing Citrix
> does. 

No. It is _not_ the 'same thing' - unless 'everything that is not
white is black'.

VNC is 'the same thing' as Citrix - it exports the whole desktop.

SP/2 thin client just runs the application windows transparently
remotely. The panel definitions are stored at the client end, being
automatically updated as required from the server, it is only the text
fields that are transferred over the connection.

> Their server runs NT; yours is Unix. 

_And_ Windows.

> How many active clients can a
> 'typical' server support? Citrix estimates 20-30, which seems low to me.

While Citrix is where remote access was with NT 3.51 and NT 4, MS held
Citrix for ransom over access to source code and stole^H^H^H^H^H
became a partner in the technology so it bacame MS NT Terminal Server
Edition to which Citrix was gracefully allowed to add some additional
optional services.

The point being that it is no longer 'Citrix' that does the exporting
of the desktop except in really old systems.

> What's your selling point? Why is this a good idea?

TSE (Citrix) uses large amounts of bandwidth and huge quantities of
server resource to run a complete Windows service.  It is expensive
and requires CALs and NT (or later) on the client.

SP/2 is much less expensive and much more efficient where specific
applications are required.  In other words it is a multi-tier
application as against a remote desktop.

The only downside is that (currently?) SP/2 thin client requires
Windows on the client machine.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Multi Users (was Alphabets)

_(reply depth: 90)_

- **From:** Bob Wolfe <rtwolfe@flexus.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-12T13:55:24+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dpvgevk97edmeihqnln56e0tqjift3g78d@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<3ed8d114.155403457@news.optonline.net> <3ED90560.E81E873B@shaw.ca> <3ed94e47.187459274@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0306012055.5f310704@posting.google.com> <3eddcd16.482110518@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0306041122.103942f4@posting.google.com> <3ee03e4f.642191669@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0306061341.728d312@posting.google.com> <3ee24057.123827445@news.optonline.net> <jimbev05vuc4a99vvetl8gtg36olfbp0cp@4ax.com> <3ee60534.150270449@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:

>Bob Wolfe <rtwolfe@flexus.com> wrote:
>
…[88 more quoted lines elided]…
>'typical' server support? Citrix estimates 20-30, which seems low to me.

It's not really the same thing as Citrix.  Citrix is generic to any
Windows application.  Ours is specific to a COBOL sp2 or COBOL
FormPrint application.  Our Thin Client only passes a very small
function and parameter block across TCP/IP, therefore it is lightning
fast....even on low-end Windows machines.  Our Thin Client overhead,
being a non-generic solution also has a small fraction of the server
overhead of Citrix.

We not only support UNIX servers, we also support Windows servers as
well as VAX/VMS servers and Open VMS servers.  We developed a server
which also runs on UNIX System Services on OS/390, but rarely
recommend it.  The S/390 architecture is not designed to run
conversational mode programs.  A 3-tier architecture is better if your
data resides on an S/390.

It would be impossible to state a specific number of active clients
which can run on a single server, because of:

1.  Resources required by the COBOL program.
2.  Available hardware resources

But suffice it to say that we have some customers running hundreds of
concurrent clients from a single server.  Our customers who have large
numbers of concurrent users (thousands) will typically use multiple
clustered/load balanced servers.

>What's your selling point? Why is this a good idea?

1.  Run native Win32 user interface across TCP/IP.  Far superior
    interface to a web browser interface for "high-frequency-useage"
    applications.....ergo business-to-business Internet applications.

2.  No client-side maintenance.  Small client-side files can be
    installed and periodically updated by our automated utilities.

3.  It won't run Excel or Word across TCP/IP, but will blow away
    Citrix on a COBOL sp2/COBOL FormPrint program.  It is very
    fast....even on a 56kb line.

4.  The server license costs a tiny fraction of a Citrix license.

5.  Run the COBOL application on a powerful and reliable server
    platform such as UNIX and still deliver a Windows GUI interface

6.  Run the EXACT same program either in:
    A.  Windows standalone mode
    B.  Windows LAN mode
    C.  Internet/intranet TCP/IP environments
    D.  Multiple server platforms with Windows clients

7.  Run across the Internet and still utilize COM Objects in your
    program.

.....lots more.



Bob Wolfe
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
When replying by e-mail, make sure that you correct the e-mail address.
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0306101216.138d9991@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <3ed842a9.118939465@news.optonline.net> <bba3e7$c7m$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ed8d114.155403457@news.optonline.net> <3ED90560.E81E873B@shaw.ca> <3ed94e47.187459274@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0306012055.5f310704@posting.google.com> <3eddcd16.482110518@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0306041122.103942f4@posting.google.com> <3ee03e4f.642191669@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0306061341.728d312@posting.google.com> <3ee24057.123827445@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 

> It could, I suppose. Citrix is most often used to REMOVE the client tier.
> Well, not really remove it, but centralize it for ease of maintenance. 

Exactly.  You want remote access and centralised control.  The
applications are all on one central server and Citrix gives the
access.  Anyone can do this without specifically writing 'multi-tier'.

> >In fact SP/2 will make my software 'tiered' as well with the thin
> >client, when required.
> 
> I don't know why anybody uses thin clients.

Citrix _is_ thin client. You were just advocating this.

SP/2 thin client is multi-tier (without any effort), you were just
advocating this.

These are two alternatives to give centralised applications and remote
access without redesigning the systems. Multi-tier without any pain,
what is wrong with that ?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(reply depth: 76)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-05-30T14:04:11+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bb7ocr$rvk$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <banspq$6hg$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ecfabd5.130744221@news.optonline.net> <baorcc$knc$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ed00d5d.155715291@news.optonline.net> <bapo8t$p3t$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ed0ff38.217639364@news.optonline.net> <barv8b$b50$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net> <3ed187e1.252629954@news.optonline.net> <bb01ar$re$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ed40410.415518198@news.optonline.net> <bb2gad$a7m$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ed5d4b8.43939830@news.optonline.net> <vdc6jpba6c6i25@corp.supernews.com> <3ed71bec.43474368@news.optonline.net>`

```

On 30-May-2003, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:

> A 'positive thinking' program puts normal processing in the forefront. A
> 'negative thinking' program talks about all the things that might go wrong
> then,
> almost as an afterthought, does normal processing if it cannot find anything
> to complain about.

My programs don't think.  They just do as they're told.

> Someone reading a program for the first time wants to know 'what does this
> program do?'. Making him wade through exception tests before getting to 'the
> meat' doesn't answer the question  clearly.

Saying
IF  my-account < 5000
or  my-account > 5999
   continue
else
    perform y
end-if

Is just a different syntax to say the same thing as
IF not ( my-account < 5000 or my-account > 5999)
   perform y
Endif

or
IF  my-account > 4999 and my-account < 6000)
   perform y
Endif

With the advantage that it the continue is already a stub for when the business
rules change.

You're the first person I have come across who thinks the first example is
somehow more difficult to read, but I have come across quite a few people who
find NOT syntax can get hard - especially with compound conditions.  The third
example looks good, except that we are considering the 5xxx range, and those
numbers were not shown.

And if I write with the possibility of change, the CONTINUE stub works well.
```

Robert Wagner wrote:
> A 'positive thinking' program puts normal processing in the forefront. A
> 'negative thinking' program talks about all the things that might go wrong then,
> almost as an afterthought, does normal processing if it cannot find anything to
> complain about. 

I love that - may I quote you to others?

I've found the easiest way to all-but-eliminate NOT logic is to use 
88-levels, with true and false conditions named.  That way, instead of 
saying "If Not WS-File-Open", it's "If WS-File-Not-Open".  A subtle 
difference, but it makes a world of difference when you start stringing 
together compound conditions.
```

- **References:** `<3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <banspq$6hg$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ecfabd5.130744221@news.optonline.net> <baorcc$knc$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ed00d5d.155715291@news.optonline.net> <bapo8t$p3t$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ed0ff38.217639364@news.optonline.net> <barv8b$b50$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net> <3ed187e1.252629954@news.optonline.net> <bb01ar$re$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ed40410.415518198@news.optonline.net> <bb2gad$a7m$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ed5d4b8.43939830@news.optonline.net> <vdc6jpba6c6i25@corp.supernews.com> <3ed71bec.43474368@news.optonline.net> <3ED7F3D9.7060701@Knology.net> <3ed80a41_9@news.athenanews.com>`

```
"Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
news:3ed80a41_9@news.athenanews.com...

> Of course, what we REALLY need is a construct that would complement the
"SET
> 88-level  TO TRUE", i.e. "SET 88-level TO FALSE".  I believe MF implement
> this as an extension. It still requires NOT, but I have no trouble with
that
> and can't understand what all the fuss is about <G>.

ISO/IEC 1989:2002 Page 516, 14.8.35.1 General Format, Format 4
(condition-setting) explicitly allows the setting of a condition-name to
FALSE.

    -Chuck Stevnes
```

What does that do to the data?  IOW, given

  01  MyFlag    Pic X(01).
      88  Its-A         Value "A".
      88  Its-B         Value "B".
      88  Its-C         Value "C".
      88  Its-a-Letter  Values "A" Thru "Z", "a" Thru "z".
...
  Set Its-a-Letter to False

False.  If I "Set Its-A to True", MyFlag would contain "A".

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(reply depth: 80)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-02T16:42:55-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bbgne0$12a9$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <banspq$6hg$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ecfabd5.130744221@news.optonline.net> <baorcc$knc$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ed00d5d.155715291@news.optonline.net> <bapo8t$p3t$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ed0ff38.217639364@news.optonline.net> <barv8b$b50$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net> <3ed187e1.252629954@news.optonline.net> <bb01ar$re$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ed40410.415518198@news.optonline.net> <bb2gad$a7m$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ed5d4b8.43939830@news.optonline.net> <vdc6jpba6c6i25@corp.supernews.com> <3ed71bec.43474368@news.optonline.net> <3ED7F3D9.7060701@Knology.net> <3ed80a41_9@news.athenanews.com> <bbfurf$gc4$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3EDBDCA5.5010608@Knology.net>`

```
The item would contain the value specified in the [WHEN SET TO] FALSE phrase
of the VALUE clause of the 88-level item.  See Page 369 for the syntax
diagram and General Rule 17 on Page 374 for its application.  Otherwise the
SET ... TO FALSE should get a syntax error (page 518, Syntax Rule 7).

   -Chuck Stevens

_(reply depth: 80)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-02T18:45:14-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bbgnie$ula$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <banspq$6hg$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ecfabd5.130744221@news.optonline.net> <baorcc$knc$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ed00d5d.155715291@news.optonline.net> <bapo8t$p3t$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ed0ff38.217639364@news.optonline.net> <barv8b$b50$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net> <3ed187e1.252629954@news.optonline.net> <bb01ar$re$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ed40410.415518198@news.optonline.net> <bb2gad$a7m$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ed5d4b8.43939830@news.optonline.net> <vdc6jpba6c6i25@corp.supernews.com> <3ed71bec.43474368@news.optonline.net> <3ED7F3D9.7060701@Knology.net> <3ed80a41_9@news.athenanews.com> <bbfurf$gc4$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3EDBDCA5.5010608@Knology.net>`

```
You example wouldn't compile.  What you would need to code (and I think this
change will answer your question) would be:

   01  MyFlag    Pic X(01).
       88  Its-A         Value "A" When set to false is "X".
       88  Its-B         Value "B" False "Y".
       88  Its-C         Value "C" When SET TO FALSE "Z".
       88  Its-a-Letter  Values "A" Thru "Z", "a" Thru "z" When False "1".
            ...
  Set Its-a-Letter to False

(Note: different "optional words" omitted)
```

```
"Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:

The elegance is in the noise words when, set, to and is. As Bill said, in case
you missed it, "value 'B' false 'Y'" means the same as "Value 'B' When set to
false is 'Y'". The false value must be a single value, not THRU and not
multiples. Of course it cannot be in the true set. 

The interesting question is what happens when you INITIALIZE (WITH FILLER, with
or without ALL TO VALUE) the group containing the indicator or the indicator
itself. One might think they all get initialized to false. Nope, indicators are
initialized to spaces or zeros depending on pic. The 88s are ignored. Thus the
indicators could contain a value neither true nor false. 

Because we cannot say 'false low-value,  space', I always say 'false low-value'
and initialize the structure by moving low-values to it rather than using
INITIALIZE. 

------------------------------------------------

>I remember using SET to FALSE with MF, but I didn't know it was as elegantly
>implemented as your example.
…[97 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

LX-i wrote:

> William M. Klein wrote:
> > You example wouldn't compile.  What you would need to code (and I think this
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> condition.  I -still- say we should rename it "COOLBOL"...

I know Pete asked Bill which COBOL and I didn't know it was Micro Focus <G>.I
wonder if Pete was turned on and thought is was 'cool' or thought like me it was
just plain 'ORRIBLE. I think this is one of those diversities which creates
confusion. Why not :-

    01  MyFlag    Pic X(01).
        88  Its-a-Letter  Values "A" Thru "Z", "a" Thru "z"
        88  Its-A           Value "A" .
        88  Its-B           Value "B" .
        88  Its-C           Value "C" .

Move something to MyFlag
Evaluate true
    when Its-a-Letter
        Evaluate true
           when  Its-A   do A.......
           when  Its-B   do B.....
           when  Its-C   do C
           when other    do "It's not the above'
        End-evaluate
    when other do 'It's not a letter'
End-Evaluate

Again style/preference - but why get convoluted  ?????

Jimmy

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3edc0ee5_6@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <banspq$6hg$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ecfabd5.130744221@news.optonline.net> <baorcc$knc$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ed00d5d.155715291@news.optonline.net> <bapo8t$p3t$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ed0ff38.217639364@news.optonline.net> <barv8b$b50$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net> <3ed187e1.252629954@news.optonline.net> <bb01ar$re$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ed40410.415518198@news.optonline.net> <bb2gad$a7m$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ed5d4b8.43939830@news.optonline.net> <vdc6jpba6c6i25@corp.supernews.com> <3ed71bec.43474368@news.optonline.net> <3ED7F3D9.7060701@Knology.net> <3ed80a41_9@news.athenanews.com> <bbfurf$gc4$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3EDBDCA5.5010608@Knology.net> <bbgnie$ula$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <3EDBF357.3060608@Knology.net> <3EDBF808.E3648900@shaw.ca>`

"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in message
news:3EDBF808.E3648900@shaw.ca...
>
>
>
> I know Pete asked Bill which COBOL and I didn't know it was Micro Focus
<G>.I
> wonder if Pete was turned on and thought is was 'cool' or thought like me
it was
> just plain 'ORRIBLE.

I wasn't "turned on" ( I don't react that way to computer programming and
never have...<G>) but I was impressed. The reason I like it, Jimmy, goes
back into those dim twilight regions of Boolean Logic. Boolean Algebras
provide for "universal truth" (A + A') and "universal untruth" (A.A').
Therefore, logically, if we can set something to "TRUE" (universal truth) we
SHOULD be able to also set it to "FALSE" (universal untruth). This makes the
system consistent.

> I think this is one of those diversities which creates
> confusion. Why not :-
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
>

The objection is not to what you wrote above, which I believe most people
would agree is fine.(Certainly, it is pretty close to how I would code
it...<G>).

The wicket gets stickier when there is a requirement for Its-a-Letter to be
forced to untrue.

Which of these would you consider clearer?:

1. Move zero (or some other non-letter) to MyFlag.

2. Set Its-a-Letter to FALSE.

(I hope you would agree that "2" documents what is happening, whereas "1"
could mean anything.)

If you are attempting to avoid convolution, you might consider the
following....

01  MyFlag    Pic X(01).
       88  Its-A           Value "A" .
       88  Its-B           Value "B" .
       88  Its-C           Value "C" .

Move something to MyFlag
Evaluate MyFlag
     when  Its-A   do A.......
     when  Its-B   do B.....
     when  Its-C   do C
     when other    do "It's not the above'
End-evaluate
if MyFlag is NOT Alphabetic
   do 'It's not a letter'
end-if
(This has the dubious advantage of NOT requiring the "Its-a-Letter" 88
level. When these levels are required to evaluate "THRU" they can generate
some nasty code on some compilers...Also, there is a very small danger (but
not zero) that you could get trapped by the collating sequence if you expect
no characters other than letters between A-Z and a-z, on some exotic
machine...<G>) The overall code generated from the above should be less on
most platforms than that generated by your code sample (a good optimizer
would equalise it, though). Personally, I don't care about this, but some
people do...<G>.

(I say "should", because some platforms call a runtime subroutine to resolve
Class tests such as this, and if we included the code from the subroutine it
probably wouldn't be less. Like I said, I don't really care about this... )

 It is also 2 less source lines... (around 15% less code overall).

(I DO care about this; the less code there is, the easier it is to read and
the less chance of error.)

Oh shit...sorry Jimmy... d'you see why I HATE posting coding samples here
and rarely do so...<G>?

Having wandered off the point and into the vagaries of different coding
methods, I hope you can now see why I get excited about the provision of
SET...TO FALSE?

Pete.
```

…[43 more quoted lines elided]…

That comment about differences on compilers and I think this has some
association with crossing over between mainframe and PCs, EBCIDIC/ASCII etc. ?

Perhaps I've just been dead lucky :-

Radio Shack - RM/COBOL 74 (single and multi user with Xenix)
PCs              - RM/COBOL 74 (V2)
                     - MS COBOL 85 (their own)
                     - MS COBOL 85 (re-badged MF)
                     - MF COBOL 85 (DOS) V 3.1
                     - VISOC and Net Express

I've never had problems with collating sequences etc. (If I have I certainly
wasn't aware of them <G>).

You mentioned recently, but I've forgotten, your years on PCs. I can appreciate
differences with the mainframe world, but has it required a mind shift as you
worked with PCs - was it just M/F and F/J  and DOS/MSDOS and Windows, or were
there other combinations which you had to take into account ?

Jimmy

"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in message
news:3EDC1735.E3EF132D@shaw.ca...
>
>
> "Peter E.C. Dashwood" wrote:
>
<snipped previous discussion>
> That comment about differences on compilers and I think this has some
> association with crossing over between mainframe and PCs, EBCIDIC/ASCII
etc. ?
>
> Perhaps I've just been dead lucky :-
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> I've never had problems with collating sequences etc. (If I have I
certainly
> wasn't aware of them <G>).
>

You may never have used the COBOL "COLLATING SEQUENCE" clause. Some people
(not me) do, and it can achieve quite remarkable effects.

> You mentioned recently, but I've forgotten, your years on PCs. I can
appreciate
> differences with the mainframe world, but has it required a mind shift as
you
> worked with PCs - was it just M/F and F/J  and DOS/MSDOS and Windows, or
were
> there other combinations which you had to take into account ?
>

No. COBOL truly is platform independent if you stick to the basics.
("basics" can include powerful constructs like SEARCH, STRING, UNSTRING,
PERFORM...VARYING, and so on.  Just avoid "vendor extensions"...)
This was a very important "rule" for many years when I worked in the
mainframe environment. ( In retrospect, it seems a bit stupid that we
enforced this. Few people were likely to sling out their mainframe and
replace it with another vendor's, although some did (usually replacing with
IBM, due to politics and Market pressure, rather than rational technical
thought...). I guess the idea was to persuade the vendor that we MIGHT do so
if we didn't get proper service... if so, it was a paper tiger....)

I had enough years on mainframes to allow me to hit the deck running when I
went to PCs. The main "mind shift" I made was going to Client/Server and, of
course, OO and components. By the time I realised that my self-imposed edict
to avoid "vendor extensions" was no longer relevant (Source Code is becoming
irrelevant anyway), I was well into PC programmming.

With regard to programming under different PC conventions, again, I didn't
find it hard to make the mental transitions required. It's funny. Now that I
am back in my own home I keep on "coming across" various books and stuff.
Last night I found "SAMS MS-DOS Developer's Guide" published in 1986.  It
was aimed at the MASM programmer (which I used to be in those days) but had
some stuff on Structured Programming and Macros that is still pretty
pertinent today. I read it again last night  for a while, before going to
sleep, and marvelled at the details on hardware and memory, even detailed
formats of the various kinds of 5.25 floppy disk, so you could write your
own device drivers. It brought back memories of TSRs (Terminate and Stay
Resident) popup programs that hooked into keyboard interrupt vectors and
detected hot keys being pressed. I remember writing stuff to do that because
someone said it couldn't be done by "ordinary" programmers <G>.

The point is that the world of OO and Components is far removed from all
that Black Arts stuff and is incredibly simple by comparison. (Yet we still
find the majority of people not prepared to go there...)

My experience of PCs has been limited to MASM, COBOL (MF and Fujitsu) under
DOS, OS/2, WARP (very briefly when I was at IBM (they gave us free copies of
it- it was excellent, but Bill released Win 95 around the same time and blew
it out of the water with marketing hype...)), and Windows from 3.1 to XP
Pro. I haven't worked with Unix or Linux and have found no need to so far...
So there haven't been other considerations than those, that I have had to
take into account.

In closing, I'll make the predictable statement you would expect from
me...<G>

Of all the platforms and all the languages and all the Operating systems,
after 37 years of making a living from Information Technology (and
Management), I find writing components (.DLLs in COM+ format), using Fujitsu
PowerCOBOL under XP Pro, the most satisfying and interesting stuff I have
ever done. I also believe the interfacing to the World Wide Web using
JavaScript, HTML, XML, and ASP is infinitely preferable than CICS or IMS/DC
programming, or even Windows desktop programming.

So that's one man's opinion...<G>

Pete.
```

months later (if memory serves), they releases OS/2 Warp Connect, which 
only had 3 floppies...  and a CD!  It had a lot more networking stuff.

at least 10x more stable than Bill's beloved 95.

Ah well...  They say Beta was a lot better than VHS too.  :)

_(reply depth: 77)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-05-31T14:13:14+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ed88542.135991038@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <banspq$6hg$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ecfabd5.130744221@news.optonline.net> <baorcc$knc$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ed00d5d.155715291@news.optonline.net> <bapo8t$p3t$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ed0ff38.217639364@news.optonline.net> <barv8b$b50$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net> <3ed187e1.252629954@news.optonline.net> <bb01ar$re$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ed40410.415518198@news.optonline.net> <bb2gad$a7m$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ed5d4b8.43939830@news.optonline.net> <vdc6jpba6c6i25@corp.supernews.com> <3ed71bec.43474368@news.optonline.net> <3ED7F3D9.7060701@Knology.net>`

```
LX-i <DanielJSNOSPAM@Knology.net> wrote:

>Robert Wagner wrote:
>> A 'positive thinking' program puts normal processing in the forefront. A
>> 'negative thinking' program talks about all the things that might go wrong
then,
>> almost as an afterthought, does normal processing if it cannot find anything
to
>> complain about. 
>
>I love that - may I quote you to others?

I'd be honored. 

>I've found the easiest way to all-but-eliminate NOT logic is to use 
>88-levels, with true and false conditions named.  That way, instead of 
>saying "If Not WS-File-Open", it's "If WS-File-Not-Open".  

It still has 'not'. A better choice would be WS-File-Closed.

I write return codes like this:

05  my-return-code   comp  pic s9(04).
     88  no-error          value zero.
     88  fatal-error        value 100 thru 9999.   
     88  out-of-balance value 20.
     88  out-of-memory  value 100.

 set no-error to true
 if  no-error
     perform a
     if  no-error
         perform b
         if  no-error
             perform c

I don't find 'not logic' hard to understand. One way to eliminate 'not' is the
suffix '-free' (which I detest). Rather than no-error, say error-free. Doesn't
it sound bright and preppy?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(reply depth: 78)_

- **From:** LX-i <DanielJSNOSPAM@Knology.net>
- **Date:** 2003-05-31T12:29:09-05:00

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
