- **Message-ID:** `<vdhobmmdq0qvcf@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <banspq$6hg$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ecfabd5.130744221@news.optonline.net> <baorcc$knc$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ed00d5d.155715291@news.optonline.net> <bapo8t$p3t$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ed0ff38.217639364@news.optonline.net> <barv8b$b50$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net> <3ed187e1.252629954@news.optonline.net> <bb01ar$re$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ed40410.415518198@news.optonline.net> <bb2gad$a7m$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ed5d4b8.43939830@news.optonline.net> <vdc6jpba6c6i25@corp.supernews.com> <3ed71bec.43474368@news.optonline.net>`

```

Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3ed71bec.43474368@news.optonline.net...
[snip]
> -- better yet --
>
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> 'negative thinking' program talks about all the things that might go wrong
then,
> almost as an afterthought, does normal processing if it cannot find
anything to
> complain about.

What you describe as 'better yet' 'talks about all the things
that might go wrong' and thus fits your definition of 'a negative
thinking program'. In any case, the discussion, when I joined
it, was not about programs, it was about conditions and their
use to promote clarity.

The literature on structured programming discusses the
decision structure 'IF' as either selection (no ELSE) or
alternative (with ELSE). The literature does not discuss the
use of the decision structure as a constraint, that is, the
opposite of selection.

Decision structures, as a constraint, always appear in the
form of alternatives. For example,

find-square-root.
  if x < 0.0
    set domain-error to true
    move 0.0 to square-root-result
  else
    compute square-root-result = function sqrt (x)
  end-if.

Thus the condition 'x < 0.0' clearly shows the constraint on the
calculaton of the square root of a number.

There are, as I was beginning to point out, cases where a
condition, expressed as a constraint, will have no proceesing
in the 'true' branch; that is,

if constraint-condition
  continue
else
  process
end-if

It is my opinion that constraints should not be translated to
selection because the intent of the condition is less clear.

Given the constraint,
"Never argue with a neurotic." -- Wayne Dyer.

The direct implementation is

if neurotic continue else argue end-if.

The translation becomes

if not neurotic argue end-if.

Which means

"Always argue with non-neurotics."

In other words, something is lost in the translation, the original
meaning is distorted, and the resulting code is less clear in
showing the intent to the 'next programmer'.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(reply depth: 77)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-05-31T21:15:11+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ed917a5.173471046@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <banspq$6hg$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ecfabd5.130744221@news.optonline.net> <baorcc$knc$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ed00d5d.155715291@news.optonline.net> <bapo8t$p3t$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ed0ff38.217639364@news.optonline.net> <barv8b$b50$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net> <3ed187e1.252629954@news.optonline.net> <bb01ar$re$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ed40410.415518198@news.optonline.net> <bb2gad$a7m$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ed5d4b8.43939830@news.optonline.net> <vdc6jpba6c6i25@corp.supernews.com> <3ed71bec.43474368@news.optonline.net> <vdhobmmdq0qvcf@corp.supernews.com>`

```
"Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote:

>Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message

>> A 'positive thinking' program puts normal processing in the forefront. A
>> 'negative thinking' program talks about all the things that might go wrong
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>thinking program'.

You're right. I should have written:

 perform validate-input
 if  no-error
     *> default processing
 else
    perform report-error
 end-if

>In any case, the discussion, when I joined
>it, was not about programs, it was about conditions and their
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
>calculaton of the square root of a number.

Why not write it as selection?

move zero to square-root-result
if x > 0
   compute square-root-result = sqrt (x)
end-if

>There are, as I was beginning to point out, cases where a
>condition, expressed as a constraint, will have no proceesing
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>end-if

I recently wrote a program that added a column of numbers and compared the sum
to a control total, rejecting the ones that did not match. The vast majority did
match, in which case there was nothing to do except exit. In order to
communicate that to the next programmer, I wrote:

if actual-amount = control-amount      *> balancing decision 
   continue
else
   if  control-amount = zero
       display 'control is missing'
   else
       set out-of-balance to true
   end-if
end-if

You would say the constraint is equality, which would produce else continue.
Most people would leave off else continue. The model you cite would require it
to be written so the next programmer knows the if was a constraint rather than a
selection.

I like the looks of the = on the first line. It communicates that the goal has
been reached AND that equality is the normal, most frequenet case. 

What does the literature say about case statements? The above could be written:

evaluate control-amount
    when = actual-amount         *> in balance
        continue
    when = zero                       *> cannot tell
        display 'control is missing'
    when other                         *> out of balance
        set out-of-balance to true
end-evaluate

>It is my opinion that constraints should not be translated to
>selection because the intent of the condition is less clear.

Suppose the constraint is 'find one between 18 and 22 years old'. I would write

if age >= 18 and <= 22
   perform courtship
else
   perform polite-goodbye
end-if

You would write:

if  age < 18 or > 22
    perform polite-goodbye .....

I feel my version better communicates the constraint and goal.

>Given the constraint,
>"Never argue with a neurotic." -- Wayne Dyer.
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>showing the intent to the 'next programmer'.

That's because natural language has an aversion to 'not'. As programming
statements, both do exactly the same thing. 

This appears to be a conflict between English and program structure. The
literature, while calling itself 'structured programming', is taking the side of
natural language whereas I use the structure of the if statement to communicate,
"this is the normal case".

Alternative hypothesis -- in the old days, programmers would write

if  age < 18 or > 22
    perform polite-goodbye
    go to the-exit.
perform eye-contact
perform smile
....

Perhaps designers of structured programming thought their paradigm would be more
palatable if it followed established patterns. 'See, all ya gotta do is replace
the go to with an else. Isn't that easy?'
```

```

Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3ed917a5.173471046@news.optonline.net...
> "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote:
>
> >Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
[snip]
> >In any case, the discussion, when I joined
> >it, was not about programs, it was about conditions and their
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
> end-if

Go ahead, it gives the same 'square-root-result'.

It does not identify the constraint as 'no negative numbers'.
There is the possibility that the 'next programmer' when
adding exception processing will fail to change 'x > 0' to
'x >= 0' thus improperly reporting '0.0' as a domain error.
In my opinion, the absence of the constraint suggests
the programmer did not do enough to make certain the
code will not fail.

>
> >There are, as I was beginning to point out, cases where a
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> I recently wrote a program that added a column of numbers and compared the
sum
> to a control total, rejecting the ones that did not match. The vast
majority did
> match, in which case there was nothing to do except exit. In order to
> communicate that to the next programmer, I wrote:
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> You would say the constraint is equality, which would produce else
continue.
> Most people would leave off else continue. The model you cite would
require it
> to be written so the next programmer knows the if was a constraint rather
than a
> selection.

No, a constraint is a pre-condition for a process. I would
call the above a post-condition check on a process. As I wrote
above,'There are ... cases where ... a constraint, will have
no proceesing in the 'true' branch' . I did not say that every
case where there is 'no processing in the 'true' branch' is a
constraint.

>
> I like the looks of the = on the first line. It communicates that the goal
has
> been reached AND that equality is the normal, most frequenet case.
>
> What does the literature say about case statements? The above could be
written:
>
> evaluate control-amount
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> end-evaluate

The literature says what it has always said about case
statements.What I wrote was 'The literature [on structured
programming] does not discuss the use of the decision
structure as a constraint'. This same comment applies
to the 'case' structiure.

>
> >It is my opinion that constraints should not be translated to
> >selection because the intent of the condition is less clear.
>
> Suppose the constraint is 'find one between 18 and 22 years old'. I would
write
>
> if age >= 18 and <= 22
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> end-if

This is not a constraint, it is an alternative. There is no
constraint on age because the process will produce a
correct result, regardless of age.

>
> You would write:
>
> if  age < 18 or > 22
>     perform polite-goodbye .....

No, I would not write that, at all; because there is no
constraint to check.

>
> I feel my version better communicates the constraint and goal.
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> statements, both do exactly the same thing.

The result may be the same, what is communicated is
different. And that is my point.

>
> This appears to be a conflict between English and program structure. The
> literature, while calling itself 'structured programming', is taking the
side of
> natural language whereas I use the structure of the if statement to
communicate,
> "this is the normal case".

You failed to recognize what was said and by whom. Once
again, the literature on structured programming does not
discuss the use of decision structures for showing constraints.
Thus, when *I* discuss constraints, *I* am not referring to
the literature on structured programming; and when *I*
expressed the opinion that 'constraints should not be
translated to selection' that too did not refer to the literature
on structured programming.

When *I* gave the example and explanation, *I* did so to
support *my* opinion. *I* assure you *I* did not take the 'side
of natural language'. If you had understood the difference
between constraint and selection, the example would have
reinforced the idea that when a condition is a 'constraint' it
implies 'never' and when 'selection' implies 'always'; and
there may be times when the 'next programmer' may have
difficulty converting 'always' to 'never' because the original
meaning was distorted.

>
> Alternative hypothesis -- in the old days, programmers would write
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> Perhaps designers of structured programming thought their paradigm would
be more
> palatable if it followed established patterns. 'See, all ya gotta do is
replace
> the go to with an else. Isn't that easy?'

I read 'all ya gotta do is' and imagine DD having a good
laugh and that too makes me smile.

The 'designers of structured programming' moved into an
area called 'formal methods' where such things as constraints,
pre-conditions, and post-conditions are discussed. The goal
has not changed, it is still to attempt to 'prove program
correctness'.
```

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:

>
>On 26-May-2003, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:
>
>> >and of course there is no COBOL "alphabetic word" to accomplish what **
does.
>>
>> Yes there are:
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>Depending on the compiler, results can be different.

In the abstract, they are identical. I was rebutting the assertion there is no
"alphabetic word".  If you want to talk about practicality, how are we to know
which is more accurate?  Should we calibrate our programs to compare results
against known values, then choose the most accurate one? That would defeat the
convenience. 

For my purposes, all are overkill. If I needed ultra-precision (can't imagine
why), I'd compute the log myself with a power series.
```

```
Robert Wagner wrote:

>>Tell us, Robert, what exactly you do that makes _your_ way so superior.
> 
> It's not my way, it is the norm. When someone proposes an alternative, the
> burden of proof is on him to show that it is better.

Well that is strange, I find the norm to be transforming < to >=.

As yours is the alternate (< to not <) then perhaps it is you that should 
bear the burden of proof.

(hint: the two are exactly equivalent and both are used as 'the norm').
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(reply depth: 69)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-05-26T18:34:13+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ed25b36.306739042@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <baorcc$knc$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ed00d5d.155715291@news.optonline.net> <bapo8t$p3t$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ed0ff38.217639364@news.optonline.net> <bas2ve$oje$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>Robert Wagner wrote:

>>>Tell us, Robert, what exactly you do that makes _your_ way so superior.
>> 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>Well that is strange, I find the norm to be transforming < to >=.

Math deals with variables named a, b, etc. For example, not (a or b) = not a and
not b. The content of the variable might be a relational test such a = (x < y).
Manipulations do not change the inner content of variables, only the
relationship between them. Thus, the negative of (x < y) is not (x < y) .. on
the first pass. 

On the second pass, we want to simplify by removing parentheses. You would say
x>=y; I would say x not < y. They are almost equally valid. I say almost because
you cannot do the same with not (x = y). It becomes x not = y. 

To my thinking, a single method which works in all cases is better than two
methods.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(reply depth: 70)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-05-27T08:34:46+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<battoi$hfh$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <bapo8t$p3t$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ed0ff38.217639364@news.optonline.net> <bas2ve$oje$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ed25b36.306739042@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:


> To my thinking, a single method which works in all cases is better than
> two methods.

I agree that it would be nice if Cobol had <> or !=.

When I do transforms in Python or C/C++ or other I do <  to >= and > to <= 
etc. !< and !> are not available. I also do = to !=.  It would be nice to 
have a single method so I do it that way in Cobol except the ! has to use 
not.

Now 30 years ago >= and <= were not available in Cobol so you were forced 
to use not < and not >.  Obviously that is what you are most familiar with. 
More recent programmers never had that 30 year old restriction and think 
that changing the operator is more natural, is 'the norm'.

If you use Cobol and C (as you said you did, but I have my doubts) then you 
have to use two methods - I use one (with a minor annoyance).
```

- **References:** `<3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <3ed0ff38.217639364@news.optonline.net> <bas2ve$oje$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ed25b36.306739042@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net> wrote:
>Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>Math deals with variables named a, b, etc.

Mr Wagner, this is wrong.  It is the equivalent of saying 'carbon is 
diamond'.

While a *form* of carbon (allotropic) is diamond not all forms of carbon 
are diamonds; likewise while a *branch* (or 'department') of mathematics 
(algebra, or 'the restoration/compensation/bone-setting') deals with 
variables.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(reply depth: 71)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-05-28T01:57:05+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ed410ee.418812536@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <3ed0ff38.217639364@news.optonline.net> <bas2ve$oje$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ed25b36.306739042@news.optonline.net> <bavir7$58r$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

>In article <3ed25b36.306739042@news.optonline.net>,
>Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net> wrote:
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>variables.

I was referring to logical algebra aka Boolean algebra aka propositional
calculus. I'm aware there are other branches of math which deal with logic, some
of which use esoteric notation and unfamiliar concepts. (I once hired a PhD who
was a guru in the subject.)

This knowledge has little or no practical use in programming  (unless you can
show us the light). For practical purposes, in a statement like 'a OR b' the
variable a can be a relational such as x<y, a boolean variable or a function
call that returns a boolean variable. It doesn't matter because transformations
are performed on the OR, not the 'contents' of a. 

Now programming language designers tell us to monkey with the contents of a
rather than use NOT. Why? Because 'Geez, I find NOT sooo confusing.' 

Are we using a coherent system or are we pandering to the lowest common
denominator?
```

```
"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3ed00d5d.155715291@news.optonline.net...
> docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> He doesn't know how to transform logical statements. If he did, he'd
understand
> why not < is superior to >=
>
> I'm not concerned with the casual reader. I'm concerned with the
professional
> programmer who maintains the code. I don't think Boolean algebra
proficienty is
> asking too much from a 'professional'.
>

I know how to transform logical statements.  I see nothing "superior" in
        not <
            over
        >=

In fact, I find that there are many reasons why avoiding "not" (negative)
logic is a "good" idea.  Not least of all is the impact of "not" in combined
abbreviated conditions (as discussed in other threads).  This is NOT <G> to
say, that I avoid "not" in COBOL completely, simply that I find nothing
"universally" superior in its usage over the perfectly valid (since '85)
">=" and "<=" relational operators.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(reply depth: 69)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-28T10:14:27-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bb2qpk$9uv$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <3eceb57b.67668169@news.optonline.net> <banspq$6hg$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ecfabd5.130744221@news.optonline.net> <baorcc$knc$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ed00d5d.155715291@news.optonline.net> <3ed3b463.336246536@news.cis.dfn.de> <bb0h3r$9jg$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ed4db52.62859927@news.cis.dfn.de>`

```

"Tukla Ratte" <tukla_ratte@tukla.net> wrote in message
news:3ed4db52.62859927@news.cis.dfn.de...

> What if I said that I find "if !(a > b)" less readable than
> "if (a <= b)" in C?

This is not to Tukla Ratte" but to the general readership, and is not to be
taken as criticism of his entries.  I am using his response strictly as a
starting-point example of my point.

For me, the biggest issue I've seen in this forum lately has been the
semantic difference among the following:

(a)  I find "if !(a>b)" less readable than "if (a <= b)".
(b)  "If !(a>b)" strikes me as less readable than "if (a <= b)".
(c)  "If !(a>b)" is less readable than "if (a <= b)".
(d)  "If !(a>b)" is always less readable than "if (a <= b)".
(e)  Everybody thinks "if !(a>b)" is less readable than "if (a <= b)".
(f)  Nobody with half a brain could conclude that "if !(a>b)" might be no
less readable than "if (a <= b)".
(g)  No real programmer would ever code "if !(a>b)" instead of "if (a <=
b)".
(h)  Nobody ever codes "if !(a>b)"; "if (a <= b)" is always used.

As far as I'm concerned, only (a) and (b) are indisputable as fact.

Most of the rest can be shown to be false statements by the very existence
of a single exception.  In the case of some, it is possible to claim truth
((f) and (g), in particular) by redefining the ad-hominem characterizations.

Such has been the tenor of many of the discussions in this forum of late,
much to my dismay.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(reply depth: 64)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-05-25T12:56:46+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bap4b6$gto$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <3eceb57b.67668169@news.optonline.net> <banspq$6hg$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ecfabd5.130744221@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:

>>If you find >= superior to not <, you've destroyed your credability in
>> re logic.


> I already have .. in a post showing how to mechanically invert a complex
> logical statement by changing '<' to 'not <' and 'not <' to '<'.
> 
> If we were required to change '<' to '>=', the transformation would be
> more prone to error.

You would have to agree that changing '< to >='  is equally valid in doing 
a mechanical transformation.  The result is equivalent.

Given that it is equivalent then why would it 'destroy credability re 
_logic_' ?

Now you also have to justify your claim to this form being 'more error 
prone' for this is just a simple unsupported assertion.

What is 'less error prone' is what one is used to doing, if he is more 
experienced in transforming using >= (which is equivalent) then he will be 
less error prone in continuing to do it that way.

It may well be true that *you* would make more errors if required to use >=.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(reply depth: 65)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-05-26T01:49:53+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ed10308.218615631@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <3eceb57b.67668169@news.optonline.net> <banspq$6hg$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ecfabd5.130744221@news.optonline.net> <bap4b6$gto$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>Robert Wagner wrote:
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>a mechanical transformation.  The result is equivalent.

True, but COBOL lacks symmetry by omitting '<>'. We are asked to transform '='
by one set of rules and '>' by another. Adding and deleting NOT provides a
single single mechanism which works in all cases. 

Moreover, if one is using symbols for relational operators, it's easy to
remember the rule 'change the words, leave the symbols alone.'

>Given that it is equivalent then why would it 'destroy credability re 
>_logic_' ?

Because you are changing relational operators just to avoid the word NOT. You
still need to use NOT on 88 conditions. It is better to use a uniform rule
throughout.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(reply depth: 66)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-25T21:54:01-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<barvki$p59$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <3eceb57b.67668169@news.optonline.net> <banspq$6hg$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ecfabd5.130744221@news.optonline.net> <bap4b6$gto$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ed10308.218615631@news.optonline.net>`

```
"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3ed10308.218615631@news.optonline.net...
> Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:
<snip>
> >
> >You would have to agree that changing '< to >='  is equally valid in
doing
> >a mechanical transformation.  The result is equivalent.
>
> True, but COBOL lacks symmetry by omitting '<>'. We are asked to transform
'='
> by one set of rules and '>' by another. Adding and deleting NOT provides a
> single single mechanism which works in all cases.

Interestingly enough both the (last) CODASYL JOD and the current Micro Focus
implementations (and possibly others) *DO* support
     <>
as an equivalent to
    Not =

I believe that adding this to the Standard is on the candidate list for the
next Standard - but I won't swear to it.

*PERSONALLY* I find that it is fairly "infrequent"programming task when I
need" to "reverse" the logic of an existing relational operator.  When I do
have such a need I "understand" that reversing:

   >  can be done by either NOT > *or* by =<
  <  can be done by either NOT <  *or* by =>
  = can be done either by NOT = *or* by  ... < OR >  (compound expression)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(reply depth: 67)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-05-26T06:20:23+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ed18f53.254536197@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <3eceb57b.67668169@news.optonline.net> <banspq$6hg$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ecfabd5.130744221@news.optonline.net> <bap4b6$gto$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ed10308.218615631@news.optonline.net> <barvki$p59$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote:

>"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
>news:3ed10308.218615631@news.optonline.net...
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
>need" to "reverse" the logic of an existing relational operator.  

I do it several times every day. My programs are logic intensive. They are
making complex decisions.

>When I do
>have such a need I "understand" that reversing:
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>  = can be done either by NOT = *or* by  ... < OR >  (compound expression)

The rule is 'leave parentheses alone'. By adding an OR (your last case) messes
that up. 

if  a = b or c = d
    continue
else
    do something

becomes

if  a < b or a > b and c < d or c > d
    do something

but that evaluates as

if  (a < b) or (a > b and c < d) or (c > d)

which is not what we intended.  

If J4 does add <>, it will probably also add the wordy version: LESS OR GREATER.
Most will find that confusing because the OR is a 'pseudo-OR' which doesn't
follow the precedence rules for a 'regular OR'. 

I avoid >= and <=. In a quest for clarity (to the logic-challenged), they have
created more confusion than clarity.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(reply depth: 68)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-05-27T14:00:44-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0305271300.3e705d95@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <3eceb57b.67668169@news.optonline.net> <banspq$6hg$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ecfabd5.130744221@news.optonline.net> <bap4b6$gto$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ed10308.218615631@news.optonline.net> <barvki$p59$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net> <3ed18f53.254536197@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 

> >*PERSONALLY* I find that it is fairly "infrequent"programming task when I
> >need" to "reverse" the logic of an existing relational operator.  
> 
> I do it several times every day. 

Why can't you get it right the first time ?

> If J4 does add <>, it will probably also add the wordy version: LESS OR
> GREATER.

It is just a symbol: a symbol for 'not equal'.

> Most will find that confusing because the OR is a 'pseudo-OR' which doesn't
> follow the precedence rules for a 'regular OR'. 

_I_ never read it as 'less or greater'.  Just as I don't read '&&' as
'ampersand ampersand' it is 'and' or '||' as 'bar bar', it is 'or'.
 
> I avoid >= and <=. In a quest for clarity (to the logic-challenged), they have
> created more confusion than clarity.

Well maybe _you_ get confused, but I never find that. I don't convert
to words then read that, I just understand the relationship by looking
at the symbol (it is a single symbol formed by 2 characters), 'or'
doesn't enter at all.
```

```
Robert Wagner wrote:

> Moreover, if one is using symbols for relational operators, it's easy to

>>Given that it is equivalent then why would it 'destroy credability re

> rule throughout.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(reply depth: 67)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-05-26T18:34:16+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ed25b7f.306812749@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <banspq$6hg$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ecfabd5.130744221@news.optonline.net> <bap4b6$gto$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ed10308.218615631@news.optonline.net> <bas3j1$otd$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>Robert Wagner wrote:
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>Now why was it that you avoid using them ?

Because they're hard to type on a keypunch machine. 

>>>Given that it is equivalent then why would it 'destroy credability re
>>>_logic_' ?
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>rote rule that you need to use is ?

I change AND, OR and NOT; leave everything else alone. Little thinking required
means low risk. 

>'change the words, leave the symbols', doesn't work in other languages that 
>I use, nor does adding 'not'.  The mechanisms that I use are more general, 
>is that inferior or superior ?

Logic works the same in all languages. Granted, the operators are symbols rather
than words, but it's not hard to distinguish between relationship tests and
logical operations. 

Adding 'not' works in every language I've seen. It's just that some don't let
you remove the parentheses. You're left with !(x < y) because the language
doesn't offer !<.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

```
Robert Wagner wrote:


> Adding 'not' works in every language I've seen. It's just that some don't
> let you remove the parentheses. You're left with !(x < y) because the
> language doesn't offer !<.

Well _I_ am not left with !( x < y ), I am left with ( x >= y ).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(reply depth: 64)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-05-27T15:38:43+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bb00q3$hf$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <bgakcv8rmdv6l7fo3p32s53l3fnf3c08j1@4ax.com> <3eceb57b.67668169@news.optonline.net> <banspq$6hg$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ecfabd5.130744221@news.optonline.net>`

```

On 24-May-2003, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:

> I already have .. in a post showing how to mechanically invert a complex
> logical statement by changing '<' to 'not <' and 'not <' to '<'.

Didn't you state that combining words and symbols is to be avoided?   If so,
">=" would be preferable to "not <", unless things have changed between that
argument and this.

> If we were required to change '<' to '>=', the transformation would be more
> prone to error.

Certainly editing existing working code to fit whatever style you prefer is a
process prone to error.

But if you were told to use one style or the other to begin with, they are
equivalent.
```

- **References:** `<3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <bgakcv8rmdv6l7fo3p32s53l3fnf3c08j1@4ax.com> <3eceb57b.67668169@news.optonline.net> <banspq$6hg$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ecfabd5.130744221@news.optonline.net> <bb00q3$hf$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ed4160c.420122506@news.optonline.net>`

```
On 27-May-2003, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:

> >Certainly editing existing working code to fit whatever style you prefer is a
> >process prone to error.
>
> My 'COBOL beaurifier' can do it in a flash, without error.

Depending on what changes you want.   I bet there are changes it cannot do by
itself.

Of course, when the changes are purely cosmetic, whatever change control
software you use will show up cosmetic changes as well as real ones.   That will
make it harder for some maintenance programmer to determine what changes were
significant for your current project, slowing down maintenance when he needs to
change it again when requirements change.
```

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 

> I didn't say *I* had difficulty reading symbols; I said 'one', the generic
> reader, might. 

'One' is first person inclusive and singular.  It is used to indicate
'we' (inclusive) but individually one at a time. And you didn't say
'might', you said 'must'.

Given that you provided no evidence that this was anything other than
your own personal experience, what leads you to believe that anyone
else has the same problem ?

If you don't have this problem then we must conclude that the argument
you presented is another arbitrary, superficial and irrelevant attempt
at supporting your _persoanl_preference_ for using words instead of
symbols.

No one minds if you do use whatever mechanisms you feel comfortable,
but I object to your pseudo-intellectual attempts at convincing others
that they should do so.


>> I find the equivalent conditional "IF A >= B" immediately readable.

> If you find >= superior to not <, you've destroyed your credability in 
> re logic.

Yet another pseudo-intellectual attempt.  In what way has he
'destroyed his credability' ?  In what way do you consider your
opinions to be superior ?

You are also, once again, making the wrong argument.  His claim was to
what he found _more_readable_.  The whole basis of this discussion was
to how readable the code is.  Arguing against his actual experience
indicates that you would prefer him to use a form _less_ readable to
him, as long as it is _your_ preference.

> This is a COBOL forum. Predilections carried forward from other languages
> offering no choice aren't very convincing here. 

You say this, presumably, because the result wouldn't agree with your
own preferences.  I suspect that you would accept snippets from other
languages, but arbitrarily, only if they supported your views.

For example Python only has 'and' and 'or' and no symbols for these.

Actually I find that keeping my style consistent with other languages
helps, not only when I switch languages, but also to others who may
read my code and may have been taught others.

> More intelligent than I?? 

You seem surprised that anyone _could_ be  ;-)

> >Besides, *your* public record shows your ignorance of linguistic
> >development, both natural and computer; your limited knowledge of
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> You're repeating what others have said. Please speak with your own voice.

I felt that he had made an independent assessment based on his reading
of two threads.  The fact that he came to similar general conclusions
as many others here should tell you something Robert.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(reply depth: 65)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-05-26T03:17:07+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ed173cd.247489374@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <3eceb57b.67668169@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305241415.75c678b5@posting.google.com> <3ed07096.181119932@news.optonline.net> <bar7q5$d6r$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>Robert Wagner wrote:

>In what way is 'not <' of "higher quality" than '>=' ?
>
>You are arguing "by adjective rather than exposition".

I answered this several times. Once more:

..You cannot put NOT in from of >=
..It introduces a pseudo-OR which is likely to confuse people
..The main reason: when negating a conditional, you are forced to change a
relational operator. If you avoid >=, all changes are to logical operators. It's
easy to remember 'change the words, leave the symbols alone'.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(reply depth: 66)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-05-26T20:42:57+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bask1l$vn9$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <3ed07096.181119932@news.optonline.net> <bar7q5$d6r$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ed173cd.247489374@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:

>>In what way is 'not <' of "higher quality" than '>=' ?
> 
> I answered this several times. Once more:
> 
> ..You cannot put NOT in from of >=

I did not see you post that before.  Not using 'not' is a _benefit_ of 
using >= as a transformation of < (and vv).

> ..It introduces a pseudo-OR which is likely to confuse people

Some find 'not' to be confusing, as 'not <' and '>=' are equivalent and 
avoiding 'not' may reduce confusion then this is no reason to avoid '>='.

In fact we have evidence here that '>=' is more readily understood than 
'not <' so your point is null.

> ..The main reason: when negating a conditional, you are forced to change a
> relational operator. 

When negating a conditional one follows a set of rules.  Being 'forced' to 
do something depend entirely on what is in the rules.  Yours is not the 
only set that works.  It seems that yours _forces_ the use of 'not'.  Bad 
juju.

> If you avoid >=, all changes are to logical
> operators. It's easy to remember 'change the words, leave the symbols
> alone'.

Yet you were advocating using words for the conditionals.

It seems that you advocate conflicting styles, or at least arbitrary ones.

As many use several languages, and your rule will not apply to other 
languages, then it may be better to learn something more substancial than a 
jingle.

 
While you may well find that your reasons work for you, others find that 
what they do is better for them.  As both sides can give equal weight to 
their points then your claim of superiority for what is a personal choice 
is not substanciated.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(reply depth: 67)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-05-26T18:34:24+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ed25ced.307178045@news.optonline.net>`

```
Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>Robert Wagner wrote:

>> ..It introduces a pseudo-OR which is likely to confuse people
>
>Some find 'not' to be confusing, as 'not <' and '>=' are equivalent and 
>avoiding 'not' may reduce confusion then this is no reason to avoid '>='.

As I've said, logic is the foundation of programming. People who have trouble
with 'not' should learn logic or find a more suitable line of work. 

How would you feel if you hired a carpenter to build a house and observed him
gluing the lumber together? When asked why he didn't use nails, his answer was
he could never get the hang of nails. He kept hitting hand with the hammer. I
don't know about you; I'd look for a more competent carpenter. 

PECD would recommend using pre-assembled walls and fastening them with a powered
nail driver. 'It's so easy, you can do it yourself. You don't even need a
carpenter. Build a house in one weekend.' <g>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(reply depth: 68)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-05-27T08:23:16+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<batt2u$h71$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <bar7q5$d6r$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ed173cd.247489374@news.optonline.net> <bask1l$vn9$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ed25ced.307178045@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:

>>> ..It introduces a pseudo-OR which is likely to confuse people
>>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> work.

And what about the people you claimed would be confused by '>=' ?

Why is this not equally true for them ?

The point being that people most easily recognise what they are used to.  
They are confused (if only briefly, ie they must inspect closely) what they 
are unfamiliar with.

You may have no '>=' in your code so it would cause a brief moment to study 
it, until it became familiar to you (ie your brain locked it in place as 
something to recognise instantly).

Others may avoid the use of not (by eg transforming < to >=) and find that 
they are less familiar than you with it and so briefly have to study its 
use rather than having an instant recognition burnt into their brain.

The symbols don't have an inherent and absolute 'best practice'. What is 
'best' is individually what is 'most used'.

As I write in several languages, and as most new Cobol programmers would 
have learnt Pascal, C or Java as a first (or second) language, then I and 
they would probably be more used to >= than the word NOT.  Certainly they 
(and I) would think it more natural to transform '<' to '>=' because that 
is what they were taught.

Someone brought up on '60 and '74 Cobol may not have seen '>=' before, 
especially if they hadn't read a manual over the last 20 years.

It certainly isn't sensible to challenge someone 'credability in logic' 
because they did it in ways that modern languages (including recent Cobol) 
do rather than the way you were _forced_ to 30 years ago when Cobol didn't 
have such a rich set of symbolic operators.
```

```
On Mon, 26 May 2003 03:17:07 GMT, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
wrote:

> Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:
> 
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> ..You cannot put NOT in from of >=

Doesn't "IF NOT (A >= B)" compile?  I don't know; I've never used such
a pointlessly complicated condition.

> ..It introduces a pseudo-OR which is likely to confuse people

There you go telling us what is happening in other people's heads.
Who has trouble with the implicit "OR"?  Have you seen studies?  Do
your peers complain about the construct?

> ..The main reason: when negating a conditional, you are forced to change a
> relational operator. If you avoid >=, all changes are to logical operators. It's
> easy to remember 'change the words, leave the symbols alone'.

I don't know what you do, but I rarely have to simply negate an
existing conditional.  When I change a conditional, it's either
because the spec has changed or because the original conditional was
buggy.  (FWIW, most of the buggy conditionals I see contain one or
more improper NOTs.)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ (NOT) >= (was: Alphabets

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bb0l5t$bvg$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <3eceb57b.67668169@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305241415.75c678b5@posting.google.com> <3ed07096.181119932@news.optonline.net> <bar7q5$d6r$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ed173cd.247489374@news.optonline.net> <3ed3d08a.343453219@news.cis.dfn.de>`

```
"Tukla Ratte" <tukla_ratte@tukla.net> wrote in message
news:3ed3d08a.343453219@news.cis.dfn.de...
> On Mon, 26 May 2003 03:17:07 GMT, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
> wrote:
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> a pointlessly complicated condition.

Like you, I haven't ever tried this, but I believe that it is true that:

   If not (A >= B) ...  *> is conforming

while

  If A NOT >= B ... *> is non-conforming

    ***

There are some technical reasons for this distinction (that I won't go into
here) but there was a long and painful interpretation of the '85 Standard
that showed WHY that 2nd example "causes problems".  The "basic" problem has
to do with parsing of the following (words causing problems over symbols
<G>)

   If A NOT equal or greater than B
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: (NOT) >= (was: Alphabets

_(reply depth: 68)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-05-29T11:08:18+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ed5d7e5.44752986@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <3eceb57b.67668169@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305241415.75c678b5@posting.google.com> <3ed07096.181119932@news.optonline.net> <bar7q5$d6r$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ed173cd.247489374@news.optonline.net> <3ed3d08a.343453219@news.cis.dfn.de> <bb0l5t$bvg$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote:

>"Tukla Ratte" <tukla_ratte@tukla.net> wrote in message
>news:3ed3d08a.343453219@news.cis.dfn.de...
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
><G>)

It's not a problem for Micro Focus, which handles everything correctly,
including >= in abbreviated compound relational conditions as well as NOT >=.

The OR in GREATER OR EQUAL is not parsed the same as a regular OR. The three
words are effectively converted to >= before precedence rules are applied. This
program demonstrates that. 

      $SET SOURCEFORMAT"FREE"
 identification division.
 program-id. test31.
*  author. Robert Wagner.
*   Testing evaluation of OR in compound conditional GREATER OR EQUAL

 data division.
 procedure division.
 main.

* Symbols vs. words. Are they the same?
* Yes, result is false in both cases
    if 1 >= 0 and 2 = 0
        display '1. true'
    else
        display '1. false'
    end-if
    if 1 greater or equal 0 and 2 equal 0
        display '2. true'
    else
        display '2. false'
    end-if

* If OR in compound relational is NOT interpreted normally, 2. would act like
this
* Yes. It is NOT interpreted normally. Result is false, same as 2.
    if (1 greater 0 or 1 equal 0) and (2 equal 0)
        display '3. true'
    else
        display '3. false'
    end-if
* If OR in compound relational IS interpreted normally, 2.would act like this
* Nope. Result is true, different from 2.
    if  1 greater 0  or (1 equal 0 and 2 equal 0)
        display '4. true'
    else
        display '4. false'
    end-if

* Remove compound relational notation. Does interpretation of OR change from 2.?
* Yes. Result is true, different from 2.
    if 1 greater 0 or 1 equal 0 and 2 equal 0
        display '5. true'
    else
        display '5. false'
    end-if

* Try an abbreviated compound condition relation.
* Result is true, different from 2.
    if 1 greater or equal 0 or -1 and 2 equal 0
        display '6. true'
    else
        display '6. false'
    end-if
* Explain why 7. is different from 2.
* The first OR was not interpreted normally, the second was.
* Result is true, same as 6.
    if (1 greater or equal 0) or (1 greater or equal -1 and 2 equal 0)
        display '7. true'
    else
        display '7. false'
    end-if

* Does Micro Focus permit not >=
* Yes, result is false
* Those who RTFM would not know this. The manual says it's not allowed.
    if 1 not >= 0
        display '8. true'
    else
        display '8. false'
    end-if

-- end of demo program ---

I speculated here that 'not >='  is disallowed because '>='  is internally
converted to  'not <'. Thus 'not >=' is equivalent to 'not not <'. That
construct is invalid because the rules of COBOL forbid double negatives. 

I no longer believe that. I now believe 'not >= ' is forbidden because committee
members couldn't wrap their minds around the logic.  Below, the 2002 standard
tries to explain why 'not >=' is forbidden in abbreviated compound condition
relations while overlooking the fact it is forbidden in ANY conditional, thus
the justification is superfluous. This is evidence of fuzzy thinking. 

24) NOT in abbreviated combined relation conditions. The word NOT is prohibited
before the relational operators
meaning 'greater than or equal to' and 'less than or equal to'.
Justification:
The previous COBOL standard was ambiguous in the action of the word NOT
preceding GREATER THAN and
LESS THAN in an abbreviated combined relation condition. Different implementors
placed different
interpretations on what was intended or diagnosed the situation, resulting in a
lack of portability.
This draft international standard prohibits the word NOT in situations where the
ambiguity would arise. This
increases the degree of portability because all implementors are required to
diagnose this use. This change
was made as a result of a request for an interpretation of the previous COBOL
standard. There will be no
difference in execution because the compiler will diagnose such a use of NOT. It
is estimated that few, if any,
programs will be affected by this change.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: (NOT) >= (was: Alphabets

_(reply depth: 69)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-29T09:09:38-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bb5bc2$24vd$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <3eceb57b.67668169@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305241415.75c678b5@posting.google.com> <3ed07096.181119932@news.optonline.net> <bar7q5$d6r$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ed173cd.247489374@news.optonline.net> <3ed3d08a.343453219@news.cis.dfn.de> <bb0l5t$bvg$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <3ed5d7e5.44752986@news.optonline.net>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3ed5d7e5.44752986@news.optonline.net...

<<Below, the 2002 standard tries to explain why 'not >=' is forbidden in
abbreviated compound condition relations while overlooking the fact it is
forbidden in ANY conditional, thus the justification is superfluous. This is
evidence of fuzzy thinking.  ...

24) NOT in abbreviated combined relation conditions.  ... The previous COBOL
standard was ambiguous in the action of the word NOT preceding GREATER THAN
and LESS THAN in an abbreviated combined relation condition. ...>>

Ummm.... I'm not sure what published standard you're quoting here, but
that's not what ISO/IEC 1989:2002 says.

Here's what I read (Page 817, F.1, Substantive changes potentially affecting
existing programs) for the justification (and, by the way, it's item 22, not
item 24 in that list):  "The previous standard was ambiguous in the action
of the word NOT preceding what are now the extended relational operators
GREATER THAN OR EQUAL TO and LESS THAN OR EQUAL TO in an abbreviated
combined relation condition.  Some implementors treated this as an error,
others differed in their implementations of this ambiguous syntax; this
resulted in a lack of portability."

I note that the syntax diagram for abbreviated combined relation conditions
in ANSI X3.23-1985 (Page VI-61) specifically and explicitly allows an
optional NOT before *any* relational operator (as listed on Page VI-55),
whether NOT is allowed in the syntax of a simple relation condition (Page
VI-54) or not.  I believe that's the ambiguity to which ISO/IEC 1989:2002
alludes on Page 817, and which it eliminates by differentiating between
"simple relational operators" and "extended relational operators" in the
equivalent syntax diagrams on Pages 119 and 136 of ISO/IEC 1989:2002.

Not sure why you think this represents "fuzzy thinking", though.

       -Chuck Stevens
```

```
Robert,
    I assume that you haven't bothered to check out the detailed reasoning
behind the change that is SUMMARIZED in the 2003 Standard.  For the full
(and IMHO correct) reasoning, see the interpretation response published
almost a full decade ago (Sept '93) in CIB-26, pages 54-55, A-225 and
titled,

   "NOT GREATER OR EQUAL"

Once you have reviewed that document, then I think you may (or may not) be
qualified to speak to the full reasoning of the Standards committees on this
topic.

NEEDLESS TO SAY, there were ZERO comments on the 2003 Standard (during its
several public review periods) in which ANY user indicated "unhappiness"
with this clarification.  Similarly, to the best of my knowledge, there have
NEVER been any user comments (since Sept '93) submitted to either J4 or WG4
indicating any problem with the correctness of the existing interpretation.
```

```
Howard Brazee wrote:


>> 'One' is first person inclusive and singular.  It is used to indicate
>> 'we' (inclusive) but individually one at a time.

> Cite?

Oh, for example:

at http://www.teacher-training.net/companion/gg/nouns/nounppro.htm

""" --------------------------------------
We can use one as a pronoun to replace anybody and we use it to talk about 
people in general but it is not used very much these days and is very 
formal:

One must accept that one has limitations.

-------------- """
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(reply depth: 66)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-05-29T05:05:56+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bb1g29$3l7$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <bb026a$1cc$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <bb0ic8$ltr$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ed41cbb.421833650@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:

>>""" --------------------------------------
>>We can use one as a pronoun to replace anybody and we use it to talk about
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> third person.

'One' is singular, but refers to all 'anyone's.  Aren't you part of 
'anybody' ?  If I said "anybody can eat here' do you think that excludes me 
and you and us, and only refers to 'them' ?

'One' is first person in the same way that 'we' and 'us' are.  It is 
inclusive.  Otherwise the quote would have been:

  'You must accept that you have limitations.'  (2nd person)

  'They must accept that they have limitations.' (3rd person)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(reply depth: 67)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-05-29T11:08:22+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ed5d795.44673470@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <bb026a$1cc$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <bb0ic8$ltr$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ed41cbb.421833650@news.optonline.net> <bb1g29$3l7$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>Robert Wagner wrote:
>
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>and you and us, and only refers to 'them' ?

Merriam-Webster thinks so:

2 a : an individual of a vaguely indicated group : anyone at all <one never
knows> b -- used as a THIRD PERSON [emphisis added] substitute for a first
person pronoun <I'd like to read more but one doesn't have the time>

usage Sense 2a is usually a sign of a formal style. A formal style EXCLUDES
[emphisis added]  the participation of the reader or hearer; thus one is used
where a less formal style might address the reader directly <for the
consequences of such choices, one has only oneself to thank -- Walker Gibson>.
This generic one has never been common in informal use in either British or
American English, and people who start sentences with one often shift to another
pronoun more natural to casual discourse <when one is learning the river, he is
not allowed to do or think about anything else -- Mark Twain>. 

-- end of quotation --

I too thought 'one' included present company, until I looked it up. You might
have done the same.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(reply depth: 68)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-05-29T12:38:56-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0305291138.707841e8@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <bb026a$1cc$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <bb0ic8$ltr$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ed41cbb.421833650@news.optonline.net> <bb1g29$3l7$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ed5d795.44673470@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 

> 2 a : an individual of a vaguely indicated group : anyone at all <one never
> knows> b -- used as a THIRD PERSON [emphisis added] substitute for a first
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> [emphisis added]  the participation of the reader or hearer; 

> I too thought 'one' included present company, until I looked it up. You might
> have done the same.

"One is _usually_ a sign of a formal style" (but need not be).
_When_ used in a formal style it _may_ exclude the first person.
When used in an informal style it may not exclude the first person.

The group or class that it does refer to is from the context.  Being
an indefinite pronoun it does not refer directly.  If the speaker is
within the group of the context (such as you in programmers) then it
does refer to them.

AS other references have already indicated.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(reply depth: 69)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-05-29T20:05:54+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bb5p72$ppr$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <bb026a$1cc$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <bb0ic8$ltr$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ed41cbb.421833650@news.optonline.net> <bb1g29$3l7$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ed5d795.44673470@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305291138.707841e8@posting.google.com>`

```

On 29-May-2003, riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

> "One is _usually_ a sign of a formal style" (but need not be).
> _When_ used in a formal style it _may_ exclude the first person.
> When used in an informal style it may not exclude the first person.

Your last sentence is ambiguous.

I generally think of "one" as including the first person - but sometimes the
context of the statement may exclude this - or even exclude everybody except for
one person.   As programmers we tend to like our semantic rules to be tight, but
in English it ain't necessarily so.
```

```
On Sat, 24 May 2003 04:13:30 GMT, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
wrote:

> tukla_ratte@tukla.net (Tukla Ratte) wrote:

< snip >

> >My response was relevant.  Jeff stated how symbolic conditionals are
> >easier for him to spot in a piece of code.  I mentioned (in case he
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> reader, might. 

I don't think I've seen anyone else in these threads express any sort
of difficulty reading symbolic (or should I say logographic?)
conditionals.  So, who is this "generic" programmer supposed to
represent, if it doesn't represent real programmers?

> See my response to Chuck Stevens in re logical algebra, then reassess whether I
> would have difficulty reading symbols. The thread subject was ' 'x < y < z'.

You have said that you have to "inspect" symbols because you have to
switch contexts from English to symbols and back to English.  Perhaps
you mean "you" to mean the "generic programmer", and not you
personally.  However, this "generic programmer" doesn't represent
anyone I have ever known, so it's hard for me to see it representing
anyone but you.

> >FWIW, *I* have to "switch gears" three times when I see "IF A NOT LESS
> >THAN B".  First, I have to backtrack and put in the missing "IS" after
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> If you find >= superior to not <, you've destroyed your credability in re logic.

Baseless assertion.

> >I've always assumed that the reason for this is because I used a more
> >symbol-oriented language (BASIC) when first learning to program.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> offering no choice aren't very convincing here. 

I am using myself as an example.  I prefer "IF A >= B" because I think
it's more readable.  You prefer "IF A NOT LESS THAN B", and my
impression from reading dozens of your posts is that you find this
latter form more readable than the former.

The *point* of my example is that my preference is just that: my
preference.  You, on the other hand, are attempting to argue that your
preference isn't simply a personal, subjective preference.  You are
trying to argue that it is objectively superior.  *That* is what
everyone else in these threads rejects.

> >> posting an ad hominem
> >
> >Is it technically an ad hominem when I'm not debating your argument?
> 
> Yes. You are arguing 'against the person' rather than against my conclusions. 

I was fairly harsh in my initial post, I will grant you that.

> >Much more intelligent people than I have already refuted your claims.
> 
> More intelligent than I?? I guess this would be a self-proving statement. 

Heh.  An ad hominen from *you*, now.

Robert, I fully realize that I have limits in both my abilities and my
knowledge.  Other people *are* smarter than I am, and they also know
more or different things than I do.  You may be one of those people!
Acknowledging this fact is usually called "modesty".  If you are
seriously implying that I called myself "stupid", then you lose even
more credibility. 

> >> does not add to your credibility nor provide useful
> >> information to others.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> programming tomorrow. 

Fine.  Here it is:

Unless you have a standard by which you must abide, use whatever
conditional format that you prefer when writing your programs.  There
is no One Right Way to write COBOL.

I suspect that I've just stated the obvious.

< snip >

> >LOL!  Google Groups contains years of postings I've made making myself
> >look immature.  You'll have to try harder than that to insult me.
> 
> I thought you were trying to insult me. In either case, I don't get off on
> insults given or received.

Liar.  You've insulted me at least twice.

> We're supposed to be helping each other here. 

I don't see how declaring me incapable of logical transformations just
because I prefer ">=" over "NOT <" (or "NOT LESS THAN") is helpful.

< snip >
```

```
tukla_ratte@tukla.net (Tukla Ratte) wrote:

>On Sat, 24 May 2003 04:13:30 GMT, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
>wrote:
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>represent, if it doesn't represent real programmers?

Perhaps not, since most 'modern' programming languages since C (70s) offer no
alternative to >=. They don't permit you to say !<. 

>You have said that you have to "inspect" symbols because you have to
>switch contexts from English to symbols and back to English.  Perhaps
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>anyone but you.

Your point is well taken. I was trying to project to the general population.
Richard convinced me that computer literate people, at least, recognize <= and
that !> is not in their vocabulary. That doesn't make <= superior in the
abstract, but it does in the pragmatic. 

But then, he used persuasive argumentation a far cry from your style. 

>Robert, I fully realize that I have limits in both my abilities and my
>knowledge.  Other people *are* smarter than I am,

Once you leave the little pond of high-school/neighborhood, there will ALWAYS be
someone smarter. How you deal with the encounter is a measure of character. You
can be hostile and resentful, or you can learn from him. 

>Unless you have a standard by which you must abide, use whatever
>conditional format that you prefer when writing your programs.  There
>is no One Right Way to write COBOL.
>
>I suspect that I've just stated the obvious.

You have stated the nearly unanimous sentiment of CLC posters. There is no such
thing as Right and Wrong, it's all Personal Preference.

Seems a little strange to me and I have conjectures about why. They'll wait
'till another post. 

>> I thought you were trying to insult me. In either case, I don't get off on
>> insults given or received.
>
>Liar.  You've insulted me at least twice.

I prefer to think you insulted yourself; I just pointed it out. 

>> We're supposed to be helping each other here. 
>
>I don't see how declaring me incapable of logical transformations just
>because I prefer ">=" over "NOT <" (or "NOT LESS THAN") is helpful.

Ok. On the strength of Richard's argument, I retract the allegation and
apologize. It works and you didn't know anything better.
```

```
tukla_ratte@tukla.net (Tukla Ratte) wrote:

>On Wed, 28 May 2003 04:31:48 GMT, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
>wrote:
>
>> tukla_ratte@tukla.net (Tukla Ratte) wrote:

>> Once you leave the little pond of high-school/neighborhood, there will
>> ALWAYS be someone smarter. How you deal with the encounter is a measure
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>thread.  You're just not one of those people.

Did these smart people teach you to use the obsolete subjective pronoun case I
after the comparative adjective than? Would you say 'He is taller than I?' You'd
probably say 'He is taller than me' or 'He is taller than I am'. 

>> >Liar.  You've insulted me at least twice.
>> 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>Oh, but *I* didn't insult you.  I'm just pointng it out.

It was unnecessary for me to point it out you are unmannerly and immature; you
just informed the world by your own words. 

If you want to flame, go to alt.flame and learn to do it with originality and
wit.  If you try adolescent name calling there, they'll make you feel like
you've been through a food processor.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(reply depth: 64)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-05-29T05:21:39+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bb1gvo$429$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <3eceb57b.67668169@news.optonline.net> <3ed3bc58.338283155@news.cis.dfn.de> <3ed41e32.422208968@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:

> You have stated the nearly unanimous sentiment of CLC posters. There is no
> such thing as Right and Wrong, it's all Personal Preference.

Just as in any other computer language.  A good example is C indented 
blocks which may be:

      if ( ... ) {
          ....
      }

or    if ( ... )
      {
          ....
      }

or    if ( ... )
          {
          ...
          }

none are 'wrong', just different.

> you didn't know anything better.

Seems a strange 'apology' to me, especially after agreeing that what he 
preferred _was_ better with:

> That doesn't make <= superior in the abstract, but it does in the
> pragmatic.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(reply depth: 65)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-05-29T11:08:30+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ed5d50e.44025608@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <3eceb57b.67668169@news.optonline.net> <3ed3bc58.338283155@news.cis.dfn.de> <3ed41e32.422208968@news.optonline.net> <bb1gvo$429$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>Robert Wagner wrote:
>
>> You have stated the nearly unanimous sentiment of CLC posters. There is no
>> such thing as Right and Wrong, it's all Personal Preference.

>> you didn't know anything better.
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>> pragmatic. 

Your argument was based on the fact that languages developed since the 70s
offered <= while excluding !>. That doesn't mean <= is superior in the abstract,
only that it is more popular. If popularity were a valid criterion for judging
truth, 'argumentum ad populum' would not be a fallacy; reality would be
determined by popular vote.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(reply depth: 66)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-05-29T13:43:52-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0305291243.2cb17b76@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <3eceb57b.67668169@news.optonline.net> <3ed3bc58.338283155@news.cis.dfn.de> <3ed41e32.422208968@news.optonline.net> <bb1gvo$429$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ed5d50e.44025608@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 

> >> you didn't know anything better.
> >
> >Seems a strange 'apology' to me, 

> Your argument was based on the fact that languages developed since the 70s

And also in most developed before the 70s.

> offered <= while excluding !>. That doesn't mean <= is superior in the 
> abstract, only that it is more popular. 

What you have completely failed to notice is that _neither_ is
superior.  You always seem to have this strange idea that some things
are inherently 'better', usually what you are used to, and things that
are outside your experience are worse.

> If popularity were a valid criterion for judging
> truth, 'argumentum ad populum' would not be a fallacy; reality would be
> determined by popular vote.

We are not talking about 'reality', its just a programming language,
where 'popularity' amongst the language designers (the committee)
_does_ determine 'truth', they do vote on it.

And what has all this to do with you making such a crap (non-)apology
?
```

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 

> >> We programmers have the ultimate vote. We've voted No to hundreds of poorly
> >> designed languages, and a few good ones. We've voted Yes to fewer than a
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> They do in the mainframe world, but the new stuff doesn't target mainframes. 

It is irrelevant if it is a mainframe or not if the employer chooses
the language.

People choosing languages for their own use on their own machines do
not do so based on whether it is 'good'. They do so based on whether
it is free, or whether it heavily advertised or whether it is 'cool'.

Hence there are millions of VB programmers.
 
> When the employer chooses lanaguages, some individual makes the decision. Why
> not be that individual?
```

```
riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 
>
>> >> We programmers have the ultimate vote. We've voted No to hundreds of
poorly
>> >> designed languages, and a few good ones. We've voted Yes to fewer than a
>> >> dozen.
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>Hence there are millions of VB programmers.

VB isn't free nor heavily advertised nor cool (it's ten years old). Its main
attraction is Ease of Use. The 'programmer' can present GUI screens with amazing
speed. 

Flexus can do the same almost as easily. Why aren't there millions of Flexus
programmers? Because it requires a COBOL compiler costing $2-5K.
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <3eceb57b.67668169@news.optonline.net> <3ed3bc58.338283155@news.cis.dfn.de> <3ed41e32.422208968@news.optonline.net> <bb1gvo$429$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ed5d50e.44025608@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305291243.2cb17b76@posting.google.com> <3ed727b0.46486933@news.optonline.net> <bb7ogp$rvt$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ed7d17a.89960365@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305311327.451cc767@posting.google.com> <3ed955ae.189355106@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 

> >People choosing languages for their own use on their own machines do
> >not do so based on whether it is 'good'. They do so based on whether
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> VB isn't free nor heavily advertised nor cool (it's ten years old). 

You may notice that my conditions were joined with 'or'.  VB _was_
heavily advertised, the runtimes _were_ and _are_ free, it _was_ cool
and now VB.NET is again thought of as 'cool' by many.

Hence, over the last 10 years, many chose VB for those reasons, and
hence there _are_ millions of VB programmers.

In any case $100 counts as close to 'free' as needed when compared to
the cost of a couple of books.

> Its main attraction is Ease of Use. The 'programmer' can present GUI screens
> with amazing speed. 

How 'cool'.  Of course many other languages can too, with appropriate
tools.

> Flexus can do the same almost as easily. Why aren't there millions of Flexus
> programmers? Because it requires a COBOL compiler costing $2-5K.

Exactly.  Cobol isn't heavily advertised, isn't cool, isn't free (or
nearly so)*.



* There are free versions of Cobol, but none that I would call
suitable for program distribution.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(reply depth: 73)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-06-03T02:42:10+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3edbf7ac.361923032@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <3eceb57b.67668169@news.optonline.net> <3ed3bc58.338283155@news.cis.dfn.de> <3ed41e32.422208968@news.optonline.net> <bb1gvo$429$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ed5d50e.44025608@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305291243.2cb17b76@posting.google.com> <3ed727b0.46486933@news.optonline.net> <bb7ogp$rvt$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ed7d17a.89960365@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305311327.451cc767@posting.google.com> <3ed955ae.189355106@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0306012044.28ba5b74@posting.google.com>`

```
riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 
>
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>hence there _are_ millions of VB programmers.

I'll grant your points are valid for amateur programming. How do those choices
affect professional programming? Because someone already knows VB from home or
school, is it more likely he'll use VB on the job (assuming he has a voice in
the decision)?

Maybe so with VB, because it feels like an 'industrial strength' language and is
heavily supported by MS. But would you bet your job on an unsupported
freeware/shareware compiler? You have with Python. I would if it really turned
me on. Most people wouldn't. Even commercial languages 'on the fringe', for
example Delphi, cause FUD. 'What if it flops? My head will roll.'

>In any case $100 counts as close to 'free' as needed when compared to
>the cost of a couple of books.

Money is important to amateurs. Job security is important to professionals. So
what if it costs $5K; it's not their money. You know that's why IBM succeeded. 
[BTW, good article on The Future of Cobol.]

>> Flexus can do the same almost as easily. Why aren't there millions of Flexus
>> programmers? Because it requires a COBOL compiler costing $2-5K.
>
>Exactly.  Cobol isn't heavily advertised, isn't cool, isn't free (or
>nearly so)*.

Some are trying to make it free, but they're going about it wrong, IMO. They
should be targeting the 'cool' platform: .NET; Unix is no longer cool. Given the
limited resources available to such an undertaking, .NET is an obvious win
because it can leverage thousands of classes developed by others. They no longer
have to write a sort, a file system, a GUI system, a debugger, etc. All they
have to do is provide glue. It's the same idea behind C 25+ years ago. The
difference is now the body of services is real and polished; then it was a
dream. 

>* There are free versions of Cobol, but none that I would call
>suitable for program distribution.

Agreed.
```

```
Are you implying that employers that don't use mainframes don't pick appropriate

to data processing - I'm including dishwashers, hammers, and copy machines).

I don't want to be the employer.   I will make recommendations, but the person
who pays the bills has final say.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(reply depth: 68)_

- **From:** Michael Wojcik <mwojcik@newsguy.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-31T17:35:40-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ED9202C.30200@newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <3eceb57b.67668169@news.optonline.net> <3ed3bc58.338283155@news.cis.dfn.de> <3ed41e32.422208968@news.optonline.net> <bb1gvo$429$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ed5d50e.44025608@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305291243.2cb17b76@posting.google.com> <3ed727b0.46486933@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:
 > We programmers have the ultimate vote. We've voted No to hundreds of
 > poorly designed languages, and a few good ones. We've voted Yes to
 > fewer than a dozen.

Fewer than a dozen?  Just including languages that I know significant
applications have been written in, relatively recently, off the top of
my head, in no particular order:

COBOL
C
C++
Visual Basic
Python
Perl
PHP
Java
Fortran
Haskell
RPG
Ada
Caml
Delphi
various assembly languages

That's fifteen so far.  Is APL still in common use?  Or PL/I - *we*
still sell a PL/I compiler, last I checked; presumably someone is using
it.  There was recently a thread on comp.programming about PL/M.  Does
anyone write significant applications in Scheme (one of my favorites
back in my school days), or other LISP variants?  Probably.  What about
Pascal derivatives other than Delphi, or Modula-2, or Oberon?  I don't
know, but a little searching would probably turn up some apps of
consequence.
```
