###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(reply depth: 73)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-06-07T07:56:24+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bbqrm8$381$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <3eda9f13.273692202@news.optonline.net> <3EDF58B5.1040208@newsguy.com> <3ee04676.644279046@news.optonline.net> <bbpu0g$m93$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net> wrote:
>
> If you find my conditions arbitrary, 

I find your conditions retrospective.  Having lost the argument you want to 
change it to discard your loss win

>>Python is a general-purpose language that has been used to build large
>>applications.  

>To me, scripting language means interpreted source.

Python is not 'interpreted source', it has a JIT compiler that compiles to 
a .pyc file the first time the soutce is run, subsequent runs use only the 
compiled version (based on date/time).  There is also an optimiser which 
produces .pyo files which will be used in proference.

Your knowledge of what Python is, is less than the required level to make 
meaningful comments (as it was with many others).

'scripting languages' are any language, whether compiled or interpreted, 
that is weakly typed.

Whether an implementation of a language is an interpreter or a compiler is, 
in general, independant of the language itself.  The usual Python is JIT 
compiled, Jython is a Python compiler that produces Java byte code, others 
may do something completely different.

There are, in fact, source code runtime interpreters for C, but that 
doesn't make it a scripting language.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

- **Date:** 2003-06-07T23:41:04-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0306072241.353a73ee@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <3eda9f13.273692202@news.optonline.net> <3EDF58B5.1040208@newsguy.com> <3ee04676.644279046@news.optonline.net> <bbpu0g$m93$1@panix1.panix.com> <bbqrm8$381$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ee23ddc.123192452@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 

> COBOL is weakly typed. Is it a scripting language?
> 
> Ok, but ANSI C is weekly typed so THAT makes it a scripting language.

If they are used for 'scripting' then they can be a scripting
language.  It is _not_ whether they are compiled or interpreted that
makes them this.

Cobol or C is a 'script' if it runs as a CGI script under httpd.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(reply depth: 73)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-06-07T20:21:39+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ee237d1.121645120@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <3eda9f13.273692202@news.optonline.net> <3EDF58B5.1040208@newsguy.com> <3ee04676.644279046@news.optonline.net> <bbpu0g$m93$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

> A language like
>EasyTrieve can be run both through an interpreted or a compiler; to you is
>that a 'scripting language' which runs on mainframes?? 

I've worked in mainframe shops that used interpreted EasyTrieve in production
batch jobs. When I asked why they didn't compile it, the response was "huh?" and
a blank stare. They'd been using it for years and didn't know compilation was an
option. 

So I'd say, as a practical matter, it's a scripting languge. 

>GWBasic had an
>interpreter that came with the older versions of MS-DOS... was that a
>'scripting language' for PCs?

Before GWBasic, the interpreter was cast in silicon and mounted on the
motherboard. Yes, that was a scripting language. It had lousy heap management,
no library, couldn't call another program (except runtime functions and assembly
language), automatic variable declaration.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(reply depth: 74)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-06-08T11:08:52+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bbtrbf$g7b$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <3EDF58B5.1040208@newsguy.com> <3ee04676.644279046@news.optonline.net> <bbpu0g$m93$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ee237d1.121645120@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:

> I've worked in mainframe shops that used interpreted EasyTrieve in
> production batch jobs. When I asked why they didn't compile it, the
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> So I'd say, as a practical matter, it's a scripting languge.

So, you agree that uou _can_ use scripting languages in production batch 
jobs.

The reason for not compiling is more likely that it makes almost no 
difference at all.
 
>>GWBasic had an
>>interpreter that came with the older versions of MS-DOS... was that a
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> motherboard. 

That is _only_ true of actual IBM brand and smoe IBM BIOS licencees - these 
came with PC-DOS, not MS-DOS.

'Older versions of MS-DOS' (note not PC-DOS) were for non-IBM machines, 
such as Sharp, Sirius, BondWell, DEC Rainbow etc.  These did not have BASIC 
in ROM.

The original MS Basic came on paper tape.  It was put in ROM only because 
paper tape readers were not common accessories.  For CP/M systems MS had 
BASIC-80, MBASIC and BASCOM compiler which were on disk.  For non-IBM 8086 
machines there was MBASIC-86.

> Yes, that was a scripting language. 

Only for very poor definitions of 'scripting'.

'Scripting' is most often associated with providing 'scripts' for running 
in some larger application:  configuration scripts for an editor, macro 
scripts for a spreadsheet, program run scripts for an operating system.  In 
the case of many so called scripting languages this is most often for CGI 
in httpd.  In this environment even C programs are called 'scripts'.

Word Basic _is_ a scripting language, ROM Basic and BASIC-80 is _not_.

MS's BASCOM was a compiler that produced .EXEs

> It had lousy heap
> management, no library, couldn't call another program (except runtime
> functions and assembly language), automatic variable declaration.

It was BASIC.  ROM BASIC was designed to run on a machine with 64Kb and no 
disk.  Other versions had different characteristics.

Of course ROM Basic, MS Basic, GWBasic, QBasic are just 3 implementations 
of the language BASIC designed by Kemeny and Kurtz.  Other implentations 
may have quite different characteristics.  CBASIC, for example, was quite 
suitable for building applications and was compiled.
```

```
>Years ago I rewrote one of them into a JIT compiler. It ran at least ten
>times faster. I just don't have numbers to support that.

I have see the type of 'benchmarks' that you do.  For example you compared 
Cobol COMP and COMP-5 using just an ADD 1 which may have resulted in the 
optimiser making it all go away creating results which are not reproducable 
with any realistic code.

Compiled code may well run 10 times faster, but that wouldn't be a 'batch 
run' doing file I/O as the file speed will be unchanged by the compile.
```

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>

```
On  8-Jun-2003, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:

> >> I've worked in mainframe shops that used interpreted EasyTrieve in
> >> production batch jobs. When I asked why they didn't compile it, the
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> I don't have any data on execution speed.

Since we can compile EasyTrieve (the compiler is paid for), companies must not
feel that the time spent compiling the code at run time isn't significant.  
This isn't quite the same thing as "execution speed", even though it is a
run-time cost.   Systems programmers pay a lot of attention to such ways of
speeding up response, especially when the solution is as easy as telling
applications programmers to compile their EasyTrieve.

I have some EasyTrieve programs which actually have user owned code concatenated
in the middle of the programmer supplied source.  They change their code before
running.
```

```
Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net> wrote:
>docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>So I'd say, as a practical matter, it's a scripting languge. 

I see... so based on the ignorance found in the shops you have worked you 
conclude that EasyTrieve is a 'scripting language' because some folks do 
not know enough to compile it.

>
>>GWBasic had an
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>language), automatic variable declaration.

Before GWBasic - about two decades before, that is - there was Kemeny and
Kurtz's Beginner's All-Purpose Symbolic Instruction Code; what seems to 
have happened is that a compiled language became an interpreted one.

DD
```

```
docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

>>Before GWBasic, the interpreter was cast in silicon and mounted on the
>>motherboard. Yes, that was a scripting language. It had lousy heap management,
>>no library, couldn't call another program (except runtime functions and
assembly
>>language), automatic variable declaration.
>
>Before GWBasic - about two decades before, that is - there was Kemeny and
>Kurtz's Beginner's All-Purpose Symbolic Instruction Code; what seems to 
>have happened is that a compiled language became an interpreted one.

Thanks in part to Bill Gates and Paul Allen, whose Basic interpreter for the
Altair spawned an empire.
```

```
>>>>no library, couldn't call another program (except runtime functions and
assembly
>>>>language), automatic variable declaration.

…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>language into a scripting one.

Gates and Allen did not write the _first_  Basic interpreter, but they
popularized it. DEC PDPs and DG Novas had interpreters before Gates and Allen
wrote theirs. They developed it on a PDP8 (if memory serves), so it is
reasonable to assume they got the idea from PDP interpreters.
```

```
Mr Wagner, so you are saying that USERS (caps original) wrote the 
interpreter?  Well, that would not be too wrong, as per your usual... 
since everyone who manipulates a computer towards a given end is, in some 
wise, a 'user'.

But... that 'given a choice' indicates that you are not considering those 
who wrote the interpreters in this manner.  USERS (caps original), it 
seem, are capable of turning one thing into another by their employment 
thereof; that being the case it seems that you disagree with Mr Lincoln, 
in that he asserted 'calling a tail a leg does not make it one'.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(reply depth: 80)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-06-09T23:03:40-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0306092203.4ec8b26e@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <3ee3684d.199605532@news.optonline.net> <bc0dms$ior$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ee3d1ef.6051725@news.optonline.net> <bc1qmj$p6s$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ee51ecb.91276843@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote

> I prefer to say USERS turned Basic into a scripting language. Microsoft
> offered
> BASCOM, a true Basic compiler, but it was largely ignored in favor of the
> interpreter.

Your preference does not necessarily create reality.

Given that there are, for most purposes, no significant difference
between the source language for MBASIC and BASCOM what in the language
was actually 'turned' ?

In any case MBASIC and BASCOM are not generally called 'scripting
languages'.  'MS Word BASIC' _is_ a scripting language (because it is
embedded in a larger application.

Perl and PHP are 'scripting languages' because they are often embeeded
in httpd as CGI scripts.

BASCOM was not 'freely' available, it cost several hundred dollars. 
For most the cost was not worth the results.  Many other professional
programmers bought CBASIC or Turbo BASIC or TRUE Basic or similar and
used it as a compiled languages (or languages).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(reply depth: 82)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-06-10T13:30:17-07:00

- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0306101230.55b0662e@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <3ee3684d.199605532@news.optonline.net> <bc0dms$ior$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ee3d1ef.6051725@news.optonline.net> <bc1qmj$p6s$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ee51ecb.91276843@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0306092203.4ec8b26e@posting.google.com> <bc4p6p$guo$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote 

> I still have no good handle on what "scripting language" means in a context that
> makes sense for Robert to discard it as being unworthy.   It seems that he is
> describing interpreted languages, but that doesn't make sense.  What's the
> difference (worthy-wise) between interpreted and compiled languages?

Especially when it has little to do with the language itself.  It is
an implementation issue in many cases - as exemplified by BASIC which
is both.

There is/was also Quick-C which was an interpreter for C.

The only reason for RW is that he many claims about how programmers
had 'voted' for several languages and he had thought of C++, Java, and
a handful of others.  He didn't want to admit that his horizon was
severely limited.  It was another excuse for why he 'meant' 'only
those he could think of'.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(reply depth: 83)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2003-06-10T21:28:55-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bc60kn$chc$1@panix1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0306092203.4ec8b26e@posting.google.com> <bc4p6p$guo$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <217e491a.0306101230.55b0662e@posting.google.com>`

```
In article <217e491a.0306101230.55b0662e@posting.google.com>,
Richard <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:
>"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote 
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>is both.

Ah, so someone *did* see this... I thought I was getting too obscure 
again.  What I was trying to point out was that the same series of 
statements in a language - in one case EasyTrieve, in another case BASIC - 
were treated differently, at one time being compiled, in another case 
being interpreted.

In both cases the language did not change... but for some reason Mr Wagner 
believes that running a source file one day through an interpreter makes 
contain a 'scripting language' while running the same source file through 
a compiler the next day makes it otherwise.

It can be said, then, that every source-file contains *two* sets of 
instructions, one 'scripting language', one 'compiled language'... and 
only by looking at what comes out of the process can one determine what is 
inside.

No, wait... what comes out of both processes will be identical results (or
so one should hope, given the Nature of Data Processing) so it really
*isn't* an instance of Schoredinger's Source.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(reply depth: 84)_

- **From:** "Warren Simmons" <wsimmons5@cox.net>
- **Date:** 2003-06-10T21:56:06-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2jAFa.65044$%42.58475@fed1read06>`
- **References:** `<3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0306092203.4ec8b26e@posting.google.com> <bc4p6p$guo$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <217e491a.0306101230.55b0662e@posting.google.com> <bc60kn$chc$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
Doc,

You and the gang are way over my head with this script discussion. However,
I don't have sence enough to stay out of it from my point of experience.

When I ran a BBS, the setup was often difficult for my slow mind. Yet,
The Modem Vendor provided a method of saving each operator action in the
process, and allowing it to be saved, and

Then they had software that would feed the "script" to the OS as though
it was a single instruction. The script was a capture of a correct entering
of commands. The commands in turn executed the program*S.

So, as an example, Steve Wright's Meta COBOL was (to me) a script as
it had to be processed like any other program to create the desired COBOL.

I use such illustrations to clear my mind only. I have no experienced with
any
other "script" but JCL.

What say you guys and gals?

Warren Simmons
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(reply depth: 85)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2003-06-11T11:35:09-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bc7i7d$2b5$1@panix1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0306101230.55b0662e@posting.google.com> <bc60kn$chc$1@panix1.panix.com> <2jAFa.65044$%42.58475@fed1read06>`

```
In article <2jAFa.65044$%42.58475@fed1read06>,
Warren Simmons <wsimmons5@cox.net> wrote:
>Doc,
>
>You and the gang are way over my head with this script discussion. However,
>I don't have sence enough to stay out of it from my point of experience.

Pfoo, Mr Simmons... that over-headed-ness does not seem to have stopped 
many of the folks here, jump on in!

>
>When I ran a BBS, the setup was often difficult for my slow mind. Yet,
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>What say you guys and gals?

The difficulty I have, Mr Simmons, is that during the course of the 
discussion here Mr Wagner seems to have been proven wrong about the number 
of languages to which 'We programmers... have voted Yes' by Mr Wojcik's 
list in:

<http://www.google.com/groups?hl=en&lr=&ie=UTF-8&selm=3ED9202C.30200%40newsguy.com>

Rather than admit to (yet again) being wrong in one of his assertions Mr 
Wagner attempted to (in a style which he has demonstrated previously and 
still insists on employing) change his assertion by adding new conditions, 
as found in:

<http://www.google.com/groups?hl=en&lr=&ie=UTF-8&selm=3eda9f13.273692202%40news.optonline.net>

One of the new conditions that Mr Wagner presented was that he was 
referring to 'compiled, not interpreted scripting languages'.

By using the examples of EasyTrieve and BASIC I was attempting to 
demonstrate that since the same source file can be either compiled or 
interpreted in EasyTrieve and BASIC and that given the same data the 
results of the compiled or interpreted code should be the same then Mr 
Wagner's criterion of 'compiled, not interpreted scripting language' was, 
in addition to being posted after he was, once again, proven wrong, 
completely and utterly idiosyncratic and spurious.

Mr Wagner, it seems, disagrees.

DD
```

```
On 10-Jun-2003, "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:

> I still have no good handle on what "scripting language" means in a context
> that
> makes sense for Robert to discard it as being unworthy.   It seems that he is
> describing interpreted languages, but that doesn't make sense.  What's the
> difference (worthy-wise) between interpreted and compiled languages?

get a hold of to cause mischief.   Stuff that looked very useful to Microsoft
(and to me) - and looked even more useful to virus writers.   I'm probably wrong
in using this term that way.
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <3eceb57b.67668169@news.optonline.net> <3ed3bc58.338283155@news.cis.dfn.de> <3ed41e32.422208968@news.optonline.net> <bb1gvo$429$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ed5d50e.44025608@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305291243.2cb17b76@posting.google.com> <3ed727b0.46486933@news.optonline.net> <3ED9202C.30200@newsguy.com> <3eda9f13.273692202@news.optonline.net> <3EDF58B5.1040208@newsguy.com> <3ee04676.644279046@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 

> >And what the hell is "not supported" supposed to mean? 
> 
> It means you cannot call their help desk on the phone. 

I seem to recall that one cannot call MF or Fujitsu's help desk on the
phone either, they require email problem notification.

Does this put Cobol into the 'not supported' categorey ?
 
> >There are
> >Haskell compilers which are still actively supported.  nhc98 was last
> >updated in March.
> 
> It runs on MacOS and POSIX, not Windows.

nhc98:

""Available for all Unix-like platforms (including MacOS-X,
Cygwin/Windows, Linux, Solaris, *BSD, AIX, HP-UX, Ultrix, IRIX,
etc.)""

also - in case you chage your assertion to 'I meant Windows without
Cygwin':

""The Glasgow Haskell compiler is a full implementation of Haskell,
[...] Available for a few Unix platforms (including Linux, Solaris,
*BSD, and MacOS-X) and also for Windows.

""Hugs 98 is conformant with Haskell 98. Available for all Unix
platforms including Linux, DOS, Windows 3.x, and Win 32 (Windows 95,
Win32s, NT) and Macintoshs. It has many libraries including Win32
libraries,""

Just a tiny amount of research might sometimes save you from looking
like the village idiot.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(reply depth: 72)_

- **From:** Michael Wojcik <mwojcik@newsguy.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-10T14:49:10-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3EE62826.4070309@newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <3eceb57b.67668169@news.optonline.net> <3ed3bc58.338283155@news.cis.dfn.de> <3ed41e32.422208968@news.optonline.net> <bb1gvo$429$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ed5d50e.44025608@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305291243.2cb17b76@posting.google.com> <3ed727b0.46486933@news.optonline.net> <3ED9202C.30200@newsguy.com> <3eda9f13.273692202@news.optonline.net> <3EDF58B5.1040208@newsguy.com> <3ee04676.644279046@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:
 > Michael Wojcik <mwojcik@newsguy.com> wrote:
 >> Robert Wagner wrote:
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
 > programming language?

For three reasons:

1. Excel didn't occur to me when I composed my off-the-cuff list, so I
didn't consider it for inclusion.

2. I haven't read that claim (which I'm suspicious of anyway).

3. I don't consider the Excel macro language a general-purpose language,
any more than I consider the sed language a general-purpose language,
even though they both may be Turing-complete.  That's a subjective
evaluation that may indeed be arbitrary, but I imposed it on my own
argument, not on yours, and didn't raise it in an attempt to salvage my
position, as you did.

 >> Python is a general-purpose language that has been used to build
 >> large applications.  It's interpreted, but it's not a "scripting
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
 > To me, scripting language means interpreted source.

Fair enough.  I don't think that's a useful definition, but I see little
benefit in trying to convince you of that.  (I also don't think it has
much etymological justification.  "scripting" languages were so named
because they were used to write "scripts", which named the actions that
other, preexisting programs should perform.  Like the scripts that human
actor perform, see?)

So I'll fall back on the more basic argument: why is a language
implemented as a source interpreter, rather than a compiler, not
elegible for the list of languages in which major applications are written?

 >>>> Haskell	Academic, not supported
 >>>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
 > an experimental telephony thing.

The same list, eh?  And how do you know what list that is?  The lists
I'm looking at include several major applications, at least to my
satisfaction.

No doubt you'll want to impose arbitrary conditions on "major
application" until none of the publicly-described Haskell ones fit.  How
tiresome.  Suffice it to say I find your bogus definitions unconvincing.

 >> And what the hell is "not supported" supposed to mean?
 >
 > It means you cannot call their help desk on the phone.

Ah.  You might have been clearer.  I'll grant you this one: commercial
support for a language is important in many instances.  Had you
originally written, say, "relatively few general-purpose programming
languages which are currently commercially supported", I suspect there
would have been less controversy.  Of course, it might have been wiser
yet to write, "I can only think of a few..." or some such, rather than
making a wild declaration and then having to wriggle when it was easily
shown wrong.

 >> There are Haskell compilers which are still actively supported.
 >> nhc98 was last updated in March.
 >
 > It runs on MacOS and POSIX, not Windows.

And the non sequitur of the year award goes to...  What's your point,
exactly?

Actually, according to the nhc98 mailing list, people have built it
under Cygwin to run on Windows.  The backend generates ISO C, so there's
nothing to prevent using it to compile Haskell programs for Windows.
Not that *that* has anything to do with Haskell's status as a language
used to write applications.
```

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-05-21T19:25:58+12:00

```
Robert Wagner wrote:

> If invoice-date < date-today shifts from words to a symbol then back to
> words.

So does: Invoice-Date     (note the hyphen)
         Date-Today

And so does, even more so:

      IF ( Invoice-Date > Date-Today )     (note parentheses)

<shrug> they are all keystrokes, its not like we have to switch to some 
other input method.
```

```
Aha .. the Socratic Method at work. You supply the premises and we'll supply the
logic. Pedantry. 

We could write 'date of invoice' (putting aside the fact that date is a reserved
word). Instead we make the qualifier 'invoice' into a prefix. Granted, it's less
literate but this ain't poetry, it's programming. 

On second thought, your point is well taken. We SHOULD write 'date of invoice'.
Thank you for eliciting that tergiversation.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(reply depth: 67)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2003-05-25T08:31:14-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<baqd2i$mod$1@panix1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3ecfb622.133381125@news.optonline.net> <baorsh$nvn$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ed0748e.182136306@news.optonline.net>`

```
In article <3ed0748e.182136306@news.optonline.net>,
Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net> wrote:
>docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
>
…[58 more quoted lines elided]…
>logic. Pedantry. 

Mr Wagner, *please* read carefully... *you* supplied the premise that 'The 
hyphen and parentheses are punctuation; they don't replace words'; *I* 
have asked you to support this with reasons.

If this be pedantry then please... make the most of it.

>
>We could write 'date of invoice' (putting aside the fact that date is a reserved
>word).

Good of you to admit this as a possibility, Mr Wagner... it certainly took 
you long enough.

>Instead we make the qualifier 'invoice' into a prefix.

Plural majestatus est, Mr Wagner... I do not know what 'we' do but I know 
that - logically speaking, of course - it can readily be said that 
'invoice' is a prefix as much as 'date' is a suffix... depending on the 
situation either one could take precedence.  Consider:

Which date is important?  The invoice-date.
What about the invoice is important?  The invoice-date.

>Granted, it's less
>literate but this ain't poetry, it's programming. 

It wasn't asked to be anything other than English, Mr Wagner... in this 
case English where hyphen in 'invoice-date' signified that the reader 
should interpret that combination of words and symbols as 'date of 
invoice', thereby using punctuation to replace words.

Since your assertion quoted above is 'The hyphen and parentheses are 
punctuation; they don't replace words' it is good to see you reverse 
yourself by stating, later, that one could write 'date of invoice'.

>
>On second thought, your point is well taken.

My point, Mr Wagner, was that you were wrong.

>We SHOULD write 'date of invoice'.

My point, Mr Wagner, had nothing to do with SHOULD or otherwise.

>Thank you for eliciting that tergiversation. 

I am not sure how to interpret your sudden slide into sesquipedalian 
verbiage, Mr Wagner... is this how you express a sort of schoolyardish 
frustration at having been caught in error?

DD
```

```
Jallan got it right. The hyphen is a ligature binding two words into one because
the compiler doesn't permit embedded spaces in data names.

>>word).

>punctuation; they don't replace words' it is good to see you reverse 
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>frustration at having been caught in error?

they wouldn't have used it.
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<299f1138.0305250707.5f9c8714@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3ecebe13.69868299@news.optonline.net> <bant9h$8hr$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ecfb622.133381125@news.optonline.net> <baorsh$nvn$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ed0748e.182136306@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote in message news:<3ed0748e.182136306@news.optonline.net>...
> docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
> 
…[65 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thank you for eliciting that tergiversation.

Both wrong.

English quite happily uses a noun as an adjective. Forms like "invoice
date", "birth date", "severance date", "mail date" or "mailing date",
"acceptance date" are normal and acceptable in English.

Since spaces are not allowed in variable names in most languages, if
we wish to use such phrases as variable names, we must connect them,
either directly abutting them as INVOICEDATE, InvoiceDate, or using a
connector symbol which must be the hyphen in COBOL and is often the
underbar in other computer languages.

The hyphen does not indicate reordering.

We would similarly use hyphen if we wanted to use instead
DATE-OF-INVOICE as a variable name in COBOL and use date_of_invoice or
possibly DateOfInvoice in some other languages.

Jim Allan
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(reply depth: 61)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-22T07:05:55-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bailg3$t2m$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0305102213.463660bf@posting.google.com> <3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <b9m745$akc$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ebf01e7.295898087@news.optonline.net> <b9ocfi$bmk$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ec03239.24014939@news.optonline.net> <299f1138.0305130818.6c218a3@posting.google.com> <3ec21484.101307304@news.optonline.net> <299f1138.0305141914.286dc784@posting.google.com> <3ec667d1.72442892@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305191427.12bf6003@posting.google.com> <3ec9d0dc.64902913@news.optonline.net> <baddsl$m0a$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ecabb10.124867872@news.optonline.net> <bag4ho$2fa$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3eccb560.254503258@news.optonline.net>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3eccb560.254503258@news.optonline.net...

> All alphabetic words are phonetic, by definition. Sure there are spelling
> irregularities and, yes, it's not perfectly phonetic because there are
more
> phonemes than letters in the alphabet.

WHAT????

TZQBLVMRF is an "alphabetic word" perfectly functional in the COBOL
language.  There is no requirement WHATEVER in COBOL that any character in a
COBOL word represent a *sound*.   There is no requirement that *anything* in
COBOL represent a sound, in English, Swahili, Tagalog, Tlingkit or Hill
Bondo.  None.

"Phonetic" and "phonemic" have *very* different meanings.  The "t" in "tell"
and the "t" in "battle" as pronounced in the US are represented by different
symbols in the International Phonetic Alphabet.  The former would, I
believe, most correctly be represented as an alveolar aspirated stop (t with
a following superscript h); the latter in standard American English would be
unaspirated (no superscript h), but in some dialects is more correctly a
glottal stop (represented by something that looks like a question mark
without the dot underneath).  They are all the same *phoneme* in American
English.

Neither one has anything *whatever* to do with COBOL.

I'm beginning to think I insulted Gilderoy Lockhart a few weeks ago.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(reply depth: 61)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-05-23T08:39:49+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bajcj2$uf5$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3ecabb10.124867872@news.optonline.net> <bag4ho$2fa$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3eccb560.254503258@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:

> I've seen shop standards which prohibited inline literals. I guess they
> want to be prepared in case the value of ONE changes from 1 to 2. <g>

In the days of 16K machines and crude compilers a new entry in the literal 
table would be created for each occurance, even if it had the same value as 
a previous one.

So if there were several ADD 1    TO .. then there would be several '1's in 
the literal table.  Putting a 77 ONE PIC <appropriate> VALUE 1. and ADD ONE 
.. saved many vital bytes.

Later compilers became sophisticated enough, when given enough memory, to 
ensure no duplication in the literal table so the saving was nullified.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(reply depth: 62)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-05-24T10:30:57+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ecf312d.99338917@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3ecabb10.124867872@news.optonline.net> <bag4ho$2fa$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3eccb560.254503258@news.optonline.net> <bajcj2$uf5$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>Robert Wagner wrote:
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>.. saved many vital bytes.

This calls to mind the RCA 301 -- the most inept computer ever made, seemingly
designed by managers rather than engineers. It had a disk drive make by
Wurlitzer which looked and worked exactly like a juke box. When it detected a
tape read error, the machine stopped and a red light came on. The operator was
expected to 'fix' the error in memory and resume at the next instruction. Its
card reader and printer were equally ideosyncratic. 

I called it CADET for Can't Add Doesn't Even Try because it didn't know how to
do arithmetic with logic gates. Instead it had 'arithmetic tables' at a fixed
location in (unprotected) low memory. To do 1+ 2, it indexed into a two
dimensional table and found the answer to be 3. Any application program could
alter the table. 

Those were the Good Old Days, when men were men (and some of the women were
too). Where's Mr. Peabody and his time machine?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ RCA301 was Re: Alphabets

_(reply depth: 63)_

- **From:** "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-05-25T18:20:01-03:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ED13381.EED05A14@istar.ca>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3ecabb10.124867872@news.optonline.net> <bag4ho$2fa$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3eccb560.254503258@news.optonline.net> <bajcj2$uf5$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ecf312d.99338917@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:
> 
> Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> card reader and printer were equally ideosyncratic.

Ah Yes, the old MRPE, DPE and the tapes with connections for threading
that I can't describe.  The shop I was at used the IBM 1402 for the card
reader punch and a drum printer on which you could execute print and
blow fuses by blanking out the print table that was in high memory.
> 
> I called it CADET for Can't Add Doesn't Even Try because it didn't know how to
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> alter the table.

On the other hand the branch instruction V1
atore-next-ins-addr,branch-location was ideal for the COBOL perform.  It
also had some nice move and scan instructions.  I found it easier to
program in machine code than use their assembler which didn't know how
to origin data areas to an even hundreds boundary.  I also liked the RPG
for it which somewhat resembled the much later DYL260 from DYLAKOR.
> 
> Those were the Good Old Days, when men were men (and some of the women were
> too). Where's Mr. Peabody and his time machine?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: RCA301 was Re: Alphabets

_(reply depth: 64)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-05-26T03:17:14+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ed18028.250653258@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3ecabb10.124867872@news.optonline.net> <bag4ho$2fa$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3eccb560.254503258@news.optonline.net> <bajcj2$uf5$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ecf312d.99338917@news.optonline.net> <3ED13381.EED05A14@istar.ca>`

```
"Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca> wrote:

>Robert Wagner wrote:
>> 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>> This calls to mind the RCA 301 -- the most inept computer ever made,
seemingly
>> designed by managers rather than engineers. It had a disk drive make by
>> Wurlitzer which looked and worked exactly like a juke box. When it detected a
>> tape read error, the machine stopped and a red light came on. The operator
was
>> expected to 'fix' the error in memory and resume at the next instruction. Its
>> card reader and printer were equally ideosyncratic.
>
>Ah Yes, the old MRPE, DPE and the tapes with connections for threading
>that I can't describe. 

The tapes had a hook, eye, leader system for mounting. It never worked right.
The first time I saw an operator dismount a tape, I couldn't believe my eyes. He
took a pair of scissors and cut the tape. To mount the next tape, he spliced it
to the leader. 

>The shop I was at used the IBM 1402 for the card
>reader punch and a drum printer on which you could execute print and
>blow fuses by blanking out the print table that was in high memory.

You missed out on the RCA card reader. It had three speeds. The programmer had
to upshift from one to the next. In high speed, it couldn't stop on one card. It
was driving the computer like a check sorter does. Here comes a card image,
ready or not.

The programmer had to know when the last card was near so he could start
downshifting. A marker was inserted a few hundred cards from the end. 

> I also liked the RPG
>for it which somewhat resembled the much later DYL260 from DYLAKOR.

Thank you. I designed and wrote DYL240, the first in the series, around 1967. A
guy named Bill Newcomer stole it and started DYLAKOR to market it. Mine was a
JIT compiler; it wrote generated code into memory and branched to it. Later
versions went to interpreted free-form code.

I had not seen fixed-form RPG at the time. That it came out similar was a
coincidence.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ 78-Levels (Was: Alphabets)

_(reply depth: 62)_

- **From:** LX-i <DanielJSNOSPAM@Knology.net>
- **Date:** 2003-05-24T21:48:30-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ED02EFE.5010600@Knology.net>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3ecabb10.124867872@news.optonline.net> <bag4ho$2fa$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3eccb560.254503258@news.optonline.net> <bajcj2$uf5$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston wrote:
> So if there were several ADD 1    TO .. then there would be several '1's in 
> the literal table.  Putting a 77 ONE PIC <appropriate> VALUE 1. and ADD ONE 
> ... saved many vital bytes.

This brings up a question...  I was reading through our COBOL manual, 
and saw the description of a 78-level "literal" definition.  It looks 
like a way to define a constant, but there's not much in the 
documentation about what exactly you're defining.  The syntax it gives is

78  literal-name VALUE [IS] literal-1.

So, I imagine one could do something similar to what you did above with...

78  One Value 1.

(Having the added bonus, I suppose, that this cannot be changed by 
procedure division code, whereas the 77-level could).

Anyone...  Is this how they work?  :)
```

```
>

You didn't say which compiler - but it is a feature of Micro Focus, as an
extension, to declare CONSTANTS  - and yes, they are cast in stone and can't be
changed.

78 MyValue            value 22.
78 MyText              value "Now is the time for all good men to etc.....".
78 Myfield               pic x(MyValue).

Add MyValue to NumberX giving NumberY
move Mytext to TextField OR display MyText

The new COBOL 2002 Standard has the word CONSTANT :-

01 MyText CONSTANT value 22.

Jimmy
```

```
James J. Gavan wrote:

> The new COBOL 2002 Standard has the word CONSTANT :-
> 
> 01 MyText CONSTANT value 22.

30 years or more ago the ICL XE13 compiler family (XE.. 11, 13, 16, 18, 20, 
A0,KB, etc.) had CONSTANT SECTION which made all declarations as constant 
values that could not be changed.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: 78-Levels (Was: Alphabets)

_(reply depth: 65)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-05-25T23:15:38+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ED14AF9.C20A1F7@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3ecabb10.124867872@news.optonline.net> <bag4ho$2fa$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3eccb560.254503258@news.optonline.net> <bajcj2$uf5$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ED02EFE.5010600@Knology.net> <3ED03162.D1E58E75@shaw.ca> <v401dvccouopn7ln5722is15s7ue7thp2u@4ax.com>`

```


Binyamin Dissen wrote:

> On Sun, 25 May 2003 05:07:12 GMT "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote:
>
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Binyamin,

I thought that X-Li might ask, but here's an example. It's OO, but the same principle
applies if this was in Global WS  :-

*>------------------------------------------------------------
Method-id. "messageBox".
*>------------------------------------------------------------
Working-storage section.
78 MC                          value 9.
78 ML                          value 35.
01 wa.
  05 pic x(ML) value "   ".                            *>  1
  05 pic x(ML) value "Make can't be blank   ".         *>  2
  05 pic x(ML) value "Frequency can't be zero ".       *>  3
  05 etc....
  05 pic x(ML) value "OK to delete this record ?".     *>  9
01 wb redefines wa.
   05 ws-message occurs MC pic x(ML).

copy "mssgparm.cpy" replacing ==(tag)== by ==ws==.
01 ws-response                      pic x(4) comp-5.
01 ws-WindowTitle   pic x(15) value "Probe records".

Linkage section.
01 lnk-response          pic x(4) comp-5.

Procedure Division returning lnk-Response.

 initialize ws-messageparams

 if  ValidationFlag = MC
     set TypeInformation to true
     set YesNo           to true

 else set TypeCritical   to true
      set OK             to true
 End-if

 move ws-Windowtitle to MessageLabel

 string
  ws-message(ValidationFlag)          delimited by size
  x"0d0a"                             delimited by size
  x"00"                               delimited by size
  into MessageText
 End-string

 invoke ErrorMessages "showMessage"
    using os-Parent, ws-MessageParams, returning lnk-Response

End Method "messageBox".
*>------------------------------------------------------------

Current literal length is 35. If I wanted to add message # 10 which had a literal of
45, I could make just two changes :-

78 MC                          value 10.
78 ML                          value 50.  (could be 45, but for expansion....).

Seems J4 agrees with you about the word CONSTANT. Now example above. Micro Focus in
early version of OO allowed for W/S in methods. If you had a variable MyField both
global and local, the local overrides the global while in the method.

J4 has decided that W/S cannot be in methods so my example above is screwed if I
substitute "Local-Storage Section". Although my Constants (78s) are OK, the
WA table of literals becomes gibberish.

Somebody wrote to J4 about Constants and I followed up tout suite !  In example above
substitute :-

-  "Local-Storage Section"
- 01 wa CONSTANT. *> at the 01 Level for a table.
  05 pic x(ML) value "   ".                                        *>  1
  05 pic x(ML) value "Make can't be blank   ".         *>  2
  05 etc....

Jimmy
```

- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3ecabb10.124867872@news.optonline.net> <bag4ho$2fa$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3eccb560.254503258@news.optonline.net> <bajcj2$uf5$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ED02EFE.5010600@Knology.net> <3ED03162.D1E58E75@shaw.ca> <v401dvccouopn7ln5722is15s7ue7thp2u@4ax.com>`

```
Significant consideration is being given to expanding the 2002 standard
syntax, or more probably functionality, to include the ability to provide
"read-only" records.  Tentative plans for the COBOL futures workshop
discussed earlier in this forum and scheduled for Wednesday June 25 in Las
Vegas (originally the last day of COBOLExpo 2003) currently include
presentation of a paper that discusses that very subject.   Personally, I
find 78-levels, the idea of the CONSTANT SECTION, and the current
elementary-constants-only syntax of the '2002 standard way too restrictive.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

```
On 25-May-2003, Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com> wrote:

> :>01 MyText CONSTANT value 22.
>
> Prefer that over 78 level.

Me too.   I don't use a compiler that has constants, but one use I could have
for a constant is as an initialized record or detail line.   As such, I want it
to be at a 01 level with filler values corresponding to the record where the
constant data will be moved to.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: 78-Levels (Was: Alphabets)

_(reply depth: 65)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-05-27T15:03:45-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0305271403.21aa04f@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3ecabb10.124867872@news.optonline.net> <bag4ho$2fa$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3eccb560.254503258@news.optonline.net> <bajcj2$uf5$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ED02EFE.5010600@Knology.net> <3ED03162.D1E58E75@shaw.ca> <v401dvccouopn7ln5722is15s7ue7thp2u@4ax.com>`

```
Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com> wrote

> :>You didn't say which compiler - but it is a feature of Micro Focus, as an
> :>extension, to declare CONSTANTS  - and yes, they are cast in stone and can't 
> :>be changed.

> A 78 with a picture? How does that work?

> :>01 MyText CONSTANT value 22.
> 
> Prefer that over 78 level.

78 lavels aren't constants, they are named literals.  Literals are, of
course, constant too.  Named literals have the advantage that they can
be used wherever a literal is required, not just as a constant value
where a variable is specified.

For example:

           78  Table-Size        VALUE 120.

           01  Table-Occurs        PIC S9(4) VALUE Table-Size.
           01  Table-A.
               03  Table-Item      OCCURS Table-Size.
                ...

              PERFORM ... UNTIL Table-Index > Table-Size

Then one place can change all dependancies for the table.

Arithmetic can also be done so that a 78 level is defined before a
record defining the start address, another defines the end address and
the difference gives a literal of the record size.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: 78-Levels (Was: Alphabets)

_(reply depth: 66)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-27T17:23:51-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bb0okc$3d3$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3ecabb10.124867872@news.optonline.net> <bag4ho$2fa$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3eccb560.254503258@news.optonline.net> <bajcj2$uf5$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ED02EFE.5010600@Knology.net> <3ED03162.D1E58E75@shaw.ca> <v401dvccouopn7ln5722is15s7ue7thp2u@4ax.com> <217e491a.0305271403.21aa04f@posting.google.com>`

```
Although what you say may OR MAY NOT be correct for Fujitsu, it certainly is
NOT true for Micro Focus.  78-levels are definitely "constants".  This is
why (to go back to one of the earlier questions), it is valid in Micro Focus
(but I don't know about Fujitsu) to code:

78  Const1  Value 12.
01  Elem  Pic X(Const1).

At least in "COBOL terms" one may *not* use a "literal" in the replication
factor of a PICTURE clause - but it is "reasonable" to use a constant there.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: 78-Levels (Was: Alphabets)

_(reply depth: 67)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-05-27T18:39:55-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0305271739.39370a99@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3ecabb10.124867872@news.optonline.net> <bag4ho$2fa$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3eccb560.254503258@news.optonline.net> <bajcj2$uf5$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ED02EFE.5010600@Knology.net> <3ED03162.D1E58E75@shaw.ca> <v401dvccouopn7ln5722is15s7ue7thp2u@4ax.com> <217e491a.0305271403.21aa04f@posting.google.com> <bb0okc$3d3$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote 

> 78  Const1  Value 12.
> 01  Elem  Pic X(Const1).
> 
> At least in "COBOL terms" one may *not* use a "literal" in the replication
> factor of a PICTURE clause - but it is "reasonable" to use a constant there.

Unfortunately the use of 'constant' refers to several different
mechanisms.

For example CONSTANT SECTION, and from what I gather an '02 CONSTANT
clause, the defined name may only be used as if it were a
working-storage item (and thus may have a picture) that may not be
changed.  A 78 isn't that, it is like a #define in C, perhaps they
should have been called macros as the value is substituted at compile
time.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(reply depth: 61)_

- **From:** jallan@smrtytrek.com (jallan)
- **Date:** 2003-05-25T07:50:43-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<299f1138.0305250650.1f65b40d@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <299f1138.0305130818.6c218a3@posting.google.com> <3ec21484.101307304@news.optonline.net> <299f1138.0305141914.286dc784@posting.google.com> <3ec667d1.72442892@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305191427.12bf6003@posting.google.com> <3ec9d0dc.64902913@news.optonline.net> <baddsl$m0a$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ecabb10.124867872@news.optonline.net> <bag4ho$2fa$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3eccb560.254503258@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote in message news:<3eccb560.254503258@news.optonline.net>...

> All alphabetic words are phonetic, by definition. Sure there are spelling
> irregularities and, yes, it's not perfectly phonetic because there are more
> phonemes than letters in the alphabet.

No.

Whose definition?

Having more phonemes than letters in the alphabet does not prevent
phonemic notation. One can use digraphs, as English does with TH and
CH, as other language versions of the Latin alphabet do more
consistantly with those or other characters. The Greeks represented
the NG sound of "wringer" by _gg_ in their alphabet.

One can also expand the alphabet with letters like ï¿½ and ï¿½ and ï¿½ and
ï¿½, or use diacritics as ï¿½, ï¿½, ï¿½.

The reason English spelling is so erratic has more to do with
vagueries of its spelling history, changes in pronunciation, and
conservatism in spelling. Some spellings descend from outright errors
such as the pseudo-learned spelling "island" for the proper "iland"
because ignorant pedants thought that "island" must be a shortening of
"isle-land". It isn't.

But by modern English dictionary *definition* _island_ is the proper
alphabetic spelling for the word we pronounce as if it were written
_eye-land_, regardless that it was never a phonetic spelling for the
word.

Alphabetic words, that is words indicated in alphabetic/syllabic/abjad
scripts, may also be represented by abbreviations, such as Mr., Ave.,
PhD, and even lb. for "pound(s)" or Xmas for "Christmas".

That these are most avoided in formal writing doesn't invalidate the
principle. Abbreviations are greatly used in field names and variable
names, such as AMT-PAYED, ADDR1, ID-NUN.

Modern writing also tends to contain acronyms that may be expanded to
the words which the acronym abbreviates or may be pronounced as if the
acronym was a word.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(reply depth: 62)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-27T10:02:55-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bb05nv$15ab$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <299f1138.0305130818.6c218a3@posting.google.com> <3ec21484.101307304@news.optonline.net> <299f1138.0305141914.286dc784@posting.google.com> <3ec667d1.72442892@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305191427.12bf6003@posting.google.com> <3ec9d0dc.64902913@news.optonline.net> <baddsl$m0a$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ecabb10.124867872@news.optonline.net> <bag4ho$2fa$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3eccb560.254503258@news.optonline.net> <299f1138.0305250650.1f65b40d@posting.google.com>`

```

"jallan" <jallan@smrtytrek.com> wrote in message
news:299f1138.0305250650.1f65b40d@posting.google.com...
> Some spellings descend from outright errors
> such as the pseudo-learned spelling "island" for the proper "iland"
> because ignorant pedants thought that "island" must be a shortening of
> "isle-land". It isn't.

Not sure that's the right etymology for the spelling of "island".  My
dictionary indicates that Old French "isle" came from Latin "insula", and if
memory serves the Great English Vowel Shift would account for the transition
from "eel" to "iyl" in pronunciation.  I think it more likely that the
*spelling* was influenced by the Normans.  The beginning of OE "iland" would
have been pronounced, I believe, approximately the same as OF "isle" at that
time.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

```
The etymology of _island_ from _ï¿½g_ or _ï¿½eg_ + _land_ can be found at
http://www.bartleby.com/61/81/I0248100.html.

It was in the sixteenth century, long after any specific Norman
influence, that pedants began introducing Latin letters back into
words that had long lost them, spelling _det_ as _debt_, _dout_ as
_doubt_ and _rein_ as _reign_.

The introduction of the _s_ into _island_ was part of this same
silliness.

Jim Allan

T

Jim Allan
```

- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <299f1138.0305141914.286dc784@posting.google.com> <3ec667d1.72442892@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305191427.12bf6003@posting.google.com> <3ec9d0dc.64902913@news.optonline.net> <baddsl$m0a$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ecabb10.124867872@news.optonline.net> <bag4ho$2fa$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3eccb560.254503258@news.optonline.net> <299f1138.0305250650.1f65b40d@posting.google.com> <bb05nv$15ab$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <299f1138.0306010946.48c03e4c@posting.google.com>`

```
"jallan" <jallan@smrtytrek.com> wrote in message
news:299f1138.0306010946.48c03e4c@posting.google.com...

> The etymology of _island_ from _�g_ or _�eg_ + _land_ can be found at
> http://www.bartleby.com/61/81/I0248100.html.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> silliness.

The Merriam-Webster website at
http://www.merriam-webster.com/cgi-bin/dictionary *seems to* indicate a
pre-thirteenth-century attestation for the spelling "island" as influenced
by the old French "isle" (almost certainly brought into English directly
with the Norman Invasion), though it doesn't cite a specific written
example.  My compact Oxford's at home, out of reach at the moment, but I
find the idea that the spelling of OE "iland" was influenced more directly
by OF "isle" in the immediate post-Norman period much more likely than it
was influenced by back-formation directly from Latin "insula" some four
centuries later; were that the case I'd expect an early "n" in the spelling
someplace.

    -Chuck Stevens
    -Chuck Stevens Frencvh .  I find this more likely than the assertion
that the spelling change was a retroversion from the influence of Latin
"insula";
>
> Jim Allan
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Jim Allan
```

- **From:** jallan@smrtytrek.com (jallan)
- **Date:** 2003-06-03T11:52:17-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<299f1138.0306031052.6bdb728@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0305191427.12bf6003@posting.google.com> <3ec9d0dc.64902913@news.optonline.net> <baddsl$m0a$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ecabb10.124867872@news.optonline.net> <bag4ho$2fa$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3eccb560.254503258@news.optonline.net> <299f1138.0305250650.1f65b40d@posting.google.com> <bb05nv$15ab$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <299f1138.0306010946.48c03e4c@posting.google.com> <bbg8b5$nek$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
"Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote in message news:<bbg8b5$nek$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>...
> "jallan" <jallan@smrtytrek.com> wrote in message
> news:299f1138.0306010946.48c03e4c@posting.google.com...
> 
> > The etymology of _island_ from _ï¿½g_ or _ï¿½eg_ + _land_ can be found at
> > http://www.bartleby.com/61/81/I0248100.html.

 
> The Merriam-Webster website at
> http://www.merriam-webster.com/cgi-bin/dictionary *seems to* indicate a
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> "insula";

Well there are a fair number of Middle English documents on the web. I
don't think you will find the spelling _island_ in any of them.

I've read a large amount of Middle English and I don't ever recall
seeing it there or in early modern English.

One *always* finds _iland_.

In the Norman French period you are talking about the _s_ in French
_isle_ (from Latin _insula_) would have been pronounced as written
(something like "eezleh" in English kludge phonetics) and a connection
between _iland_ and _isle_ would be unlikely to occur to anyone.

I'm not sure just when French dropped pronouncing of _s_ between a
vowel and following consonant, replacing it in writing first by a
superscript _s_, then later by a circumflex accent, hence modern
French _ï¿½le_ (and modern French _hï¿½tel_ for _hostel_, _pï¿½tï¿½_ cognate
with English _paste_).

The same idiotic ignorant pedantry led to Carlile being respelled as
Carlisle, though there was never an _s_ in the second element of
_castra lugwallum_.

Jim Allan
```

```
>French _�le_ (and modern French _h�tel_ for _hostel_, _p�t�_ cognate

My junior-year French professor, Mr Bruce Venable - may he sleep with the
angels! - ('Three-Dollar Bruce', as we used to call him) taught us,
e'er-so-pithily, that 'The circumflex is the tombstone of an 's' that has
passed on.'

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(reply depth: 59)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-05-21T19:48:01+12:00

```
Robert Wagner wrote:

> I was disabused of that notion months ago. I'm just explaining the reasons
> for my  preferences. If you don't want to consider reasonable 
> explanations, 

Oh, I considered them, I put down an example that you may write and how I 
would do it.  I found that your 'reason' was wrong (for me), I could 
understand my example far more quickly and easily.  I found no difficulty 
at all in using all the keys on the keyboard and felt no need to restrict 
myself to 26 or so.

Now it may be that your results would be the opposite, because that is what 
you are used to.

> go ahead and write code the way you always have.

I certainly do not write 'the way I always have', and never have done.  My 
style changes as I find improvements and take from other languages that I 
learn.

I just see no benefit at all from the use of words for = < > etc., not 
since these symbols became available on the printers and card punches 35 
years ago or so.  Of course before that I would have used them.
```

```
"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3ecb0e2a.146144909@news.optonline.net...
<snip>
>
> I was disabused of that notion months ago. I'm just explaining the reasons
for
> my  preferences. If you don't want to consider reasonable explanations, go
ahead
> and write code the way you always have.
>

Ah, "there's the rub"

You start out with some semblance of rationality.  You acknowledge that you
have learnt NOT to try and force your STYLE on others.  You then indicate
that you were simply explaining your preference.

  So far you are totally reasonable and what you are saying is appropriate
and accepted by many/most posters.

Then you RUIN it by assuming that explanations of YOUR style preferences
should impact how other code.

Once you "internalize" that stating your preferences, even explaining your
preferences is perfectly reasonable BUT that expecting others to agree that
YOUR STYLE PREFERENCES are "better" than their style preferences will result
in flames, "ad hominem" comments, and the general "hope that you will go
away"!!!!
```

```
>"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message

…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
>away"!!!!

I see your point in re arrogance. But my most severe critic, Richard, didn't
object. He said his style is constantly evolving (as is mine), he just disagreed
on the particular points we were discussing. That's rational. 

If someone convinced me that his particular style point was better than mine,
I'd adopt it. My preferences are not carved in granite. IMO, there is too little

"Men cling to arguments like a drunk clings to a lamppost .. not for

 . a-paragragh.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: PREFERENCES vs "better" styles (was: Alphabets

_(reply depth: 61)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-05-23T07:55:18+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<baj9vl$tf0$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3ecb0e2a.146144909@news.optonline.net> <bag7pe$p1r$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <3eccb112.253400801@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:

>>> If you don't want to consider reasonable explanations,
>>> go ahead and write code the way you always have.

> I see your point in re arrogance. But my most severe critic, Richard,
> didn't object. 

I found your parting shot to be marginal.  It _almost_ sounded like 'If you 
don't want to do it my better way, carry on writing like you did in the 
60s.'.

> He said his style is constantly evolving (as is mine), he
> just disagreed on the particular points we were discussing. That's
> rational.

Not only did I disagree about the style of using words, but I thought that 
your arguments attempting to support this style were fatuous, arbitrary and 
irrelevant.
 
> If someone convinced me that his particular style point was better than
> mine, I'd adopt it. My preferences are not carved in granite. IMO, there
> is too little discussion of style in CLC BECAUSE of the emotional defenses
> it evokes.

If people (such as you) just said 'this is the style that I like' then 
there would be positive discussion.  However, when attacks are made on 
particular style points attempting to force them to change, then you will 
get responses.
 
> "Men cling to arguments like a drunk clings to a lamppost .. not for
> illumination but for support."  -- Giles StAubin

Yes, I have noticed how long you will hang on to an argument about things 
that are completely irrellevant, such as 'linguistics' and what the Goths 
did.
 
> Since reading here, I've changed my style of periods to
> 
>  . a-paragragh.
> 
> No more in-line periods. They appear only on paragraph headers.

I dislike that entirely.  It is an improvement over your previous style of 
a full stop on the end of the last statement of a paragraph. In fact you 
had made a point of objecting to full stops at the end of statements 
because they may be missed when indenting.

Howver, the full-stop in front of the next procedure label has the 
structural failing that it is syntactically part of the previous procedure 
but occurs in the lines of another.  You are physically overlapping the 
extents of the procedures.

There is also a discontiuity in that the first label does not have a full 
stop in front of it and the full stop for the last procedure does not have 
a following label to put it in front of.

As a practical issue I sometimes build programs by having 'stubs' for 
paragraphs that I am performing but haven't yet written.  A procedure may 
be empty but an empty one does not have a full stop.  Other times I may 
want to comment out the code in a procedure leaving the label to be 
performed.  Commenting out lines is easy - keyboard macro - and I would 
also comment out the full stop that is the end of the procedure that is on 
its own line.  However with the style you adopted it makes too much work - 
_because_ you have overlapped the procedures.

But that's OK.  If I ever have to work on code that does have this style I 
can just make up a keyboard macro and redo the whole program.  If I don't 
have to work on it I don't care.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: PREFERENCES vs "better" styles (was: Alphabets

_(reply depth: 62)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-05-24T22:17:08+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ecfe051.144181751@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3ecb0e2a.146144909@news.optonline.net> <bag7pe$p1r$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <3eccb112.253400801@news.optonline.net> <baj9vl$tf0$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>Robert Wagner wrote:
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>60s.'.

If it sounded like that, I apologize. I wasn't thinking of the 60s, I was
thinking of <ahem> 'unusual' stylistic preferences. I won't add fuel to the fire
by giving examples of some I've seen.

>> If someone convinced me that his particular style point was better than
>> mine, I'd adopt it. My preferences are not carved in granite. IMO, there
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>get responses.

It is impossible to advocate one style without disparaging its corollary. I
thought full stops vs. explicit terminators was such an either/or choice.


>> Since reading here, I've changed my style of periods to
>> 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>because they may be missed when indenting.

Well, moving the full stop to the next paragraph answers that objection.

>Howver, the full-stop in front of the next procedure label has the 
>structural failing that it is syntactically part of the previous procedure 
>but occurs in the lines of another.  You are physically overlapping the 
>extents of the procedures.

So what? If EVERY paragraph name has a full stop, one can cut and paste
paragraphs all day without error. Just make sure there's a full stop at the
bottom. Better, put a name on it:

. end-of-program.

Standard COBOL will not compile an empty paragraph, but most compilers will. If
that gives you heartburn, add EXIT.

>There is also a discontiuity in that the first label does not have a full 
>stop in front of it and the full stop for the last procedure does not have 
>a following label to put it in front of.

The first one does. I omit the full stop on the procedure division header, which
has a side benefit of permitting me to cut and paste the parameter list to or
from the call. 

I'm considering omitting the first paragraph name. It has no function. It is
NEVER referenced. Why is it there?

>As a practical issue I sometimes build programs by having 'stubs' for 
>paragraphs that I am performing but haven't yet written.  A procedure may 
>be empty but an empty one does not have a full stop.  

Sure it does, on the next paragraph or end-of-program.

Other times I may 
>want to comment out the code in a procedure leaving the label to be 
>performed.  Commenting out lines is easy - keyboard macro - and I would 
>also comment out the full stop that is the end of the procedure that is on 
>its own line.  However with the style you adopted it makes too much work - 
>_because_ you have overlapped the procedures.

I don't follow. Can you provide an example?

I put the full stop in column 2, which gives the option of commenting the entire
paragraph including its name. 

>But that's OK.  If I ever have to work on code that does have this style I 
>can just make up a keyboard macro and redo the whole program.  If I don't 
>have to work on it I don't care.

My editor doesn't have a macro language of its own. One can write macros in
COBOL, C or any other language that compiles to an .exe. I load them like dll's.

Of course it also has keystroke-recording macros, but their ability to make
decisions is limited to Find and FindNext (using regular expressions). They
cannot compare across line breaks.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: PREFERENCES vs "better" styles (was: Alphabets

_(reply depth: 63)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2003-05-24T18:37:44-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<baos7o$pi6$1@panix1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3eccb112.253400801@news.optonline.net> <baj9vl$tf0$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ecfe051.144181751@news.optonline.net>`

```
In article <3ecfe051.144181751@news.optonline.net>,
Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net> wrote:

[snip]

>It is impossible to advocate one style without disparaging its corollary.

Mr Wagner, this is wrong.  Consider:

'I advocate (solution x) as it produces the most efficient machine-code.'

'I advocate (solution y) as it produces the most easily-maintainable 
program source code.'

Both of these express advocacy based on a criterion; neither states that 
any other criterion is inferior.

On the other hand, Mr Wagner, were you to have said (note the use of the
subjunctive) '*I* (i.e., Mr Wagner) find it impossible to advocate one
style without disparaging its corollary' then you might have come closer
to a statement of fact.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: PREFERENCES vs "better" styles (was: Alphabets

_(reply depth: 63)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```

On 24-May-2003, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:

> It is impossible to advocate one style without disparaging its corollary. I
> thought full stops vs. explicit terminators was such an either/or choice.

I apologize for not being more clear in my discussions.   Do you have any advice
on how I could have improved on my communication skills to make sure I was not
making this an either/or choice?



> Standard COBOL will not compile an empty paragraph, but most compilers will.
> If that gives you heartburn, add EXIT.

If I am modifying a program with EXIT type logic and get compiler warnings for
empty paragraphs, I do not insert EXIT, I insert CONTINUE.   This has the
further advantage in that I can have:

 EXIT-PARAGRAPH.
     CONTINUE.
D   DISPLAY "Reached the exit".

And have it work whether or not I have WITH DEBUGGING MODE on.
```
