###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(reply depth: 59)_

- **From:** jallan@smrtytrek.com (jallan)
- **Date:** 2003-05-25T07:21:57-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<299f1138.0305250621.3fea0e32@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3ec667d1.72442892@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305191427.12bf6003@posting.google.com> <3ec9d0dc.64902913@news.optonline.net> <bae29l$lpi$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ecb0e2a.146144909@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner orginally posted at
http://groups.google.com/groups?selm=3eba027b.118744531%40news.optonline.net&oe=utf-8&output=gplain

> The only reactionary languages, to my knowledge, are Chinese,
> math and programming.  
> Programmers use symbols such as = and > to represent words without
> understanding that they're taking step backward in linguistics. They think
> all that matters is less typing. 

Many have disagreed.

I argued that most of the ideographic symbols used mathematical
notations and programming (and logic and chemistry and linguistics)
are not a step backward in respect to understanding in those
disciplines, but a step forward, despite any particular misuse or
abuse that might be decried.

Modern mathematics and logic largely grew from and fed back into
improvements in notation.

There were statements from individual programmers against the canard
that those using symbols (and those who created so many programming
languages that use symbols) "think all that matters is less typing."

Many, including myself, find that use of symbols adds clarity, that
">=" is clearer than "ge" even though it involves more typing, and far
clearer than a long phrase of words in the same symbols used for
variables.

The ideographic symbols are easier to parse mentally when considering
relation concepts than are words.

You have admitted this Roger, for Arabic digits making up numbers and
for  common arithmetic symbols, that those those who invented these 
notations and have spread them and those who use them do not "think
all that matters is less typing".

Yet it seems to be, as a matter of principle, that "less typing" or
less writing, brevity in notation, is a good thing, *if it does not
lead to confusion*. Who for example would want to be forbidden to use
the COMPUTE statement in COBOL, to be forced to work out any
calculation line by line?

If I can express myself more clearly and make do with less characters
by using Arabic digits and arithmetical operators instead of spelling
the statements out in full, of course I should go with the shorter and
clearer notation when the exact words that people use to read my
notation are unimportant.

Yet you resisted claims that exactly the same logic applies to use of
the symbols "<", "<=", ">", ">=" and to the symbol "=" when it is used
in a condition.

You do admit to using these symbols in conditions at times when they
make the condition clearer to you at
http://groups.google.com/groups?selm=3ebe5d08.253684134%40news.optonline.net&oe=utf-8&output=gplain

> I sometimes use symbols in conditions when doing so makes the statement fit on
> one line. They also seem clearer in a range test, because the limits are closer
> together: if foo not < 2 and not > 5. But using = and > routinely offends my
> linguistic sense.

You posted at http://groups.google.com/groups?selm=3ec2273b.106098230%40news.optonline.net&oe=utf-8&output=gplain

> I find words faster to comprehend.

Yet you finds the Arabic ideographic digits superior (for most
purposes) to words and arithmetic symbols acceptable and do not want
to replace them with spelled-out words.

You talked about redundancy at
http://groups.google.com/groups?selm=3ec21484.101307304%40news.optonline.net&oe=utf-8&output=gplain

> I don't like that notation because a single typing error can change > GT to LT or
> GE. One must inspect the two symbols as closely as >. 

> 'Greater' had redundancy built in. An error in one letter cannot change it to a
> word that compiles. 

But again that argument against using "<", ">" and relational "="
applies ust as well against the symbols your happy to accept, the
Arabic digits, the arithmetic operators, and single letter variables
I, J, K used as counters. These last ought to be very easy to mistype,
all three adjoining and struck by the same finger by a touch-typist on
a standard keyboard.

From http://groups.google.com/groups?selm=3eca52b4.98147976%40news.optonline.net&oe=utf-8&output=gplain

> From That feature is called elision. Multiplication is there, and being implied by
> omission. It would also be correct to say 'five of something' or 'five instances
> of something', just as it would be mathematically correct to say 5ï¿½a. 

> COBOL is one of the few programming languages that offers elision, as > in 'if a
> equal to 1 or 2 or 3'. 

First, I pointed out that "multiplication" (if you must call the
normal use of numbers to indicate how many "multiplication") is
regularly  indicated in algebraic notation by placing letters against
each other without spacing.

Calling the most normal syntactic way that a language or notation
represents something is elision is very odd.

It odd to claiming that in English spelling the normal lack of
hyphenation between syllables is elision. Putting multiplication dots
between all members of a term in algebraic notation would be as
needlessly redundant and unattractive as hyphenating all English words
in spelling.

But the elision of COBOL mentioned is real elision, following a
pattern of elision in English.

Why is this done? Just to avoid typing? Do you use this feature?

(Personally, I see nothing wrong with it conceptually, and nothing
*much* wrong with it in COBOL's implementation. The difference in
treatment of NOT before <= and >= as against the other operators is
inconsistant.)

>> If five-tenths is equal to one-half
>> If five divided by ten is equal to one divided by two
>> If five-tenths is equal to one divided by two
>> If five divided by ten is equal to one-half
>> If five is to ten as one is to two

> In programming, the second interpretation is used. 

No.

My point is that in programming (as well as in mathematics) all these
interpretations are *equally* valid for a reader for parsing and
pondering the meaning of the code and also for working the other way,
for determining how to code a calculation.

Something close to the second statement would be the most normal
transformation of the symbols when reading aloud or silently to
oneself. But it doesn't have to be the one used when another one makes
more sense to the reader in a particular context.

Restricting oneself to a single word-by-word interpretation of symbols
nullifies some of their value. They are ideographs, not logographs.
Similarly in translation for another spoken language into English one
does not usually proceed word for word, and may at times quite
correctly translate the same word in the source language by different
words in English.

> My objection is to the context in which symbols are used. In 'if invoice-date
> less than date-today', we see all words. In 'if invoice-date < date-today', we
> must mentally shift gears from words to symbol to words. To what end?  Just to
> save typing.

Again and again and again you don't notice that your arguments apply
just as cogently to the symbols you accept.

If gears are shifted, they are shifted for me just as much and exactly
in the same way for:

COMPUTE SALES-TAX = SALES-TTL - REFUND * SALES-TAX-RATE

You accept that Arabic numerals are clearer, arithmetic operators are
clearer, but seem unable to grasp that myself and others, for *exactly
the same reasons*, also find  other symbols also clearer in a
logical/mathematical context than the corresponding spelled-out words.

I vaguely recall at some time in primary school coming to the
realization that mostly one read from pages word for word exactly what
the pages said, but that for numbers, and especially for dollar
amounts, one read not the exact words but extracted the meaning from
characters that represented the idea and then expressed (silently or
aloud) that idea in *any* words that fit.

The sentence "I spent $10.25" might be read aloud as "I spent ten
dollars and twenty-five cents" or as "I spent ten twenty-five".

I don't ever recall such shifting of gears troublesome. I you do, then
you may indeed have some reason for preferring the spelled-out words
to symbols. But that can't apply to those of us who feel the opposite
about shifting into fast gear, and it doesn't logically explain why
you aren't bothered by gear shifting in other cases of ideographs.

Again that tired accusation (denying all words to the contrary and
your own experience with Arabic numbers and arithmetic operators) that
people *only* use symbols to save typing, not because they are
clearer.

The question is why certain symbols are not clear to you.

>> (Also, many compilers will complain about undefined variables, but
not
>> about literals, which many typos in variables are caught where
typos
>> in literals are not.)

> Not so with Fortran and Basic. They say, 'Ohh, a new variable I haven't seen
> before. I'll set it up.' That's what aversion to typing give us.

Garbage.

That is what people experimenting with new computer languages give us
and will continue to give us, flaws as well as virtues.

When I use any language with a compiler allows enabling checking for
predefining of variables, I always turn it on. Visual Basic's compiler
has an "/optionexplicit" setting. In modern Fortran all variables must
be defined.

For languages that don't insist on it, I still always try to
pre-define variables, even in the smallest temporary two-line program.
I use prefix camel notation in FoxPro to avoid type problems and scope
problems and possible misidentitication of a memory variable with a
field name. My counters are m.lnI, m.lnJ, m.lnK.

I do *whatever* extra typing is necessary to ensure clarity and
safety, clarity for me.

I rightly won't do extra typing for a more profuse notation when a
more concise notation seems clearer to me.

If you felt spelled-out words are superior and that extra typing
always  helps clarity, you could avoid COMPUTE altogether in COBOL,
just use the ADD, SUBTRACT, MULTIPLY, and DIVIDE statements and do a
PERFORM loop to caculate _to the power of_.

Does the new COMPUTE command in COBOL only save typing? Or does it
also make things clearer when several calculations can be put on one
line instead of each one on a separate line? All other general
computer langauges have a syntax similar to COBOL's COMPUTE statement?

Verbosity is not always clarity, may often be the opposite of clarity.
Look at legal jargon. Look at COBOL, blamed for its needless
verbosity, a reasonable experiment at the time. But it is not one that
*any* other creators of computer languages have felt worth imitating,
so far as I know.

> "Exceeds" is one word. 

You miss the point that an ideograph can often be expressed in one
word, but it does not always have to be expressed in the same one
word.

> I tried to give reasons above: 
> .. An incomplete notational system.
> .. Not as familiar as math to many/most readers.

Finally some reasons.

First, the notation system expressible by symbols is only incomplete
just the same way that the notation in most computer languages is
incomplete. Even c has the sizeof operator which is a word not a
symbol. (And $ was actually available! How did Kernaghan and Ritchie
miss it?) There is nothing corresponding to the ** operator in c. You
have to use a function.

COBOL actually has a reasonably complete set of arithmetic operators
represented by symbols. It has a complete set of equality/inequality
operators represented by symbols. Only the three Boolean operators are
represented by words. All their spelled-out words are very short and
perhaps therefore do not so much call out for symbols as some other
operators.

But I can't understand the logic that because COBOL doesn't use
symbols for all operators, I should therefore as much as possible not
use symbols for any of them, even though I find expressions with
symbols clearer. The more logical approach is to take what I can get.

If COBOL did introduce such symbols as & | ~, would the
equal/inequality operators suddenly become acceptable to you, or would
you still refuse to use them because & | ~ are not familar to generic
readers, whoever they are.

As to "<" and ">" not being familiar to most readers, your suggestion
that "EQUAL" should be distinguished from "=" as relative "EQUAL"
versus assignment "=" is not familiar to the same kind of readers who
would not be familiar with "<" and ">".

Again your arguments conflict with each other.

I think it probably took me about ten minutes when first encountering
"<" and ">" along with "<=" and ">=" in BASIC to become totally
familiar with them.

How many readers of COBOL code are not familiar with these symbols? Do
you advocate avoiding them just on the chance that some non-programmer
might want to read COBOL code and might not understand those symbols?

(Should mathematicians then avoid them and any other *special* symbols
for the same reasons?)

By the same logic arithmetic signs should never have been introduced
in schools because people were previously unfamiliar with them.

A few new symbols in any programming language are no more likely to
cause difficulties than the spelled-out keywords that the reader is
unfamiliar with and differences in syntax from what the reader is used
to.

But in fact the symbols "<" and ">" are familar to almost anyone who
has worked with any notation involving types of inequality.

They are used in all spreadsheets I am familiar with, in most computer
languages, including almost all those taught at schools below
unversity level, including COBOL. (HTML and related SGML languages
would be would be exceptions.)

Should Microsoft and other spreadsheet manufacturers implement
"greater than or equal" as an option in their spreadsheets to aid all
those using them who have found ">=" to so hard to grasp, to help
those who want to read the formulas that use them without difficulty?

Do you have similar problems with * for multiplication and ** or ^ for
_to the power of_?

I've worked with people on spreadsheets, and as you indicate, it is
AND and OR and NOT that cause trouble there and in programming
languages.

Yet these operators are spelled out in full in Excel and in many
programming languages.

No-one using spreadsheets seems to have problems with something like
">=". If there is a problem it is with the concepts behind them, not
with the symbols, for example the normal forgetting that the opposite
of "<" is not ">" but ">=".

COBOL allowed from its beginnings:

A = B OR NOT >= C OR D

You would seem to prefer:

A EQUAL TO B OR NOT GREATER OR EQUAL TO C OR D

Or perhaps you doen't like the compressed notation which saves typing,
and would prefer people to output:

A EQUAL TO B OR A NOT GREATER OR EQUAL TO C OR A GREATER OR EQUAL TO D

Cobol also allows something closer to what other languages would
consider a more normal notation:

A = B OR A NOT >= C OR A >= D
or
A = B OR NOT A >= C OR A >= D

All the symbol notations get rid of those stupid extra OR's that
appear in the full word notation, the OR's that one might say when
pronouncing the symbols, but which only clutter up the notation when
written.

The word notation may be more readible to some if it comes to simply
reading the words, but it is not more *parsible* to most when one
wants to study such a line of code for its meaning.

"GREATER THAN OR EQUAL TO" is not idiomatic English in any case.

It is a mathematics jargon phrase used to transliterate directly the
mathematical notation equivalent to ">=".

In English people (outside of a mathematics or programming environment
influenced by this notation) do not normally say something like "an
offer greater or equal to five thousand dollars" but rather "an offer
of five thousand dollars or more" (suggesting some kind of range
notation) or "an offer of at least five thousand dollars".

The phrase "at least" is better in expressing the meaning of the
operator in something close to a single word. Unfortunately it is not
intuitive that an operator ideograph ">=" with a greater-than sign in
it should be acurrately expressable by a phrase containing "least".
Yet it is.

If COBOL were really English-like, we might have had something like:

EQUALS which when negated could be expressed as DIFFERS FROM,
LESS THAN which when negated could be expressed as AT MOST,
MORE THAN which when negated could be expressed as AT LEAST.

Instead we have literal transliterations of the operator symbols.

Might as well use the symbols themselves, especially as the words were
quite possibly only introduced into COBOL as substitutes to use in
environments where the symbols themselves did not display or print
properly.

(I probably normally subvocalize something like "greater-equal" for
">=" for that operator if I at all say it rather than just see the
concept.)

It seems from the discussion that any mental shifting of gears that
might occur between processing spelled-out words and processing
ideographs doesn't bother most people here. It is usually not
noticeable to us, just as we don't notice it when reading $500.00 or
-25.5% or "Smith & Co."

It also appears that most people here find the symbols clearer than
the words despite your continued claim that people *only* use them
because they are shorter.

As far as I know, no other language has imitated COBOL in introducing
verbose phrases for equality/inequality operators. Why not?

Your problems with "<", ">" and "=" in conditions are not supported by
any rationale that holds against the widespread and honest perception
of myself and others that statements containing them *are* more
readable than statements containing words, are more quickly and easily
understood.

That is what counts most.

Logic about why we should not find such statements more readable can't
prevail against the fact that we do, especially when these rationales
mostly apply equally to the arithmetic notation that you aren't
bothered by.

From your arguments I get the impression that you have an irrational
dislike for symbols (which is logically not worse than an irrational
liking for symbols). You are slow to take to them.

The dislike can be overcome in individual cases by familiarity, as it
has been with Arabic digits and arithmetic symbols. But you have
trouble seeing that many others (presumably the majority to judge from
the use of symbols in programming languages) don't have any difficulty
in accepting symbols as long as new ones don't come too quickly in too
great a quantity. But the same is true for new spelled-out words.

But if you find that Arabic digits and arithmetic symbols are an
improvement over spelled-out words, even if you generally dislike
symbols, then you should be able to understand that if you had been
introduced to "<" and ">" earlier and made to use them, you would feel
exactly the same about those symbols for the same reasons, and also
with symbols for "and", "or" and "not".

Some, probably most of us, generally dislike hiding operators in words
just as with arithmetic operators. Symbols help the mind to parse the
expression, which is why arithmetic operators and the other operators
were adopted in use for notation long before computers.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(reply depth: 60)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)

```
jallan@smrtytrek.com (jallan) wrote:

>Robert Wagner orginally posted at
>http://groups.google.com/groups?selm=3eba027b.118744531%40news.optonline.net&oe=utf-8&output=gplain
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>Many have disagreed.

Are you saying the greater than number of people believing something, the more
likely it is to be true? That's he fallacy of argumentum ad populum. 

>I argued that most of the ideographic symbols used mathematical
>notations and programming (and logic and chemistry and linguistics)
>are not a step backward in respect to understanding in those
>disciplines, but a step forward, despite any particular misuse or
>abuse that might be decried.

I think those ideographs are a carry-forward from the days when statements were
handwritten. 

Chemists no longer use the old count-the-atoms notation such as C6 H12 O6.
Glucose, fructose and galactose all have that same formula. They use graphics to
show structure, or at least they show each radical separately. 

>Modern mathematics and logic largely grew from and fed back into
>improvements in notation.

Following that logic, we should we use one character variable names, because
that's what they use in math.

>If I can express myself more clearly and make do with less characters
>by using Arabic digits and arithmetical operators instead of spelling
>the statements out in full, of course I should go with the shorter and
>clearer notation when the exact words that people use to read my
>notation are unimportant.

>Yet you resisted claims that exactly the same logic applies to use of
>the symbols "<", "<=", ">", ">=" and to the symbol "=" when it is used
>in a condition.

A hasty generalization. Because ideographs are good for numbers does not show
they are good for other purposes. 

>You do admit to using these symbols in conditions at times when they
>make the condition clearer to you at
>http://groups.google.com/groups?selm=3ebe5d08.253684134%40news.optonline.net&oe=utf-8&output=gplain
>
>> I sometimes use symbols in conditions when doing so makes the statement fit
on
>> one line. They also seem clearer in a range test, because the limits are
closer
>> together: if foo not < 2 and not > 5. But using = and > routinely offends my
>> linguistic sense.

I wish we could use THRU in IF, as we can in EVALUATE and VALUE. 

>From
http://groups.google.com/groups?selm=3eca52b4.98147976%40news.optonline.net&oe=utf-8&output=gplain
>
>> That feature is called elision. Multiplication is there, and being implied by
>> omission. It would also be correct to say 'five of something' or 'five
instances
>> of something', just as it would be mathematically correct to say 5�a. 
>
>> COBOL is one of the few programming languages that offers elision, as > in
'if a
>> equal to 1 or 2 or 3'. 
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>each other without spacing.

That forces you to use single-character variable names. 

>It odd to claiming that in English spelling the normal lack of
>hyphenation between syllables is elision. Putting multiplication dots
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>Why is this done? Just to avoid typing? Do you use this feature?

Yes, as much as possible. I miss it when programming in other languages. I keep
thinking how ironic their supporters call Cobol verbose as I'm duplicating the
subject over and over. 

>(Personally, I see nothing wrong with it conceptually, and nothing
>*much* wrong with it in COBOL's implementation. The difference in
>treatment of NOT before <= and >= as against the other operators is
>inconsistant.)

That's a good reason to avoid <=.  <= means NOT >. NOT <= would be NOT NOT >.
Double negatives are not permitted in COBOL, which is why NOT <=  is not
permitted.

>> My objection is to the context in which symbols are used. In 'if invoice-date
>> less than date-today', we see all words. In 'if invoice-date < date-today',
we
>> must mentally shift gears from words to symbol to words. To what end?  Just
to
>> save typing.
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>COMPUTE SALES-TAX = SALES-TTL - REFUND * SALES-TAX-RATE

I see that. You were so distracted by gear shifting that you created a program
bug. You're about to charge sales tax on the refund rather than the sale.

>You accept that Arabic numerals are clearer, arithmetic operators are
>clearer, but seem unable to grasp that myself and others, for *exactly
>the same reasons*, also find  other symbols also clearer in a
>logical/mathematical context than the corresponding spelled-out words.

We get something in return for math notation: the ability to maniuplate and
transform statements, not to mention adding a column of numbers. When
manipulating logic statements, changes are to the logical operators AND, OR and
NOT; there is no need to change relational operators (assuming we avoid >=). In
other words, they chose the wrong three words to express symbolically. 

I wouldn't object to > if we had symbols for AND, OR and NOT. Then we would have
a complete notational system for logic. Suppose Cobol provided a symbol for
subtraction (comparison) while making you write words for addition (or) and
multiplication (and). Would you support using such an incomplete notation?

>Again that tired accusation (denying all words to the contrary and
>your own experience with Arabic numbers and arithmetic operators) that
>people *only* use symbols to save typing, not because they are
>clearer.

>The question is why certain symbols are not clear to you.

I don't find the symbol > any more nor less clear than 'greater'. They mean the
same thing. 

>>> (Also, many compilers will complain about undefined variables, but
>not
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>and will continue to give us, flaws as well as virtues.

They did it to avoid the 'trouble' of defining variables. 

>When I use any language with a compiler allows enabling checking for
>predefining of variables, I always turn it on. Visual Basic's compiler
>has an "/optionexplicit" setting. In modern Fortran all variables must
>be defined.

Yes, we learned that the hard way. 

Modern Fortran sounds like an oxymoron. 

>I do *whatever* extra typing is necessary to ensure clarity and
>safety, clarity for me.

Most experienced programmers do the same. We learned that the hard way too. 

>If you felt spelled-out words are superior and that extra typing
>always  helps clarity, you could avoid COMPUTE altogether in COBOL,
>just use the ADD, SUBTRACT, MULTIPLY, and DIVIDE statements and do a
>PERFORM loop to caculate _to the power of_.

I don't object to arithmetic symbols. 

>Does the new COMPUTE command in COBOL only save typing? Or does it
>also make things clearer when several calculations can be put on one
>line instead of each one on a separate line?

I agree. COMPUTE is not new. 

>Verbosity is not always clarity, may often be the opposite of clarity.
>Look at legal jargon. Look at COBOL, blamed for its needless
>verbosity, a reasonable experiment at the time. But it is not one that
>*any* other creators of computer languages have felt worth imitating,
>so far as I know.

I dispute the assertion that COBOL is verbose. Write the same program in COBOL
and C. You'll find the line count about the same, unless you contrive a case to
make one language look better. 

>> I tried to give reasons above: 
>> .. An incomplete notational system.
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>have to use a function.

Nor does C any string handling. How unreasonable is that in a general purpose
language? C doesn't have SEARCH, you're forced to roll your own. It punishes you
for writing structures by requiring full-path qualification, even though the
variable name is unique. How's that for discouraging good programming practice?

>COBOL actually has a reasonably complete set of arithmetic operators
>represented by symbols. It has a complete set of equality/inequality
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>operators.

After 40 years, we're accustomed to AND, OR and NOT. <shrug>

>But I can't understand the logic that because COBOL doesn't use
>symbols for all operators, I should therefore as much as possible not
>use symbols for any of them, even though I find expressions with
>symbols clearer. The more logical approach is to take what I can get.

I'm more of a purist. 

>If COBOL did introduce such symbols as & | ~, would the
>equal/inequality operators suddenly become acceptable to you, or would
>you still refuse to use them because & | ~ are not familar to generic
>readers, whoever they are.

If COBOL offered those symbols, I'd use all symbols in conditionals. 

Now that you mention it, Micro Focus has the equivalent of #define. I wonder if
I could create the symbols that way.

>As to "<" and ">" not being familiar to most readers, your suggestion
>that "EQUAL" should be distinguished from "=" as relative "EQUAL"
>versus assignment "=" is not familiar to the same kind of readers who
>would not be familiar with "<" and ">".

I didn't argue for a context-free language. On the contrary, I've argued against
it here. Every C programmer has made this mistake at least once 'if (a = b) ...'
The compiler happily does the assignment, then tests for truth. Or 'if (a-string
== b-string) ...', the compiler compares two pointers. What poor design.

>I think it probably took me about ten minutes when first encountering
>"<" and ">" along with "<=" and ">=" in BASIC to become totally
>familiar with them.

Fair enough. That answers my objection about symbols being unfamiliar to
novices. 

>How many readers of COBOL code are not familiar with these symbols? Do
>you advocate avoiding them just on the chance that some non-programmer
>might want to read COBOL code and might not understand those symbols?

I'll grant they are widely known, and withdraw my argument on that ground.

>COBOL allowed from its beginnings:
>
>A = B OR NOT >= C OR D

Syntax error. You can't put NOT in front of >=. 

>You would seem to prefer:
>
>A EQUAL TO B OR NOT GREATER OR EQUAL TO C OR D

I would prefer 

A EQUAL TO B OR LESS THAN C OR D
-or-
A = B OR < C OR D

>Cobol also allows something closer to what other languages would
>consider a more normal notation:
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>A = B OR NOT A >= C OR A >= D

Too verbose, and another bug.  Neither is equivalent to the statemenets above.

above: 	A = B OR A < C OR A < D
below: 	A = B OR A < C OR A NOT < D 

>All the symbol notations get rid of those stupid extra OR's that
>appear in the full word notation, the OR's that one might say when
>pronouncing the symbols, but which only clutter up the notation when
>written.

Another good reason to avoid >=. Take for example:

A GREATER THAN OR EQUAL TO  B AND A EQUAL TO C

does it mean
(A > B) OR (A = B AND A = C)

One would think so, by applying the normal rules of COBOL logic. But it doesn't,
it evaluates this way

(A > B OR A = B) AND (A = C)

The OR in GREATER OR EQUAL is not a normal OR. Does that clarify? I contend it
will confuse people.

>The word notation may be more readible to some if it comes to simply
>reading the words, but it is not more *parsible* to most when one
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>Yet it is.

What's wrong with 'an offer not less than five thousand dollars'? On the trading
floors they say 'five thousand dollars or better', where or better is often
omitted or changed to 'limit'. It means five thousand or more if I'm the seller;
five thousand or less if I'm the buyer. If you call your broker and say 'sell
one hundred MSFT limit 24', that means 24 or higher. It might be trading at 25
when you call, in which case you'll get close to 25. But in a thinly traded
stock, say Joes Computer and Bait Shop, there may be only two buy offers open --
one at 17 and the other at 25. If the 25 is filled before your order hits the
floor, you don't want to be matched with the 17, you want your order to stay
open waiting for a buyer. 

We can say the same thing in SQL:

select max(price) in (select price from open-buy where price >= 24)

>If COBOL were really English-like, we might have had something like:
>
>EQUALS which when negated could be expressed as DIFFERS FROM,
>LESS THAN which when negated could be expressed as AT MOST,
>MORE THAN which when negated could be expressed as AT LEAST.

You got 'em backwards. The negative of LESS THAN is AT LEAST. 

>As far as I know, no other language has imitated COBOL in introducing
>verbose phrases for equality/inequality operators. Why not?

You would say because they were invented after keypunch machines and old
printers were obsolete. I would say because programmers imagine themselves to be
mathematicians. 

>Your problems with "<", ">" and "=" in conditions are not supported by
>any rationale that holds against the widespread and honest perception
…[32 more quoted lines elided]…
>were adopted in use for notation long before computers.

Thank you for the LONG, well-reasoned article.
```

```
On 25-May-2003, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:

> Are you saying the greater than number of people believing something, the more
> likely it is to be true? That's he fallacy of argumentum ad populum.

While that is a fallacy, it is reality that the more people disagree with a
statement, the greater is the need for the person making the statement to back
up his statement - if he wants to be listened to.


> That's a good reason to avoid <=.  <= means NOT >. NOT <= would be NOT NOT >.
> Double negatives are not permitted in COBOL, which is why NOT <=  is not
> permitted.

I don't get this.   You could just as validly say that > means NOT <=.   Maybe
someone who has worked on the CoBOL standards committee could verify your claim
as to their reasoning.


> >COMPUTE SALES-TAX = SALES-TTL - REFUND * SALES-TAX-RATE
>
> I see that. You were so distracted by gear shifting that you created a program
> bug. You're about to charge sales tax on the refund rather than the sale.

He's about to compute sales tax on the refund rather than the sale.   That is
not the same thing as charging it.   Whether or not it is a program bug cannot
be determined by this one line.
```

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote in message news:<3ed15160.238675055@news.optonline.net>...
> jallan@smrtytrek.com (jallan) wrote:
> 
> >Robert Wagner orginally posted at
> >http://groups.google.com/groups?selm=3eba027b.118744531%40news.optonline.net&oe=utf-8&output=gplain

> >
> >Many have disagreed.
> 
> Are you saying the greater than number of people believing something, the more
> likely it is to be true? That's he fallacy of argumentum ad populum. 

I said: "Many have disagreed".  I am not saying anything beyond that.

I was summarizing the thread.

But if we are talking about ways of perceiving, then what others
perceive is evidence, which it would not be if talking about matters
of fact, such as whether COBOL does or does not allow "<>" as an
operator.

I would take evidence from this small sample as proof that the
majority perception here applies universally. But it does put the
burden of proof more onto you.

> >I argued that most of the ideographic symbols used mathematical
> >notations and programming (and logic and chemistry and linguistics)
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> show structure, or at least they show each radical separately. 

Wrong. 

The old notation continues to be used alongside newer notations. (And
the graphic diagrams do not spell out the full name in graphics
either.)

This is not surely not just laziness.

Here's one paper at http://www.courses.fas.harvard.edu/~apphy298/Papers/Bawendi/MurrayetalAnnuRevMatSci,_30.pdf,
just the first I found on the web.

Find a chemistry paper of reasonable length that always spells out
elements and compounds in full, that does not use abbreviations, both
in text and in diagrams.

> >Modern mathematics and logic largely grew from and fed back into
> >improvements in notation.
> 
> Following that logic, we should we use one character variable names, because
> that's what they use in math.

No.

Nowhere have I or anyone said that all notations must be identical.

Nor is their any logic dictating that only one notation system should
be used.

Not all mathemitcal and logical notations are identical. People keep
fiddling with them. New conventions take the place of old conventions,
either in particular diciplines or generally.

Nor are all computer languages identical, nor do they all use symbols
identically. The symbol "!" can mean either NOT or OR. The symbol "^"
may mean "to the power of" or "exclusive or", "=" can mean either
assignment or testing for equivalance or both.

It doesn't bother most people more than other differences between
langauges.

> >If I can express myself more clearly and make do with less characters
> >by using Arabic digits and arithmetical operators instead of spelling
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> they are good for other purposes. 

What hasty generalization?

Is this not what you are claiming? Did you not claim that you accepted
fully symbols for arithmetic operators?

What is incorrect about my generalization of your position at that
time and the difficulties you were having in defending it?

> >First, I pointed out that "multiplication" (if you must call the
> >normal use of numbers to indicate how many "multiplication") is
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> That forces you to use single-character variable names. 

Yes. You win one way and lose another. For most mathemtical use the
one symbol method is seen as a win, allowing more information to be
notated more concisely and clearly.
 
> That's a good reason to avoid <=.  <= means NOT >. NOT <= would be NOT NOT >.
> Double negatives are not permitted in COBOL, which is why NOT <=  is not
> permitted.

Yes, <= can be translated mentally as "not greater than" and so was
not necessary in COBOL, though it was introduced recently along with
its extended equivalent "GREATER THAN OR EQUAL TO".

So avoid the new term if you wish.

But is harder to read the extended wording "NOT GREATER THAN" as "less
than or equal" without the words getting in one's way, somewhat like
trying watching a film with a soundtrack in a language you understand,
but also with subtitles, and trying to concentrate on both at the same
time.

> >> My objection is to the context in which symbols are used. In 'if invoice-date
> >> less than date-today', we see all words. In 'if invoice-date < date-today',
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> bug. You're about to charge sales tax on the refund rather than the sale.

Correct as to the error. But I think I made it because I was
concentrating on producing a statement which would make my point about
verbosity and was not concentrating on its meaning which is irrelevant
for the example.

I don't see that typing the words "MINUS" and "TIMES" if I were
allowed to do so in COBOL would have prevented me from making that
error.

Writing it in one-line statments would have done so.

> I wouldn't object to > if we had symbols for AND, OR and NOT. Then we would have
> a complete notational system for logic. Suppose Cobol provided a symbol for
> subtraction (comparison) while making you write words for addition (or) and
> multiplication (and). Would you support using such an incomplete notation?

You are in fact supporting an incomplete notation, if you insist that
all operators or no operators be indicated by symbols. The arithmetic
operators are indicated by symbols.

> I don't find the symbol > any more nor less clear than 'greater'. They mean the
> same thing. 

I find the symbols far clearer when attempting to parse the structure
of a statement, just as I do for the symbols for arithmetic operators.

 As Jeff York said:

> So what?  They're a damned-sight easier to spot! :-)

> They did it to avoid the 'trouble' of defining variables. 

Yes, and in Fortran defining variables is now necessary. And it is
preferred in Visual Basic (and probably other Basics) where one may
turn off such checking only to avoid having to change old programs
which work as they stand.
 
> Modern Fortran sounds like an oxymoron. 
 
Sigh! So what does modern COBOL sound like? Gratuitous and not to any
point.

> >Does the new COMPUTE command in COBOL only save typing? Or does it
> >also make things clearer when several calculations can be put on one
> >line instead of each one on a separate line?
> 
> I agree. COMPUTE is not new.

My meaning was that the COMPUTE instruction was not part of original
COBOL, and was not *needed* in COBOL. Everything it does can be done
in other more verbose ways.
 
> >Verbosity is not always clarity, may often be the opposite of clarity.
> >Look at legal jargon. Look at COBOL, blamed for its needless
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> make one language look better. 

No.

You can make the line count the same, if you start with a COBOL
program and translate roughly line for line and don't use features
allowed in c which are not found in COBOL.

Going the other way is more difficult. Here are some *normal* c
statements:

     if (a == ( b = c++)) {

     total *= ( a >= b ? c : d ) / 5

They would have to be expanded into multiple lines in COBOL.

Even when COBOL can be translated into c (or BASIC) line for line,
there will be more lines in the destination code that are shorter than
lines that are longer. "MOVE A TO B" is unncessarily verbose for "B =
A". The dBASE languages originally imitated this with "store A to B",
but at least by version III dBASE allowed "B = A" as an alternative.

Almost no-one now uses "store" other than in an expression like "store
0 to m.lnI, m.lnJ, m.lnK" where it can replace multiple assignment
statements.

> Nor does C any string handling. How unreasonable is that in a general purpose
> language? C doesn't have SEARCH, you're forced to roll your own. It punishes you
> for writing structures by requiring full-path qualification, even though the
> variable name is unique. How's that for discouraging good programming practice?
> 

Again, no. 

C has numerous string handling library functions. The standard
libraries were almost always implemented along c (though a few early
implementations did of non-standard libaries and functions). The
standard libraries are *essential* parts of ANSI c.

(C also has no input/output without these libraries, cannot even
display or print a character or write it to a file without them. So
what? It is part of c that the language itself is very small and
distinct from the functions which are something outside that core
language, and can be redefined at whim.)

For searching an array, which is mostly about the same as a COBOL
table, use the standard bsearch function. See
http://www.cise.ufl.edu/class/cop4600/docs/C_Ref_Card.pdf for standard
c functions.

I don't see how "extra typing" to indicate that something is part of a
structure is necessarily bad. It is no more redundant than the
standard practise of many COBOL programmers to use IN-, WS- and OUT-
as prefixes for COBOL variables.

You can always set a pointer or preprocessor alias to a particular
element in a struct or union if you need to use it a lot.

> >COBOL actually has a reasonably complete set of arithmetic operators
> >represented by symbols. It has a complete set of equality/inequality
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

> After 40 years, we're accustomed to AND, OR and NOT. <shrug>
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> >symbols clearer. The more logical approach is to take what I can get.
 
> >If COBOL did introduce such symbols as & | ~, would the
> >equal/inequality operators suddenly become acceptable to you, or would
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Most irrational.

Because you can't use symbols for all operators, you will use them for
as few as you can.

> I didn't argue for a context-free language. On the contrary, I've argued against
> it here. Every C programmer has made this mistake at least once 'if (a = b) ...'
> The compiler happily does the assignment, then tests for truth. Or 'if (a-string
> == b-string) ...', the compiler compares two pointers. What poor design.
> 

Not poor design.

It is more powerful behvavior that one has to get used to in c,
perhaps by mentally saying "is set to" for "=" and "evaluates to" for
"==".

Of course, for Fortan and Pascal and some other languages it is ":="
for assignment equals and "=" for relational equals.

In English works the word "equals" does just as well for assignment
and testing for equality, and so spelling it out for one of the uses
is not much help. The beginning programmer still has to get used to a
new distinction in meaning.

> >I think it probably took me about ten minutes when first encountering
> >"<" and ">" along with "<=" and ">=" in BASIC to become totally
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> I'll grant they are widely known, and withdraw my argument on that ground.

> >A = B OR NOT >= C OR D
> 
> Syntax error. You can't put NOT in front of >=. 

Not a syntax error.

The statement is expanded by the compiler somewhat as A = B OR NOT (A
>= C) OR A >= D.

See http://www.helsinki.fi/atk/unix/dec_manuals/cobv27ua/cobrm_020.htm#expanded_equivs
and http://docs.hp.com/cgi-bin/doc3k/BB243390047.15064/45.

> >All the symbol notations get rid of those stupid extra OR's that
> >appear in the full word notation, the OR's that one might say when
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

A GREATER THAN OR EQUAL TO B AND A EQUAL TO C

That seems somewhat confusing.

A >= B and A = C

There is good reason to get rid of "GREATER THAN OR EQUAL TO", not to
get rid of ">=" which you can always read as "EXCEEDS" or "AT LEAST"
if you must verbalize it, or as "greater-equals".

Your belief that "NOT <" is innately superior to ">=" is idiosyncratic
and logically unsupportable as indicated elsewhere in this thread.

> What's wrong with 'an offer not less than five thousand dollars'? On the trading
> floors they say 'five thousand dollars or better', where or better is often
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> select max(price) in (select price from open-buy where price >= 24)

Nothing is wrong with any of those. Why would I think there was?

> >If COBOL were really English-like, we might have had something like:
> >
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> You got 'em backwards. The negative of LESS THAN is AT LEAST. 

Yes! I should stick to symbols. :-)

> >As far as I know, no other language has imitated COBOL in introducing
> >verbose phrases for equality/inequality operators. Why not?
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

I would not say that.

I think they were adopted because the symbols were available and those
who introduced them felt they made things clearer. (In original COBOL
the symbol were barely available, not at all available in most
business environemnts, but were introduced anyway.)

That this feeling was correct is supported when those who made later
languages did not drop the symbols introduced by earlier languages and
certainly did not introduce the same or different long-winded word
substitutes that COBOL provided.

Your "imagine themselves to be mathemmaticians" is an unfounded ad
hominem accusation.

Some programmers are mathematicians.

Some are not.

What has this to do with preferring symbols for operators?

Give some indication that those who included symbols in programming
languages (including "<", "=", ">") in COBOL, did so not because they
found statements containing symbols easier for themselves to parse,
but because "they imagined themselves to be mathematicians".

I am not a mathematician, though I know some math. I find such symbols
easier to parse, and not just in programming.
```

_(reply depth: 62)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)

```
jallan@smrtytrek.com (jallan) wrote:

>robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote in message
news:<3ed15160.238675055@news.optonline.net>...
>> jallan@smrtytrek.com (jallan) wrote:

>> >Many have disagreed.
>> 
>> Are you saying the greater than number of people believing something, the
more
>> likely it is to be true? That's he fallacy of argumentum ad populum. 
>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>burden of proof more onto you.

I present facts from my experience and logic tying them together. I wish others
would do the same rather than telling us how they feel. Feelings are not
relevent to the issue and venting them here is a self-indulgence. 

>> >I argued that most of the ideographic symbols used mathematical
>> >notations and programming (and logic and chemistry and linguistics)
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>> I think those ideographs are a carry-forward from the days when statements
were
>> handwritten. 
>> 
>> Chemists no longer use the old count-the-atoms notation such as C6 H12 O6.
>> Glucose, fructose and galactose all have that same formula. They use graphics
to
>> show structure, or at least they show each radical separately. 
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>either.)

I didn't say they spelled out names of elements. What gave you that idea?

>This is not surely not just laziness.
>
>Here's one paper at
http://www.courses.fas.harvard.edu/~apphy298/Papers/Bawendi/MurrayetalAnnuRevMatSci,_30.pdf,
>just the first I found on the web.

It is more about engineering than chemistry. Yes, it uses old notation. 

>Not all mathemitcal and logical notations are identical. People keep
>fiddling with them. New conventions take the place of old conventions,
>either in particular diciplines or generally.

People keep fiddling with programming languages too. Not just their grammar but
also their structural model. The goal is making programmers more efficient or
obsolete. 

>> That's a good reason to avoid <=.  <= means NOT >. NOT <= would be NOT NOT >.
>> Double negatives are not permitted in COBOL, which is why NOT <=  is not
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>its extended equivalent "GREATER THAN OR EQUAL TO".

NOT GREATER had been there for decades; compilers understood it. When <= is
introduced, how would a compiler writer handle it?

a.  Write all new compiler code.
b.  Tokenize <= the same as 'not >', a one-line change.

FWIW, Micro Focus permits 'not <=', but does not accept not not. 

>> I wouldn't object to > if we had symbols for AND, OR and NOT. Then we would
have
>> a complete notational system for logic. Suppose Cobol provided a symbol for
>> subtraction (comparison) while making you write words for addition (or) and
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>operators are indicated by symbols.

All or none within each 'branch of computer math'. Arithmetic has a complete
symbology; logic is 50-50.

>> I don't find the symbol > any more nor less clear than 'greater'. They mean
the
>> same thing. 
>
>I find the symbols far clearer when attempting to parse the structure
>of a statement, just as I do for the symbols for arithmetic operators.

You convinced  me, which is why I requested symbols for and/or/not.

>My meaning was that the COMPUTE instruction was not part of original
>COBOL, and was not *needed* in COBOL. Everything it does can be done
>in other more verbose ways.

Exponentiation had no wordy counterpart. COMPUTE appeared early in the history
of COBOL, certainly by 68, probably before.

>> I dispute the assertion that COBOL is verbose. Write the same program in
COBOL
>> and C. You'll find the line count about the same, unless you contrive a case
to
>> make one language look better. 
>
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>They would have to be expanded into multiple lines in COBOL.

Those are contrived. In the large volume of commercial C I've maintained, I've
never seen a ternary operator, seldom an assignment nested in an if statement. 

>Even when COBOL can be translated into c (or BASIC) line for line,
>there will be more lines in the destination code that are shorter than
>lines that are longer. "MOVE A TO B" is unncessarily verbose for "B =
>A".

More typically "strcpy(b, a);" Punctuation are lexemes requiring almost as much
mental effort as words. The score: COBOL-4, C-7.

If C's string handling were adequate, there wouldn't have been dozens of
scripting languages invented to overcome the deficiency. 

>> Nor does C have any string handling. How unreasonable is that in a general
purpose
>> language? C doesn't have SEARCH, you're forced to roll your own. It punishes
you
>> for writing structures by requiring full-path qualification, even though the
>> variable name is unique. How's that for discouraging good programming
practice?

>(C also has no input/output without these libraries, cannot even
>display or print a character or write it to a file without them. So
>what? It is part of c that the language itself is very small and
>distinct from the functions which are something outside that core
>language, and can be redefined at whim.)

I concur there. File systems don't belong in the language except at a high level
of abstraction -- START, READ NEXT, etc. COBOL has evolved to treating the file
system as external. 

>I don't see how "extra typing" to indicate that something is part of a
>structure is necessarily bad. It is no more redundant than the
>standard practise of many COBOL programmers to use IN-, WS- and OUT-
>as prefixes for COBOL variables.

First you say COBOL is too verbose, now you don't mind "extra typing". You can't
have it both ways. 

In practice, C programmers have been conditioned to avoid structures more than
one layer deep, whereas COBOL programmers routinely define them multiple layers
deep. I think that's a serious structural issue.

>You can always set a pointer or preprocessor alias to a particular
>element in a struct or union if you need to use it a lot.

Workarounds. 

>> >If COBOL did introduce such symbols as & | ~, would the
>> >equal/inequality operators suddenly become acceptable to you, or would
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>as few as you can.

I gave my reason -- regularity -- and attempted to remedy the problem. How is
that irrational?

>> >A = B OR NOT >= C OR D
>> 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>>= C) OR A >= D.

Maybe. The meaning was vague in the 85 standard. As a result, compilers
interpreted it in various ways. The 2002 standard says it is not valid.
```

```
Robert Wagner wrote:
>>My meaning was that the COMPUTE instruction was not part of original
>>COBOL, and was not *needed* in COBOL. Everything it does can be done
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> of COBOL, certainly by 68, probably before.

I thought this too, but there -is- a "more verbose" way of doing it... 
For example, if you had something today that says "Compute X = Y ** Z", 
you could, in COBOL 68 syntax...

001-EXPONENTIATION.
     MOVE Y TO X.
     SUBTRACT 1 FROM Z GIVING A.
     PERFORM 002-COMPUTE-IT A TIMES.

002-COMPUTE-IT.
     MULTIPLY X BY Y.

-Much- more verbose....  :)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(reply depth: 63)_

- **From:** jallan@smrtytrek.com (jallan)
- **Date:** 2003-06-08T18:16:01-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<299f1138.0306081716.10d129e2@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3ec667d1.72442892@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305191427.12bf6003@posting.google.com> <3ec9d0dc.64902913@news.optonline.net> <bae29l$lpi$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ecb0e2a.146144909@news.optonline.net> <299f1138.0305250621.3fea0e32@posting.google.com> <3ed15160.238675055@news.optonline.net> <299f1138.0306011419.53805bb3@posting.google.com> <3edb253a.308040929@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote in message news:<3edb253a.308040929@news.optonline.net>...
> 
> It is more about engineering than chemistry. Yes, it uses old notation. 
> 

And so do many pure chemistry papers.

> >Yes, <= can be translated mentally as "not greater than" and so was
> >not necessary in COBOL, though it was introduced recently along with
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

This is irrelevant. Compilers these days often optimize the code
written in any case. Unless you know the details of how a particular
compiler works you have no way of knowing and probably should not care
what underlying machine instructions appear for <= and >= or other
operators.

What is the more efficient way to code on one compiler and one
operating system may be less so on another compiler and/or another
operating system. At one time branching was generally more efficient
than hardware table lookup. Today it is often the reverse, depending
on applicaton.

> All or none within each 'branch of computer math'. Arithmetic has a complete
> symbology; logic is 50-50.

Far too purist!

No notation is perfect.

> 
> Exponentiation had no wordy counterpart. COMPUTE appeared early in the history
> of COBOL, certainly by 68, probably before.

I am aware of that COMPUTE was an early command. But it was obviously
introduced to provide more concise but *unneeded( power for
arithmetic.

As to exponentiation, LX-i has answered that. I already provided the
looping method in a previous post in any case.

But, admittedly, part of COBOLs reputation for verbosity comes from
its early forms which necessitated far more coding than is now needed.

The intended point was that changes are often made to computer
languages not because they are *needed*, but because they are believed
to be desireable.
In this case they provided a less long-winded and easier-to-read
method of performing computations.

A side-effect is that it is now easier to mess up the order than it
once was, if you forget about priority of operations or don't put in
necessary parentheses.

"With great power comes great responsibility." -- Stan Lee

> >
> >     if (a == ( b = c++)) {
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> never seen a ternary operator, seldom an assignment nested in an if statement. 

Well, then you've been maintainng baby c written by people who haven't
a clue how to make c sing.

These are normal enough for application code. I could have presented
far more complex real examples from working applications and operating
system code.

One of the nice things about programming in c (and similar languages)
is you can get so much logic onto a single line where you can see it
all at once.

The ternary structure appears in xBase languages also as the
immediation if function: iif( , ,). The same line could be coded in
xBase as:

    lnTotal = m.lnTotal * iif(m.lnA > m.lnB, m.lnC, m.lnD) / 5

Again, this is very normal code.

> >Even when COBOL can be translated into c (or BASIC) line for line,
> >there will be more lines in the destination code that are shorter than
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> mental effort as words. The score: COBOL-4, C-7.

Agreed on the puncutation, or close enough as no matter. I was
thinking of numeric variables in my example.

Actually your score here is COBOL-9, C-11, using "MOVE``TO`" and
"strcpy(,`);" reasonably putting a syntactically unnecessary space in
the c to aid readiblity.

But strcpy can  also return the string as copied, which is sometimes
wanted. That would be another line in COBOL.

But compare COBOL verb "PERFORM" with c command "for" for looping or
with no nocommand at all in c for for calling a procedure/function
other than its name and parentheses.

> If C's string handling were adequate, there wouldn't have been dozens of
> scripting languages invented to overcome the deficiency. 

What do you think these scripting languages are written in? COBOL?

Perl was originally intended to replace the Unix specialized "general
expression" string-handling utilities awk and sed which is why it and
its successors have so much extra string-handling functionality built
into them.

But c out of the box has as much string-handling ability as any other
general normal purpose language. Mostly they have comparable functions
with different names. COBOL is the language lacking what is normal in
c and other lanaguage.

Here's a site with a semi-general routine to bring COBOL up to the
norm of string handling:
http://www.csis.ul.ie/COBOL/examples/Strings/RefMod.htm.

Of course your strings have to be exactly the right length to use this
utility, or you need make extra versions for every different lengths
of strings you wish to use with it.

*Sigh*!

> >I don't see how "extra typing" to indicate that something is part of a
> >structure is necessarily bad. It is no more redundant than the
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> have it both ways. 

Sure I can.

I don't mind doing extra typing when it helps *me*. I do mind doing it
to no purpose.

I like short, concise and clear.

But when there are good reasons to use conventions like IN-, WS-, and
OUT- in COBOL and for doing similar things in other languages, then I
don't mind.

Yet why PERFORM instead of DO, for example? Or why not just give the
procedure name by itself as in c, xBase, etc.
 
> In practice, C programmers have been conditioned to avoid structures more than
> one layer deep, whereas COBOL programmers routinely define them multiple layers
> deep. I think that's a serious structural issue.

If you are talking about REDEFINES, such a thing hardly exists in most
other languages, not just c. Of course it is not used.

String functions tend to be used instead to pull out parts of a string
or to concatenate two or more strings into a single string.

But STRING and UNSTRING in COBOL are notoriously inefficient for some
reason, though wonderful to work with for many purposes. Otherwise
COBOL is lacking in built in string manipulation facililities.

> >You can always set a pointer or preprocessor alias to a particular
> >element in a struct or union if you need to use it a lot.
> 
> Workarounds. 

Of course. Normally it isn't done.

Normally you've assigned a short pointer name to the structure. Code
is clearer when you say something like "in->fullname" instead of just
"fullname". If it's part of a structure, then why not indicate it is
part of a structure?

This is called self-documentation.

Self-documentation is a claim often made for COBOL, and a bogus one as
any COBOL programmer knows. :-(

But it doesn't hurt for a language to force some clarity on the
programmer.

Personally I liked flavors of BASIC where suffixes like $, #, %, &
indicated the type of variable and were *necessary*.

When sub-elements are part of the a great element, many COBOL
progammers indicate this when there is any chance of confusion. It
really is not much of an issue.

> >The statement is expanded by the compiler somewhat as A = B OR NOT (A
> >>= C) OR A >= D.
> 
> Maybe. The meaning was vague in the 85 standard. As a result, compilers
> interpreted it in various ways. The 2002 standard says it is not valid.

A reasonable change or clarification. The use of NOT in these
circumstances was confusing.


Jim Allan
```

```
Something that most of this conversation has missed is notational music.
That particular system is logo based, and clearly superior to alphabets.
The tool is dependant on the job, not implicit.  The coke sign does a pretty
good job too.

Donald.
```

```
Robert Wagner wrote:

> That doesn't bother me because language hasn't produced an adequate
> substitute for math. Romans tried and failed miserably with their XVCII
> dates.

What 'maths' did the Romans try to produce a substitute for with 'XVCII' 
[sic].

In what way did they 'fail miserably' ?

Notes: * Roman notation is not just for dates.
       * Can we do maths on dates in Roman notation ?
       * XVCII is an invalid number

>If using a logogram such as > offends you, how do you feel about using a
>logogram such as 5?

What about " ' , . @ $ ! ? * & ; : %  ?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(reply depth: 52)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-05-14T11:47:53+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ec21f7c.104115258@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3ebf01e7.295898087@news.optonline.net> <b9ocfi$bmk$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ec03239.24014939@news.optonline.net> <b9q0b7$uj0$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>Robert Wagner wrote:
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>[sic].

Ok, it wasn't a substitute for anything, but it was still a flawed system. How
hard is the concept zero?

I wonder how Romans did engineering, logistics and business with an inadequate
numeric system. 

>In what way did they 'fail miserably' ?
>
>Notes: * Roman notation is not just for dates.
>       * Can we do maths on dates in Roman notation ?
>       * XVCII is an invalid number

The system failed because it's no good for arithmetic. You can't add a column of
Roman Numerals. The reason it survived for writing dates (only) is because we
don't usually do arithmetic on them. As I said, you can't add 1 to a date. 

>>If using a logogram such as > offends you, how do you feel about using a
>>logogram such as 5?

I have no objection to numerals. 

>What about " ' , . @ $ ! ? * & ; : %  ?

No objection except &, and = used as a relational operator. If we spelled out
the others, they would be hard to distinguish from the words they punctuate. 

Currency symbols aren't very useful. In the financial services industry, we use
abbreviations such as DM, JY and NZD.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(reply depth: 53)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-05-15T07:49:18+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b9u6lk$p5r$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3ec03239.24014939@news.optonline.net> <b9q0b7$uj0$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ec21f7c.104115258@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:

> Ok, it wasn't a substitute for anything, but it was still a flawed system.
> How hard is the concept zero?

The Romans had the concept of zero, they just didn't have a symbol for it 
that was used in their numbering system.  That is, they had a numbering 
system that didn't need zero.
 
> I wonder how Romans did engineering, logistics and business with an
> inadequate numeric system.

Because it wasn't inadequate.

> The system failed because it's no good for arithmetic. You can't add a
> column of Roman Numerals. 

No. No. _YOU_ can't add a column of Roman numbers, _I_ can.  The Romans 
could, and do subtraction, multiplication and division (the last being a 
bit long winded but works).

> The reason it survived for writing dates (only)
> is because we don't usually do arithmetic on them. As I said, you can't
> add 1 to a date.

MMIII + I -> MMIV

That works fine.  Geez Robert do you run around in a funk on New Year's Eve 
because you don't know what next year will be called until the Calendars 
arrive ?
    

>> What about " ' , . @ $ ! ? * & ; : %  ?

> No objection except ...

So you object to the ones you have to 'inspect closely' because you haven't 
learnt to recognise them.
```

```
> >> I wonder how Romans did engineering, logistics and business with an
> inadequate
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> available, or perhaps you'd prefer comp-r.

Nope, I believe our numbering system is superior, and even if it was slightly
inferior, it would be more suitable because it is more familiar.   But our
numbering system isn't perfect either.

When we are talking programming, it is important to define what solutions are
"adequate".   Quite often this is the goal we should be working for.   An
adequate system is one that gets the job done.    Sometimes the process isn't
elegant (a word I like from my physics background), but if it works, it works.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Combat programming (was Alphabets)

_(reply depth: 56)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-05-18T01:28:36+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ec6b610.92477218@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3ebf01e7.295898087@news.optonline.net> <b9ocfi$bmk$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ec03239.24014939@news.optonline.net> <b9q0b7$uj0$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ec21f7c.104115258@news.optonline.net> <b9uag5$9ed$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ec2f444.158595845@news.optonline.net> <ba06t2$744$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:

>When we are talking programming, it is important to define what solutions are
>"adequate".   Quite often this is the goal we should be working for.   An
>adequate system is one that gets the job done.    Sometimes the process isn't
>elegant (a word I like from my physics background), but if it works, it works.

I've heard that argument used to defend the most hideous practices.

Me: This is spaghetti code. 
Pragmatist: I don't know what you're complaining about. It gets the job done.
Me: It's not maintainable. Look, you have the same code duplicated in twenty
programs. What if it changes? 
Pragmatist: I use grep to find the programs, then change only the ones that need
to be changed. Again, it works. 
Me: How do you know which need to be changed? You have test, production and
thousands of obsolete versions all glommed together in a single directory. 
Pragmatist: I just remember.

I worked at one place where programmers actually _deleted_ source code and
maintained systems with machine language patches to object code. I suspected
they kept a hard-copy listing, but I never found it. Their attitude was 'What's
the beef? It works and we've been maintaining it this way for years.'

Management had brought me in to make substantial changes. I was forced to write
a disassembler for an obsolete machine (RCA 301), then use it to reconstruct
source in a reiterative process. I would figure out what some variables were.
Then, seeing them in context, gave a clue about other variables. It took me four
months to decode 20 programs. In the end, the pragmatists who thought they had a
sinecure cleaned out their desks and left. 

The reconstructed code was not a line-for-line translation of the original, it
had elegance. It used calls and macros (inline procedures). 

At another place, the 'locals' wrote a program to sabotage mine. Theirs ran in
supervisor state and caused my programs to 'make mistakes' or terminate normally
before they were done (by changing the 'resume address' to the OS entry for
normal termination). My internal controls detected it was happening. Then I
snatched a copy of their executable before they had a chance to overwrite it
with garbage. I never did find the source, but reconstructed it with a manual
disassembly. It had been written in PL/I (it's easy to tell from machine
language) and its sole mission was to seek and destroy my programs. 

It wasn't difficult to identify the author because he was the only one in the
shop who knew PL/I. I didn't crucify him because he was an intelligent,
hard-working guy who foolishly took the wrong side. Instead, I made an
'innocent'  reference to his program (I noticed you running MEMZ1. What does it
do?) and he cleaned out his desk. His cohorts left a few weeks later. 

Ten years later, he replaced me as programming manager. Twenty years later, I
count him as a friend, although I wouldn't turn my back to him.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Combat programming (was Alphabets)

_(reply depth: 59)_

- **From:** Jeff York <pogo50@talk21.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-20T14:15:55+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<g3akcvsue4u6s0uelg9om5vj31dsj5j0sh@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3ebf01e7.295898087@news.optonline.net> <b9ocfi$bmk$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ec03239.24014939@news.optonline.net> <b9q0b7$uj0$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ec21f7c.104115258@news.optonline.net> <b9uag5$9ed$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ec2f444.158595845@news.optonline.net> <ba06t2$744$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ec6b610.92477218@news.optonline.net> <baarso$edi$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ec9d447.65777476@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:

>"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
>
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
>The primary argument against periods is that they reduce maintainability.

Why?

>There appears to be a contradiction between your advocacy of maintainability and
>periods. 

I fail to see how adherence to an artificially-created "approved
style" can automatically create good or maintainable code.  Over the
years I've worked on code that would have given Djikstra nightmares
because of the "go to"s that were used - but were still models of
clarity and maintainability.  Equally, I've worked on "structured"
stuff that would have given said august gentleman wet dreams, yet were
utterly impenetrable.

Good code isn't produced by the "style du jour", it's produced by
clarity of thought.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Combat programming (was Alphabets)

_(reply depth: 59)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-05-20T14:21:05+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<baddki$lvd$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3ebf01e7.295898087@news.optonline.net> <b9ocfi$bmk$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ec03239.24014939@news.optonline.net> <b9q0b7$uj0$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ec21f7c.104115258@news.optonline.net> <b9uag5$9ed$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ec2f444.158595845@news.optonline.net> <ba06t2$744$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ec6b610.92477218@news.optonline.net> <baarso$edi$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ec9d447.65777476@news.optonline.net>`

```

On 20-May-2003, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:


> >Unless the paradigm shift is to save others from the onerous task of typing
> >or removing periods.

> The primary argument against periods is that they reduce maintainability.
> There appears to be a contradiction between your advocacy of maintainability
> and  periods.


Maintainability is the ability to easily read and understand the code, and make
changes with minimized risk.

Since we both agree that we should use terminators such as END-IF, use
indentation, and eschew NEXT SENTENCE, removing periods doesn't make the code
more easy to read or change.   Full stops actually make it easier to browse
through code to make sure the statement being perused is not a subordinate
clause to some earlier condition.

That leaves your stated reason for removing them (besides to be more "modern"):
so you could cut and paste without editing.   That's not what I call
maintainability.
```

```
My text editor can do scoped changes i.e. only within the hilighted block of
code. Also, it  can it delete period-space, but not periods inside quotes nor in
numeric literals. Can yours? If not, its a maintenance issue.

That covers deleting periods. To insert them, a macro would be required. Have
you written one?
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<baild8$djg$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3ebf01e7.295898087@news.optonline.net> <b9ocfi$bmk$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ec03239.24014939@news.optonline.net> <b9q0b7$uj0$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ec21f7c.104115258@news.optonline.net> <b9uag5$9ed$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ec2f444.158595845@news.optonline.net> <ba06t2$744$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ec6b610.92477218@news.optonline.net> <baarso$edi$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ec9d447.65777476@news.optonline.net> <baddki$lvd$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ecca791.250967222@news.optonline.net>`

```
On 22-May-2003, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:

> >That leaves your stated reason for removing them (besides to be more
> >"modern"):
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> you written one?

You did not explicitly disagree with my earlier statement in that post:

> >Maintainability is the ability to easily read and understand the code, and
> >make changes with minimized risk.

If you agree with this, then making scoped change isn't an issue.   Aligning
code and adding or subtracting periods isn't "maintainability".   I'm not after
saving keystrokes.    You have claimed that you aren't interested in saving
keystrokes either (replacing ">" with "greater than" being one example).

If you disagree with this, please explain your disagreement.   If we come to an
agreement on the goals, we can determine whether such macros further those
goals.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Combat programming (was Alphabets)

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ecfc4ce.137137529@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3ebf01e7.295898087@news.optonline.net> <b9ocfi$bmk$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ec03239.24014939@news.optonline.net> <b9q0b7$uj0$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ec21f7c.104115258@news.optonline.net> <b9uag5$9ed$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ec2f444.158595845@news.optonline.net> <ba06t2$744$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ec6b610.92477218@news.optonline.net> <baarso$edi$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ec9d447.65777476@news.optonline.net> <baddki$lvd$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ecca791.250967222@news.optonline.net> <baild8$djg$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:

>
>On 22-May-2003, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:

>You did not explicitly disagree with my earlier statement in that post:
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>goals.

Of course I agree, but we'd have to define "easily read and understand". For
instance, many have inordinate difficulty understanding conditional statements
with more than n (pick a number between 3 and 10) clauses. I don't. If a program
is going to make 12 decisions ONE WAY OR ANOTHER, I find it easier to understand
when they are together rather than sprinkled all around in the name of
'simplicity'. That doesn't simplify, that makes the program harder to understand
.. in my ever so humble opinion.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Combat programming (was Alphabets)

_(reply depth: 61)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-05-23T08:13:56+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bajb2j$tsk$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3ec9d447.65777476@news.optonline.net> <baddki$lvd$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ecca791.250967222@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:

>>That leaves your stated reason for removing them (besides to be more
>>"modern"):
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> Have you written one?

You seem to be basing your whole argument against full stops on the 
assumption that if he is using full stops then he must be putting them at 
the end of each statement.  Thus you can think of all the problems that may 
occur.

In fact that is where you were putting full stops until recently.  You even 
commented that full stops on their own line were 'seldom used'.

In fact I have only put full stops on their own line in the body of 
procedures for the last couple of decades, even when using '74 Cobol. When 
done this way they do not present the problems that you contrive.

Your crusade against full stops (to which you have a strong emotional 
attachment) is misdirected.  You have no reason to believe that using more 
than one full stop within a procedure, in the way that Howard may be doing 
it, actually results in loss of maintainability.  It is merely your 
assumption that he is doing it in specific ways.
```

```
Good point. If he is putting them on a separate line, it is not a maintenance
issue.
```
