###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Combat programming (was Alphabets)

_(reply depth: 59)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-05-20T13:52:01-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0305201252.47f63821@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3ebf01e7.295898087@news.optonline.net> <b9ocfi$bmk$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ec03239.24014939@news.optonline.net> <b9q0b7$uj0$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ec21f7c.104115258@news.optonline.net> <b9uag5$9ed$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ec2f444.158595845@news.optonline.net> <ba06t2$744$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ec6b610.92477218@news.optonline.net> <baarso$edi$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ec9d447.65777476@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 

> The primary argument against periods is that they reduce maintainability.

There may be an argument against putting full stops on the end of a
statement as it may be missed if one cuts and pastes or indents. 
However, if proper scope termination has been used then these will be
detected by the compiler.

Full stops on their own line have no effect on maintainability at all.

If you have difficulty seeing them then get better tools.  My editor
shows full stops on a red background compared to normally blue.

> There appears to be a contradiction between your advocacy of maintainability
> and periods.

You are making the wrong argument: 

  Lack of scope terminators reduces maintainability.
  Lack of scope terminators requires full stops.

  NEXT SENTENCE reduces maintainability.
  NEXT SENTENCE requires full stops.

To improve maintainability it is only necessary to ensure all scope
terminators are in place and to replace NEXT SENTENCE.  It is then
irrelevent whether zero scope full stops on their own lines are there
or not.

As it happens once these items are in place there is no structural
need for full stops within the paragraph.  Arguing that 'removing the
full stops improves maintainability' is backwards and misses the point
entirely.

When there is no structural need for the full stop they may be used
for other purposes, such as having them after a deeply nested IF on
its own line as a signal that the logic is back to zero scope - so
make sure that it is.
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bag41o$25p$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```

On 20-May-2003, riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

> You are making the wrong argument:
>
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> entirely.

Absolutely

> When there is no structural need for the full stop they may be used
> for other purposes, such as having them after a deeply nested IF on
> its own line as a signal that the logic is back to zero scope - so
> make sure that it is.

Which actually makes maintenance easier - when you see a full stop, you know
that the code following it is not a subordinate clause.   This can make it
easier to scan for whatever bug/change you are looking for.
```

riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 
>
…[31 more quoted lines elided]…
>entirely.

Assuming you have old code that works correctly, how do you "ensure all scope
terminators are in place?" Don't you simply replace each period with one or more
terminators? It sounds like we're saying the same thing. 

>When there is no structural need for the full stop they may be used
>for other purposes, such as having them after a deeply nested IF on
>its own line as a signal that the logic is back to zero scope - so
>make sure that it is.

If ALL scope terminators are in place, why is the full stop a good idea? As a
safety feature, just in case they're not? What happens when you paste the flawed
block into a larger statement? All code following it becomes 'broken'.
```

- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3ebf01e7.295898087@news.optonline.net> <b9ocfi$bmk$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ec03239.24014939@news.optonline.net> <b9q0b7$uj0$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ec21f7c.104115258@news.optonline.net> <b9uag5$9ed$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ec2f444.158595845@news.optonline.net> <ba06t2$744$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ec6b610.92477218@news.optonline.net> <baarso$edi$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ec9d447.65777476@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305201252.47f63821@posting.google.com> <3ecca96d.251443691@news.optonline.net> <bailmj$t36$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3ecfc71b.137727115@news.optonline.net> <bb006l$6h$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ed3f2fa.411143190@news.optonline.net>`

```

On 27-May-2003, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:

> >The vendors I have seen willing to touch working code have been more likely
> >to
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> What does that say about typical COBOL programming?

It says more about how hard it is to find CoBOL programmers.   The people who
make these decisions generally are not qualified to determine how much of the
maintenance difficulty is due to poor programming - but they should be qualified
to determine how much of the maintenance difficulty is due to the difficulty in
finding good programmers.

So their maintenance decisions are based upon:
1.   How expensive is it to have disposable systems (Just like the printer I
threw away instead of paying $100 to have someone look at it).
2.   How expensive is it to hire the right people to maintain a system.
3.   How expensive is it to have full time employees who will stay around and
know the system.
4.   Fashion.

Quality of 20 year-old legacy code isn't directly a concern.
```

Chuck Stevens wrote:
> "Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
> news:3ecca96d.251443691@news.optonline.net...
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> convince the owner of that code that it absolutely has to be fixed?

I don't.  :)  But when I become the owner (and have the time), I 
"maintain" it...  And it becomes a lot easier.  This week, I was very 
thankful I had done that.  I didn't have access to my Windows-based 
editor, and was limited to using the 24x80 screen on the mainframe.  It 
was -so- nice to put an If in the blank line above a statement and an 
End-If at the bottom of the block...

I'm sold on "one full stop per paragraph"...  I realize it's a matter of 
style, but it's a style that I've found very, very useful.
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bailod$dmt$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3ebf01e7.295898087@news.optonline.net> <b9ocfi$bmk$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ec03239.24014939@news.optonline.net> <b9q0b7$uj0$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ec21f7c.104115258@news.optonline.net> <b9uag5$9ed$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ec2f444.158595845@news.optonline.net> <ba06t2$744$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ec6b610.92477218@news.optonline.net> <baarso$edi$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ec9d447.65777476@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305201252.47f63821@posting.google.com> <3ecca96d.251443691@news.optonline.net>`

On 22-May-2003, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:

> >As it happens once these items are in place there is no structural
> >need for full stops within the paragraph.  Arguing that 'removing the
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> more terminators? It sounds like we're saying the same thing.

You ensure all scope indicators are in place exactly the same if you use periods
or if you don't use periods.

When you find old un-terminated code, you add terminators.   You choose to also
remove periods.  This is a stylistic choice that should not effect the code (if
your terminators are correct and if you replace NEXT SENTENCE appropriately).


> If ALL scope terminators are in place, why is the full stop a good idea? As a
> safety feature, just in case they're not?

I suppose that can help.   But mainly, it makes it easier for maintenance coders
to back-track from some code they are reviewing to a full stop period - and know
that that's where the current sentence starts.

> What happens when you paste the
> flawed block into a larger statement? All code following it becomes 'broken'.

We review and edit the new or changed code.   Don't you?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Combat programming (was Alphabets)

_(reply depth: 62)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-05-24T22:17:06+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ecfc7a4.137863913@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3ebf01e7.295898087@news.optonline.net> <b9ocfi$bmk$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ec03239.24014939@news.optonline.net> <b9q0b7$uj0$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ec21f7c.104115258@news.optonline.net> <b9uag5$9ed$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ec2f444.158595845@news.optonline.net> <ba06t2$744$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ec6b610.92477218@news.optonline.net> <baarso$edi$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ec9d447.65777476@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305201252.47f63821@posting.google.com> <3ecca96d.251443691@news.optonline.net> <bailod$dmt$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:

>
>On 22-May-2003, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>You ensure all scope indicators are in place exactly the same if you use
periods
>or if you don't use periods.

Yes, you do it by looking at indentation. If the program doesn't use proper
indentation, you run it through a beautifier which supplies that. 

>When you find old un-terminated code, you add terminators.   You choose to also
>remove periods.  This is a stylistic choice that should not effect the code (if
>your terminators are correct and if you replace NEXT SENTENCE appropriately).

Granted, it's a stylistic choice. I still don't see the purpose of superfluous
periods. 

>> If ALL scope terminators are in place, why is the full stop a good idea? As a
>> safety feature, just in case they're not?
>
>I suppose that can help.   But mainly, it makes it easier for maintenance
coders
>to back-track from some code they are reviewing to a full stop period - and
know
>that that's where the current sentence starts.

In the maintenance programming I've done and supervised, knowing where a
'sentence' starts is not a major concern. Flow of execution, in general, is
secondary. I usually start with a data item and want to see every line of code
which touches that data, or every line which modifies it. 

When I get down to looking at procedural code, I rely on indentation. It makes
little sense to use one scoping method for nested ifs and a different one for
'level zero'. 

>> What happens when you paste the
>> flawed block into a larger statement? All code following it becomes 'broken'.
>
>We review and edit the new or changed code.   Don't you?

Of course. When a program doesn't work, the first thing I do is read the code to
see what it's doing. If I can't see the error after several readings, I'll use a
debugger as a last resort. I've noticed many people's approach is opposite mine.
The first thing they do is fire up the debugger so the machine can tell them
what the code does. They read the code only as a last resort.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Combat programming (was Alphabets)

_(reply depth: 63)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-05-25T15:44:20-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0305251444.32fa4691@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3ec21f7c.104115258@news.optonline.net> <b9uag5$9ed$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ec2f444.158595845@news.optonline.net> <ba06t2$744$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ec6b610.92477218@news.optonline.net> <baarso$edi$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ec9d447.65777476@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305201252.47f63821@posting.google.com> <3ecca96d.251443691@news.optonline.net> <bailod$dmt$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ecfc7a4.137863913@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 

> Of course. When a program doesn't work, the first thing I do is read the code
> to see what it's doing. If I can't see the error after several readings, I'll
> use a debugger as a last resort. 

> I've noticed many people's approach is opposite mine.

Which may make other peoples approach superior.

> The first thing they do is fire up the debugger so the machine can tell them
> what the code does. 

The Computer is somewhat more accurate about what will happen than
reading it will show, which is why you may have to read it several
times.

> They read the code only as a last resort.

Given that they wrote it based on what they expect it to do then
reading it will only confirm that is what they wrote.  Running the
debugger will tell them what the compiler thought they wrote.  The
difference explains the bugs.

If you have avoided debuggers or are generally unfamilar with them
(because they are only used infrequently as a last resort) then it
will take you longer to run through a session.

If they have used debuggers frequently then it is quick for them to
run through the code _reading_the_source_ as they execute it.

While your way may be better for you, their way may be better for
them.

How hard is that to understand ?
```

>run through the code _reading_the_source_ as they execute it.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Combat programming (was Alphabets)

_(reply depth: 65)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-05-26T20:17:19+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<basihj$v3n$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3ecfc7a4.137863913@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305251444.32fa4691@posting.google.com> <3ed177b3.248487519@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:

>>While your way may be better for you, their way may be better for
>>them.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> measure.

I've seen and commented on some of your code that yoiu posted here. It 
would have worked _most_ of the time.   ;-)

It may well be that you produce much more bug-free code, but how much is 
simply because of 30+ years of doing it.

Younger, less experienced programmers will produce less bug-free code, but 
if you took away their debuggers then their productivity may go down.

If you used the debugger more frequently and got over the 80% setup time by 
learning what to do and having the setups readily available, then your 
productivity may go up.

In any unfamiliar mechanism there is an overhead of the learning curve.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Combat programming (was Alphabets)

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)

Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>Robert Wagner wrote:

>It may well be that you produce much more bug-free code, but how much is 
>simply because of 30+ years of doing it.

Only some. I've mentored programmers to high proficiency in 6-12 months.

>Younger, less experienced programmers will produce less bug-free code, but 
>if you took away their debuggers then their productivity may go down.

For awhile, until they develop confidence in their ability to read and
understand. 

The productivity issue here is more than debugging, it is in writing code that
works right, or almost right, the first time. When I see someone running a
debugger twenty times, that's a Bad Sign. It means he doesn't know what his own
code does until the machine tells him. That's a very inefficient way to develop
new code. 

>If you used the debugger more frequently and got over the 80% setup time by 
>learning what to do and having the setups readily available, then your 
>productivity may go up.

Yes, I've been there on occasion (e.g. hacking machine language). 

>In any unfamiliar mechanism there is an overhead of the learning curve.

True.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Combat programming (was Alphabets)

_(reply depth: 61)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-05-23T08:27:58+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bajbst$u7q$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3ec9d447.65777476@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305201252.47f63821@posting.google.com> <3ecca96d.251443691@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:

> Assuming you have old code that works correctly, how do you "ensure all
> scope terminators are in place?" Don't you simply replace each period with
> one or more terminators? It sounds like we're saying the same thing.

Exactly.  I phrase my argument so that you would actually see that it is 
'the same thing'.  However, your _crusade_ is against full stops when this 
is not the real problem.  The 'problem' is not using modern structuring 
mechanisms.

Having the full stops disappear is a side issue, and not of any real 
importance.

Now having full stops on the end of statements is something that I would 
crusade against.  They should be on a line by themselves, but that is a 
different argument than yours of trying to eliminate all trace of them.
 
>>When there is no structural need for the full stop they may be used
>>for other purposes, such as having them after a deeply nested IF on
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> If ALL scope terminators are in place, why is the full stop a good idea?

What did I just say ? - it is a signal to the next programmer that this 
_is_ back to zero scope and this should be ensured.

> As a safety feature, just in case they're not? What happens when you paste
> the flawed block into a larger statement? 

It _isn't_ a 'flawed block'.  It is a block that is correctly terminated 
back to zero scope an _then_ has a full stop on its own line as a signal 
that this is and must be the case.

> All code following it becomes 'broken'.

Only if you contrive some case that doesn't exist.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Combat programming (was Alphabets)

_(reply depth: 62)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-05-22T20:51:48+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bajd94$n2k$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3ec9d447.65777476@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305201252.47f63821@posting.google.com> <3ecca96d.251443691@news.optonline.net> <bajbst$u7q$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```

On 22-May-2003, Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

> > If ALL scope terminators are in place, why is the full stop a good idea?
>
> What did I just say ? - it is a signal to the next programmer that this
> _is_ back to zero scope and this should be ensured.

A maintenance programmer seeing a full stop line knows immediately that the code
he is looking at isn't part of any earlier code.   It's like having a paragraph
without the label.   While it shouldn't do anything to the program (assume all
terminators are in place) - it tells the maintenance programmer that the code
preceding that full stop is separate.

It is similar to a blank line in readability - but 100% trustworthy.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Combat programming (was Alphabets)

_(reply depth: 62)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)

- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3ec9d447.65777476@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305201252.47f63821@posting.google.com> <3ecca96d.251443691@news.optonline.net> <bajbst$u7q$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>Robert Wagner wrote:
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>mechanisms.

I concur. But when I tried to teach modern structuring, CLC readers chose to
raise hell about periods while ignoring the more substantive material. 

>Now having full stops on the end of statements is something that I would 
>crusade against.  They should be on a line by themselves, but that is a 
>different argument than yours of trying to eliminate all trace of them.

I assumed we were talking about full stops on the end of statements. It would
have been helpful if someone had pointed out they're on a line by themselves. 

>>>When there is no structural need for the full stop they may be used
>>>for other purposes, such as having them after a deeply nested IF on
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>_is_ back to zero scope and this should be ensured.

Lack of indentation tells him the same thing. 

>> As a safety feature, just in case they're not? What happens when you paste
>> the flawed block into a larger statement? 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>that this is and must be the case.

I fear the full stop will encourage sloppy coding. Someone inserts one more if
into the deeply nested structure but fails to terminate it because .. what the
hell, the full stop will take care of it. Now I paste the broken if somewhere
else and fail to notice there are five end-ifs rather than six. All the code
following is subordinate to the last if.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Combat programming (was Alphabets)

_(reply depth: 63)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-05-25T12:40:08+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bap3bv$gf6$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3ecca96d.251443691@news.optonline.net> <bajbst$u7q$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ecfdc5c.143168718@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:

> I concur. But when I tried to teach modern structuring, CLC readers chose
> to raise hell about periods while ignoring the more substantive material.

No. They reacted to your insults about programmers who used full stops and 
to your annoying generalisations and statements of absolutes.


> I assumed we were talking about full stops on the end of statements. It
> would have been helpful if someone had pointed out they're on a line by
> themselves.

Would you have noticed ?  Didn't you notice ?  Did it take you this long to 
notice in spite of my (at least) referring specifically to 'on a line by 
themselves' several times ?

You have a habit of saying 'no one produced any cogent arguments' or 
similar when they were there to see.

> Lack of indentation tells him the same thing.

No.  Whan a case statement in Cobol is coded as a series of IF .. ELSE then 
indenting at the same IF level would cause the code to migrate to the 
right.  I commonly keep the IFs aligned:

     IF ( condition 1 )
         ...
     ELSE
     IF ( condition 2 )
     ELSE
     ....
     END-IF
     END-IF
     ...

This suits *my* style better - I dislike EVALUATE TRUE.  It is only 
necessary to count the IFs and add that number of END-IFs.
 
> I fear the full stop will encourage sloppy coding. Someone inserts one
> more if into the deeply nested structure but fails to terminate it because
> .. what the hell, the full stop will take care of it. 

You will find, if you examine some deeply nested code, that while the full 
stop may bring the code back to level zero, it will not make the logic work 
correctly around the deeply nested missing scope terminator.

> Now I paste the
> broken if somewhere else and fail to notice there are five end-ifs rather
> than six. All the code following is subordinate to the last if.

If you fail to check the code then it is your failure.
```

I used to feel the same about EVALUATE TRUE. Now my rule is 'one condition, use

EVALUATE always? I don't have an answer except tradition. 

>> I fear the full stop will encourage sloppy coding. Someone inserts one

>correctly around the deeply nested missing scope terminator.

It will when the unterminated if is the last one. 

>> Now I paste the
>> broken if somewhere else and fail to notice there are five end-ifs rather
>> than six. All the code following is subordinate to the last if.
>
>If you fail to check the code then it is your failure.

My goal is code that works; it is not punishing the guilty.
```

> >What did I just say ? - it is a signal to the next programmer that this
> >_is_ back to zero scope and this should be ensured.

One you can trust, the other can fool people.

Lots of people cut and paste code and often don't go to the work of aligning
everything correctly (some people even find removing periods to be onerous).

When you are searching for problems, it is nice to have spots where you
ABSOLUTELY KNOW are anchor points.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Combat programming (was Alphabets)

_(reply depth: 65)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-05-29T02:36:37+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bb17a9$v6f$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3ecfdc5c.143168718@news.optonline.net> <bb00dp$dl$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ed3f673.412031895@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:

> The only gotcha is that period at the end of the paragraph. I wish
> compilers had an option to raise an error when the level is non-zero at
> end of paragraph.

You can do that, put:

      paragraph.

          PERFORM
              nested if staements
          END-PERFORM
          .

Because the in-line PERFORM requires the content to be an imperitive 
statement and not a conditional it will complain if the number of scope 
terminators is incorrect (or should).  You may need to turn off extensions 
to get this error.
```

Robert Wagner wrote:
  > Japanese have abandoned kanji for daily communication. Its use is
  > limited to 'fine literature'.

Nani?  It's been a few years since I was last in Japan, but folks still
appeared to be using kanji in "daily communication" (assuming that means
things like restaurant menus, maps, subway signs, personal notes, and so
forth).  True, I did get one letter written solely in kana, but it was
from a six-year-old.

Take a look at, say, http://www.honda.co.jp/, with a Japanese-capable
browser.  Just the mix of kanji, kana, and romaaji I'd expect.

It seems to be generally accepted that Japanese is too homophonic to be
conveniently written with a purely phonetic representation.

You're correct. A phonetic syllabary is close enough to an alphabet for this

I guess the stopped H is one of the 42 phonemes. 

>It's a mistake to see written alphabets as purely phonetic.  The words

Some languages are almost purely phonetic. That presents problems when the first
sound changes due to tense, case or number. You can't look up non-root words in
a dictionary. 

>Consider the words "Write", "Right", "Rite", and "Wright".  All of
>them are pronounced the same but have very different meanings. 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>such as "constitution right" and "the right answer". 

I don't know. I'm sure there is an explanation for each case. 

"Wright" was once pronounced "work". People with names like Wainwork probably
changed it to Wainwright in an attempt to sound more dignified or correct.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Phonemes

_(reply depth: 40)_

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2003-05-08T06:52:04+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3EB9FEE1.C937F53F@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3eb5c014.5428448@news.optonline.net> <kDsta.1407$ER4.161315@news20.bellglobal.com> <3eb76766.113815115@news.optonline.net> <b984j8$c2h$1@panix1.panix.com> <3eb86c21.14703208@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:
> (snip)
> Preliterate words were comprised of phonemes which could have been expressed in
> an alphabet, if one had existed. Same for words when the writing system was
> hieroglyphic, cuneiform or logographic (e.g. Chinese).

I have read that Hieroglyphics had a large phonetic component. 
Chinese is an interesting case, because the language has very few
words in excess of one syllable.  Pitch is a large component of spoken
Chinese, which makes it almost impossible to render Chinese in a Roman
alphabet.  There's no way to indicate pitch.  

Pitch is present in English, commonly as a rising tone at the end of a
sentence that is a question.  

> 
> The number of phonenes in all natural languages is surprisingly consistant,
> between 20 and 40, usually around 25.

I believe this assertion is incorrect, based on a vague memory from a
college English course over 30 years ago.  I believe I was told there
are six different "k" sounds in spoken English:

Kill, Call, Cool
Skill, Scald, School

The point being that the "k" sound is modified by the presence or
absence of an initial "s", and by the type of vowel following the "k"
sound.

Also, there are two "th" sounds in English.  The "th" sound in the
word "the" is a voiced fricative.  There is a noticeable buzz to the
sound.  But the "th" sound in the word "thin" is unvoiced, with almost
a lisping quality to it.  Try saying "The Thin Man" while swapping the
two "th" sounds.  

There are also two "p" sounds in English.  Try holding your hand in
front of your mouth and saying the word "paper".  You should feel an
explosive breath on the initial "p".  Try it again saying the word
"cupful", and there is no explosive breath (sort of like the "b" sound
in "baby").  In English, the non-explosive "p" can never appear as the
initial consonent of a word.  But it can be the initial consonent in
some non-English languages (Korean, I believe).

I think the upper end to the number of phonemes is around 60 for an
individual language, and that English approaches or exceeds 40, with
over 600 unique phonemes identified across hundreds of human languages
studied by cultural linguists.
```

> sentence that is a question.

consistant,
> > between 20 and 40, usually around 25.

Is it too much to ask that people who are  having an "expert" discussion on
languages, at least ensure their spelling is correct  (there are two errors
in the above two lines)?

(It offends inexperts (like myself), who happen to love languages...<G>)

> I believe this assertion is incorrect, based on a vague memory from a
> college English course over 30 years ago.  I believe I was told there
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>
And yet, when I say them, they all sound  pretty much alike to my inexpert
ear...<G>

> Also, there are two "th" sounds in English.  The "th" sound in the
> word "the" is a voiced fricative.  There is a noticeable buzz to the
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Jeez, Arnold, I tried it for 10 minutes and I can't "swap" them, because
they sound identical...<G>

> There are also two "p" sounds in English.  Try holding your hand in
> front of your mouth and saying the word "paper".  You should feel an
> explosive breath on the initial "p".

Yes, definitely (Hooray!). But I also felt one on the next  "p" as well...
(does that count...?)


> Try it again saying the word
> "cupful", and there is no explosive breath (sort of like the "b" sound
> in "baby").

Yeah, a much tinier explosion (though not entirely absent).... Cool! So why
is that?

> In English, the non-explosive "p" can never appear as the
> initial consonent of a word.  But it can be the initial consonent in
> some non-English languages (Korean, I believe).

That would be consonant with my experience..<G>.

>
> I think the upper end to the number of phonemes is around 60 for an
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

What about Daffy and Donald? Toon Duck is a valid form of English...<G>

Dunno what the point of all this was, but it was fun doing the
experiments...<G>

Pete.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Phonemes

_(reply depth: 42)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-08T13:32:04-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b9e7rb$vdj$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3eb5c014.5428448@news.optonline.net> <kDsta.1407$ER4.161315@news20.bellglobal.com> <3eb76766.113815115@news.optonline.net> <b984j8$c2h$1@panix1.panix.com> <3eb86c21.14703208@news.optonline.net> <3EB9FEE1.C937F53F@worldnet.att.net> <3eba4bfa_2@127.0.0.1>`

```
<snip>
>
> > Also, there are two "th" sounds in English.  The "th" sound in the
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> >

One of the things that impacts whether two sounds are indeed separate
"phonemes" or just VARIANTS of the same sound as used in a specific spoken
language is whether or not there are two words that are distinguished by the
two sounds. (This is why the various "k" sounds listed in the snipped part
of this post are NOT considered "phonemes" in English - but may be in some
other language).

Now, the question is whether or not the two "th" sounds in English are or
are not separate phonemes?   When I was studying such things, I was able to
find one (and ONLY one) pair of words that are distinguished  by whether or
not the "th" is voiced.  Even these two are not always (by all speakers of
English) distinguished  by this.  Can you think of the two words?

Answer below:

  (this space left intentionally blank)





















  Either   (with "voiced' th)
    vs
  Ether   (with unvoiced th)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Phonemes

_(reply depth: 42)_

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2003-05-09T04:30:42+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3EBB2F3F.AFCB5C1A@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3eb5c014.5428448@news.optonline.net> <kDsta.1407$ER4.161315@news20.bellglobal.com> <3eb76766.113815115@news.optonline.net> <b984j8$c2h$1@panix1.panix.com> <3eb86c21.14703208@news.optonline.net> <3EB9FEE1.C937F53F@worldnet.att.net> <3eba4bfa_2@127.0.0.1>`

```
"Peter E.C. Dashwood" wrote:
> (snip)
> > I believe this assertion is incorrect, based on a vague memory from a
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> ear...<G>

Well, I have to agree with you.  I cannot really hear the difference
in those six "k" sounds.  But I was assured they were different!

> 
> > Also, there are two "th" sounds in English.  The "th" sound in the
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> they sound identical...<G>

I can swap them.  It's hard to do, and it sounds funny.  "Thin Man"
starts to sound like "Vin Man".

I once had a French teacher in high school (a native of France) who
asserted that the "th" sound was considered a speech impediment in
francais.

> 
> > There are also two "p" sounds in English.  Try holding your hand in
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> > some non-English languages (Korean, I believe).


Why is that?  I have no idea, but I suppose there is an
anthropological field of descriptive phonemics that notes the patterns
in a particular language.  It would tend to explain why a person who
learns English as an adult may have an "accent" that doesn't
disappear.  They have probably fixed on a limited set of phonemes in
their native language and mapped the closest approximations to the
subset of phonemes that are used in English.  But they're not exactly
the same phonemes used by native English speakers.  I'm sure I would
have a lot of trouble learning to speak Chinese, for example.  I doubt
that I could learn the phonemes they use, because I would associate
the sounds I hear to the most similar sounds I can speak.  
 
> (snip)
> What about Daffy and Donald? Toon Duck is a valid form of English...<G>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Pete.

There was no particular point.  I just thought it was interesting.

With kindest regards,
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Phonemes

_(reply depth: 43)_

- **From:** "Russell Styles" <RWS0202@comcast.net>
- **Date:** 2003-05-09T00:50:29-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Y8-cnW_NRK41riajXTWcow@comcast.com>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3eb5c014.5428448@news.optonline.net> <kDsta.1407$ER4.161315@news20.bellglobal.com> <3eb76766.113815115@news.optonline.net> <b984j8$c2h$1@panix1.panix.com> <3eb86c21.14703208@news.optonline.net> <3EB9FEE1.C937F53F@worldnet.att.net> <3eba4bfa_2@127.0.0.1> <3EBB2F3F.AFCB5C1A@worldnet.att.net>`

```
<snip>

> > In English, the non-explosive "p" can never appear as the
> > initial consonent of a word.  But it can be the initial consonent in
> > some non-English languages (Korean, I believe).



>
> Why is that?  I have no idea, but I suppose there is an
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>


    I saw a program on PBS that talked about this.  Your
supposition is correct.  They tested some (very) young children
of English speaking parents (American) to see if they could tell
two syllables apart, as spoken by a member of an obscure
American Indian tribe.  The two sounds are, to English ears,
totally indistinguishable.

    The child was seated (on his mother's lap)
in a darkened room facing away from a darkened cabinet behind dark glass
containing some animated toys - bear with cymbals, that sort of thing.

    They repeated one of the syllables repeatedly.  Every now and then, they
would switch to the second syllable.  After a time delay, they would light
up
the cabinet, and make the toys move.

    The younger children always looked in that direction BEFORE the power
turned on.

    The older children did not.

    The cutoff was quite young.  Less than two years * think.
```

>     I saw a program on PBS that talked about this.  Your
> supposition is correct.  They tested some (very) young children
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>     The cutoff was quite young.  Less than two years * think.

I heard of the same test with Japanese.   The nonsense sounds included "la".  
One year old Japanese children heard the "l" sound and responded.   Two year old
Japanese kids did not hear that difference.   They had learned enough Japanese
filter it out as irrelevant.

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>

"Arnold Trembley" <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net> wrote in message
news:3EB9FEE1.C937F53F@worldnet.att.net...
>  Pitch is a large component of spoken
> Chinese, which makes it almost impossible to render Chinese in a Roman
> alphabet.  There's no way to indicate pitch.

The Romanization of Chinese called Pinyin can include superscript diacritics
indicating the four "tones" in Mandarin (the written form of three of which
are also found in written French), but these diacritics are rarely used
outside of Chinese textbooks.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Phonemes

_(reply depth: 43)_

- **From:** SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.NOSPAM.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-11T10:56:40-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<kvosbvgplflslp26mbuqtnfh8oaq81e4ed@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3eb5c014.5428448@news.optonline.net> <kDsta.1407$ER4.161315@news20.bellglobal.com> <3eb76766.113815115@news.optonline.net> <b984j8$c2h$1@panix1.panix.com> <3eb86c21.14703208@news.optonline.net> <3EB9FEE1.C937F53F@worldnet.att.net> <b9e7gm$2l2g$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3ebce941.158495372@news.optonline.net>`

```
On Sat, 10 May 2003 17:29:56 GMT, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
enlightened us:

>"Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:
>
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>markers. French-English dictionaries which use them are frustrating.

Not all are.  The Thai dictionary is sorted by (and has to be) by tone
markers because 1 Thai word can have up to 6 (depending on dialect)
distinct meanings depending on tone.  There are 44 "letters" in the
Thai dictionary so the "letter" is a major sort and tone is a minor
sort.  I would expect other tonal languages would follow this
conscript as well.

Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

Next time you think you're perfect try walking on water.
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

Remove nospam to email me.

Steve
```

Of course, computerized sorting can be a problem in that everybody doesn't agree
what "alphabetical order" means when we have mixed case, markers, names with
prefixes such as Mc and Mac, or include spaces.

- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3eb5c014.5428448@news.optonline.net> <kDsta.1407$ER4.161315@news20.bellglobal.com> <3eb76766.113815115@news.optonline.net> <b984j8$c2h$1@panix1.panix.com> <3eb86c21.14703208@news.optonline.net> <3EB9FEE1.C937F53F@worldnet.att.net> <b9e7gm$2l2g$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3ebce941.158495372@news.optonline.net> <b9oc91$bf4$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```


Howard Brazee wrote:

> On 10-May-2003, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> prefixes such as Mc and Mac, or include spaces.

Interesting the last point you made. Certainly during my time in UK, (can't vouch
for how it is now), but alphabetical listings, and particularly telephone
directories made a distinction between Mac and Mc - can't recall which of the two
was listed first, but the Mcs and Macs appeared BEFORE any other "M" listings.

Somewhat surprised to see the N. American version where they are alphabetized on the
letter positioning - it was new to me, but can see it is more logical. (The only
minor problem with that is if you think the name is 'McMaster', and you don't find
it, then you have to go hunting through the 'Ma's" until you hit "MacMaster").

Still  not quite the same problem as trying to find the 'phonetic' Chumley or
Fansahw.
One local Unisys salesmen here years ago was astounded when I told him his name was
pronounced Fanshaw in the UK <G>

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Natural languages

_(reply depth: 45)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-05-13T14:20:36+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b9quvk$jhi$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3eb5c014.5428448@news.optonline.net> <kDsta.1407$ER4.161315@news20.bellglobal.com> <3eb76766.113815115@news.optonline.net> <b984j8$c2h$1@panix1.panix.com> <3eb86c21.14703208@news.optonline.net> <3EB9FEE1.C937F53F@worldnet.att.net> <b9e7gm$2l2g$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3ebce941.158495372@news.optonline.net> <b9oc91$bf4$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3EC03488.C02018EA@shaw.ca>`

```
I found the following in rec.arts.sf.written:

kamikaze@kuoi.asui.uidaho.edu (Mark 'Kamikaze' Hughes) writes:
>   It's not the same as algebra.  I dislike Lisp syntax, but nowhere near
> as much, and the semantics are logical enough.  (+ 5 (* 2 3)) is almost
> as readable to me as 5 2 3 * +, and much more than 5+(2*3).  I can
> remember several items on a stack better than one half-completed
> operation.

The mouse the cat the dog the jester the courtier the princess the
king smiled at favored laughed at petted chased bit died.
```

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-05-13T03:02:07+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ec031dc.23921937@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3eb5c014.5428448@news.optonline.net> <kDsta.1407$ER4.161315@news20.bellglobal.com> <3eb76766.113815115@news.optonline.net> <b984j8$c2h$1@panix1.panix.com> <3eb86c21.14703208@news.optonline.net> <3EB9FEE1.C937F53F@worldnet.att.net> <b9e7gm$2l2g$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3ebce941.158495372@news.optonline.net> <b9oc91$bf4$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:

>
>On 10-May-2003, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>Of course, computerized sorting can be a problem in that everybody doesn't
agree
>what "alphabetical order" means when we have mixed case, markers, names with
>prefixes such as Mc and Mac, or include spaces.   

Suppose I heard a piece of classical music called an etude. I don't know what an
etude is, so I go to a French-English dictionary It says there is no such word.
But ft I enter "�tude", it gives me the definition. What is the 'accent aigu'
represented by �? It means no accent, the letter e by itself. So why doesn't the
dictionary respond to the letter e by itself? 

Because techies assigned � an  ASCII code different from e. It's that simple.
Even though the words represet the same thing, they are unequal to the literal
minded techie.

�tude.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Phonemes

_(reply depth: 45)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-05-13T04:33:39+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3EC071BC.7F0028AF@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3eb5c014.5428448@news.optonline.net> <kDsta.1407$ER4.161315@news20.bellglobal.com> <3eb76766.113815115@news.optonline.net> <b984j8$c2h$1@panix1.panix.com> <3eb86c21.14703208@news.optonline.net> <3EB9FEE1.C937F53F@worldnet.att.net> <b9e7gm$2l2g$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3ebce941.158495372@news.optonline.net> <b9oc91$bf4$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ec031dc.23921937@news.optonline.net>`

```


Robert Wagner wrote:

> "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
>
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> ï¿½tude.

Jeez Robert why are you so clinically analytical about EVERYTHING.

I wouldn't know an etude if it got up and hit me ! But I'm guessing there are
probably many among my favourites, possibly from Chopin and Debussy ? I don't give a
damn if it is a sonata, etude, overture, opera, modern musical or whatever.

I don't hit the reference books to analyse what I'm enjoying. Does it spiritually
uplift me, whether it's something from Cavalira Rusticana, Chopin, the Russian
'greats', Madama Butterfly, the magnificence of Gustav Holst's 'Planet Suite', the
jingoism and mournfulness of Elgar, perhaps Eric Copeland (and he wasn't a Commie
!), the wit of G & S operettas, Bernstein's electrifying 'West Side Story', the
memorable music and lyrics of Cole Porter, Lerner and Lowe, or Rodgers, Hart,
Hammerstein  etc., etc or 'Etceterah' as in 'The King and I".

So you're 60 - roughly about two more decades. Throw away the surgical mask and
gloves, unless there's something SARS-wise going on in your part of the world that
we don't know about.. Get out of the clinic and just enjoy for joy's sake !

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Phonemes

_(reply depth: 46)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-05-13T13:15:13-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0305131159.7a50c302@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3eb5c014.5428448@news.optonline.net> <kDsta.1407$ER4.161315@news20.bellglobal.com> <3eb76766.113815115@news.optonline.net> <b984j8$c2h$1@panix1.panix.com> <3eb86c21.14703208@news.optonline.net> <3EB9FEE1.C937F53F@worldnet.att.net> <b9e7gm$2l2g$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3ebce941.158495372@news.optonline.net> <b9oc91$bf4$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ec031dc.23921937@news.optonline.net> <3EC071BC.7F0028AF@shaw.ca>`

```
"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote

> Jeez Robert why are you so clinically analytical about EVERYTHING.

I would have thought it more appropriate to use just the first 4
letters (that is 'four' for those who dislike logographs) of one of
those words.

There has been a recurring theme in his complaints about many things:
he failed.

Instead of working out why he failed and adapting in a process called
learning, he spent his effort on working out who to blame - it was
unacceptable that it would be RW.

He mixed up < and > so it it techies who don't know linguistic history
to be blamed.

He didn't clear some fields properly so it was INITIALISE to blame.

His first 1401 program didn't work so it was 'ignorant hardware
designers' to blame.

In order to justify this blaming he constructs arguments based on
fragments he has picked up and makes up the connections: he is trying
to show that '<' is older than 'less than' (or at least the form is)
and thus using the words is 'more advanced'.  This would mean that his
failure about '<' and '>' is because they are retrograde, it is the
programmer that wrote them's fault.

Now it seems he can't get a job because 'Cobol is obsolete'. That is
_our_ fault, and the J4's.  If we and J4 had forced him to learn OO
Cobol a decade ago and forced all code to be rewritten in lower case
with no full stops then it would be seen as a modern language and RW
would get a job.  This is what RW meant by his wanting to 'rub our
noses in it' - he wants to prove it us to blame.

There is only one person not to blame for all RW's failures.
```

Robert Wagner wrote:

> Because techies assigned ï¿œ an  ASCII code different from e. It's that
> simple. Even though the words represet the same thing, they are unequal to
> the literal minded techie.

Duuhh. ASCII is _AMERICAN_ Standard Code for Interchange of Information. It 
doesn't define anything at all about accented letters.

Also if two characters are given the same code as you seem to suggest then 
how would there be any indication of which to display ?

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Phonemes

_(reply depth: 41)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-05-10T17:29:56+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ebcd731.153870746@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3eb5c014.5428448@news.optonline.net> <kDsta.1407$ER4.161315@news20.bellglobal.com> <3eb76766.113815115@news.optonline.net> <b984j8$c2h$1@panix1.panix.com> <3eb86c21.14703208@news.optonline.net> <3EB9FEE1.C937F53F@worldnet.att.net>`

```
Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net> wrote:

>Robert Wagner wrote:
>> (snip)
>> Preliterate words were comprised of phonemes which could have been expressed
in
>> an alphabet, if one had existed. Same for words when the writing system was
>> hieroglyphic, cuneiform or logographic (e.g. Chinese).
…[42 more quoted lines elided]…
>studied by cultural linguists.

One reference (Columbia) says 42 phonemes are used in English dictionary
pronunciations. I should have said most alphabets contain about 25 letters.
Syllabic systems such as kana use about 80. 

I just found this discussion forum containing very knowledgable answers:

http://linguist.emich.edu/~ask-ling/archive-most-recent/threads.html

A current thread supports my notion that words are defined independently from
their spoken and written forms, by giving examples of switching alphabets (e.g.
Turkish) without changing the language.  Another thread on 'click' languages
contains interesting technical detail on how the sounds are produced. Several
posters included in that category the kiss and tsk-tsk sounds, which one writer
called 'paralinguistic', meaning alongside but outside accepted definition of
phonemes. 

Thanks for the information.
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b8ttnc$hnh$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3e98df71.30393272@news.optonline.net> <b815pt$362$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ea5c65f.9265079@news.optonline.net> <b865nj$ete$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ea71d51.97072274@news.optonline.net> <b88sun$o3s$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3EA8F0CB.BB80CBE2@worldnet.att.net> <c99d007f.0304290953.4eba78a2@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0304291737.118171b2@posting.google.com> <joe_zitzelberger-F026C6.08553501052003@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0305011416.540be8e@posting.google.com> <joe_zitzelberger-C2560B.08471002052003@corp.supernews.com>`

```

On  2-May-2003, Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com> wrote:

> Consider a table that must remain sorted and accept frequent inserts or
> deletes.  With a tree structure the insert or delete is mostly harmless,
> but with the table it required moving all subsequent elements down one
> to make a hole for the new element.

Alternatively consider a table that will always be fixed.   There will never be
inserts or deletes.


Different situations call for different tools.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sorting algorithms

_(reply depth: 30)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-05-02T12:19:30-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0305021119.2fa3e79d@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3ea71d51.97072274@news.optonline.net> <b88sun$o3s$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3EA8F0CB.BB80CBE2@worldnet.att.net> <c99d007f.0304290953.4eba78a2@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0304291737.118171b2@posting.google.com> <joe_zitzelberger-F026C6.08553501052003@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0305011416.540be8e@posting.google.com> <joe_zitzelberger-C2560B.08471002052003@corp.supernews.com> <b8ttnc$hnh$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote 

> > Consider a table that must remain sorted and accept frequent inserts or
> > deletes.  With a tree structure the insert or delete is mostly harmless,
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> inserts or deletes.

Or another way of doing 'inserts' is to have a field in the table that
is 'next index'.  A insert is done by putting the new item at the end
of the table and at the logical position linking it into the chain of
'next'.

No data shuffling, just follow the chain for sorted order,

This also answers another thread about why there would ever be a usage
index field.

> Different situations call for different tools.

Absolutely.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sorting algorithms

_(reply depth: 29)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-05-03T22:04:49+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3eb40ce4.90839988@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3e98df71.30393272@news.optonline.net> <b815pt$362$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ea5c65f.9265079@news.optonline.net> <b865nj$ete$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ea71d51.97072274@news.optonline.net> <b88sun$o3s$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3EA8F0CB.BB80CBE2@worldnet.att.net> <c99d007f.0304290953.4eba78a2@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0304291737.118171b2@posting.google.com> <joe_zitzelberger-F026C6.08553501052003@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0305011416.540be8e@posting.google.com> <joe_zitzelberger-C2560B.08471002052003@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com> wrote:


>Consider also a table that needs a large allocation for a worst case, 
>but the normal case is quite small.  Certainly you could preallocate and 
>let the paging system take care of of for your, but they would require 
>extensive knowledge of the target machine. 

Not necessarily. You can allocate it dynamically to the exact size you need. Of
course you need to know the number of entries. When loading from a database, you
can get it _quickly_ with COUNT(). When loading from an indexed file, do a START
on high-values, READ PREVIOUS and get the RBA of the last record. Worst case,
allocate it big, load it, resize it to actual. 

I liked the rest of your post. I was pleased to see someone defend dynamic
allocation.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sorting algorithms

_(reply depth: 30)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-05-04T10:41:14+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b91gjv$9g9$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0305011416.540be8e@posting.google.com> <joe_zitzelberger-C2560B.08471002052003@corp.supernews.com> <3eb40ce4.90839988@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:

> Not necessarily. You can allocate it dynamically to the exact size you
> need. Of course you need to know the number of entries. When loading from
> a database, you can get it _quickly_ with COUNT(). 

When 'loading' from a database it is already in a cusor, why bother putting 
these into a table ?

> When loading from an
> indexed file, do a START on high-values, READ PREVIOUS and get the RBA of
> the last record. 

The 'RBA' seems to be somewhat specific to an environment, IBM maniframe 
perhaps. Is it some sort of physical record address ?  How can you tell the 
number of logical records in a group from a physical address ?  Did you 
perhaps reorganise the file immediately before wanting a count ?

> Worst case, allocate it big, load it, resize it to actual.

Why bother resizing it to actual ?  In many cases this could require a 
realloc() which _copies_ the data incurring severe overhead.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sorting algorithms

_(reply depth: 31)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-05-04T02:22:41+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3eb46298.10592729@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0305011416.540be8e@posting.google.com> <joe_zitzelberger-C2560B.08471002052003@corp.supernews.com> <3eb40ce4.90839988@news.optonline.net> <b91gjv$9g9$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>Robert Wagner wrote:
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>these into a table ?

So you can search them many times, without sequentially reading through the
cursor. For faster access, you might build a tree or load keys into a table
suitable for SEARCH ALL. 

>> When loading from an
>> indexed file, do a START on high-values, READ PREVIOUS and get the RBA of
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>number of logical records in a group from a physical address ? 

No, it stands for Relative Byte Address. Dividing it by the record size gives
the number of records in the file. It is NOT a hardware address such as sector
number. 

I do this stuff all the time to optimize searches. Suppose the search key is
'ABC and DEF and GHI'. I determine the number of dictionary entries for each key
by doing a start on ABC followed by a start on ABC-high value. The word with the
lowest number of hits become the primary key to go against the dictionary. The
subsequent search is purely sequential -- does this record contain all three
keywords? 

> Did you 
>perhaps reorganise the file immediately before wanting a count ?

If you didn't, the allocation might be slightly overshot. Not a big deal.

>> Worst case, allocate it big, load it, resize it to actual.
>
>Why bother resizing it to actual ?  In many cases this could require a 
>realloc() which _copies_ the data incurring severe overhead.

That sounds like an artifact of some C library function. In the environments I'm
familiar with, an API call to resize the thing smaller simply frees the tail
end.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sorting algorithms

_(reply depth: 32)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-05-04T17:00:45+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b926ro$jd9$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3eb40ce4.90839988@news.optonline.net> <b91gjv$9g9$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3eb46298.10592729@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:

>>> When loading from an
>>> indexed file, do a START on high-values, READ PREVIOUS and get the RBA
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> as sector number.

If the file is indexed then the records need not be in any particular 
physical sequence (on many systems), this means that getting the RBA of two 
records indicates nothing about the number of records that are logically 
between them, only what is physically.
 
> I do this stuff all the time to optimize searches. Suppose the search key
> is 'ABC and DEF and GHI'. I determine the number of dictionary entries for
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> this record contain all three keywords?

With MF, for example (which you seem to use on the Sun), records are not 
necessarily written in the same physical order even if they are written 
chronologically in ascending key sequence.

>> Did you
>>perhaps reorganise the file immediately before wanting a count ?
> 
> If you didn't, the allocation might be slightly overshot. Not a big deal.

Or might be negative, or may be any number at all.
> 
> That sounds like an artifact of some C library function. In the
> environments I'm familiar with, an API call to resize the thing smaller
> simply frees the tail end.

Weren't you using a Sun ?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sorting algorithms

_(reply depth: 33)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-05-04T23:05:53+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

>Robert Wagner wrote:

…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>between them, only what is physically.

Whoops, you are correct. I neglected to mention this works only when there are
no inserted records. 

>> I do this stuff all the time to optimize searches. Suppose the search key
>> is 'ABC and DEF and GHI'. I determine the number of dictionary entries for
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>chronologically in ascending key sequence.

Why aren't they? I've never encountered that .. on MF nor any other indexed file
system. 

>>> Did you
>>>perhaps reorganise the file immediately before wanting a count ?
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…

I was thinking of deleted records, not insertions. My dictionaries are rebuilt
