>> That sounds like an artifact of some C library function. In the
>> environments I'm familiar with, an API call to resize the thing smaller
>> simply frees the tail end.
>
>Weren't you using a Sun ?

Yes. Are you saying Sun copies the memory segment? If it did, all pointers into
it would be invalid.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sorting algorithms

_(reply depth: 34)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-05-06T12:09:48+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b94a69$fre$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3eb46298.10592729@news.optonline.net> <b926ro$jd9$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3eb5828b.84319232@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:

> Whoops, you are correct. I neglected to mention this works only when there
> are no inserted records.

In other words, for all practical purposes, other than very limited 
specific circumstances, it is useless.

> Why aren't they? I've never encountered that .. on MF nor any other
> indexed file system.

I got the impression that your experience was limited to C-ISAM only for MF.

>>Weren't you using a Sun ?
> 
> Yes. Are you saying Sun copies the memory segment? If it did, all pointers
> into it would be invalid.

Sun uses a version of Unix SysV, from the C Software Development set for 
SysV:

"""'Realloc' changes the size of the block pointed to by 'ptr' to 'size' 
bytes and returns a pointer to the (_possibly_moved_) block. ..."""

Any use of realloc() should be aware of what may happen and should ensure 
that only the new pointer is used.

Once again your 'advice' is rather poor because your understanding of the 
issues is below the standard needed to get fully working code.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sorting algorithms

_(reply depth: 35)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-05-05T02:38:28+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3eb5c3d7.6391589@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3eb46298.10592729@news.optonline.net> <b926ro$jd9$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3eb5828b.84319232@news.optonline.net> <b94a69$fre$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>Robert Wagner wrote:
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>specific circumstances, it is useless.

It is useful for finding the search key with the fewest entries in the
dictionary. It is not useful for calculating exact memory size for a table.

>> Why aren't they? I've never encountered that .. on MF nor any other
>> indexed file system.
>
>I got the impression that your experience was limited to C-ISAM only for MF.

Right, over 40 years I've never seen another file system. Never worked with
VSAM nor the brilliant Realia file system. 

>>>Weren't you using a Sun ?
>> 
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>that only the new pointer is used.

No it doesn't. Sun Solaris' vnodes are nothing like SysV's vnodes. Granted, they
are closer to SysV than BSD, but they don't require a copy on resize. 

Over simplified, Solaris gives each task 2G of address space to play in. It
doesn't tinker with addresses within that space. 

>Once again your 'advice' is rather poor because your understanding of the 
>issues is below the standard needed to get fully working code.

That explains why my brain-dead code never seems to work right. I'm just an
Unworthy person. Should I end it all now?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sorting algorithms

_(reply depth: 36)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-05-04T22:23:33-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0305042123.4977db1c@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3eb46298.10592729@news.optonline.net> <b926ro$jd9$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3eb5828b.84319232@news.optonline.net> <b94a69$fre$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3eb5c3d7.6391589@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 

> >In other words, for all practical purposes, other than very limited 
> >specific circumstances, it is useless.
> 
> It is useful for finding the search key with the fewest entries in the
> dictionary. It is not useful for calculating exact memory size for a table.

 ... when used with C-ISAM on Sun and MF and only if the disctionary
file has been freshly written with no inserts or deletes and as long
as compression is not used and ...

 
> >> Why aren't they? I've never encountered that .. on MF nor any other
> >> indexed file system.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> VSAM nor the brilliant Realia file system. 

I should rephrase that to be "On MF your experience was limited to
C-ISAM only".

A large part of MF systems would use file systems where your mechanism
would not work at all.

You claimed to use Fujitsu too, with their file system, if you could
get the 'RBA' it would not indicate anything useful about the logical
relationship to other records.
 
> >Once again your 'advice' is rather poor because your understanding of the 
> >issues is below the standard needed to get fully working code.
> 
> That explains why my brain-dead code never seems to work right. 

Your brain-dead code may well work in the specific environment that
you are restricted to, but you were advocating these mechanisms for
general use without qualifying that they may not actually work unless
they are exactly like your system and your usage.

If you were to recompile your programs for, say, Windows they would
probably not work.  If you were to run them on another Unix they may
fail.

As an aside on realloc(), if it has decide to move the block, for
whatever reason that it may choose to do so, the original pointers are
not necessarily invalidated, they still may point into the heap where
the block was copied from,and the data may still be as it was before
the realloc().  The problem is that this has been marked as reusable
and another malloc() or realloc() may simply use part of that block
causing corruption.

It may be that you have done a realloc() (assuming this is what you
resize with) and continued using the old pointers with no significant
problems because you didn't do any more malloc()s until after you had
free()ed that block (or tried to).  Of course the free() of the old
block would fail silently and the new block would not get free()ed so
there would be a memory leak. It's unlikely that you would run out
memory though.

Just because a program gets to the end and gives the right results
doesn't make it 'correct'.

It is exactly because of these types of programming error (eg
continuing to use the old pointers after a realloc()) that causes me
to reject C style dynamic memory as a general solution for such things
as lists.

> I'm just an Unworthy person. Should I end it all now?

That would be most helpful, thank you.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sorting algorithms

_(reply depth: 37)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-05-05T11:33:24+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3eb6451b.39488382@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3eb46298.10592729@news.optonline.net> <b926ro$jd9$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3eb5828b.84319232@news.optonline.net> <b94a69$fre$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3eb5c3d7.6391589@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305042123.4977db1c@posting.google.com>`

```
riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>as compression is not used and ...

A similar technique will work on other file systems. The point here is the
concept of optimizing searches by computing the difference between highest and
lowest record. I got the idea from database optimizers, which do something
similar except they do it on the index position rather than the data position.
You can see them doing it on an EXPLAIN PLAN.

>Your brain-dead code may well work in the specific environment that
>you are restricted to, but you were advocating these mechanisms for
>general use without qualifying that they may not actually work unless
>they are exactly like your system and your usage.

None of us can test our ideas on EVERY platform before posting them here. 

>If you were to recompile your programs for, say, Windows they would
>probably not work.  If you were to run them on another Unix they may
>fail.

Or they work fine. You see the glass as half empty.

>It may be that you have done a realloc() (assuming this is what you
>resize with) 

Using the C function IS a potential problem, but for a different reason. Micro
Focus does not provide a cbl_realloc_mem. The C functions use a private heap
rather than the OS-provided pool which COBOL uses. Thus, the C function may be
unhappy with a pointer outside its private heap. If one plans to use realloc(),
it would be best to acquire the memory with malloc().
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sorting algorithms

_(reply depth: 38)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-05-07T07:41:02+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b96eqs$d45$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3eb5c3d7.6391589@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305042123.4977db1c@posting.google.com> <3eb6451b.39488382@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:

>> ... when used with C-ISAM on Sun and MF and only if the disctionary
>>file has been freshly written with no inserts or deletes and as long
>>as compression is not used and ...
> 
> A similar technique will work on other file systems. 

The qualification I pssted above would still hold unless the file system 
shuffled all the records to make an insertion.

> The point here is the
> concept of optimizing searches by computing the difference between highest
> and lowest record. I got the idea from database optimizers, which do
> something similar except they do it on the index position rather than the
> data position. 

Using the _index_ is rather different from using the data positions.  The 
index _is_ shuffled and kept contiguous and ordered, the data records are 
not.  

While there may be a small number of indexed file systems that write fixed 
length records contiguously and in strict chronological order, such as 
C-ISAM, many do not.  Also your 'technique' relies, not only on the 
specific characteristic of the indexed files, but also on having written 
all the records in one pass in ascending key sequence.

> None of us can test our ideas on EVERY platform before posting them here.

No, but many of us know that other platforms are different.  

You described it as if it was a generally applicable mechanism and failed 
to qualify that it is only useful in such restricted circumstances that it 
may as well be ignored.

It is almost as if you didn't _know_ how highly specific it was. It seems 
(from your continuing arguments) that you had no idea why it worked (for 
you) or why it would not (for everything else).  This is just another 
re-run of your 'indexed files are ascii flat files' nonsense.

>>If you were to recompile your programs for, say, Windows they would
>>probably not work.  If you were to run them on another Unix they may
>>fail.
> 
> Or they work fine. You see the glass as half empty.

Many of us know that other platforms are different.  For all versions of 
Fujitsu that I have seen it won't even get close.  For MF it _may_ work but 
only if C-ISAM is specified (it is not the default on non-Unix) and _only_ 
for your code, not for _any_ general case.

The only thing that I know about the 'brilliant' Realia indexed files is 
(as I have been told) it has no sharing or locking mechanism so so 
multi-user the files have to be opened and closed for every access (or 
group of accesses) so that others can interleave theirs.  This means that 
locks can only be held by flags on the records or somesuch.

This means that file access can be fast as no account need be made for 
other users, but it is all lost in open/closes.

Would it work for your code ?

>>It may be that you have done a realloc() (assuming this is what you
>>resize with)
> 
> Using the C function IS a potential problem, but for a different reason.

No. That reason _is_ a problem if not catered for.

> Micro Focus does not provide a cbl_realloc_mem. The C functions use a
> private heap rather than the OS-provided pool which COBOL uses. Thus, the
> C function may be unhappy with a pointer outside its private heap. If one
> plans to use realloc(), it would be best to acquire the memory with
> malloc().

It is essential to use consistent memory management actually.  It is 
possible that MF avoids cbl_realloc_mem for exactly the reason I have 
given, it is too dangerous to let children play with   ;-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sorting algorithms

_(reply depth: 39)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-05-06T08:00:23+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3eb75a3a.110441993@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3eb5c3d7.6391589@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305042123.4977db1c@posting.google.com> <3eb6451b.39488382@news.optonline.net> <b96eqs$d45$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>Robert Wagner wrote:
>
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
>all the records in one pass in ascending key sequence.

I know that VSAM, Realia and Btrieve write records in chronological order.
Unfortunately VSAM stores its ACB in protected memory inaccessible to the
application, so you can't get the RBA without resorting to assembly language.

You are missing the point. When optimizing a search, the program doesn't need
the exact number of records between two keys, only an approximation. If the
numbers are wrong by a large margin, the worst that can happen is choosing the
wrong primary key, thereby making the search run longer than necessary. The
'error' might cost milliseconds to seconds. That's not a disaster.

The alternative is no optimization, which makes EVERY query sub-optimal.

>> None of us can test our ideas on EVERY platform before posting them here.
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>may as well be ignored.

The principle can be adapted to most indexed file systems. You accuse me of
black-and-white thinking, yet you reject a good idea because it cannot be
plugged into every file system off-the-shelf, ready-to-wear, guaranteed to work.

>>>If you were to recompile your programs for, say, Windows they would
>>>probably not work.  If you were to run them on another Unix they may
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>for your code, not for _any_ general case.

This is FUD. It 'may' not work. If we limited out choices to certainties, we'd
still be living in adobe pueblos.

>The only thing that I know about the 'brilliant' Realia indexed files is 
>(as I have been told) it has no sharing or locking mechanism 

This is true.

>.. so 
>multi-user files have to be opened and closed for every access (or 
>group of accesses) so that others can interleave theirs.  This means that 
>locks can only be held by flags on the records or somesuch.

Not true. A crude approach is to open files with the equivalent of VSAM's 'share
option 4', which means no protection afforded by the file system, anything goes
(aka 'you bet your job'). Then create your own Lockfile in a RAMdisk on the
server, wherein you keep track of the keys open for update. That's how IBM
mainframes did it for years, where it was called Shared DASD. 

A better approach is to move all file io into access modules, which is a good
idea in any case, then have those modules executing on the file server rather
than the client. Applications talk to the io mods by sending messages through
the network. This is the genesis of the three-tier client-server model where the
first tier is the application, which does nothing but UI; the second tier is
Business Rules so dear to the hearts of systems analysts (running on an
application server for maintenance convenience); the third tier is the file io
modules running on the file/database server. Some shops call the latter Data
Access Modules (DAMs).

The glue that sticks them together is not trivial. My server-side message router
has more than 2,000 lines of COBOL source. 

The solution is not in file sharing, it is in architectural layers. 

>This means that file access can be fast as no account need be made for 
>other users, but it is all lost in open/closes.
>
>Would it work for your code ?

It has worked for my code. My message router could handle 500 messages per
second running on slow machines and a 10M network. Estimating average record
size at 100 bytes, that's half the raw network speed.  It even had acceptable
speed  over a dial-up network. It's not practical to do file sharing over a slow
network because transferring 512 byte sectors brings performance to a crawl. For
testing and single-user shops, client and server could be the same physical
machine.

>> Micro Focus does not provide a cbl_realloc_mem. The C functions use a
>> private heap rather than the OS-provided pool which COBOL uses. Thus, the
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>given, it is too dangerous to let children play with   ;-)

Compilers shouldn't be targeted to the Lowest Common Denominator. When they are,
some will find a way to circumvent their silly restrictions.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sorting algorithms

_(reply depth: 40)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-05-06T14:55:36-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0305061355.183e3702@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3eb5c3d7.6391589@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305042123.4977db1c@posting.google.com> <3eb6451b.39488382@news.optonline.net> <b96eqs$d45$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3eb75a3a.110441993@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 

> I know that VSAM, Realia and Btrieve write records in chronological order.
> Unfortunately VSAM stores its ACB in protected memory inaccessible to the
> application, so you can't get the RBA without resorting to assembly language.

And the mechanism is only of use if files are written chronologically
in ascending key sequence.
 
> The alternative is no optimization, which makes EVERY query sub-optimal.

You still don't get it do you.  The vast majority of systems won't
have data records physically ordered on the disk in ascending key
sequence.  The mechanism that you described is very specialised where
you seem to have every word in an ISAM file and the records contain
other words which exist as separate records and you want to find all
records which have two or more words together in the same record.  How
many do that ?  You obviously.  Anyone else have that exact
requirement (or similar) _and_ have every record in ascending key
order ?  I don't think so.

Yet you promote it as if it were a general mechanism, without this
there is _no_ optimisation.

> The principle can be adapted to most indexed file systems. You accuse me of
> black-and-white thinking, yet you reject a good idea because it cannot be
> plugged into every file system off-the-shelf, ready-to-wear, guaranteed to work.

No one is trying to stop you using this mechanism for the singular
application in which it may work if the data is carefully contrived.

It is not just the file system, it is that the data has to be
contrived to fit the problem, and the file contrived to fit the data,
and the problem is not one that exists generally.

In any case the example you gave required 6 STARTs and 6 READs in
order to determine some 'optimum' way to read records at one of 3
points.

The actual optimum may be to dispense with the 6 starts and reads as
being entirely overhead, choose one and just start/read.

Not only must the application be exactly as your, and the data file
exactly suitable, and the system able to produce RBAs but also the
performance characteristics must be such that 6 START/READ must be
faster than 'n' READ NEXT where 'n' is the difference between the
shortest list of records and the average list.

In other words you haven't shown that even for your tiny corner of
applicability that is _is_ an 'optimisation' rather than some 'clever'
trick you are trying to use to attempt to impress.

So how do you get MF on Sun to give you the RBA ?


> Not true. A crude approach is to open files with the equivalent of VSAM's 'share
> option 4', which means no protection afforded by the file system, anything goes
> (aka 'you bet your job'). Then create your own Lockfile in a RAMdisk on the
> server, wherein you keep track of the keys open for update. 

While this may prevent corruption of data records due to multiple
users accessing the same record simultaneously, it won't neccessarily
prevent the index records from being corrupted if two users
simultaneously insert or delete different records.

> That's how IBM
> mainframes did it for years, where it was called Shared DASD. 

I bet they did something specific with atomic updates to the index
too.
 
> The solution is not in file sharing, it is in architectural layers. 

With products such as MF File Share this is automagic.  The file I-O
(eg READ and WRITE statments) is directed to the server. Fujitsu do
similar. SQL is the same.  Doing it for Realia would seem to require
that Cobol READ/WRITE statements be replaced with a CALL mechanism.

> Compilers shouldn't be targeted to the Lowest Common Denominator. When they are,
> some will find a way to circumvent their silly restrictions.

Yes and children will continue to play with matches.
```

riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 
>
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>many do that ? 

Many do that. Think of a Web search engine like Google. Now scale it down to
finding a product in the company's catalog (widget left handed thread) or a
customer (Acme Zealand) or an invoice (John Smith 3/01/2003). My application is
finding stocks and bonds in a reference file containing 6M entries.

It seems to me almost needs to search large reference files. If it's a database,
you use SQL (name like john% and name like smith% and invoice-date = 3/01/2003).
If you're using COBOL indexed files you can produce similar functionality using
the technique I described, or you can burden the user with an awkward query
interface, or your queries can run too slowly.

>In any case the example you gave required 6 STARTs and 6 READs in
>order to determine some 'optimum' way to read records at one of 3
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>being entirely overhead, choose one and just start/read.

Version 1.0 did exactly that. There are more than 500,000 entries containing
FNMA (Federal National Mortgage Association) or Federal, so queries took
minutes. Searching on a specific interest rate or maturity date reduced it to
milliseconds. 

>Not only must the application be exactly as your, and the data file
>exactly suitable, and the system able to produce RBAs but also the
>performance characteristics must be such that 6 START/READ must be
>faster than 'n' READ NEXT where 'n' is the difference between the
>shortest list of records and the average list.

You demonstrated that n = 12, that the difference between START/READ and READ
NEXT is 2:1. 

>So how do you get MF on Sun to give you the RBA ?

I don't have the code handy at home. I'll post it tomorrow. 

>> Not true. A crude approach is to open files with the equivalent of VSAM's
'share
>> option 4', which means no protection afforded by the file system, anything
goes
>> (aka 'you bet your job'). Then create your own Lockfile in a RAMdisk on the
>> server, wherein you keep track of the keys open for update. 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>simultaneously insert or delete different records.

Good point. When multiple clients are cacheing index sectors, I see where that
could be a problem. It was never a problem to me, but then I didn't use that
approach for more than a few months before switching to the client-server model.
>
>> That's how IBM
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>too.

Yes. They didn't lock record keys, they locked disk sectors, which included
indexes. It was a PITA because records seemingly far removed from yours could
cause yours to be locked. 

>> The solution is not in file sharing, it is in architectural layers. 
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>that Cobol READ/WRITE statements be replaced with a CALL mechanism.

File io should already be encapsulated in an 'io module' called by the
application. Moving to client-server requires replacing the client-side io
module with a communication thing and moving the io module to execute on the
file server. 

>> Compilers shouldn't be targeted to the Lowest Common Denominator. When they
are,
>> some will find a way to circumvent their silly restrictions.
>
>Yes and children will continue to play with matches.

Spoken like a sysadmin who regards programmers as children .. and then wonders
why they don't respect him. 

----
A good way to do this on databases is forget about locks. Create a SERIAL field
with the version number of the row. Write a trigger to update it on each update.
Now read the row, including the version. When you're ready to update say "WHERE
... and version = x". If the version number has changed since your read, the
update will fail.
```

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 

> >You still don't get it do you.  The vast majority of systems won't
> >have data records physically ordered on the disk in ascending key

> Many do that. Think of a Web search engine like Google. 

Do you really think that Google is going to manage to keep its data
file physically in ascending key sequence ?

> Now scale it down to
> finding a product in the company's catalog (widget left handed thread) or a
> customer (Acme Zealand) or an invoice (John Smith 3/01/2003). 

On some systems, notably those that have web access, I have a word
file for the products that is updated as products are added and
changed.  This is a dynamic file that is multi-user.

In order to use your 'optimisation' I would need to keep the word file
in physical sequence of ascending keys _and_ have it in an indexed
file that would allow calculation of record count from physical
position.

> My application is
> finding stocks and bonds in a reference file containing 6M entries.

Is it multi-user ? is it being updated all the time ?  perhaps you
never noticed  that between complete rebuilds your 'optimisation' gave
silly numbers.
 
> It seems to me almost needs to search large reference files. If it's a database,
> you use SQL 

Which optimises based on the index, not on physical position of data
records.

> the technique I described, or you can burden the user with an awkward query
> interface, 

Your technique makes no difference to the query interface at all.

> or your queries can run too slowly.

Your technique does not assure a faster run time.

(though it _may_ do so, or may not, in specific conditions where the
file system blah blah, physical order of records, blah blah - which
noone has)
 
> Version 1.0 did exactly that. There are more than 500,000 entries containing
> FNMA (Federal National Mortgage Association) or Federal, so queries took
> minutes. Searching on a specific interest rate or maturity date reduced it to
> milliseconds. 

All your technique did was to attempt to chose one set of keys over
another set of keys to use as the start/read for the search.  The
difference in length between one set and another may be significant
for your particular case, but I suspect that you are comparing the
'minutes' for the _whole file_ with milliseconds for _any_ of the
actual set of keys being read.
 
> You demonstrated that n = 12, that the difference between START/READ and READ
> NEXT is 2:1. 

A test _may_ produce this result or some other depending on many
factors. For example if the records are actually physically in key
order then READ NEXT is likely to be _much_ faster due to read-ahead
buffering than some random storage.

So once the file is in the order you require for your technique the
'optimising' may be less significant.

But using 12 as a number then it needs to be shown that given a random
search of type 'a and b and c' the average number in all paths
_differs_ from the shortest length by 12.  Actually it would need to
be more because a random choice would get the shortest path 1 in 3
times anyway, and the longest in 1 in 3.
 

> >So how do you get MF on Sun to give you the RBA ?
> 
> I don't have the code handy at home. I'll post it tomorrow. 

> >Yes and children will continue to play with matches.
> 
> Spoken like a sysadmin who regards programmers as children .. 

From experience, no doubt.

Children can show a tendancy to cling to odd or naieve ideas mainly
because they lack a foundation on which they can critically appraise
them. Once they have been educated they will have the skills and
understanding to know which ideas can be discarded and which need
testing.

People who cling to odd or naieve ideas and show that they don't have
a foundation on which to base a critical appraisal of them, and who
refuse to believe that their ideas are naieve or odd, eventually get
treated as if they are children.


> ----
> A good way to do this on databases is forget about locks. Create a SERIAL field
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> update will fail.

Why is this a 'good' way ?  What 'bad' problem does it solve ?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sorting algorithms

_(reply depth: 43)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-05-08T08:39:07+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3eba036f.118988725@news.optonline.net>`

```
riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 

>> Now scale it down to
>> finding a product in the company's catalog (widget left handed thread) or a
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>position.

No, just reorganize it monthly. Mid-month it will overlook insertions and
deletions. That doesn't matter for optimization decisions. 

>> My application is
>> finding stocks and bonds in a reference file containing 6M entries.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>silly numbers.

Yes it is multi-user. The dictionary is not being updated right now; it is being
rebuilt from scratch monthly. But the underlying data file is updated daily.

>All your technique did was to attempt to chose one set of keys over
>another set of keys to use as the start/read for the search.  The
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>actual set of keys being read.

I'm comparing minutes to search 500,000 records, not the whole file,  vs.
milliseconds to search a small number of records. 

>But using 12 as a number then it needs to be shown that given a random
>search of type 'a and b and c' the average number in all paths
>_differs_ from the shortest length by 12.  Actually it would need to
>be more because a random choice would get the shortest path 1 in 3
>times anyway, and the longest in 1 in 3.

You are missing the point by looking through a microscrope rather than a
telescope. If the number of query targets is small, the search will be fast no
matter what technique is used. If I 'waste time' doing six starts and read
nexts, response will still be in milliseconds. I'm concerned about long running
searches that take minutes. 

When the estimated search time is longer than 30 seconds, I put up a window
saying what it is and asking for permission to continue, with a suggestion to
make the key more specific. 

>> >So how do you get MF on Sun to give you the RBA ?

LIke this:

  $SET FCDREG

 LINKAGE SECTION.
 01  fcd.
      $SET SOURCEFORMAT"FIXED"
           copy '$COBDIR/cpylib/XFHFCD.CPY'.
      $SET SOURCEFORMAT"FREE"

open
 set address of fcd to address of fh--fcd of [file name]

start/read next
    move fcd-relative-key to ...

>> >Yes and children will continue to play with matches.
>> 
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>testing.

>People who cling to odd or nieve ideas and show that they don't have
>a foundation on which to base a critical appraisal of them, and who
>refuse to believe that their ideas are nieve or odd, eventually get
>treated as if they are children.

Do you educate children by flaming them?

robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote

> No, just reorganize it monthly. Mid-month it will overlook insertions and
> deletions. That doesn't matter for optimization decisions. 

You have a very shallow view of the critique of your 'baby'.  You
completely ignore the fact that the indexed file does not hold the
records in a way that getting the 'RBA' says anything useful.  It is
_NOT_ C_ISAM.

> I'm comparing minutes to search 500,000 records, not the whole file,  vs.
> milliseconds to search a small number of records. 

If choosing one of 3 paths gives milliseconds then why would you
search 500,000 records ?

> You are missing the point by looking through a microscrope rather than a
> telescope. If the number of query targets is small, the search will be fast no
> matter what technique is used. 

Exactly, so why would _I_ waste time using your mechanism ?


> Do you educate children by flaming them?

Did you think I was referring to you ?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sorting algorithms

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-05-09T02:03:20+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ebadce8.24242106@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3eb5c3d7.6391589@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305042123.4977db1c@posting.google.com> <3eb6451b.39488382@news.optonline.net> <b96eqs$d45$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3eb75a3a.110441993@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305061355.183e3702@posting.google.com> <3eb844b2.4606057@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305062149.7fc7b2b9@posting.google.com> <3eba036f.118988725@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305081244.7ed440a3@posting.google.com>`

```
riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>_NOT_ C_ISAM.

Worst case, if the RBA isn't useful, build an index-to-the-index containing keys
and record counts. Then go through abc* adding the record counts for words
beginning with abc. 

The important concept is optimizing the search, not the technical details of how
that's done. 

>> I'm comparing minutes to search 500,000 records, not the whole file,  vs.
>> milliseconds to search a small number of records. 
>
>If choosing one of 3 paths gives milliseconds then why would you
>search 500,000 records ?

Because an unoptimized search doesn't know better. It takes the first word as
the primary key. 

>> You are missing the point by looking through a microscrope rather than a
>> telescope. If the number of query targets is small, the search will be fast
no
>> matter what technique is used. 
>
>Exactly, so why would _I_ waste time using your mechanism ?

To give users faster response when searching an ocean of data. 

>> Do you educate children by flaming them?
>
>Did you think I was referring to you ?

This a rhetorical trick. You liken me to a child by referring to children just
out of the blue sky, without an explicit link. Then, when challenged, you say
'Who me? I never said you were a child.' 

The context is the link. If we were discussing teaching or child psychology, a
generic reference to children would be appropriate. In a technical debate, it is
a reference to the other party.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sorting algorithms

_(reply depth: 44)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-05-08T15:05:36-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0305081405.6d0f4abf@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3eb5c3d7.6391589@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305042123.4977db1c@posting.google.com> <3eb6451b.39488382@news.optonline.net> <b96eqs$d45$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3eb75a3a.110441993@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305061355.183e3702@posting.google.com> <3eb844b2.4606057@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305062149.7fc7b2b9@posting.google.com> <3eba036f.118988725@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote

> >In order to use your 'optimisation' I would need to keep the word file
> >in physical sequence of ascending keys _and_ have it in an indexed
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> deletions. That doesn't matter for optimization decisions. 

You failed to notice the several "and"s required. Quite often your
arguments seem to be based on the idea that the rest of the world is
just a bigger version of your desk.

Not all file systems behave like your C-ISAM in many ways. If it
doesn't put records physically in chronological order contiguously
then it won't work.

Also it may 'overlook' insertions where these are _between_ the range
ends, but not if they are the records being used as the end points.

Here is an improved system to replace yours that does it musch better
and works on all indexed file systems:

* allocate a numeric field for each key and alternate key.

* process sequentially through the file using each key and set the
designated field to the ordinal logical position.

* on inserts, for each key, do a read next and use that record's
number.

* if the read next fails (at end) read prior and add 1 to that.

* On determining distance between keys, if any give a number less than
some predetermined value then use that search rather than looking for
a shorter one.

* the number should be a factor of the number of ranges yet to check.

Advantages:
  * entirely standard Cobol '85
  * can be used with '74 by replacing 'read prior' with some other
  * can be used with any Cobol version (not just MF)
  * can be used with any indexed mechanism (not just C-ISAM)
  * will work with compressed data (yours will not)
  * can be used with any key or alternate key (not just primary)
  * does not get disrupted by inserts or appends
  * can be reprocessed while in use (rebuilds cannot)
  * doesn't waste more time optimising than the search will take
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sorting algorithms

_(reply depth: 45)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-05-10T17:29:57+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ebcf2bc.160922257@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3eb5c3d7.6391589@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305042123.4977db1c@posting.google.com> <3eb6451b.39488382@news.optonline.net> <b96eqs$d45$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3eb75a3a.110441993@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305061355.183e3702@posting.google.com> <3eb844b2.4606057@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305062149.7fc7b2b9@posting.google.com> <3eba036f.118988725@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305081405.6d0f4abf@posting.google.com>`

```
riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote
>
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>then it won't work.

In that case, I'd construct a word/count file .. or move to a database.

>Also it may 'overlook' insertions where these are _between_ the range
>ends, but not if they are the records being used as the end points.

In the improbable case where an insertion is an end point, I'll use the 'wrong'
word as primary key. You just gave me a useful idea -- if the number of records
is substantially greater than the estimate, abandon that key and use another. 

>Here is an improved system to replace yours that does it much better
>and works on all indexed file systems:
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>designated field to the ordinal logical position.

Good idea! Why didn't I think of that?

>* on inserts, for each key, do a read next and use that record's
>number.

That's useful only when insertions hit an end point. I'd leave it zero and have
the search program read next or previous until it finds a non-zero. 

>* On determining distance between keys, if any give a number less than
>some predetermined value then use that search rather than looking for
>a shorter one.

A further improvement might be sorting the input words on size and looking for
the largest first, because they're more likely to have a small count. It
wouldn't help me because the vast majority of my searches have only two or three
input words.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sorting algorithms

_(reply depth: 40)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-05-07T13:46:39+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b9b2nv$kj1$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3eb5c3d7.6391589@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305042123.4977db1c@posting.google.com> <3eb6451b.39488382@news.optonline.net> <b96eqs$d45$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3eb75a3a.110441993@news.optonline.net>`

```

On  6-May-2003, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:

> I know that VSAM, Realia and Btrieve write records in chronological order.
> Unfortunately VSAM stores its ACB in protected memory inaccessible to the
> application, so you can't get the RBA without resorting to assembly language.

FYI:    People often think VSAM is IBM's implementation of ISAM.    This is not
correct.  VSAM is a file management system that includes KSDS, RRDS, and ESDS
files for Keyed, Relative, and flat files.    KSDS was much more efficient than
IBM's ISAM, so ISAM has pretty much died out in IBM mainframes.    But I haven't
seen an RRDS or ESDS file in a long time, and even KSDS files are dying out as
they are replaced by DBMS's.

I still see flat files a lot - just not ESDS VSAM flat files.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sorting algorithms

_(reply depth: 41)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-05-09T09:46:06+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ebb6a4c.60443615@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3eb5c3d7.6391589@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305042123.4977db1c@posting.google.com> <3eb6451b.39488382@news.optonline.net> <b96eqs$d45$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3eb75a3a.110441993@news.optonline.net> <b9b2nv$kj1$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:

>
>On  6-May-2003, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>IBM's ISAM, so ISAM has pretty much died out in IBM mainframes.    But I
haven't
>seen an RRDS or ESDS file in a long time, and even KSDS files are dying out as
>they are replaced by DBMS's.
>
>I still see flat files a lot - just not ESDS VSAM flat files.

My experience has been the same. 

I can think of only one reason to use ESDS rather than QSAM -- packaging files
in a DB2 catalog for convenience of backup and portability. The ability to
package VSAM inside DB2 seems to be seldom used. KSDS files stored this way also
benefit from superior management tools found in the DB2 world, for example
Omegamon.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ CICS and I/O (was: Sorting algorithms

_(reply depth: 44)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-09T23:37:53-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b9hvn1$c7j$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3eb5c3d7.6391589@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305042123.4977db1c@posting.google.com> <3eb6451b.39488382@news.optonline.net> <b96eqs$d45$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3eb75a3a.110441993@news.optonline.net> <b9b2nv$kj1$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ebb6a4c.60443615@news.optonline.net> <c99d007f.0305090656.7cbcf8@posting.google.com> <3ebc38c5.113308727@news.optonline.net>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3ebc38c5.113308727@news.optonline.net...
> joe_zitzelberger@vsam.org (Joe Zitzelberger) wrote:
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> >> >
<snip>
> >ESDS files have a huge advantage over QSAM -- they can be used in CICS...
> >
> >Being otherwise identical I use them all the time.
>
> Why can't QSAM files be used in CICS programs? Just write a SELECT and FD
like
> you would in a batch program. Assuming the file is short and defined in
the
> region's JCL, it shouldn't cause a problem. Am I missing something?
>

Yes, you are missing a LOT.  CICS (in MVS-ish and VSE-ish environments - not
so much in others) does a significant amount of pseudo-operating system
"stuff".  If you have an application program that uses "standard" O/S
services (such as those used for I/O) you will cause SIGNIFICANT problems to
your CICS region.  I won't go into all the details, but if you have any
"personal" contact with CICS SysProgs, they should be able to give you more
detail on this.

Bottom-Line:
  Thou SHALT NOT use non-CICS file access within an online CICS program.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: CICS and I/O (was: Sorting algorithms

_(reply depth: 45)_

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2003-05-10T07:40:12+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3EBCAD28.FA89862@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3eb5c3d7.6391589@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305042123.4977db1c@posting.google.com> <3eb6451b.39488382@news.optonline.net> <b96eqs$d45$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3eb75a3a.110441993@news.optonline.net> <b9b2nv$kj1$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ebb6a4c.60443615@news.optonline.net> <c99d007f.0305090656.7cbcf8@posting.google.com> <3ebc38c5.113308727@news.optonline.net> <b9hvn1$c7j$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" wrote:
> 
> "Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
…[33 more quoted lines elided]…
>  wmklein <at> ix.netcom.com

I have used QSAM files in CICS, but you cannot use normal COBOL
statements to access them.  You have to define DCT (Destination
Control Table) entries, and then you use EXEC CICS commands to read or
write them as Transient Data Queues.

I support an application that uses this technique to read application
configuration data (control cards) from a flat file or a member in a
PDS (partitioned data set).  We also use the same technique to write
to a Network Log file.  

But on balance I would say that ESDS VSAM files are much more
convenient to use, because a single ESDS file could be made available
for READ, WRITE, UPDATE, and BROWSE access (obviously, you cannot
DELETE a record from an ESDS VSAM file).  When using QSAM files in
CICS, generally you can write to an output file, but not read it, or
you can read from an input file, but not write to it.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: CICS and I/O (was: Sorting algorithms

_(reply depth: 46)_

- **From:** jacksleight@hotmail.com (Jack Sleight)
- **Date:** 2003-05-14T11:44:57-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8a2d426e.0305141044.6c9b1cab@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3eb5c3d7.6391589@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305042123.4977db1c@posting.google.com> <3eb6451b.39488382@news.optonline.net> <b96eqs$d45$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3eb75a3a.110441993@news.optonline.net> <b9b2nv$kj1$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ebb6a4c.60443615@news.optonline.net> <c99d007f.0305090656.7cbcf8@posting.google.com> <3ebc38c5.113308727@news.optonline.net> <b9hvn1$c7j$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3EBCAD28.FA89862@worldnet.att.net>`

```
Just to give this dead thing a kick.

From what I recall, PS files can be used in CICS. The thing that makes
it a bad idea is that the entire region goes into a wait state when
the access method issues a WAIT macro after the EXCP is issued, and
doesn't recover until the associated ECB is POSTed after data
transfer. This means all CICS transaction activity requiring the
services of the region is suspended until the POST is issued.

With the coming of multi-CICS regions, the effects may not be as
straightforward, but the principle is still operative.

So, while PS file use it possible, it's frowned upon and should be
used sparingly.

Regards, Jack.




Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net> wrote in message news:<3EBCAD28.FA89862@worldnet.att.net>...
> "William M. Klein" wrote:
> > 
…[51 more quoted lines elided]…
> you can read from an input file, but not write to it.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: CICS and I/O (was: Sorting algorithms

_(reply depth: 47)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-14T14:25:51-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b9u582$tn5$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3eb5c3d7.6391589@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305042123.4977db1c@posting.google.com> <3eb6451b.39488382@news.optonline.net> <b96eqs$d45$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3eb75a3a.110441993@news.optonline.net> <b9b2nv$kj1$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ebb6a4c.60443615@news.optonline.net> <c99d007f.0305090656.7cbcf8@posting.google.com> <3ebc38c5.113308727@news.optonline.net> <b9hvn1$c7j$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3EBCAD28.FA89862@worldnet.att.net> <8a2d426e.0305141044.6c9b1cab@posting.google.com>`

```
Jack,
  With currently supported OS/390 or z/OS COBOL compilers (e.g. IBM COBOL
for this-and-that and Enterprise COBOL), there is actually a "technical"
problem (or potential problem) with using "native" COBOL verbs of OPEN,
CLOSE, READ, WRITE, REWRITE against PS (or any other type) of files (rather
than databases).

All such I/O statements are "turned into" CALLs (at run-time) to various IGZ
routines from the LE run-time library. (e.g. IGZEQOC and IGZEVAM).  Such
routines are *ONLY* provided in the NON-CICS run-time library.  Therefore,
if you do *not* have the non-CICS library in the link-list (or comparable
access), then when a program running under CICS tries to "find" them, this
will fail.  This is in contrast to CICS-appropriate routines for other
features where there is an IGZ9 routine with an IGZE alias to perform
CICS-appropriate run-time behavior.
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3eb5c3d7.6391589@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305042123.4977db1c@posting.google.com> <3eb6451b.39488382@news.optonline.net> <b96eqs$d45$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3eb75a3a.110441993@news.optonline.net> <b9b2nv$kj1$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ebb6a4c.60443615@news.optonline.net> <c99d007f.0305090656.7cbcf8@posting.google.com> <3ebc38c5.113308727@news.optonline.net>`

```
In article <3ebc38c5.113308727@news.optonline.net>,
 robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:
> 
> Why can't QSAM files be used in CICS programs? Just write a SELECT and FD 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

You could cause a blocking IO that could freeze the entire region while 
waiting for your request.

You can use them in a limited sort of way via TD-Queues.  They still 
need to be defined in JCL, but CICS manages to avoid the blocking IO.  
ESDS files are free from all of the above problems.
```

- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3eb5c3d7.6391589@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305042123.4977db1c@posting.google.com> <3eb6451b.39488382@news.optonline.net> <b96eqs$d45$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3eb75a3a.110441993@news.optonline.net> <b9b2nv$kj1$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ebb6a4c.60443615@news.optonline.net> <c99d007f.0305090656.7cbcf8@posting.google.com> <3ebc38c5.113308727@news.optonline.net> <joe_zitzelberger-B5A794.09372010052003@corp.supernews.com>`

Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com> wrote:

>In article <3ebc38c5.113308727@news.optonline.net>,
> robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>waiting for your request.

Someone should inform IBM. They say it, along with batch-style dynamic calls,
which also freeze the region, are recommended practice. 

Freezing the region was more of a concern in the Good Old Days, when we had one
big region for everybody. Now that we have many little regions, the worst that
can happen is delaying one application by a few milliseconds.

In the long run, freezing the region once to load a called program is better
than the cumulative slowdown caused by thousands of LINKs.

>You can use them in a limited sort of way via TD-Queues.  They still 
>need to be defined in JCL, but CICS manages to avoid the blocking IO.  
>ESDS files are free from all of the above problems.
```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3ebdc2bd.214179934@news.optonline.net...
> "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote:
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> >> >You could cause a blocking IO that could freeze the entire region
while
> >> >waiting for your request.
> >>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> >recommended, prohibited, etc for COBOL programs under CICS.  See the
section
> >of the current CICS Application Programming Guide starting at:
> >
> >
http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/DFHP3P02/1.4.1
> >
> >NOTE PARTICULARLY,
> >  The introductory sentence,
> >
> >"With COBOL you must use only EXEC CICS commands to invoke operating
system
> >services. Some COBOL statements must not be used."
>
> That's Old School .. what we (well, most of us) believed for 30+ years.
Now IBM
> says it's ok to read small QSAM files and do dynamic calls out of a CICS
region.
> Unfortunately manuals haven't been updated to reflect that advice.
>
> Twenty years ago I was loading dynamically called programs into VSE GETVIS
space
> and calling them directly. The speed difference over CICS LINK was 120:1.

Always "interested" in what you learnt 30 years ago (especially when you
admit that the knowledge IS 20 years old). If you actually read current IBM
documentation, you would see that they *DO* recommend the use of CALL
statements over EXEC CICS LINK ... but that is because there is a separate
IGZE run-time module that "handles" CALLs under CICS withOUT using native
O/S services - but instead uses an approved CICS interface.

For *current* "performance" implications of LINK/CALL under CICS, see

http://www-1.ibm.com/support/docview.wss?rs=203&q=7001475&uid=swg27001475&lo
c=en_US&cs=utf-8?=en

where the indicate paper includes (among other things),

"Performance considerations using CICS (measuring call overhead only):
    One testcase was 445% slower using EXEC CICS LINK compared to using
COBOL dynamic CALL | with CBLPSHPOP(ON)
     The same testcase was 4547% slower using EXEC CICS LINK compared to
using COBOL dynamic | CALL with CBLPSHPOP(OFF)
     The same testcase was 753% slower using COBOL dynamic CALL with
CBLPSHPOP(ON) compared | to using COBOL dynamic CALL with CBLPSHPOP(OFF)

 In order to show the magnitude of the difference in CPU times using each of
the above methods, here are the CPU times that were obtained from running
each of these tests on our system and may not be represen tative of the
results on your system.

 CPU Time
(seconds)

   EXEC CICS LINK                                                 0.790
  COBOL dynamic CALL CBLPSHPOP(ON)           0.145
  COBOL dynamic CALL CBLPSHPOP(OFF)         0.017"

The only thing that is
  "Old School .. what we (well, most of us) believed for 30+ years"

is trying to decide what to do and not do in a CICS application without
reading the CURRENT documentation.

If you actually BELIEVE your statement,

 "Unfortunately manuals haven't been updated to reflect that advice."

Please submit a Reader's Comment Form (from the back of any printed IBM
manual - or available via email or the web at sites they provide).
Actually, I would (personally) be interested in WHAT manual think is a
current IBM manual that does NOT reflect "appropriate" behavior under CICS.
```

>
http://www-1.ibm.com/support/docview.wss?rs=203&q=7001475&uid=swg27001475&lo
c=en_US&cs=utf-8?=en
>
>where the indicated paper includes (among other things),
…[34 more quoted lines elided]…
>current IBM manual that does NOT reflect "appropriate" behavior under CICS.

I was responding to the quotation Joe posted. The document he cited also says:

"  Some features of COBOL 
    that are of interest to the CICS programmer are: 

 The ability to use COBOL CALL statements to call assembler language, 
 other COBOL programs, and PL/I or C/C++ programs."

With the addition of your explanation about a custom IGZE library, it makes
sense. CALL issued in a CICS program is not using an operating system service,
it is using a 'CICS service' albeit not wrappered by EXEC CICS .. END-EXEC. 

They measured the speed of a dynamic CALL (with push/pop off) vs. LINK at 46:1,
which is about half the difference I saw. That half is explained in the document
you cited, which says on page 18:
"Static CALL literal was 45% to 55% faster than dynamic CALL literal."

They do not say whether the measurement was made under batch or CICS. I assume
it was batch but also assume CICS overhead is comparable. In both cases it winds
up searching a table of loaded programs to see whether the target is already
resident. 

My solution made dynamic CALLs as fast as static CALLs after the first one. If
they had done the same, they would have seen 90:1.

See, my twenty year-old experience wasn't far off the mark.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sorting algorithms

_(reply depth: 50)_

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-11T16:00:56-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<joe_zitzelberger-8C8C49.16005611052003@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <joe_zitzelberger-B5A794.09372010052003@corp.supernews.com> <3ebd3d39.179994187@news.optonline.net> <3ebdc2bd.214179934@news.optonline.net> <b9ljo8$r9q$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ebe875c.264521910@news.optonline.net>`

```
In article <3ebe875c.264521910@news.optonline.net>,
 robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:
> 
> The two references supplied by Messrs. Klein and Zitzelberger say CICS 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> the time. 

This is a frequent source of discussion as many people did indeed learn 
that those were problems in very early versions of CICS.  It surfaces 
very six months or so on the CICS list under the topic 'should I allow 
dynamic calls' or 'should I use call or link'.

I gather that some early manuals listed a prohibition against Cobol 
dynamic CALLs and recommended LINK instead.  Though such recommendations 
were before my CICS time.

The problem did not exist in any of the versions I have used routinely.  
I've been working mostly in CICS on MVS since 1994 -- e.g. CICS 3.3 and 
above -- and the problem was not there.  And I think it did not exist in 
some of the versions I used occasionally (1.5 and 2.2), though it might 
have, I didn't do a great deal with those, I may not have noticed.

Anyway, the problem has been gone at least as long as macro level code.  
Cobol CALLs of any flavor are now full CICS citizens.

Cobol calls are serviced by the CICS runtime, which loads modules from 
the DFHRPL concatenation.  Programs marked RENT are shared in common 
CICS storage.  For programs without WORKING-STORAGE, or non-INITIAL 
programs that have been execute at least once in the current task the 
effective overhead is the same as a single branch instruction.

Access to the system linklist concatenation is not possible from Cobol, 
though you can still use assembler modules to issue LINKs and LOADs.  
This can cause your region to behave poorly.  Primary concerns are 
blocking the entire region while loading a module from the linklist and  
allocating storage to the CICS task for a module that might never get 
DELETEd.  I don't think you will find many (any?) CICS SysProgs that 
would approve of such behavior.

The CICS LINK command often gets thrown into this discussion.  Perhaps 
because of early recommendations to use it in place of CALL.  They are  
not the same.  Performing a CICS LINK is about the same as creating a 
new process and waiting for it to finish -- much higher overhead than 
the simple call.  On the advantage side, that new process can run in the 
same region, or on the other side of the planet, even on a different 
operating system and hardware.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sorting algorithms

_(reply depth: 52)_

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-12T09:39:54-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<joe_zitzelberger-BBE29F.09395312052003@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <joe_zitzelberger-B5A794.09372010052003@corp.supernews.com> <3ebd3d39.179994187@news.optonline.net> <3ebdc2bd.214179934@news.optonline.net> <b9ljo8$r9q$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ebe875c.264521910@news.optonline.net> <joe_zitzelberger-8C8C49.16005611052003@corp.supernews.com> <3ebef828.293402108@news.optonline.net>`

```
In article <3ebef828.293402108@news.optonline.net>,
 robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:

> >Cobol calls are serviced by the CICS runtime, which loads modules from 
> >the DFHRPL concatenation.  Programs marked RENT are shared in common 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> dynamic link is 50% slower on every call. 

No, but I was making non-language specific associations (BAS -vs- ATTACH 
& WAIT).  The extra 50% is checking to see if the module is already 
loaded...or, looked at another way, 150% of almost nothing is still 
almost nothing.  The difference is so negligible it isn't worth 
considering.


> >Access to the system linklist concatenation is not possible from Cobol, 
> >though you can still use assembler modules to issue LINKs and LOADs.  
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> concerned with the call than the load. 

The call, either static or dynamic, is incredibly fast.  Almost a BAS 
and nothing more.


> >The CICS LINK command often gets thrown into this discussion.  Perhaps 
> >because of early recommendations to use it in place of CALL.  They are  
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> machine? Almost never, right?


If you set up your regions the normal way, everything is on a different 
region.  (With no limitation on regions being on the same LPAR, physical 
hardware, etc)  Terminal connections run through the TOR, application 
requests are shipped to one of possibly many AORs, file access is routed 
through the FORs, you can set up special regions to deal with various 
other things as well.  The dyanmic load balancing that CICS/TS does 
makes it certain that applications designed to LINK only to the same 
region will quickly fail.

Of course, you can always just limit youself to a single AOR...but you 
will have lost all the benefits.
```

It sounds like you have been away from CICS since 2.2 or maybe 
3-something.  The OS LINKS and LOADS are gone along with OS-Getmains and 
a few others.  All of that is CICS managed these days, and they provide 
a replacement for each that works just as well. 

<opinion>
In exchange for following the rules you get the BEST and FASTEST 
transaction processing environment every created on any platform.
</opinion>

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sorting algorithms

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-05-11T16:07:54+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ebe6e5f.258123807@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3eb5c3d7.6391589@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305042123.4977db1c@posting.google.com> <3eb6451b.39488382@news.optonline.net> <b96eqs$d45$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3eb75a3a.110441993@news.optonline.net> <b9b2nv$kj1$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ebb6a4c.60443615@news.optonline.net> <c99d007f.0305090656.7cbcf8@posting.google.com> <3ebc38c5.113308727@news.optonline.net> <joe_zitzelberger-B5A794.09372010052003@corp.supernews.com> <3ebd3d39.179994187@news.optonline.net> <joe_zitzelberger-D4744E.21442210052003@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com> wrote:

>It sounds like you have been away from CICS since 2.2 or maybe 
>3-something.  

It's been three years.

>The OS LINKS and LOADS are gone along with OS-Getmains and 
>a few others.  All of that is CICS managed these days, and they provide 
>a replacement for each that works just as well. 

LINK and LOAD have always been CICS functions, and known for slow performance.
GETMAIN was also a CICS function, not to be confused with an OS getmain. 

Traditionally, CICS programs always did things through CICS. They weren't
permitted to talk to the underlying operating system.

CICS is like Windows 3 -- a wannabe operating system that runs on top of a real
operating system, doing its own memory management and task dispatching. The
rationale in both cases is screen-oriented services. Both require radical
changes in application program design to handle callbacks/events. I prefer a
solution like Flexus, which hides OS architecture from application programs.

---------------------------------------------------------

><opinion>
>In exchange for following the rules you get the BEST and FASTEST 
…[36 more quoted lines elided]…
>>
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0305111442.497f64ac@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <b96eqs$d45$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3eb75a3a.110441993@news.optonline.net> <b9b2nv$kj1$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ebb6a4c.60443615@news.optonline.net> <c99d007f.0305090656.7cbcf8@posting.google.com> <3ebc38c5.113308727@news.optonline.net> <joe_zitzelberger-B5A794.09372010052003@corp.supernews.com> <3ebd3d39.179994187@news.optonline.net> <joe_zitzelberger-D4744E.21442210052003@corp.supernews.com> <3ebe6e5f.258123807@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 

> CICS is like Windows 3 -- a wannabe operating system that runs on top of a
> real operating system, 

In what way was MS-DOS even close to 'a real operating system' ?

> doing its own memory management and task dispatching. 

Given that MS-DOS* never had any relevant memory management and task
dispatching, how else would this be done ?

> The
> rationale in both cases is screen-oriented services. Both require radical
> changes in application program design to handle callbacks/events. I prefer a
> solution like Flexus, which hides OS architecture from application programs.

In what way does Flexus SP2 _not_ give callbacks/events ?  It happens
that the design returns from the CALL rather than using a separate
ENTRY, but that is just a minor implementation detail.

*MS-DOS 4.0 and 4.1 (not to be confused with the much late 4.01) _did_
provide memory management and task dispatching as it was a
multi-tasking OS.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sorting algorithms

Peter E.C. Dashwood wrote:

>> Just because a program gets to the end and gives the right results
>> doesn't make it 'correct'.
> 
> I'm afraid it does in my book, Richard...<G>

It doesn't make it 'correct' if a small change can make it display bugs in 
another part of the program.

In the situation I described the addition of another malloc() would corrupt 
data being used by the 'wrong' pointers.

> That has to be my "Quote of the week" <G>...

I recall one quite simple program on the 1900 which was tested and found to 
work with all data thrown at it.  When it went into the production batch 
stream it went straight to end of run.  Back into testing it was perfect.

On the 1900 the W-S memory was not cleared, if there were no VALUE clauses 
the memory remained as it was from the previous program.  In testing the 
previous program was random, in the batch stream the previous program was 
always the same and always left a specific value in an address which 
matched the EoF flag in the new program.  Result was the program went 
directly to close down procedure and STOP RUN.

>> > I'm just an Unworthy person. Should I end it all now?
>>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> encouraged?

Geez, Pete, I'm sure he meant the thread.  Aren't you just projecting your 
fantasies here   ;-)
```

> >> Just because a program gets to the end and gives the right results
> >> doesn't make it 'correct'.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> another part of the program.

It has to give the right results and furthermore it can't give the wrong
results.


> I recall one quite simple program on the 1900 which was tested and found to
> work with all data thrown at it.  When it went into the production batch
> stream it went straight to end of run.  Back into testing it was perfect.

OK, if it doesn't give the right results all the time, then it's not correct all
the time.   That's usually what we mean - the clock that's right twice a day is
not "correct".
```

>Peter E.C. Dashwood wrote:

…[6 more quoted lines elided]…

A 'small change' can make *any* individual statement 'correct' or 
'incorrect'; this caveat is... curious, to say the least.  Consider:

In Exodus XX.15 it is clearly stated (KJV) that 'Thou shalt not steal.'

... and now, with a small change...

In Exodus XX.15 it is clearly stated (KVJ) that 'Thou shalt not sweat.'

From your assertion, since a small change - two letters! - renders the 
first statement 'buggy' then that first statement, as it stands, is not 
'correct'.

DD

<docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:b96hqm$4b$1@panix1.panix.com...
> In article <b96fja$ddq$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>,
> Richard Plinston  <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

in
> >another part of the program.
>

> DD

A long time ago, I read a "good book" which gave the
commandment, "Question the premise." If the context is
'small change' and 'program', why make '*any* individual
statement' a condition of the discussion? Since Richard made
no assertion concerning '*any* individual statement', your
stated conclusion seems to follow, not from his assertion,
but rather from your amendment of his assertion.

In any case, having considered the two statements, I find
that I cannot call either 'buggy' or 'incorrect'.

I think the second statement (NVI) is "Never let them see
you sweat." Or, perhaps I did not recall it correctly.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sorting algorithms

_(reply depth: 41)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-05-06T14:16:14+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b98g3e$6mg$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0305042123.4977db1c@posting.google.com> <3eb66609_2@127.0.0.1> <b96fja$ddq$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <b96hqm$4b$1@panix1.panix.com> <vbe2s7mnl4ni74@corp.supernews.com>`

```
One good thing that the OO movement has done is that it has forced people to
consider errors differently.    In most mainframe computers, we say that the
programs must be 100% correct, or they're not useable.    That isn't real life.
 What is needed is the ability to continue working effectively even with
problems.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sorting algorithms

_(reply depth: 42)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-05-07T03:00:28+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3eb86f93.15585189@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0305042123.4977db1c@posting.google.com> <3eb66609_2@127.0.0.1> <b96fja$ddq$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <b96hqm$4b$1@panix1.panix.com> <vbe2s7mnl4ni74@corp.supernews.com> <b98g3e$6mg$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:

>One good thing that the OO movement has done is that it has forced people to
>consider errors differently.    In most mainframe computers, we say that the
>programs must be 100% correct, or they're not useable.    That isn't real life.
> What is needed is the ability to continue working effectively even with
>problems.

On recent mainframe projects, I've seen many logic bugs in production code.
They're obvious just by looking at the code. 

When I point them out to managers, the response is 'nobody has complained about
it' (denial). When I point them out to programmers, the response is 'the code
passed our qa checks.' (Obviously tests didn't exercise that section of code). 

So I produce real-world cases where the logic went awry. They brush it aside. 

Why is everyone reluctant to trust logic to tell him what code does? Why do they
insist on some outsider -- the machine, user or debugger -- to inform them of
what their code is really doing?

It seems to me a Real Programmer should be able to look at code and SEE what it
does. Relying on feedback from outsiders portends poor quality code.
```

news:3eb86f93.15585189@news.optonline.net...
>
> On recent mainframe projects, I've seen many logic bugs in production
code.
> They're obvious just by looking at the code.
>
> When I point them out to managers, the response is 'nobody has complained
about
> it' (denial). When I point them out to programmers, the response is 'the
code
> passed our qa checks.' (Obviously tests didn't exercise that section of
code).
>
> So I produce real-world cases where the logic went awry. They brush it
aside.
>
> Why is everyone reluctant to trust logic to tell him what code does? Why
do they
> insist on some outsider -- the machine, user or debugger -- to inform them
of
> what their code is really doing?
>
> It seems to me a Real Programmer should be able to look at code and SEE
what it
> does. Relying on feedback from outsiders portends poor quality code.

I've had similar experiences.  I see a lot of people standing around
speculating about what the code does, or what it 'probably' does, or what it
'ought' to do, without actually reading the code itself.  When I suggest
that they stop speculating and RTFC, they look at me like I just escaped
from a straight jacket.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sorting algorithms

_(reply depth: 43)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-05-07T13:52:01+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b9b321$kqm$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0305042123.4977db1c@posting.google.com> <3eb66609_2@127.0.0.1> <b96fja$ddq$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <b96hqm$4b$1@panix1.panix.com> <vbe2s7mnl4ni74@corp.supernews.com> <b98g3e$6mg$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3eb86f93.15585189@news.optonline.net>`

```

On  6-May-2003, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:

> Why is everyone reluctant to trust logic to tell him what code does? Why do
> they
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> it does. Relying on feedback from outsiders portends poor quality code.

Except we are moving towards having objects that we cannot see.   We have our
code in black boxes that someone else maintains.   This is supposed to be a Good
Thing.

And it has happened in the past.   Early CoBOL programmers made sure to get
listings that included the assembly code.   With optimizers and better trust in
the compilers, this is much less common now.   We trust our compiler.    Mr.
Dashwood trusts his objects.   Not so much that we don't test.   But enough that
we don't look at the modules' source code.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sorting algorithms

_(reply depth: 44)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-05-09T09:46:05+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ebb713a.62217864@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0305042123.4977db1c@posting.google.com> <3eb66609_2@127.0.0.1> <b96fja$ddq$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <b96hqm$4b$1@panix1.panix.com> <vbe2s7mnl4ni74@corp.supernews.com> <b98g3e$6mg$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3eb86f93.15585189@news.optonline.net> <b9b321$kqm$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:

>On  6-May-2003, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>code in black boxes that someone else maintains.   This is supposed to be a
Good
>Thing.

Mainframers have always been leery of third-party code. For good reason. I was
stung many times. My rule of thumb was better to write it myself. Use
third-party only as a last resort, and insist on getting source code. 

But that wasn't true in all cases. We used third-party _system_ software without
qualm. In products like the BMC suite, code quality was so obvious that we
didn't need to peek inside for reassurance. 

In the non-mainframe computer world, software sans source is normal practice. We
don't hesitate to use large libraries, OLE components, etc. because we can't
'see what they're doing' by inspecting source. We can see it on their outer
surface. 

>And it has happened in the past.   Early CoBOL programmers made sure to get
>listings that included the assembly code.   With optimizers and better trust in
>the compilers, this is much less common now.   We trust our compiler.    Mr.
>Dashwood trusts his objects.   Not so much that we don't test.   But enough
that
>we don't look at the modules' source code.

I like your analogy between trusting the compiler and trusting the component.
```

Yes, I remember that (with affection).

We had a new 1901A that was delivered from Putney. As soon as they powered
it up it started running... doing the diagnostics they had run on it before
they packed it. After travelling half-way round the World, it still
remembered what it had been doing... (Ah, the days of non-volatile
memory...ferrite do-nuts threaded on wire  by hand, under microscopes, and
hundreds of Koreans having to wear glasses so IBM could make profits
equivalent to the GNP of NZ at the time...

I was reminded of Luis Ponce de Leon, who returned to his University after
five years of imprisonment and torture by the Inquisition, and resumed his
lectures with the words: "As we were saying yesterday..."

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sorting algorithms

_(reply depth: 43)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2003-05-06T07:25:50-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b9863u$kg3$1@panix1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <b974ng$m10$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3EB72D0F.A0856EB9@shaw.ca> <b97do4$q33$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
In article <b97do4$q33$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>,
Richard Plinston  <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:
>James J. Gavan wrote:
>
>> Me, 70 +, Pete around 60 and you around 50 ? 
>
>55 today.

Appropriate greetings and all that... personally I don't consider 
birthdays to be anything special, *everyone* I know has one.

>
>> Perhaps the group should be re-christened comp.lang.cobol.oldfarts <G>
>
>I'll be 19 till I die.

Hmmmmmm... consider:  A person of advanced years says 'I've thought the 
same way about this thing ever since I was a young'un!'

What can be found here?  On the one hand a young'un had the ability to see 
something with the eyes of a person of advanced years... and that can be a 
wonderful, impressive thing.

On the other hand... a person of advanced years is seeing something the 
same way as a young'un would, ignoring the experience of the intervening 
decades... and that can be a sad thing.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sorting algorithms

_(reply depth: 44)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2003-05-06T08:57:27-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vbfcrh17pbqgf9@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <b974ng$m10$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3EB72D0F.A0856EB9@shaw.ca> <b97do4$q33$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <b9863u$kg3$1@panix1.panix.com>`
